# TradeNet Admin Report — Improvement Tips

> **Security, Performance, Architecture, UI/UX, Code-Quality & DevOps review** of the Myanmar Ministry of Commerce *TradeNet Admin Report* platform (ASP.NET Core 8 backend + React 19 / Vite frontend).
> This document records every confirmed weakness found during a full-codebase audit, then turns them into a prioritized, actionable remediation plan.

**Audit date:** 2026-06-06 &nbsp;|&nbsp; **Scope:** `Backend/` (541 C# files), `Frontend/` (439 TS/TSX files), build & deployment, dependencies.

**Total confirmed findings: 122** &nbsp;—&nbsp; 🔴 9 Critical · 🟠 14 High · 🟡 45 Medium · 🔵 47 Low · ⚪ 7 Info.

## Executive Summary

### Overall risk rating: 🔴 **CRITICAL**

This is a **public-internet-facing government system** that handles trade, licence and permit data for the Ministry of Commerce. The audit found that while the *newer* reporting and Excel-export subsystems are genuinely well-engineered, the **authentication, secret-management and credential-handling layer is broken end-to-end**. Several issues are not theoretical — they are directly exploitable today by anyone who can reach the API or read the git repository.

Plainly, for a non-technical stakeholder:

1. **The keys to the production database are published in the source code.** The live database password (the all-powerful `sa` account) and the secret used to sign login tokens are written in plain text in a file that is committed to git history. Anyone who has ever cloned the repository — current or former staff, contractors, or anyone who obtained a copy — can connect directly to the production trade database over the internet and read, change or delete everything, and can also forge a valid login as any administrator. *Deleting the file is not enough; the credentials must be rotated.* ([`prod-sa-creds-committed-in-git`](#f-prod-sa-creds-committed-in-git), [`weak-hardcoded-jwt-key`](#f-weak-hardcoded-jwt-key), [`secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options`](#f-secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options))

2. **Every user's password is stored in plain text and can be read by anyone on the network.** A single web address (`GET /api/ChatList`) returns the entire user table — usernames, plain-text passwords, and permission levels — **with no login required**. The admin User screen also displays a password column and can export it to Excel. This is a complete compromise of the login system. ([`chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump`](#f-chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump), [`plaintext-password-storage-and-auth`](#f-plaintext-password-storage-and-auth), [`passwords-rendered-in-userlist`](#f-passwords-rendered-in-userlist))

3. **The "locked" doors can be walked through.** Login-token validation is weakened (issuer/audience checks are off), a generic base controller is marked *anonymous* and may expose the user table to unauthenticated callers, file upload requires no login and is vulnerable to path traversal, and the API documentation (Swagger) is open in production. ([`allowanonymous-generic-crud-base`](#f-allowanonymous-generic-crud-base), [`upload-unauth-path-traversal`](#f-upload-unauth-path-traversal), [`swagger-exposed-in-production`](#f-swagger-exposed-in-production))

4. **Below the security emergency sit real reliability and usability problems:** report pages can silently skip or duplicate rows when paging (a data-integrity issue for audit-grade reports), the entire frontend ships as one un-split bundle, the report grid cannot sort or search despite advertising it, there is no automated test/CI gate before deploying to production, and the production frontend container is broken. ([`offset-without-stable-order-by`](#f-offset-without-stable-order-by), [`no-route-code-splitting`](#f-no-route-code-splitting), [`no-table-sort-or-search`](#f-no-table-sort-or-search), [`no-dotnet-ci-tests-not-run`](#f-no-dotnet-ci-tests-not-run))

|---|---|---|
| 🔴 Critical | 9 | Exploitable now / secret exposure / data loss — fix immediately. |
| 🟠 High | 14 | Serious risk or near-term failure — fix this sprint. |
| 🟡 Medium | 45 | Should be fixed; meaningful risk or debt. |
| 🔵 Low | 47 | Hardening / polish / hygiene. |
| ⚪ Info | 7 | Notes, non-issues confirmed, or context. |
| **Total** | **122** | |

| DevOps, Build & Deployment | 1 | 1 | 6 | 3 | 0 | 11 |
| Dependencies & Supply Chain | 0 | 2 | 1 | 8 | 0 | 11 |

> **Note on the bright spots.** The audit also confirmed solid engineering worth preserving: the async Excel-export queue (`Backend/Service/ExcelExport`) uses the options pattern, structured logging, scoped `DbContext` access, atomic DB leasing and full `CancellationToken` propagation; the `_Fast` report paths use `AsNoTracking` + DTO projection and push paging into SQL; and the report stored-proc / Dynamic-LINQ paths are parameterized, so SQL injection is *not* present there. The remediation plan below is about bringing the *legacy* surface up to the standard the newer code already sets.

---

## How to Read This Document

**Severity** reflects real-world impact *in this specific application* (verified against the actual code), not a generic CVSS score:

- 🔴 **Critical** — exploitable now, secret exposure, or data loss.
- 🟠 **High** — serious risk or likely near-term failure.
- 🟡 **Medium** — meaningful risk or technical debt that should be scheduled.
- 🔵 **Low** — hardening, polish, hygiene.
- ⚪ **Info** — context, confirmed non-issues, or minor notes.

**Effort** is a rough engineering estimate: **Trivial** (minutes) · **Small** (hours) · **Medium** (1–3 days) · **Large** (week+ / structural).

Every finding has a stable **ID** (e.g. `chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump`). The roadmap and checklist reference these IDs, and each is a clickable link into the [Detailed Findings](#detailed-findings-by-area). Findings were produced by a multi-agent audit and each was **adversarially re-verified against the source**; verifier corrections are included inline where they refine severity or location.

Each detailed finding ends with a **🛠 How to Fix** block: the strategy, an ordered list of concrete steps grounded in this codebase, a code patch where applicable, how to verify the fix, and pitfalls/rollback notes. These were generated by reading the actual cited source, so file paths, method names and attributes match the real code.

---

## P0 — Fix Immediately (Security Emergency)

> These items mean the system can be fully compromised today. Treat the leaked credentials as **already burned**. Recommended order:

### Step 1 — Assume breach: rotate every secret now (hours)

The production `sa` password (`Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022`, server `203.81.66.111,14330`) and the JWT signing key (`"This is my supper secret key for jwt"`) are in `Backend/appsettings.json`, which is tracked in git **and present in history** (commit `07d95d8`). They cannot be un-leaked.

- [ ] **Rotate the SQL `sa` password immediately**, and create a **least-privilege application login** (not `sa`) for the app.
- [ ] **Generate a new random 256-bit+ JWT signing key**; deploying it invalidates all existing tokens (acceptable — assume they are forged).
- [ ] **Restrict the database server firewall** so `203.81.66.111,14330` is not reachable from the public internet.
- [ ] **Move all secrets out of source**: `dotnet user-secrets` in dev, environment variables / a secret store in prod; keep only a non-secret `appsettings.json` + `appsettings.Example.json`. Adopt the options pattern the ExcelExport subsystem already uses.
- [ ] **Purge the secrets from git history** (`git filter-repo` / BFG), `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json`, force-push, and confirm `.gitignore` now actually untracks it.
- [ ] Also remove the hardcoded SA password from `docker-compose.dev.yml` ([`compose-hardcoded-sa-password`](#f-compose-hardcoded-sa-password)).

Findings: [`prod-sa-creds-committed-in-git`](#f-prod-sa-creds-committed-in-git), [`weak-hardcoded-jwt-key`](#f-weak-hardcoded-jwt-key), [`secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options`](#f-secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options), [`compose-hardcoded-sa-password`](#f-compose-hardcoded-sa-password).

### Step 2 — Stop the credential leak (hours)

- [ ] **Remove or `[Authorize]`-lock `GET /api/ChatList`** and never return the `User` entity directly — project to a DTO that excludes `Password` ([`chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump`](#f-chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump)).
- [ ] **Hash all passwords** with `PasswordHasher<T>` / bcrypt / Argon2; look up by name then verify the hash, never query by password; force a reset/rehash for existing rows ([`plaintext-password-storage-and-auth`](#f-plaintext-password-storage-and-auth), [`plaintext-password-and-asparallel-auth`](#f-plaintext-password-and-asparallel-auth)).
- [ ] **Strip the password field everywhere it is serialized**: add `[JsonIgnore]` / a DTO on the `User` read path, and remove `'password'` from `UserList` `displayData` so it cannot be shown or exported ([`passwords-rendered-in-userlist`](#f-passwords-rendered-in-userlist), [`userlist-password-column`](#f-userlist-password-column)).

### Step 3 — Make authentication secure-by-default (1–2 days)

- [ ] Add a **global fallback authorization policy** (`RequireAuthenticatedUser`) in `Program.cs` so endpoints are protected unless they explicitly opt out ([`inconsistent-authorization-across-controllers`](#f-inconsistent-authorization-across-controllers)).
- [ ] **Remove `[AllowAnonymous]` from `BaseAPIController`** — in ASP.NET Core `[AllowAnonymous]` *wins* over `[Authorize]`, so the inherited attribute may already expose the entire `User` CRUD surface anonymously. **Verify at runtime and treat as Critical until disproven**, then replace the generic entity in/out with DTOs to stop mass-assignment ([`allowanonymous-generic-crud-base`](#f-allowanonymous-generic-crud-base), [`mass-assignment-baseapicontroller-put`](#f-mass-assignment-baseapicontroller-put)).
- [ ] **Add `[Authorize]` to `ChatController` and `UploadController`** ([`chatcontroller-unauth-data`](#f-chatcontroller-unauth-data), [`upload-unauth-path-traversal`](#f-upload-unauth-path-traversal)).
- [ ] **Harden file upload**: generate a server-side safe filename (GUID + validated extension), reject any path separators, validate content-type server-side ([`upload-controller-path-traversal-and-leak`](#f-upload-controller-path-traversal-and-leak)).
- [ ] **Enable JWT issuer & audience validation** (`ValidateIssuer`/`ValidateAudience = true`) ([`jwt-issuer-audience-validation-disabled`](#f-jwt-issuer-audience-validation-disabled)).

### Step 4 — Close cheap front doors (hours)

- [ ] **Disable Swagger UI / OpenAPI in production** (move behind the `IsDevelopment()` check or auth) ([`swagger-exposed-in-production`](#f-swagger-exposed-in-production)).
- [ ] **Stop returning raw exception text** from Login/Upload; return generic messages and log the detail server-side ([`verbose-exception-leak-on-login`](#f-verbose-exception-leak-on-login)).
- [ ] **Remove `console.log` of submitted credentials** on the Login/SignUp/PasswordReset pages ([`credentials-logged-to-console`](#f-credentials-logged-to-console)).

---

## Prioritized Remediation Roadmap

### P0 — Emergency (hours → a few days)

Active or trivially-achievable compromise. Stop the bleeding before anything else. Detailed above. **(22 items)**

| Finding | Sev | Effort | Area |
|---|---|---|---|
| [`hardcoded-prod-credentials-jwt-key`](#f-hardcoded-prod-credentials-jwt-key) — Production SQL 'sa' credentials and weak literal JWT signing key checked into appsettings.json | Critical | Small | Backend |
| [`chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump`](#f-chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump) — Unauthenticated endpoint dumps entire Users table including plaintext passwords | Critical | Small | Backend |
| [`weak-hardcoded-jwt-key`](#f-weak-hardcoded-jwt-key) — Weak, human-readable JWT signing key hardcoded in source and committed | Critical | Small | Backend |
| [`passwords-rendered-in-userlist`](#f-passwords-rendered-in-userlist) — Admin user list renders a cleartext 'password' column from the API | Critical | Small | Frontend |
| [`secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options`](#f-secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options) — Connection strings and JWT signing key read via raw IConfiguration from checked-in appsettings.json (no options/secret binding) | Critical | Medium | Backend |
| [`plaintext-password-and-asparallel-auth`](#f-plaintext-password-and-asparallel-auth) — Authentication compares plaintext passwords and misuses AsParallel() on an EF IQueryable | Critical | Medium | Backend |
| [`hardcoded-prod-sa-credentials-in-git`](#f-hardcoded-prod-sa-credentials-in-git) — Production SQL Server 'sa' credentials hardcoded in appsettings.json and committed to git | Critical | Medium | Backend |
| [`plaintext-password-storage-and-auth`](#f-plaintext-password-storage-and-auth) — Passwords stored and compared in plaintext | Critical | Medium | Backend |
| [`prod-sa-creds-committed-in-git`](#f-prod-sa-creds-committed-in-git) — Live production SQL 'sa' credentials and JWT key committed and still tracked in git | Critical | Medium | DevOps, Build & Deployment |
| [`userlist-password-column`](#f-userlist-password-column) — User list renders a 'password' column and can export it client-side | High | Trivial | Frontend |
| [`allowanonymous-generic-crud-base`](#f-allowanonymous-generic-crud-base) — Generic [AllowAnonymous] CRUD base controller exposes the User entity table over HTTP | High | Small | Backend |
| [`upload-controller-path-traversal-and-leak`](#f-upload-controller-path-traversal-and-leak) — UploadController is unauthenticated, vulnerable to path traversal, and leaks exception messages | High | Medium | Backend |
| [`inconsistent-authorization-across-controllers`](#f-inconsistent-authorization-across-controllers) — Inconsistent authorization: ChatController and UploadController fully anonymous; BaseAPIController marked [AllowAnonymous] exposing User table | High | Medium | Backend |
| [`swagger-exposed-in-production`](#f-swagger-exposed-in-production) — Swagger UI and OpenAPI spec served unconditionally in production | Medium | Trivial | Backend |
| [`verbose-exception-leak-on-login`](#f-verbose-exception-leak-on-login) — Login and upload endpoints return raw exception messages to clients | Medium | Trivial | Backend |

| [`credentials-logged-to-console`](#f-credentials-logged-to-console) — Login/SignUp/PasswordReset log submitted form values (including password) to console | Medium | Trivial | Frontend |
| [`jwt-issuer-audience-validation-disabled`](#f-jwt-issuer-audience-validation-disabled) — JWT issuer and audience validation disabled | Medium | Small | Backend |
| [`chatcontroller-unauth-data`](#f-chatcontroller-unauth-data) — ChatController endpoints are unauthenticated | Medium | Small | Backend |
| [`compose-hardcoded-sa-password`](#f-compose-hardcoded-sa-password) — Hardcoded SQL SA password and personal host path baked into docker-compose | Medium | Small | DevOps, Build & Deployment |
| [`upload-unauth-path-traversal`](#f-upload-unauth-path-traversal) — Unauthenticated file upload with path traversal and ineffective validation | Medium | Medium | Backend |
| [`mass-assignment-baseapicontroller-put`](#f-mass-assignment-baseapicontroller-put) — Generic CRUD PUT/POST allows mass-assignment of any entity field including UserController | Medium | Medium | Backend |

### P1 — High priority (this sprint)

Data-integrity, exploitable-with-effort, supply-chain, observability and "cannot ship safely" items. Schedule immediately after P0. **(27 items)**

|---|---|---|---|

### P3 — Low / hardening (backlog)

Polish, hygiene, accessibility, minor performance and developer-experience improvements. **(47 items)**

| Finding | Sev | Effort | Area |

---

## Detailed Findings by Area

### Backend — Security

> The backend has several Critical, exploitable issues. The most severe is an unauthenticated endpoint (GET /api/ChatList) that dumps the entire Users table — including plaintext passwords and permission levels — to anyone on the network. Passwords are stored and compared in cleartext, secrets (production SQL Server `sa` credentials and a weak literal JWT signing key) are hardcoded in appsettings.json and committed to git history, and JWT issuer/audience validation is disabled. CORS is misconfigured (AllowCredentials with a localhost wildcard), Swagger is exposed unconditionally in production, the file-upload endpoint is unauthenticated and vulnerable to path traversal, and login errors leak exception text. The 158 report controllers and the Excel export controller are consistently `[Authorize]`-protected, and the report stored-proc/Dynamic-LINQ paths are parameterized or reflection-guarded, so injection there is mitigated — but the auth, secret-management, and credential-handling layer is broken end to end.

<a id="f-chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Unauthenticated endpoint dumps entire Users table including plaintext passwords

**ID:** `chatlist-unauth-user-password-dump` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Broken Authentication / Sensitive Data Exposure &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs:15`
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs:25-41`
- `Backend/Model/User.cs:18-24`

**Problem.** ChatList is an [ApiController] with no [Authorize] attribute (class or method). Its GET action (GET /api/ChatList) executes `_context.Users.ToListAsync()` and returns the full list of User entities. The User model (API.Model.User) exposes Name, Password, and Permission as public string properties with no [JsonIgnore], so the response serializes every admin account's credentials verbatim. The result is also cached in memory for an hour, so it is served fast and repeatedly.

**Impact.** Any unauthenticated actor who can reach the API (and CORS/Swagger make discovery easy) can retrieve every admin username, plaintext password, and permission/role with a single GET. This is a complete compromise of the authentication system for a government trade-reporting platform: an attacker logs in as any admin, including high-privilege roles, and gains full access to all 158 reports and the underlying TradeNet production database. This is the single highest-impact issue in the codebase.

**Recommendation.** Remove this endpoint or, at minimum, add [Authorize] and never return the User entity directly. Return a projected DTO that excludes Password (and ideally only the fields the chat UI needs). Mark Password with [JsonIgnore] on the model as defense in depth. Re-scope: a 'chat user list' should not be the full credentials table.

**Example:**

```
// current
public async Task<IActionResult> GetChatList() {
    chatList = await _context.Users.ToListAsync(); // List<User> incl. Password
    return Ok(chatList);
}
// fixed
[Authorize]
public async Task<IActionResult> GetChatList() {
    var users = await _context.Users
        .Select(u => new { u.Id, u.Name }) // no Password/Permission
        .ToListAsync();
    return Ok(users);
}
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Lock down the anonymous credential-dump endpoint: add [Authorize] to the ChatList controller and stop returning the User entity — project to a Password-free DTO ({ Id, Name, Permission }, which is all the chat UI uses). Add [JsonIgnore] on User.Password as defense-in-depth so no other endpoint can ever serialize it, and drop the unused password field from the frontend type.

1. In Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` (only the Authorization one is strictly needed here) and decorate the class `public class ChatList : ControllerBase` (line 15) with `[Authorize]` directly under the existing `[ApiController]`/`[Route]` attributes (lines 13-14). This matches the per-controller pattern already used in ReportLookupsController.cs and the Report controllers — there is no global FallbackPolicy in Program.cs, so the attribute is what actually enforces auth.
2. In ChatList.cs change the memory-cache value type and the query so Password is never materialized or serialized. Replace `List<User>? chatList` with a list of an anonymous/record type and replace `chatList = await _context.Users.ToListAsync();` with a projection `await _context.Users.Select(u => new { u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission }).ToListAsync();`. Use a `var`-typed cache via the object overload (cache the projected list as `object`/`var`) since anonymous types can't be a generic type argument — see the patch for the concrete shape using a private record `ChatUserDto`.
3. Add a `ChatUserDto` record (Id, Name, Permission — no Password) inside the Backend.Controllers namespace (either a nested type in ChatList.cs or a small Dtos file) so the cache can be typed `List<ChatUserDto>?` and the projection is strongly typed. Note the cache key 'ChatListCache' previously stored `List<User>` with plaintext passwords in process memory — switching the projection also purges that sensitive data from the in-memory cache.
4. In Backend/Model/User.cs add `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` and annotate the Password property (line 21) with `[JsonIgnore]` as defense-in-depth, so even if any other controller returns a raw User entity the password is never emitted on the wire. Keep `[Required]` for EF.
5. In Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatListItem.ts remove the unused `password: 'string';` field from the `ChatListItem` interface (line 6) so the TS type matches the new DTO. The UI (ChatList.tsx / ChatListMobile.tsx) only reads `item.id`, `item.name`, `item.permission`, so no rendering changes are needed.
6. Follow-up (track separately, do not block this fix): passwords are stored and compared in plaintext — JWTManagerService.Authenticate (Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32) does `x.Password == users.Password`. Until passwords are hashed, ANY read of the Users table is a credential leak. File a hardening task to hash passwords (e.g. ASP.NET Core PasswordHasher / BCrypt) and update the login comparison accordingly.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs
+ using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;

  [ApiController]
  [Route("api/[controller]")]
+ [Authorize]
  public class ChatList : ControllerBase
  {
      // ...ctor unchanged...

      [HttpGet]
      public async Task<IActionResult> GetChatList()
      {
          var cacheKey = "ChatListCache";
-         if (!_memoryCache.TryGetValue(cacheKey, out List<User>? chatList))
-         {
-             chatList = await _context.Users.ToListAsync();
+         if (!_memoryCache.TryGetValue(cacheKey, out List<ChatUserDto>? chatList))
+         {
+             chatList = await _context.Users
+                 .Select(u => new ChatUserDto(u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission)) // no Password
+                 .ToListAsync();
              var cacheEntryOptions = new MemoryCacheEntryOptions()
                  .SetAbsoluteExpiration(TimeSpan.FromHours(1));
              _memoryCache.Set(cacheKey, chatList, cacheEntryOptions);
          }
          if (chatList == null || !chatList.Any())
          {
              return NotFound("No users found.");
          }
          return Ok(chatList);
      }
  }

+ // Password-free shape the chat UI actually consumes
+ public record ChatUserDto(string Id, string Name, string Permission);

// Backend/Model/User.cs  (defense-in-depth)
+ using System.Text.Json.Serialization;
  ...
  [Required]
+ [JsonIgnore]
  public string Password { get; set; }

// Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatListItem.ts
  interface ChatListItem {
    id: 'string';
    name: 'string';
-   password: 'string';
    permission: 'string';
  }
```

**Verify:**
- Anonymous request must now be rejected: `curl -i http://localhost:<port>/api/ChatList` (no Authorization header) returns 401 Unauthorized instead of 200 with a JSON body.
- Authenticated request must not leak passwords: `curl -s http://localhost:<port>/api/ChatList -H "Authorization: Bearer <valid-jwt>" | grep -i password` returns nothing; the JSON contains only id, name, permission per item.
- Grep the response/DTO: confirm `ChatList.cs` no longer references `_context.Users.ToListAsync()` and the cache type is `List<ChatUserDto>`.
- Build/run backend (`dotnet build` in Backend) — confirm it compiles with the new record and the [JsonIgnore] import.
- Smoke-test the chat UI: open the Chat page while logged in; the contact list still renders names and permission (titles/descriptions), confirming the DTO covers the UI needs.
- Defense-in-depth check: temporarily hit any other endpoint that returns a User (or write a quick unit test serializing a User) and confirm Password is omitted due to [JsonIgnore].

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Anonymous types cannot be used as the generic type argument to IMemoryCache.TryGetValue/Set, so you must introduce the named `ChatUserDto` record (or a class) — don't try `out List<anonymous>`.; Ordering: [Authorize] only works because the request pipeline calls app.UseAuthentication()/app.UseAuthorization() (Program.cs lines 166-167). Do not remove or reorder those; there is no global FallbackPolicy, so removing the attribute re-opens the hole.; Cache invalidation: the old 'ChatListCache' entry may already hold a List<User> with plaintext passwords in process memory for up to 1 hour. Changing the cached type means a fresh value is computed, but if you hot-reload without restarting, restart the API to flush the stale sensitive entry. A cold restart fully clears it.; [JsonIgnore] uses System.Text.Json (the ASP.NET Core default). If any code path serializes User with Newtonsoft.Json instead, add Newtonsoft's [JsonIgnore] too — but this project uses the default System.Text.Json.; This change stops the dump but does NOT fix plaintext password storage (JWTManagerService.cs:32). Don't close the underlying credential-storage risk based on this fix alone; the hashing follow-up is required for true remediation.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Minor location nuance: the no-[Authorize] condition spans the whole class (line 13 [ApiController] through the action), and the plaintext-password fact that makes the leak directly exploitable lives in Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32 (not listed in the finding's locations). The cited locations are all accurate; JWTManagerService.cs:32 could be added as supporting evidence. The endpoint being named 'ChatList' and serving a chat-user list while actually returning the credentials table reinforces the over-scoping called out in the recommendation. Recommendation is sound (remove/Authorize the endpoint, return a Password-free DTO, add [JsonIgnore] as defense-in-depth) and should ideally also note that passwords must be hashed — currently any DB-table read anywhere is a credential leak.


<a id="f-weak-hardcoded-jwt-key"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Weak, human-readable JWT signing key hardcoded in source and committed

**ID:** `weak-hardcoded-jwt-key` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Secret Exposure / Broken Authentication &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/appsettings.json:10`
- `Backend/Program.cs:74-90`
- `Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:41-51`

**Problem.** JWT:Key is the literal string "This is my supper secret key for jwt" — a low-entropy, guessable English phrase used as the HMAC-SHA256 signing secret for all tokens. It is committed to git. Tokens are signed with HmacSha256 over this key (JWTManagerService:50,80,119).

**Impact.** An attacker who reads the repo (or guesses/brute-forces this dictionary-style phrase) can forge valid JWTs for any user and any role claim, bypassing authentication on every [Authorize] endpoint, including all 158 reports and Excel export. Combined with the ChatList leak above, even attackers without repo access can pivot. Forged admin tokens give full report/data access.

**Recommendation.** Generate a cryptographically random 256-bit+ key (e.g., 32+ random bytes base64-encoded), store it outside source control (env var/secrets manager), and rotate it (which invalidates all existing tokens — acceptable given they must be assumed forged). Purge the old key from git history.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2-3 hours (code change ~30 min; secret provisioning, git history rewrite/force-push coordination, and DB password rotation make up the rest))_**

Replace the committed low-entropy JWT signing key with a cryptographically random 256-bit secret loaded from environment/secret store (never source control), enforce a minimum key length at startup, then stop tracking appsettings.json and purge the leaked secrets (JWT key AND the sa DB password on lines 26-27) from git history and rotate them. Token rotation invalidates existing tokens, which is acceptable since all prior tokens must be assumed forgeable.

1. Generate a new random 256-bit key: run `openssl rand -base64 48` (produces a 48-byte/384-bit base64 string). Keep the output for the deployment secret store; do NOT paste it into any tracked file.
2. In Backend/appsettings.json line 10, remove the real secret. Either delete the JWT:Key line entirely or set it to an empty placeholder "" so the existing guard in Program.cs:75-78 (`if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(jwtKey)) throw ...`) forces the value to come from config providers/env. The key MUST NOT be a real value in any committed file.
3. Provide the key at runtime via an environment variable. ASP.NET Core config binds `JWT__Key` (double underscore) to `JWT:Key` automatically — no code change needed for the lookup itself. For local dev, use the already-wired User Secrets (Backend csproj has UserSecretsId f930729b-3190-42a6-9201-49e396edac05): run `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<base64-from-step-1>"` from the Backend project dir. For UAT/prod set the `JWT__Key` env var in the host/container.
4. Harden the startup guard in Program.cs (currently lines 74-79). After reading `jwtKey` and before `Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes`, add a minimum-length check: HMAC-SHA256 needs >= 32 bytes (256 bits). This prevents re-introducing a weak key. Use IsNullOrWhiteSpace instead of IsNullOrEmpty so an all-whitespace placeholder is also rejected.
5. Apply the same defensive read in Service/JWTManagerService.cs. Lines 41, 71, and 111 each do `iconfiguration["JWT:Key"] ?? ""` — the `?? ""` silently falls back to an empty key and would sign tokens with a zero-length secret if config is missing. Extract a single private helper `GetSigningKey()` that validates non-empty/length and reuse it in Authenticate (line 41), AuthenticateTradenet2 (line 71), and RefreshToken (line 111).
6. Stop tracking the secret file. NOTE: `.gitignore` line 162 already lists `Backend/appsettings.json`, but the file is still tracked (committed in dadadc6 before the rule was added), so `git check-ignore Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing and the rule is currently inert. Run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` so the ignore rule takes effect, and commit a sanitized template `Backend/appsettings.example.json` (placeholders only) for onboarding.
7. Same-effort secret purge (per verifier note): the DB sa credentials on appsettings.json lines 26-27 (`User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022` for both TemplateDB and TradeNetDBTest on 203.81.66.111,14330) are also leaked. Move those connection strings to env vars / secret store the same way (ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB, ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest) and rotate the sa password.
8. Rewrite git history to purge the file from every commit (secrets appear from Initial commit dadadc6). Use git-filter-repo: `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths`, then add a sanitized version back. Coordinate a force-push and have all collaborators re-clone, since this rewrites commit SHAs.
9. Rotate the exposed secrets: the new JWT key from step 1 is the rotation (the old key is dead the moment appsettings is changed and the env value is used), and rotate the sa DB password. After deploy, all previously issued tokens become invalid — communicate that users must re-authenticate.

```csharp
// Program.cs (around lines 74-79) — enforce a strong, externally-supplied key
var jwtKey = builder.Configuration["JWT:Key"];
- if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(jwtKey))
- {
-     throw new InvalidOperationException("JWT:Key configuration value is missing.");
- }
- var Key = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(jwtKey);
+ if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(jwtKey))
+ {
+     throw new InvalidOperationException(
+         "JWT:Key is missing. Provide it via the JWT__Key environment variable or user-secrets, not appsettings.json.");
+ }
+ var Key = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(jwtKey);
+ if (Key.Length < 32) // HMAC-SHA256 requires a >= 256-bit secret
+ {
+     throw new InvalidOperationException("JWT:Key must be at least 32 bytes (256 bits) of high-entropy data.");
+ }

// JWTManagerService.cs — replace the silent `?? ""` fallback used at lines 41, 71, 111
- var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(iconfiguration["JWT:Key"] ?? "");
+ var tokenKey = GetSigningKey();   // see helper below

// Add once to the JWTManagerService class:
+ private byte[] GetSigningKey()
+ {
+     var key = iconfiguration["JWT:Key"];
+     if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(key) || Encoding.UTF8.GetByteCount(key) < 32)
+     {
+         throw new InvalidOperationException("JWT:Key is missing or too short (need >= 256-bit secret).");
+     }
+     return Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(key);
+ }

// appsettings.json line 10 — remove the real secret (left empty so the guard fires)
  "JWT": {
-   "Key": "This is my supper secret key for jwt",
+   "Key": "",
    "Issuer": "https://sakbran.github.io",
    "Audience": "sakbran.github.io"
  }
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds with the new guards.
- Negative startup: run with no JWT key configured (unset JWT__Key, empty appsettings value, no user-secret) and confirm the app throws the InvalidOperationException at startup instead of booting with a weak/empty key.
- Weak-key startup: set `JWT__Key` to a short string (e.g. "short") and confirm startup throws the 'at least 32 bytes' error.
- Happy path: set `JWT__Key` to the step-1 base64 value (env var or `dotnet user-secrets set`), start the app, POST to the login endpoint, confirm a token is issued and an [Authorize] report endpoint accepts it.
- Forgery check: take any token signed with the OLD key 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' and confirm it now returns 401 against an [Authorize] endpoint.
- Git hygiene: `git ls-files Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing (untracked); after the history rewrite `git log -S"supper secret" --all` and `git log -S"Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022" --all` return no commits.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Rotating the key invalidates every currently issued token (Authenticate 1-day, AuthenticateTradenet2 and RefreshToken 31-day tokens) — all users must re-authenticate; schedule a maintenance window. The history rewrite (git filter-repo) changes commit SHAs and requires a coordinated force-push plus re-clone by all collaborators; do it after the deployment is staged. appsettings.json is ALREADY in .gitignore (line 162) yet still tracked because it was committed first — you must `git rm --cached` it or the rule stays inert and re-edits keep getting committed. JWTManagerService signs with HmacSha256Signature (lines 50/80/119) while Program.cs validates HmacSha256 — leave the algorithm as-is; only the key changes. Editing appsettings.json alone is insufficient: the old secret stays recoverable from git, so rotation (a brand-new value) is mandatory, not optional.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Critical is appropriate and locations are exact. Two minor caveats, neither weakening the finding: (1) The impact mentions "the ChatList leak above" as a secondary pivot path — that is an external cross-reference to another finding I did not validate here; it is not load-bearing for this finding's core claim. (2) The "158 reports" figure is approximate phrasing (controller count not exactly recounted), immaterial to severity. The lack of issuer/audience validation (Program.cs:83-84) actually makes forgery easier than the finding states, reinforcing Critical. Recommendation (random 256-bit key, move to secrets manager/env, rotate, purge git history) is sound; note appsettings.json ALSO leaks DB sa credentials on lines 26-27, which should be remediated in the same secret-purge effort.


<a id="f-hardcoded-prod-sa-credentials-in-git"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Production SQL Server 'sa' credentials hardcoded in appsettings.json and committed to git

**ID:** `hardcoded-prod-sa-credentials-in-git` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Secret Exposure &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/appsettings.json:26-27`

**Problem.** Both the TemplateDB and TradeNetDBTest connection strings embed the production database server (203.81.66.111,14330) with `User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022`. appsettings.json is tracked in git (git ls-files confirms), and the password appears in historical commits 0453b61 and 07d95d8, so it is in the repository history, not just the working tree. `sa` is the SQL Server super-admin account.

**Impact.** Anyone with read access to the repo (or any clone/fork/backup, including the git history) obtains sysadmin-level credentials to the live Myanmar Ministry of Commerce trade database. This permits full read/write/drop of all trade, licence, permit, and account data, plus potential lateral movement via xp_cmdshell. Rotating the file alone is insufficient because the secret is in history.

**Recommendation.** Treat this credential as fully compromised: rotate the sa password immediately and stop using sa for the application (create a least-privilege login). Move all connection strings to environment variables / a secrets manager (user-secrets in dev, env vars or Azure/AWS secret store in prod). Remove appsettings.json from tracking, add it to .gitignore, and purge the secret from git history (git filter-repo / BFG). Restrict the DB server's network exposure.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. Half a day (DB rotation + least-priv login ~1-2h, env/user-secrets wiring ~1h, untrack + history purge + force-push coordination ~2-3h))_**

Treat the leaked `sa` password (and the weak JWT key) as compromised: rotate at the DB and replace `sa` with a least-privilege login, externalize all secrets into environment variables / user-secrets (no code change needed — .NET's default config already overlays them over appsettings.json), untrack `Backend/appsettings.json` (it is gitignored but still committed), and purge the secret from git history.

1. DB-side first (do before touching git, so the live secret is dead the moment it could leak further): connect to SQL Server at 203.81.66.111,14330 and run `ALTER LOGIN [sa] WITH PASSWORD = '<new-strong-random>';` to rotate. Then create a least-privilege application login instead of using sysadmin sa: `CREATE LOGIN [tradenet_app] WITH PASSWORD='<random>';` and in BOTH databases run `CREATE USER [tradenet_app] FOR LOGIN [tradenet_app];` plus `ALTER ROLE db_datareader ADD MEMBER [tradenet_app];`, `ALTER ROLE db_datawriter ADD MEMBER [tradenet_app];` (grant EXECUTE on the report stored procs the app calls). The app only needs read/write + EXEC on TemplateDB and TradeNetDB, never sysadmin.
2. Externalize the three connection strings and the JWT key. No code change is required: Program.cs:65-67 already call builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("TemplateDB")/("TradeNetDBTest") and Program.cs:74 + JWTManagerService.cs:41/71/111 read builder.Configuration["JWT:Key"], and CreateBuilder already layers environment variables and user-secrets ABOVE appsettings.json. In production set env vars (double-underscore = config nesting): `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest`, `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection`, and `JWT__Key` (a 32+ byte random value, NOT 'This is my supper secret key for jwt'). Set these in the systemd unit / Docker env / AWS secret store the API runs under.
3. For local dev, move secrets into the dotnet user-secrets store instead of the file: from Backend/ run `dotnet user-secrets init` then `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB" "<conn>"`, `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest" "<conn>"`, `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<random>"`. (Confirm/add a <UserSecretsId> to Backend/API.csproj — `dotnet user-secrets init` adds it.)
4. Scrub the working-tree file so no real secret remains. Edit Backend/appsettings.json: blank the JWT:Key value (line 10), and replace the TemplateDB (line 26) and TradeNetDBTest (line 27) connection-string values with placeholders that contain no host/password (or remove the production entries entirely and keep only the harmless localhost DefaultConnection). Commit this as a template/example.
5. Untrack the file from git (it is already in .gitignore at line 162 but remains committed, which is why it still leaks): `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` then commit. The .gitignore line 162 (`Backend/appsettings.json`) already prevents re-adding it. Optionally rename the scrubbed template to Backend/appsettings.json.example and keep IT tracked so new devs have a shape to copy.
6. Purge the secret from history. With the repo mirrored/backed up, run BFG or git-filter-repo to remove the password (and the old JWT key) from every commit, e.g. create replacements.txt with `Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022==>REMOVED` and `This is my supper secret key for jwt==>REMOVED`, then `git filter-repo --replace-text replacements.txt` (or `bfg --replace-text replacements.txt`). Force-push all branches/tags (`git push --force --all && git push --force --tags`) and have every collaborator re-clone. Note this rewrites SHAs including 07d95d8 and 0453b61.
7. Restrict network exposure of 203.81.66.111,14330 — the DB port should not be reachable from the public internet; lock it to the API host(s) via firewall/security group so a leaked string is not directly usable from outside.
8. Treat any GitHub fork/PR/backup as still holding the old secret even after history rewrite; the rotation in step 1 is what actually neutralizes it.

```json
// Backend/appsettings.json  (scrubbed template — commit this, then untrack the file)
// BEFORE
{
  "JWT": {
    "Key": "This is my supper secret key for jwt",
    ...
  },
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "DefaultConnection": "Server=localhost;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;Trusted_Connection=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;",
    "TemplateDB":"Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;",
    "TradeNetDBTest": "Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TradeNetDB;User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;"
  }
}
// AFTER  (no real secrets; real values come from env vars / user-secrets)
{
  "JWT": {
    "Key": "",
    ...
  },
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "DefaultConnection": "Server=localhost;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;Trusted_Connection=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;"
    // TemplateDB / TradeNetDBTest supplied via ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB,
    // ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest env vars (prod) or user-secrets (dev)
  }
}

# Then remove from tracking (file stays in .gitignore line 162):
#   git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json && git commit -m "chore: untrack appsettings.json, externalize secrets"
```

**Verify:**
- `git ls-files Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing (no longer tracked).
- `git grep -I 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022' $(git rev-list --all)` returns no hits after the history rewrite (also check for the old JWT string).
- Run the API with env vars set (ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB / __TradeNetDBTest / JWT__Key) and hit a report endpoint via Swagger — data loads, confirming GetConnectionString picks up the env-supplied strings (Program.cs:65-67).
- Authenticate and call a [Authorize] endpoint to confirm token issue/validation still works with the new JWT__Key (JWTManagerService + Program.cs:74-89).
- Attempt to log in to the DB with the old sa password and confirm it is rejected; confirm the app's new least-privilege login can read/write the report tables but cannot perform sysadmin ops (e.g. xp_cmdshell denied).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Ordering: rotate the sa password (step 1) BEFORE the git-history rewrite/force-push — the rewrite does not invalidate clones/forks/backups, only the live rotation does.; Any environment that depends on the TemplateDB/TradeNetDBTest strings will break until the env vars / user-secrets are configured (verifierNotes confirms DefaultConnection is only a fallback for local Windows-auth dev, so rotating sa won't break trusted-connection local dev).; git-filter-repo / BFG rewrites every commit SHA (including 07d95d8 and 0453b61) and detaches existing remotes — coordinate a force-push and have all collaborators re-clone; back up the repo (mirror clone) first.; If you keep a tracked Backend/appsettings.json.example, make sure it contains ONLY placeholders so you don't re-introduce a secret.; Granting the new app login: don't forget EXECUTE on the report stored procedures (the reports run procs, not just table reads) or report endpoints will 500.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Remediation in the finding is sound and complete (rotate sa, drop sa for a least-privilege login, move strings to env/user-secrets/secret manager, untrack + gitignore, purge history via filter-repo/BFG, restrict DB network exposure). Two additional observations worth surfacing to the team: (a) the same file (line 10) hardcodes a trivially-guessable JWT signing key, which is itself a separate auth-bypass-class issue and is also in history; (b) DefaultConnection uses localhost/Trusted_Connection and is only the fallback, so rotating the sa password will not break local dev that relies on Windows auth, but any environment relying on the TemplateDB string will break until reconfigured.


<a id="f-plaintext-password-storage-and-auth"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Passwords stored and compared in plaintext

**ID:** `plaintext-password-storage-and-auth` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Cryptographic Failure / Broken Authentication &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32`
- `Backend/Model/User.cs:21`
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs:31`

**Problem.** Authentication compares the supplied password directly against the stored value: `Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password)`. There is no hashing, salting, or verification function anywhere — User.Password is a plain string column. The same plaintext is also exposed via ChatList.

**Impact.** The credential database stores recoverable plaintext passwords. Any DB read (via the leaked sa credentials, a SQL issue, a backup, or ChatList) immediately yields usable login credentials, which users commonly reuse elsewhere. There is no protection against offline disclosure. The equality comparison is also non-constant-time, but that is minor next to plaintext storage.

**Recommendation.** Hash passwords with a memory-hard algorithm (ASP.NET Core's PasswordHasher<T> / Argon2 / bcrypt). On login, look up by Name then verify the hash; never query by password. Migrate existing rows (force reset or rehash-on-next-login). Remove Password from all serialized responses.

**Example:**

```
// current
_userService.Retrieve.Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password)
// fixed
var u = await _userService.Retrieve.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Name == users.Name);
if (u == null || _hasher.VerifyHashedPassword(u, u.PasswordHash, users.Password) == PasswordVerificationResult.Failed)
    return null;
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours)_**

Stop storing/comparing passwords in plaintext: hash with ASP.NET Core's PasswordHasher<User> on write, and on login look up the user by Name then verify the hash (never query by password). Separately, lock down / DTO-project the anonymous ChatList endpoint so it can never serialize the password column.

1. Add a hashed-password column to the model. In Backend/Model/User.cs add `public string PasswordHash { get; set; } = "";` and keep `Password` only as a transient input field. Decorate the plaintext `Password` property with `[NotMapped]` (add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations.Schema;`) so EF no longer persists raw passwords, OR add `PasswordHash` as the real column and drop `Password` from the table via migration. Recommended: keep `Password` as `[NotMapped]` (it is the login form input bound in AuthController.Login) and add a mapped `PasswordHash`.
2. Register the hasher in Backend/Program.cs DI, next to the existing JWT registration around line 92: `builder.Services.AddScoped<Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.IPasswordHasher<API.Model.User>, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.PasswordHasher<API.Model.User>>();`. PasswordHasher<T> ships in the Microsoft.AspNetCore.App shared framework, so no new NuGet package is required for this .NET 8 web project.
3. Inject the hasher into Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;`, add a `private readonly IPasswordHasher<User> _hasher;` field, and add the parameter `IPasswordHasher<User> hasher` to the constructor (line 21-29), assigning `_hasher = hasher;`.
4. Rewrite the login comparison in JWTManagerService.Authenticate (line 32-39). Replace the `Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password)` query: first load the user by Name only (`var tempUser = await _userService.Retrieve.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Name == users.Name);`), return null if it is null, then `var verify = _hasher.VerifyHashedPassword(tempUser, tempUser.PasswordHash, users.Password); if (verify == PasswordVerificationResult.Failed) return null;`. Remove the old `AsParallel().Any()` block. Use the already-loaded `tempUser` for the token claims below (it is non-null at this point).
5. Optionally handle the SuccessRehashNeeded result: if `verify == PasswordVerificationResult.SuccessRehashNeeded`, re-hash and persist (`tempUser.PasswordHash = _hasher.HashPassword(tempUser, users.Password); await _userService.Update(tempUser.Id, tempUser);`) before issuing the token.
6. Create a hashing path for any user creation/seeding so PasswordHash is populated with `_hasher.HashPassword(user, plaintext)` and the plaintext is never written. There is currently no registration controller in this repo (only Service/JWTManagerService.Authenticate consumes credentials), so seed/admin-provisioning code that inserts into ApplicationDbContext.Users must call the hasher; never set User.PasswordHash from a raw value.
7. Lock down the ChatList endpoint in Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs. It currently has no [Authorize] and returns the full `List<User>` (including Password) from `_context.Users.ToListAsync()` (line 31, served via _memoryCache). Either delete this controller (it appears to be leftover chat scaffolding unrelated to reports) or: add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `[Authorize]` on the class, and project to a DTO that omits the password, e.g. `await _context.Users.Select(u => new { u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission }).ToListAsync();` and cache/return that anonymous-typed/DTO list instead of `List<User>`.
8. Add an EF Core migration for the schema change: from the Backend project dir run `dotnet ef migrations add AddUserPasswordHash` then `dotnet ef database update` against the TemplateDB connection (ApplicationDbContext owns DbSet<User>). Note Backend/DBContext/TradeNetDbContext.cs line 447 also has a DbSet<User> for the external TradeNet DB; do NOT migrate or alter that one — only ApplicationDbContext owns the admin login Users table.
9. Migrate existing plaintext rows: write a one-off backfill that reads each existing Users row's plaintext value and sets `PasswordHash = hasher.HashPassword(user, plaintext)`, then clears/drops the plaintext column. If plaintext is unrecoverable or you prefer zero plaintext-at-rest risk, force a password reset for all admin accounts instead of backfilling.

```csharp
// Backend/Model/User.cs
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations.Schema; // add

public class User
{
    public User()
    {
        this.Id = Guid.NewGuid().ToString();
        this.Name = "";
        this.Password = "";
        this.Permission = "";
        this.PasswordHash = "";
    }
    [Key] public string Id { get; set; }
    [Required] public string Name { get; set; }

    [NotMapped]                      // transient login input only - never persisted
    public string Password { get; set; }

    public string PasswordHash { get; set; }   // mapped, stores Argon2/PBKDF2 hash
    [Required] public string Permission { get; set; }
}

// Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity; // add

private readonly IPasswordHasher<User> _hasher; // add field
// ctor: add IPasswordHasher<User> hasher param -> _hasher = hasher;

public async Task<TokenModel?> Authenticate(User users)
{
    // before:
    // IQueryable<User> UsersRecords = _userService.Retrieve
    //     .Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password);
    // if (!UsersRecords.AsParallel().Any()) return null;
    // var tempUser = await UsersRecords.FirstOrDefaultAsync();

    // after:
    var tempUser = await _userService.Retrieve
        .FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Name == users.Name);
    if (tempUser == null) return null;

    var verify = _hasher.VerifyHashedPassword(tempUser, tempUser.PasswordHash, users.Password);
    if (verify == PasswordVerificationResult.Failed) return null;
    if (verify == PasswordVerificationResult.SuccessRehashNeeded)
    {
        tempUser.PasswordHash = _hasher.HashPassword(tempUser, users.Password);
        await _userService.Update(tempUser.Id, tempUser);
    }
    // ... existing token generation uses tempUser ...
}

// Backend/Program.cs (near line 92)
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPasswordHasher<User>, PasswordHasher<User>>();

// Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs
[Authorize]                              // add
public class ChatList : ControllerBase
{
    // ...
    chatList = await _context.Users
        .Select(u => new { u.Id, u.Name, u.Permission }) // omit Password/PasswordHash
        .ToListAsync();
}
```

**Verify:**
- Run the API and POST to /api/Auth with a valid Name/Password JSON body; confirm a 200 with a TokenModel (token issued). POST with a wrong password and confirm 404/null (login fails).
- Query the Users table after a successful login or seed and confirm PasswordHash contains a non-reversible hash (PasswordHasher v3 output starts with base64 beginning 'AQAAAA...') and that no plaintext Password column value remains.
- GET /api/ChatList without a bearer token and confirm 401 (after [Authorize]); with a valid token confirm the JSON payload contains only Id/Name/Permission and NO Password or PasswordHash field.
- Confirm the project still builds: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds (PasswordHasher<T> resolves from the shared framework, no missing-package error).
- Confirm `dotnet ef migrations list` shows the new AddUserPasswordHash migration and `dotnet ef database update` applied the PasswordHash column to the ApplicationDbContext Users table.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Ordering: add and apply the PasswordHash column (migration) BEFORE deploying the new Authenticate code, otherwise login throws on a missing column. After deploy, existing rows have empty PasswordHash and every login fails until you backfill (hash existing plaintext) or force resets — do the backfill in the same release. AuthController.Login binds the incoming JSON straight to the User model, so the request still sends `Password`; keep that property (as [NotMapped]) — do not rename the inbound field or the frontend login breaks. Do NOT touch the second DbSet<User> in TradeNetDbContext.cs (external TradeNet DB) — only ApplicationDbContext owns the admin login table. ChatList is cached in IMemoryCache for 1 hour; if you only add [Authorize] without changing the projection, the cached List<User> still holds passwords in memory and the DTO change is what actually removes them — apply both. Rollback: the schema change is additive (new column) so reverting code is safe, but once plaintext is dropped it cannot be recovered, so keep a backup of the Users table before dropping the plaintext column.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Critical is correct. Two distinct critical issues are bundled here and both verify: (1) plaintext password storage + non-hashed login comparison, (2) the same plaintext exposed to anonymous callers through the unprotected ChatList endpoint (no [Authorize], no DTO projection, no global auth fallback). The recommendation (PasswordHasher<T>/Argon2/bcrypt, look up by Name then verify hash, never query by password, strip Password from serialized responses) is sound. I'd add: ChatList should also be removed or locked behind authorization and should project to a DTO that omits Password regardless of hashing. The non-constant-time comparison note is accurate but minor as stated.


<a id="f-swagger-exposed-in-production"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Swagger UI and OpenAPI spec served unconditionally in production

**ID:** `swagger-exposed-in-production` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Information Disclosure &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Trivial &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:109-115`
- `Backend/Program.cs:100-103`

**Problem.** app.UseSwagger() and app.UseSwaggerUI() are called outside the IsDevelopment() guard (which only wraps the developer exception page), so the full interactive API explorer at /swagger is available in every environment, including production.

**Impact.** Anyone reaching the server gets a complete, navigable map of all 158 report endpoints, request/response schemas, and parameter names — ideal reconnaissance for crafting attacks (e.g., enumerating report inputs, finding the unauthenticated ChatList/Upload endpoints). It is unnecessary exposure for a government system.

**Recommendation.** Wrap UseSwagger/UseSwaggerUI inside if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()), or require authentication/IP allow-listing to reach /swagger in non-dev environments.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 15 min)_**

Move the existing `app.UseSwagger()` / `app.UseSwaggerUI(...)` block in Backend/Program.cs inside the already-present `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` guard so the interactive API explorer and OpenAPI spec are only served in development, not in production/UAT.

1. Open Backend/Program.cs. Locate the request-pipeline section starting at line 99 (comment 'Configure the HTTP request pipeline.').
2. The dev/prod branch at lines 100-107 currently only wraps UseDeveloperExceptionPage (dev) and UseHsts (prod). The Swagger calls at lines 109-115 sit AFTER this block, unconditionally. Cut the entire block: `app.UseSwagger();` plus the full `app.UseSwaggerUI(options => { ... });` (lines 109-115).
3. Paste that Swagger block inside the existing `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` body, right after `app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();` (line 102). Keep the SwaggerUI options exactly as-is: SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "TradeNet Admin Report API v1"), RoutePrefix = "swagger", DisplayRequestDuration().
4. Leave the `else { app.UseHsts(); }` branch unchanged, and leave the service registrations (AddEndpointsApiExplorer / AddSwaggerGen at lines 27-63) unchanged — those build the spec but do not expose any endpoint, so they are harmless to keep.
5. Confirm placement: the Swagger block must remain AFTER `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97) and BEFORE `app.UseHttpsRedirection();` (line 117). Inside the dev `if` is the correct spot.
6. Optional hardening if QA/ops need Swagger in UAT: instead of the IsDevelopment guard, gate on a config flag (e.g. read builder.Configuration.GetValue<bool>("Swagger:Enabled") into a local before app.Build(), then `if (enableSwagger)`), and keep it false in the production appsettings. Do not enable it in the public production environment.

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs — request pipeline (around lines 99-115)

// BEFORE
        if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
        {
            app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
        }
        else
        {
            app.UseHsts();
        }

        app.UseSwagger();
        app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
        {
            options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "TradeNet Admin Report API v1");
            options.RoutePrefix = "swagger";
            options.DisplayRequestDuration();
        });

        app.UseHttpsRedirection();

// AFTER
        if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
        {
            app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();

            app.UseSwagger();
            app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
            {
                options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "TradeNet Admin Report API v1");
                options.RoutePrefix = "swagger";
                options.DisplayRequestDuration();
            });
        }
        else
        {
            app.UseHsts();
        }

        app.UseHttpsRedirection();
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` from /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend (no compile errors).
- Production behavior — run with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production (e.g. `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production dotnet run`) then `curl -ik https://localhost:<port>/swagger/index.html` and `curl -ik https://localhost:<port>/swagger/v1/swagger.json` — both must return 404 (not 200).
- Development behavior — run with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development; `curl -k https://localhost:<port>/swagger/v1/swagger.json` returns 200 and the spec, and /swagger UI loads, confirming dev tooling still works.
- Confirm normal report endpoints still respond in Production mode (e.g. a JWT-authenticated report request) so the move did not disturb the rest of the pipeline.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Environment is driven by ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT. Verify the actual UAT/production hosts set this to Production (or anything != Development); if a server is misconfigured to Development, Swagger will still appear. Check the deployment env var, not just the code.; UAT host reportuat.myanmartradenet.com is in the CORS allow-list — if QA relies on Swagger there, use the optional config-flag approach (Swagger:Enabled) rather than IsDevelopment, but keep the flag off in the truly public production appsettings.; This finding is recon-only. The verifier flagged a separate, likely higher-severity issue: unauthenticated ChatList/UploadController endpoints. Hiding Swagger does NOT secure those — track and fix them independently by adding [Authorize].; Rollback is trivial: move the two Swagger calls back outside the if/else block.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Locations are accurate: Program.cs:109-115 (UseSwagger/UseSwaggerUI) and Program.cs:100-103 (IsDevelopment guard wrapping only the dev exception page). Secondary observation worth flagging separately: the unauthenticated ChatList/UploadController endpoints are a distinct and likely higher-severity issue in their own right — Swagger merely makes them easier to discover. Severity left at Medium: defensible given the recon value combined with real unauthenticated endpoints, though a case could be made for Low since the bulk of endpoints remain behind JWT auth.


<a id="f-verbose-exception-leak-on-login"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Login and upload endpoints return raw exception messages to clients

**ID:** `verbose-exception-leak-on-login` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Information Disclosure &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Trivial &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:39-42`
- `Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:39-42`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:94-99`

**Problem.** AuthController.Login catches Exception and returns StatusCode(500, ex.Message). On the unauthenticated /api/Auth login path, ex.Message can surface SQL/EF errors that reveal connection details, table/column names, or server information. UploadController similarly stuffs ex.Message into a response.

**Impact.** On the most-probed (anonymous) endpoint, an attacker can trigger and read backend error text — database structure, server identity, or stack-derived hints — aiding SQL/EF enumeration and confirming the live DB target. It leaks internal detail without any auth.

**Recommendation.** Return a generic error (e.g., 'Authentication failed' / 'Upload failed') and log the exception server-side with a correlation id. Never echo ex.Message to clients on public endpoints.

**Example:**

```
// current
catch (Exception ex) { return StatusCode(500, ex.Message); }
// fixed
catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError(ex, "Login failed"); return StatusCode(500, "An error occurred."); }
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 15 min)_**

Stop echoing raw exception text on the anonymous login endpoint: inject ILogger<AuthController>, log the full exception server-side with a correlation id, and return a generic 500 body. Leave UploadController alone — its dict is discarded and the 500 body is already empty (no leak, per verifier).

1. Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs.
2. Add a logging import at the top: `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;` (the default WebApplication host already registers ILogger<T> in DI, so no Program.cs change is needed).
3. Add a private readonly field `private readonly ILogger<AuthController> _logger;` alongside the existing `_jWTManager` field.
4. Update the constructor signature from `AuthController(IJWTManagerService jWTManager)` to `AuthController(IJWTManagerService jWTManager, ILogger<AuthController> logger)` and assign `this._logger = logger;` in the body.
5. In the `catch (Exception ex)` block on the Login method (currently line 39-42), replace `return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, ex.Message);` with code that generates a correlation id, logs the full exception with that id, and returns a generic message carrying only the correlation id.
6. Do NOT modify UploadController.Postupload — the verifier confirmed its `dict` (which holds ex.Message) is discarded and the returned `HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError)` has an empty body, so there is no message leak there.
7. Build the Backend project to confirm it compiles: `dotnet build` from the Backend folder.

```csharp
// AuthController.cs

// add import (with the other usings)
using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;

// fields
private readonly IJWTManagerService _jWTManager;
private readonly ILogger<AuthController> _logger;            // ADD

// constructor
public AuthController(IJWTManagerService jWTManager, ILogger<AuthController> logger)
{
    this._jWTManager = jWTManager;
    this._logger = logger;                                  // ADD
}

// catch block in Login(...)
// before:
catch (Exception ex)
{
    return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, ex.Message);
}
// after:
catch (Exception ex)
{
    var correlationId = Guid.NewGuid().ToString();
    _logger.LogError(ex, "Login failed. CorrelationId: {CorrelationId}", correlationId);
    return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError,
        new { message = "An error occurred while processing your request.", correlationId });
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: from /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend run `dotnet build` — it should compile (ILogger<AuthController> resolves from the built-in DI container).
- Force an exception on the anonymous login path (e.g. stop SQL Server or send a malformed body) and POST to /api/Auth. Confirm the 500 response body is the generic `{ "message": "An error occurred while processing your request.", "correlationId": "..." }` and contains NO SQL/EF text, table/column names, or connection details.
- Confirm the full exception (with the same correlationId) appears in the server logs/console, so operators can still diagnose.
- Confirm a successful login (valid credentials) still returns 200 with the token and an unknown user still returns 404 — the happy path and NotFound branch are unchanged.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Scope: only AuthController.Login leaks. Do not 'fix' UploadController.Postupload for a leak — its dict is discarded and the 500 body is empty (verifier-confirmed). (Its missing [Authorize]/[AllowAnonymous] plus no FallbackPolicy in Program.cs is a separate access-control concern, out of scope here.); The constructor signature change is a breaking DI change only if AuthController is manually instantiated anywhere; it is resolved by the MVC container, so adding the ILogger parameter is safe — no Program.cs registration needed.; Out of scope but worth flagging separately (verifier's tangential notes): JWTManagerService compares passwords in plaintext (x.Password == users.Password) and the JWT signing key falls back to an empty string when unconfigured. Do not bundle these into this finding.; Rollback is trivial: revert the catch block to the single StatusCode(... ex.Message) line and drop the logger field/parameter.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** UploadController.Postupload should be removed from the finding — it does not leak (local dict discarded, empty 500 body). Note Postupload also has no [Authorize]/[AllowAnonymous] and Program.cs sets no FallbackPolicy, so it is effectively anonymous, but that is a separate access-control concern and still does not produce a message leak. The recommendation (generic error + server-side logging with correlation id) is valid for the AuthController case. Tangential observations not part of this finding: JWTManagerService compares passwords in plaintext (x.Password == users.Password) and the JWT signing key falls back to empty string if unconfigured.


<a id="f-jwt-issuer-audience-validation-disabled"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — JWT issuer and audience validation disabled

**ID:** `jwt-issuer-audience-validation-disabled` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Broken Authentication / JWT Misconfiguration &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:81-90`

**Problem.** TokenValidationParameters set ValidateIssuer = false and ValidateAudience = false, even though ValidIssuer/ValidAudience are configured. Only the signature and lifetime are validated.

**Impact.** Tokens issued for any issuer/audience are accepted as long as they are signed with the (weak, exposed) key. If that key is ever reused across other systems/environments, a token minted elsewhere is honored here. It removes a defense-in-depth layer that would otherwise constrain where a valid token may be used; most impactful in combination with the weak signing key.

**Recommendation.** Set ValidateIssuer = true and ValidateAudience = true and ensure tokens are issued with matching iss/aud claims (the SecurityTokenDescriptor in JWTManagerService does not currently set Issuer/Audience — add them). This is cheap and meaningfully tightens token scope.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Turn on issuer/audience validation in the JWT bearer setup in Program.cs, and make the token-minting paths in JWTManagerService stamp matching iss/aud claims so every issued token still validates. Both must change together or every existing/new token will be rejected.

1. In Backend/Program.cs (AddJwtBearer delegate, lines 81-90), change the TokenValidationParameters so ValidateIssuer = true and ValidateAudience = true. Leave ValidIssuer = builder.Configuration["JWT:Issuer"] and ValidAudience = builder.Configuration["JWT:Audience"] as-is (they already point at the appsettings values "https://sakbran.github.io" and "sakbran.github.io").
2. In Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs, read the issuer and audience once from configuration inside each issuing method (or add private readonly fields set in the constructor from iconfiguration["JWT:Issuer"] and iconfiguration["JWT:Audience"]).
3. In JWTManagerService.Authenticate (tokenDescriptor at lines 42-51), add Issuer = iconfiguration["JWT:Issuer"] and Audience = iconfiguration["JWT:Audience"] to the SecurityTokenDescriptor initializer.
4. Apply the same two properties to the SecurityTokenDescriptor in AuthenticateTradenet2 (lines 72-81) and in RefreshToken (lines 112-120) — all three mint tokens with the same signing key, so all three must carry the iss/aud or they will fail validation after step 1.
5. Confirm the same JWT section keys exist in every environment's config. appsettings.json has JWT:Issuer/Audience; appsettings.Development.json has no JWT section, so it inherits from appsettings.json — verify the deployed/production config (and any environment-specific override) supplies JWT:Issuer and JWT:Audience, otherwise ValidIssuer/ValidAudience become null and validation throws.
6. Rebuild the Backend project and restart the API so the new validation parameters take effect.

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs (lines 81-90) BEFORE
o.TokenValidationParameters = new TokenValidationParameters
{
    ValidateIssuer = false,
    ValidateAudience = false,
    ValidateLifetime = true,
    ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true,
    ValidIssuer = builder.Configuration["JWT:Issuer"],
    ValidAudience = builder.Configuration["JWT:Audience"],
    IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Key)
};

// AFTER
o.TokenValidationParameters = new TokenValidationParameters
{
    ValidateIssuer = true,
    ValidateAudience = true,
    ValidateLifetime = true,
    ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true,
    ValidIssuer = builder.Configuration["JWT:Issuer"],
    ValidAudience = builder.Configuration["JWT:Audience"],
    IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Key)
};

// Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs — every SecurityTokenDescriptor
// (Authenticate ~L42, AuthenticateTradenet2 ~L72, RefreshToken ~L112)
var tokenDescriptor = new SecurityTokenDescriptor
{
    Subject = new ClaimsIdentity(new Claim[]
    {
        new Claim(ClaimTypes.Name, tempUser?.Id ?? ""),
        new Claim(ClaimTypes.Role, tempUser?.Permission ?? "")
    }),
    Issuer = iconfiguration["JWT:Issuer"],     // add
    Audience = iconfiguration["JWT:Audience"], // add
    Expires = DateTime.Now.AddDays(1),
    SigningCredentials = new SigningCredentials(new SymmetricSecurityKey(tokenKey), SecurityAlgorithms.HmacSha256Signature)
};
```

**Verify:**
- Log in to obtain a fresh token, then paste it into https://jwt.io (or decode locally) and confirm the payload now contains "iss": "https://sakbran.github.io" and "aud": "sakbran.github.io".
- Call an [Authorize] endpoint with the new token -> expect 200. This proves tokens minted after the change still validate with issuer/audience checks on.
- Forge a token signed with the same key but a different iss or aud (e.g. via a quick JwtSecurityTokenHandler test) and hit a protected endpoint -> expect 401 Unauthorized, whereas before the change it would have been accepted.
- Verify any token issued BEFORE the change (no iss/aud claims) is now rejected with 401, confirming validation is active.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Ordering/coupling: do NOT enable validation in Program.cs without also adding Issuer/Audience to all THREE SecurityTokenDescriptors (Authenticate, AuthenticateTradenet2, RefreshToken) in the same deploy — otherwise newly issued tokens lack iss/aud and every request fails with 401. Breaking change for live sessions: all tokens minted before this change have no iss/aud and will be rejected, so deploying invalidates existing sessions (users must re-authenticate); roll out during a low-traffic window. Config dependency: if a deployed environment lacks JWT:Issuer/JWT:Audience, ValidIssuer/ValidAudience resolve to null and the middleware throws on validation — confirm those keys are present everywhere before enabling. This change does not address the related weak/exposed signing key ("This is my supper secret key for jwt") — that is a separate finding and remains the larger risk. Rollback: revert both files (set the two flags back to false) and restart.


<a id="f-chatcontroller-unauth-data"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — ChatController endpoints are unauthenticated

**ID:** `chatcontroller-unauth-data` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Broken Access Control &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs:14`
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs:21-53`

**Problem.** ChatController has no [Authorize] on the class or its GET (read messages by room) and POST (insert message) actions. The room key is fully client-supplied, so any anonymous caller can read messages for any room and post arbitrary messages/timestamps.

**Impact.** Unauthenticated reading of potentially sensitive internal chat content (room ids are guessable, e.g., user-AND-user) and unauthenticated writes (spam/injection of message rows). For an internal government admin tool, chat content may include operational details that should not be public.

**Recommendation.** Add [Authorize] to ChatController and derive the participant identity from the authenticated principal rather than trusting client-supplied room values; validate the caller is a participant of the room.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Lock down the chat endpoints by adding [Authorize] and deriving participant identity from the JWT (ClaimTypes.Name holds the user Id) instead of trusting client-supplied values: on GET, verify the caller is one of the two room participants; on POST, force the Room/Sender to the authenticated user. Apply the same [Authorize] to the sibling ChatList controller (and the empty Chat.cs) so the whole chat surface requires a token.

1. Open Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `using System.Security.Claims;` to the existing usings block.
2. Add the `[Authorize]` attribute on the `ChatController` class (line 14, alongside the existing `[ApiController]` / `[Route("api/[controller]")]`). The app already wires JWT bearer in Backend/Program.cs (AddAuthentication/AddJwtBearer at line 68, UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization at lines 166-167), so [Authorize] takes effect immediately.
3. In the `Get([FromQuery] string room)` action: move the existing `string.IsNullOrEmpty(room)` BadRequest check to the top (before the Split calls), then after computing FirstValue/SecondValue read `var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;` and return `Forbid()` unless `callerId` equals FirstValue or SecondValue. Note: ClaimTypes.Name carries the user's Id (set in Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs line 46: `new Claim(ClaimTypes.Name, tempUser?.Id)`), and the room format is `{userId}-AND-{otherUserId}` (built in Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatList.tsx line 26).
4. In the `Post(ChatModel message)` action: read `var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;`, `Forbid()` if it is null/empty or if it is not one of the two `-AND-` halves of `message.Room`, then set `message.Sender = callerId;` (overriding any client-supplied Sender) before the existing `message.Timestamp = DateTime.Now;` and AddAsync/SaveChangesAsync. The ChatModel.Sender property is the field the frontend sends from auth.user.id (Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx lines 76/98) and renders 'isMine' from (line 144), so forcing it server-side is safe.
5.
