Apply the same fix to the siblings called out in verifierNotes: add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` and `[Authorize]` to the `ChatList` class in Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs (line 15) so GetChatList (which returns the full Users table) requires a token. Also add `[Authorize]` to the empty `Chat` class in Backend/Controllers/Chat.cs (line 11) to harden it by default for any future action.
6. Build the backend: from the repo root run `dotnet build Backend/Backend.csproj` (or the solution) to confirm the new usings/attributes compile.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs

// add imports
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;
using System.Security.Claims;

[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
[Authorize]                       // <-- ADDED: class-level auth
public class ChatController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly ApplicationDbContext _context;
    public ChatController(ApplicationDbContext context) => _context = context;

    [HttpGet]
    public async Task<IActionResult> Get([FromQuery] string room)
    {
        if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(room))
            return BadRequest("Room parameter is required.");

        var FirstValue  = room.Split("-AND-").FirstOrDefault();
        var SecondValue = room.Split("-AND-").Skip(1).FirstOrDefault();

        // ADDED: caller must be one of the two room participants
        var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;
        if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(callerId) ||
            (callerId != FirstValue && callerId != SecondValue))
            return Forbid();

        var FixedRoomNo  = $"{FirstValue}-AND-{SecondValue}";
        var FixedRoomNo2 = $"{SecondValue}-AND-{FirstValue}";

        var isRoomExists = await _context.ChatModels.AnyAsync(m => m.Room == FixedRoomNo || m.Room == FixedRoomNo2);
        if (!isRoomExists)
            return Ok(new List<ChatModel>());

        var messages = await _context.ChatModels
            .Where(m => m.Room == FixedRoomNo || m.Room == FixedRoomNo2)
            .OrderByDescending(m => m.Timestamp)
            .Take(10)
            .OrderBy(m => m.Timestamp)
            .ToListAsync();
        return Ok(messages);
    }

    [HttpPost]
    public async Task<IActionResult> Post(ChatModel message)
    {
        // ADDED: derive identity from token; do not trust client Sender/Room blindly
        var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;
        if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(callerId))
            return Forbid();

        var first  = message.Room?.Split("-AND-").FirstOrDefault();
        var second = message.Room?.Split("-AND-").Skip(1).FirstOrDefault();
        if (callerId != first && callerId != second)
            return Forbid();

        message.Sender = callerId;          // force server-side identity
        message.Timestamp = DateTime.Now;   // already server-set
        await _context.ChatModels.AddAsync(message);
        await _context.SaveChangesAsync();
        return Ok();
    }
}
```

**Verify:**
- Without a token: `curl -i "https://<host>/api/Chat?room=1-AND-2"` returns 401 Unauthorized (was 200). Same for POST /api/Chat and GET /api/ChatList.
- With a valid token for user A: GET /api/Chat?room=A-AND-B returns 200 with that room's last 10 messages; GET /api/Chat?room=X-AND-Y (where A is neither X nor Y) returns 403 Forbidden.
- With user A's token, POST /api/Chat with body {"room":"A-AND-B","sender":"B","type":"text","content":"x"} succeeds but the persisted row's Sender is 'A' (server-forced), not 'B'. POST with room 'X-AND-Y' returns 403.
- Smoke-test the UI: log in, open a chat thread (route /Test/Edit/{myId}-AND-{otherId}), confirm messages still load and send (axiosInstance already attaches `Bearer <token>` per Frontend/src/services/AxiosInstance.ts lines 10-11), and that own messages still render right-aligned via the `msg.sender === auth?.user?.id` check.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Forcing message.Sender = callerId server-side is the desired behavior, but verify nothing relies on a Sender different from the room participants. The frontend ChatBox does not set an Authorization header for the MQTT broker (broker.emqx.io) — only the REST calls go through axiosInstance, which already adds the bearer token, so adding [Authorize] will not break the existing UI; any non-UI/integration caller hitting these endpoints anonymously will start getting 401 and must send a token. Order: add the using directives before referencing ClaimTypes/Authorize or the build fails. ChatList's GetChatList currently caches and returns the entire Users table to anonymous callers — adding [Authorize] is the key win there. Rollback is simply removing the [Authorize] attributes and the participant checks.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Location citation is accurate (line 14 = class; 21-53 covers both actions). The same unauthenticated exposure also applies to the sibling Backend/Controllers/Chat.cs and Backend/Controllers/ChatList.cs (no [Authorize]); if a follow-up fix is scoped, those should be remediated alongside ChatController. The recommendation is sound — add [Authorize] and derive participant identity from User.Claims rather than trusting the client-supplied room value.


<a id="f-excel-export-idor"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Excel export jobs have no per-user ownership scoping (authenticated IDOR)

**ID:** `excel-export-idor` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Broken Access Control (IDOR) &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/ExcelExportController.cs:34-95`

**Problem.** GetJobs returns every export job (explicitly 'shared visibility'), and Download/Delete look jobs up by Guid id only, with no check that the job's RequestedByUserName matches the caller. The controller is [Authorize], so callers are authenticated, but any authenticated user can list, download, and delete any other user's generated report export.

**Impact.** An authenticated user can enumerate and download report exports they were not authorized to generate (which may cover date ranges / form types beyond their remit) and can delete other users' exports (data-availability impact). Lower severity than the anonymous issues because it requires a valid token, but it is still cross-user data access on a sensitive trade-reporting system.

**Recommendation.** If exports are genuinely meant to be shared among all admins, document and accept it; otherwise scope GetJobs/Download/Delete to jobs where RequestedByUserName == User.Identity.Name (or enforce a role check), and return 404/403 for jobs the caller does not own.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

x

1. Open Backend/Controllers/ExcelExportController.cs. Add `using System.Security.Claims;` to the using block (it is NOT currently imported; the file only has System, System.Linq, System.Threading.Tasks, API.* and Microsoft.* usings).
2. Add a small private helper to read the caller's username the same way the enqueue path records it. Right after the constructor add: `private string? CurrentUserName => User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;`. Note: the JWT (Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:46,76,116) issues `new Claim(ClaimTypes.Name, ...)`, and every report controller enqueues with `User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value` (e.g. ImportPermitVoucherReportController.cs:84), so this matches stored ExcelExportJob.RequestedByUserName exactly.
3. In Download(Guid id), after the existing null check (`if (job == null) return NotFound();`), add an ownership guard that returns NotFound (not Forbid) so the job's existence is not leaked: `if (!string.Equals(job.RequestedByUserName, CurrentUserName, StringComparison.Ordinal)) return NotFound();`. Keep this BEFORE the status/file-existence checks.
4. In Delete(Guid id), add the identical ownership guard immediately after the `if (job == null) return NotFound();` line, before the `_fileStore.Delete` / `_db.ExcelExportJobs.Remove` calls. This is the most important one (it removes the file and DB row).
5. In GetJob(Guid id) (the polling endpoint), add the same ownership guard after the null check so a user cannot poll/inspect another user's job metadata via its DTO.
6. Leave GetJobs() returning all jobs ordered by CreatedAtUtc (the `<summary>All exports ... (shared visibility)</summary>` is a deliberate shared-drive design per the verifier). Optionally, if the team decides exports must NOT be shared, also filter it with `.Where(j => j.RequestedByUserName == CurrentUserName)` — but document that decision; the audit only requires Download/Delete (and ideally GetJob) to be scoped.
7. Defensive note: if CurrentUserName resolves to null/empty (it should not for an [Authorize]-gated endpoint, but legacy tokens at JWTManagerService.cs:46 store an empty string when tempUser is null), the StringComparison.Ordinal check still fails closed for any job whose RequestedByUserName is a real username, which is the safe behavior.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/ExcelExportController.cs

// 1) add using (top of file, with the other usings)
using System.Security.Claims;

// 2) add helper right after the constructor
private string? CurrentUserName => User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;

// 3) Download — add guard after the null check
public async Task<IActionResult> Download(Guid id)
{
    var job = await _db.ExcelExportJobs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(j => j.Id == id);
    if (job == null)
    {
        return NotFound();
    }

    // Scope to the requester: don't expose other users' exports.
    if (!string.Equals(job.RequestedByUserName, CurrentUserName, StringComparison.Ordinal))
    {
        return NotFound();
    }

    if (job.Status != ExcelExportJobStatus.Completed)
    {
        return Conflict($"Export is not ready (status: {job.Status}).");
    }
    // ... unchanged (file existence check + File(...) ) ...
}

// 4) Delete — add the same guard after the null check
public async Task<IActionResult> Delete(Guid id)
{
    var job = await _db.ExcelExportJobs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(j => j.Id == id);
    if (job == null)
    {
        return NotFound();
    }

    if (!string.Equals(job.RequestedByUserName, CurrentUserName, StringComparison.Ordinal))
    {
        return NotFound();
    }

    try { _fileStore.Delete(job.FilePath); } catch { /* best effort */ }
    _db.ExcelExportJobs.Remove(job);
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
    return NoContent();
}

// 5) GetJob — add the same guard after the null check
public async Task<ActionResult> GetJob(Guid id)
{
    var job = await _db.ExcelExportJobs.FirstOrDefaultAsync(j => j.Id == id);
    if (job == null)
    {
        return NotFound();
    }
    if (!string.Equals(job.RequestedByUserName, CurrentUserName, StringComparison.Ordinal))
    {
        return NotFound();
    }
    return Ok(ToDto(job));
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` from the Backend project root — confirm no compile error (the new `using System.Security.Claims;` resolves ClaimTypes).
- Manual two-user test: log in as user A, enqueue an export from any report controller's POST .../Excel, note the returned job GUID. Log in as user B (different username), then `GET /api/ExcelExport/{A's id}/download` and `DELETE /api/ExcelExport/{A's id}` with B's bearer token — both must now return 404. With A's own token they still return the file / 204 NoContent respectively.
- Confirm `GET /api/ExcelExport/{A's id}` (GetJob) returns 200 for A and 404 for B.
- Confirm GetJobs (`GET /api/ExcelExport/jobs`) still lists all jobs (unchanged shared list) unless the team chose to also scope it.
- Optional integration test: assert that Delete by a non-owner does not remove the row — query ExcelExportJobs after the 404 and verify the job still exists.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Return NotFound (not Forbid/403) for non-owned jobs so you don't reveal that a given GUID exists. Do NOT change how RequestedByUserName is stored — it is set from User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value at enqueue (ExcelExportJobService.cs:103 via the report controllers), so the check must read the same claim; using User.Identity.Name also works since the default handler maps ClaimTypes.Name to it, but FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name) is the exact match. Any export jobs already in TemplateDB created before this change keep their original RequestedByUserName, so they remain accessible only to their original requester — no data migration needed, but be aware reused/dedup'd "closed period" jobs (ExcelExportJobService.cs:69-88) are matched by FilterHash and may have been created by a different user, so a second user who triggers the same closed-period export will reuse the first user's job GUID and would now get a 404 on download; if that breaks the reuse UX, either also stamp the current user on reuse or relax Download to a role check instead of strict ownership. There is no role-based authorization anywhere in the backend, so do not attempt a role-bypass branch unless you first add a real admin role/policy.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** The "shared visibility" comment on GetJobs indicates the broad list may be a deliberate design choice for admins, which supports the finding's own recommendation ("if genuinely meant to be shared, document and accept it"). The stronger, harder-to-defend part of the issue is cross-user Download and especially Delete (file + DB row removal). If the team intends a shared exports drive, the minimal fix is to scope at least Delete (and ideally Download) to RequestedByUserName == User.Identity.Name or a role check. No role-based authorization exists in the backend at all, so a same-issue pattern likely affects other controllers, but that is out of scope for this specific finding. Severity Medium is appropriate; not adjusting.


<a id="f-upload-unauth-path-traversal"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Unauthenticated file upload with path traversal and ineffective validation

**ID:** `upload-unauth-path-traversal` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Broken Access Control / Path Traversal / Unrestricted Upload &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:23 (no [Authorize]); :43,:83 (unsanitized client filename into Path.Combine); :104-147 (SaveAsWebpAsync writes via Directory.CreateDirectory + FileStream). Backend/Program.cs:22,168 (AddControllers with no fallback authz policy) and :169 (UseStaticFiles serves wwwroot).`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:22-23`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:42-87`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:104-147`

**Problem.** UploadController has no [Authorize]. Postupload takes the destination file name from the caller-controlled `filename` query/route value and passes it straight into `Path.Combine("wwwroot", "Image", filename)` (line 83) and into SaveAsWebpAsync, which calls Directory.CreateDirectory(Path.GetDirectoryName(filePath)) and writes there. `filename` is never sanitized, so values like `..\..\appsettings.json` or an absolute path escape the intended Image directory (Path.Combine treats a rooted segment as absolute). Extension/size checks are also weak: the validation branch returns early with the file unsaved only for disallowed types, but the actual save uses `filePath.Split(".")[0]` which mishandles names containing multiple dots, and the loop iterates httpRequest.Form.Files while always saving Form.Files[0].

**Impact.** An anonymous attacker can write arbitrary files into wwwroot (web-served) or, via traversal, elsewhere on disk the process can reach — enabling overwrite of config/static assets or planting content. Because uploads land under wwwroot which is served by app.UseStaticFiles(), an attacker-controlled file could be retrieved back. No authentication is required to do any of this.

**Recommendation.** Add [Authorize]. Never trust the client-supplied file name: generate a server-side name (e.g., Guid) and use Path.GetFileName() to strip directory components; validate the final resolved path stays under the intended root. Validate content (magic bytes) not just extension, enforce the size limit before reading, and write outside wwwroot if the files are not meant to be public.

**Example:**

```
// current
var filePath = Path.Combine("wwwroot", "Image", filename); // filename attacker-controlled
// fixed
var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + ".webp";
var root = Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "App_Data", "Image");
var filePath = Path.Combine(root, safeName);
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2 hours)_**

Lock down `UploadController` by requiring authentication and never trusting the client-supplied `filename`: generate a server-side GUID name, write under a fixed resolved root validated to prevent traversal, and fix the validation loop so checks apply to the file actually being saved. Return the server-generated name so the frontend can reference the stored asset.

1. In `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs`, add a class-level `[Authorize]` attribute on `UploadController` (line 23), matching the convention used in `Backend/Controllers/ExcelExportController.cs:19`. The `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` import is already present (line 12), and there is no global fallback authz policy in `Program.cs`, so this attribute is what actually closes the anonymous hole.
2. Rewrite the body of `Postupload` (lines 49-92) so it no longer iterates `httpRequest.Form.Files` while always re-reading index 0. Take a single file via `httpRequest.Form.Files.GetFile("file")` or `httpRequest.Form.Files.FirstOrDefault()` into a local `postedFile`, and run the extension/size validation against that exact `postedFile` before saving (the current loop validates `Form.Files[0]` but passes the loop variable `file` to `SaveAsWebpAsync`).
3. Stop using the caller-supplied `filename` for the path. Generate `var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + ".webp";`. Build the destination root from the injected `_hostingEnvironment`: `var root = Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.WebRootPath ?? Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "wwwroot"), "Image");` (kept under wwwroot because these chat images are intentionally served back via `app.UseStaticFiles()` in `Program.cs:169`). Compute `var filePath = Path.Combine(root, safeName);`.
4. Add a defense-in-depth canonical-path guard before writing: resolve `Path.GetFullPath(filePath)` and `Path.GetFullPath(root + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar)`, and if the resolved file path does not start (OrdinalIgnoreCase) with the resolved root, return `BadRequest`. This makes traversal impossible even if `safeName` generation is ever changed.
5. Validate file extension/size on `postedFile` (not the trusting `filename`). Keep `MaxContentLength = 1024 * 500` but note the message text says 1 MB while the limit is 500 KB — align them (recommend keeping 500 KB and fixing the message to 'up to 500 KB'). Tighten `AllowedFileExtensions` to image types actually accepted by the chat upload (`accept="image/*"`): `.webp,.jpg,.jpeg,.png,.gif,.svg` — drop `.PDF/.pdf` unless PDFs are genuinely needed, since the save path always re-encodes to `.webp`.
6. In `SaveAsWebpAsync` (lines 104-147), replace the fragile `filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"` in both branches (lines 113 and 136) with `Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp")`. Because `filePath` is now a GUID name, this is safe, but `Split(".")` mishandles any name containing dots and should not survive. Keep the existing `Directory.CreateDirectory(Path.GetDirectoryName(filePath)!)` (line 107) — it is now creating only the validated `Image` root.
7. Return the server-generated name to the caller instead of echoing the client filename. Replace `dict.Add("success", filename)` with returning `safeName` (e.g. `return Ok(new { success = safeName });` after refactoring the action to `Task<IActionResult>`), and update `Backend/Controllers/Chat`/frontend consumers if any read the echoed name. Note `HttpResponseMessage` return type never actually serializes `dict` to the body today; consider switching the action signature to `Task<IActionResult>` returning `Ok(...)`/`BadRequest(...)` so the success name is genuinely sent.
8. Update the frontend caller in `Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx:219-223`: it currently appends the raw client file name as `?filename=...`. Since the server now ignores `filename` and returns the stored name, remove the query param (or keep it ignored) and read the returned `safeName` from the response in the `onChange` success branch to reference the uploaded image.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs

// --- class declaration: add [Authorize] (import already at line 12) ---
[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]
[Authorize]                         // ADDED: endpoint was anonymous, no global fallback policy
public class UploadController : ControllerBase

// --- Postupload: BEFORE ---
[HttpPost("Postupload")]
public async Task<HttpResponseMessage> Postupload(String filename)
{
    ...
    foreach (var file in httpRequest.Form.Files)
    {
        var postedFile = httpRequest.Form.Files[0];
        if (postedFile != null && postedFile.Length > 0)
        {
            ...
            else
            {
                var filePath = Path.Combine("wwwroot", "Image", filename); // attacker-controlled
                await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath);
                dict.Add("success", filename);
                return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.OK);
            }
        }
    }
    return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);
}

// --- Postupload: AFTER ---
[HttpPost("Postupload")]
public async Task<IActionResult> Postupload()      // filename param dropped; server names the file
{
    var httpRequest = HttpContext.Request;
    var postedFile = httpRequest.Form.Files.GetFile("file")
                     ?? httpRequest.Form.Files.FirstOrDefault();
    if (postedFile is null || postedFile.Length == 0)
        return BadRequest("No file uploaded.");

    const int MaxContentLength = 1024 * 500; // 500 KB
    var allowed = new HashSet<string>(StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase)
        { ".webp", ".jpg", ".jpeg", ".png", ".gif", ".svg" };
    var ext = Path.GetExtension(postedFile.FileName);
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(ext) || !allowed.Contains(ext))
        return BadRequest("Please upload an image of type .webp/.jpg/.png/.gif/.svg.");
    if (postedFile.Length > MaxContentLength)
        return BadRequest("Please upload a file up to 500 KB.");

    // Server-generated name — client name is never used in the path.
    var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + ".webp";
    var root = Path.Combine(
        _hostingEnvironment.WebRootPath
            ?? Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "wwwroot"),
        "Image");
    var filePath = Path.Combine(root, safeName);

    // Defense-in-depth: ensure the resolved path cannot escape the Image root.
    var rootFull = Path.GetFullPath(root + Path.DirectorySeparatorChar);
    if (!Path.GetFullPath(filePath).StartsWith(rootFull, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase))
        return BadRequest("Invalid file path.");

    try
    {
        var saved = await SaveAsWebpAsync(postedFile, filePath);
        return Ok(new { success = Path.GetFileName(saved) });
    }
    catch (Exception)
    {
        return StatusCode((int)HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError, "Upload failed.");
    }
}

// --- SaveAsWebpAsync: replace BOTH Split(".") usages (lines 113 & 136) ---
// BEFORE: var newWebpFilePath = filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp";
// AFTER:
var newWebpFilePath = Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp");
// ...and likewise:
var webpFilePath = Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp");
```

**Verify:**
- Anonymous request is rejected: `curl -k -i -X POST "https://localhost:5001/api/Upload/Postupload" -F "file=@test.png"` returns 401 Unauthorized (it previously accepted the upload).
- Authenticated happy path: repeat the curl with `-H "Authorization: Bearer <valid-jwt>"` and a small PNG; expect 200 with body `{"success":"<32-hex>.webp"}`, and confirm a file named with a GUID (not the client name) appears in `Backend/wwwroot/Image/`.
- Path traversal is neutralized: with a valid token, attempt `?filename=..%5C..%5Cappsettings.json` style values (now ignored) and confirm nothing is written outside `wwwroot/Image` and no file overwrites `appsettings.json` — verify via `git status` / file listing of the repo root and `wwwroot`.
- Multi-dot name handling: upload a file named `my.photo.v2.png`; confirm exactly one GUID `.webp` file is created (regression test that `Path.ChangeExtension` replaced the broken `Split('.')[0]`).
- Build/static-serve check: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds, and the returned GUID image is retrievable at `/Image/<guid>.webp` via `app.UseStaticFiles()`, confirming chat image display still works end-to-end through `Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx`.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Per the verifier note, the save path forces a `.webp` extension on every write, so this is NOT a config/executable overwrite vector — scope the risk to arbitrary `.webp` writes (asset overwrite, disk-fill DoS, publicly servable planted webp); do not claim you fixed arbitrary-file overwrite. Frontend coupling: `ChatBox.tsx` currently sends the file name in `?filename=` and relies on a 200/201 status; if you change the action signature to `IActionResult`/`Ok(...)` the success status still returns but the response body shape changes, so update any code that reads the echoed name to read the returned `safeName`. Changing `AllowedFileExtensions` to drop `.pdf` will reject PDFs that may have worked before (they were re-encoded as webp via SkiaSharp and likely failed decode anyway) — confirm no flow depends on PDF upload first. The existing message says "1 mb" while the limit is 500 KB; keep the limit and fix the text, or raise both together, but don't leave them inconsistent. Order: add `[Authorize]` and the path guard together — deploying the name/path change without `[Authorize]` still leaves the endpoint anonymous (writable webp + DoS).

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Two factual corrections for the report: (a) the .webp extension is forced on every save path (Split(".")[0] + ".webp"), so config/executable overwrite as described is not possible — limit the impact to arbitrary .webp writes (asset overwrite, disk-fill DoS, publicly servable planted webp). (b) Confirmed there is no global authorization fallback policy, so the missing [Authorize] truly leaves the endpoint anonymous. The "loop iterates Form.Files but always saves Form.Files[0]" detail is accurate but is a functional bug, not a security amplifier. Severity adjusted High -> Medium primarily due to the forced-extension constraint; if the deployment's working directory/wwwroot or adjacent .webp assets are sensitive, it could be argued back up toward High.


<a id="f-mass-assignment-baseapicontroller-put"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Generic CRUD PUT/POST allows mass-assignment of any entity field including UserController

**ID:** `mass-assignment-baseapicontroller-put` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Mass Assignment / Broken Access Control &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs:65-110`
- `Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs:15-19`

**Problem.** BaseAPIController<T>.PutData/PostData bind the entire entity T from the request body and write it directly (Entry(obj).State = Modified; SaveChangesAsync). UserController : BaseAPIController<User> exposes this for the User entity, whose fields include Password and Permission. Any authenticated user (the methods are [Authorize] but with no role restriction) can POST/PUT a User with an arbitrary Permission value or change another user's record by id.

**Impact.** An authenticated low-privilege user can escalate their own privileges by setting Permission, or set/reset another user's Password/Permission via PUT /api/User/{id}, because there is no per-field protection, no ownership check, and no role gate. This is a privilege-escalation and account-takeover vector within the authenticated surface.

**Recommendation.** Do not expose a generic CRUD controller over the User entity. Use explicit DTOs that exclude Password/Permission, add role-based authorization (e.g., [Authorize(Roles="Admin")]) for user management, and enforce ownership checks. Hash passwords on write (see plaintext finding).

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours (1-2 hours if password hashing is tracked/handled by the separate plaintext-password finding))_**

Stop exposing the generic CRUD base over the User entity: override the write endpoints in UserController with explicit DTOs that never bind Password/Permission from the client, gate user-management behind [Authorize(Roles="Admin")], enforce ownership (a non-admin may only edit their own record), and hash passwords on write. Leave BaseAPIController generic for other entities but harden it where cheap.

1. Confirm the exposure: UserController.cs:15 declares `public class UserController : BaseAPIController<User>` with an empty body, so it inherits BaseAPIController.cs:67 `PutData(string id, T obj)` and :92 `PostData(T data)` which bind the whole `User` (Password + Permission from Model/User.cs:21-23) and write it via `Entry(obj).State = Modified` / `AddAsync`. Permission is the JWT role source (JWTManagerService.cs:47), so a low-priv user POSTing/PUTting Permission='Admin' is privilege escalation.
2. Add DTOs. Create `Backend/Model/Dtos/UserDtos.cs` (namespace `API.Model`) with `UserCreateDto` and `UserUpdateDto` as shown in the patch. `UserUpdateDto` MUST NOT contain a `Permission` property; password change is an explicit optional `NewPassword`. Add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;`.
3. Rewrite `Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs`: stop inheriting `BaseAPIController<User>`. Change the declaration to `public class UserController : ControllerBase`, inject `ApplicationDbContext` (already imported via `using API.DBContext;`), and add `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;` and `using System.Security.Claims;`. Implement only the vetted Get/Create/Update/Delete methods from the patch. This removes the inherited generic `PutData`/`PostData`/`PostDataList`/`DeleteData`/`Get` routes for the User entity entirely.
4. Gate privileged operations with `[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]` on Create and Delete. The role string must match the `Permission` value stored for admin rows in the `Users` table (there is no HasData seed in code — rows are inserted directly in the DB; verify the actual admin Permission value via the DB and use that exact string). Note `AuthenticateTradenet2` (JWTManagerService.cs:77) issues role `"User"`, so those tokens will correctly be denied admin operations.
5. Enforce ownership in `Update`: read the caller id from `User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value` (the JWT sets ClaimTypes.Name = user Id at JWTManagerService.cs:46) and allow the write only if `User.IsInRole("Admin")` or `callerId == id`; otherwise return `Forbid()`. Bind only `Name` and optional `NewPassword`; never assign `Permission` from the request in this method.
6. Never write the client-supplied object. In Update, load the tracked entity with `_context.Users.FindAsync(id)`, mutate allowed fields on it, and save — do not do `Entry(clientObj).State = Modified`. In Create, construct a new `User` server-side and only copy whitelisted fields.
7. Hash passwords on write (ties into the related plaintext-password finding): use `BCrypt.Net.BCrypt.HashPassword(...)` for `Create` and for the optional password change in `Update`. Add the `BCrypt.Net-Next` NuGet package if not already referenced (`dotnet add Backend package BCrypt.Net-Next`). If the password-hashing remediation is handled in a separate finding, coordinate so both use the same hashing API and the login path (`JWTManagerService.Authenticate`, currently comparing `x.Password == users.Password` at line 32) is updated to verify the hash.
8. Harden the base class for the remaining entities (defense in depth): in `BaseAPIController.cs`, remove the class-level `[AllowAnonymous]` at line 15 (it is already overridden by method-level `[Authorize]`, so this is cleanup that prevents a future write endpoint from being added without auth). Consider also confirming no other security-sensitive entity (e.g. TokenModel) is exposed through a generic `BaseAPIController<T>` subclass.
9. If anything in the frontend calls `POST /api/User` or `PUT /api/User/{id}` with a full User object, update those callers to send the new DTO shape (search `Frontend/src` for `/api/User` / axios calls). The `CreatedAtAction("GetData", ...)` in the old base referenced a non-existent action name; the new code uses `nameof(Get)`.

```csharp
// Backend/Model/User.cs — add DTOs (or put in a Backend/Model/Dtos/UserDtos.cs file)
// Request bodies that DO NOT contain Permission/Password unless explicitly intended.
public class UserUpdateDto
{
    [Required] public string Name { get; set; } = "";
    // optional: only set when the caller wants to change the password
    public string? NewPassword { get; set; }
    // Permission is intentionally absent — only an admin path may set it (see UserAdminUpdateDto)
}

public class UserCreateDto
{
    [Required] public string Name { get; set; } = "";
    [Required] public string Password { get; set; } = "";
    [Required] public string Permission { get; set; } = "User";
}

// Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs — replace the empty pass-through class.
// Inheriting BaseAPIController<User> re-exposes its [HttpPut]/[HttpPost] for User; we must
// hide them. Simplest safe option: DON'T inherit the generic base for User. Derive from
// ControllerBase and implement only vetted endpoints.
namespace API.Controllers
{
    [ApiController]
    [Authorize]
    [Route("api/[controller]")]
    public class UserController : ControllerBase
    {
        private readonly ApplicationDbContext _context;
        public UserController(ApplicationDbContext context) => _context = context;

        // GET stays available to any authenticated user only if you need it; otherwise gate it too.
        [HttpGet("{id}")]
        public async Task<ActionResult<User>> Get(string id)
        {
            var u = await _context.Users.FindAsync(id);
            return u == null ? NotFound() : u;  // consider returning a DTO without Password
        }

        [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
        [HttpPost]
        public async Task<ActionResult<User>> Create(UserCreateDto dto)
        {
            var u = new User { Name = dto.Name, Permission = dto.Permission };
            u.Password = BCrypt.Net.BCrypt.HashPassword(dto.Password); // align with plaintext-password finding
            await _context.Users.AddAsync(u);
            await _context.SaveChangesAsync();
            return CreatedAtAction(nameof(Get), new { id = u.Id }, u);
        }

        [HttpPut("{id}")]
        public async Task<IActionResult> Update(string id, UserUpdateDto dto)
        {
            var u = await _context.Users.FindAsync(id);
            if (u == null) return NotFound();

            var callerId = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;        // JWT puts Id here (JWTManagerService.cs:46)
            var isAdmin  = User.IsInRole("Admin");
            if (!isAdmin && callerId != id) return Forbid();              // ownership check

            u.Name = dto.Name;                                            // Permission NEVER bound here
            if (!string.IsNullOrEmpty(dto.NewPassword))
                u.Password = BCrypt.Net.BCrypt.HashPassword(dto.NewPassword);

            _context.Entry(u).State = EntityState.Modified;               // tracked entity, not client object
            await _context.SaveChangesAsync();
            return NoContent();
        }

        [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
        [HttpDelete("{id}")]
        public async Task<IActionResult> Delete(string id)
        {
            var u = await _context.Users.FindAsync(id);
            if (u == null) return NotFound();
            _context.Users.Remove(u);
            await _context.SaveChangesAsync();
            return NoContent();
        }
    }
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` in `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend` succeeds; confirm UserController no longer derives from BaseAPIController<User>.
- Escalation attempt blocked: log in as a non-admin (token role 'User'), then `curl -X PUT https://<host>/api/User/<ownId> -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"Name":"me","Permission":"Admin"}'` — the Permission field must be ignored (DTO has no such property) and the stored Permission must remain unchanged. Query the Users row to confirm.
- Cross-user edit blocked: as a non-admin, `PUT /api/User/<someOtherUserId>` returns 403 Forbidden.
- Admin-only gates: as a non-admin, `POST /api/User` and `DELETE /api/User/{id}` return 403; as an admin (Permission matching the Roles string) they succeed.
- Password hashing: after Create or a NewPassword update, inspect the Users.Password column — it must be a BCrypt hash (`$2a$...`), not plaintext; and login via `POST /api/Auth` still returns a token (after the matching login-verification change).
- Route removal: confirm `POST /api/User/PostDataList` and the generic `PutData` route no longer exist for User (404), proving the generic write surface is gone for this entity.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Inheritance trap: as long as UserController inherits BaseAPIController<User>, the inherited [HttpPut]/[HttpPost] routes remain registered even if you add new methods — you must drop the inheritance (or override+hide every write action) or the vulnerable endpoints stay live.; Role string must match DB data: admin Permission values live in the database, not in code seeds. If you gate with Roles="Admin" but the DB stores e.g. "admin" or "Administrator", admins get locked out. Confirm the exact stored value first; ClaimTypes.Role is fed verbatim from Permission (JWTManagerService.cs:47).; Password hashing must be rolled out together with login verification — if you start hashing on write but leave `Authenticate` doing `x.Password == users.Password` (JWTManagerService.cs:32), every newly-saved/changed user will be unable to log in. Existing plaintext rows also won't match a hash; plan a migration/rehash-on-next-login. Coordinate with the related plaintext-password finding.; Frontend coupling: any existing client code posting a full User object (including Permission) will break against the new DTOs; update callers in Frontend/src.; Token claim source: ownership check relies on ClaimTypes.Name holding the user Id (set at JWTManagerService.cs:46). The RefreshToken path (line 116) also sets Name=UserId, so that holds; just don't assume the username is in ClaimTypes.Name.; Rollback: revert UserController.cs and remove the DTO file; the base class [AllowAnonymous] removal is independent and safe to keep.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Locations are accurate. Severity Medium is fair (arguably could be argued High for account takeover, but tempered by the fact that no role-based authorization is currently enforced anywhere, so escalation grants nothing extra at present). Two related facts strengthen the finding: (1) User.Permission is wired directly into ClaimTypes.Role in JWTManagerService.cs:47, so it is a true role field; (2) the same generic CRUD base exposes unprotected PUT/POST/DELETE for every entity, not just User — User is just the highest-impact instance. Also note the base class [AllowAnonymous] at BaseAPIController.cs:15 is overridden by the method-level [Authorize] attributes, so the finder's claim that the endpoints are authenticated-but-unrestricted is correct.


<a id="f-cors-allowcredentials-localhost-wildcard"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Over-permissive CORS: AllowCredentials with localhost wildcard and AllowAnyHeader/Method

**ID:** `cors-allowcredentials-localhost-wildcard` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** CORS Misconfiguration &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:118-156`
- `Backend/Program.cs:119-156`

**Problem.** The CORS policy calls .AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader().AllowCredentials() while WithOrigins includes "http://localhost:*" (a wildcard-port localhost origin) plus numerous localhost variants and a trailing-slash production origin. Allowing credentials together with a wildcard/loopback origin is exactly the combination the CORS spec and browsers warn against. The repeated builder.WithMethods(...) calls are also redundant/confusing (the last AllowAnyMethod wins).

**Impact.** Any page the victim loads on any localhost port (e.g., a malicious local dev server, an Electron/Capacitor app, or malware-served content on the loopback) can make credentialed cross-origin requests to the API and read the responses on the admin's behalf — including pulling report data or, combined with the JWT in localStorage/cookies, acting as the admin. This widens the attack surface considerably for a sensitive internal tool.

**Recommendation.** Remove localhost wildcards from production. Maintain an explicit allow-list of real front-end origins only, drop the trailing-slash duplicates, and keep AllowCredentials only for those exact origins. Gate localhost origins behind IsDevelopment(). Replace the redundant WithMethods calls with a single explicit method list.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Tighten the single inline `app.UseCors(...)` policy in Backend/Program.cs: keep an explicit production allow-list with AllowCredentials, gate all localhost/capacitor dev origins behind `app.Environment.IsDevelopment()`, drop the inert trailing-slash and non-functional `http://localhost:*` entries, and replace the redundant chain of `WithMethods(...)` calls plus `AllowAnyMethod()` with a single explicit method+header set.

1. Open Backend/Program.cs and locate the #region Cors block at lines 118-157 (the inline `app.UseCors(builder => { ... })` middleware overload — note the lambda parameter is named `builder`, which shadows the outer `WebApplication.CreateBuilder` builder; it is a CorsPolicyBuilder here).
2. Delete the five redundant `builder.WithMethods("GET"|"PUT"|"POST"|"DELETE"|"*")` lines (122-126) and the standalone `builder.WithHeaders("Authorization")` line (127). They are overridden later by `.AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader()` on line 153 so they currently do nothing.
3. Build the origin list in two parts. Declare the production origins as an explicit array WITHOUT trailing slashes and WITHOUT any localhost entries: "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com", "https://www.mpuecomuat.com", "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com". Remove the duplicate trailing-slash form "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com/" (browsers never send a trailing-slash Origin, so it is dead config).
4. Gate every localhost/capacitor origin behind the dev check. Reuse the existing `app.Environment.IsDevelopment()` (already used at line 100) so the dev origins are only added when running in Development. Drop the non-functional "http://localhost:*" entry entirely (ASP.NET Core `WithOrigins` does exact string match in net8.0 — it is not a wildcard and never matched anything) and drop the trailing-slash duplicates ("http://localhost:5173/", "http://localhost/", "https://localhost/", "http://localhost:8100/", "http://localhost:3000/").
5. Replace `.AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader()` with explicit allowances scoped to what the SPA actually needs: `.WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS")` and `.WithHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type")`. Keep `.AllowCredentials()` — it is now safe because every remaining origin is an explicit, fully-qualified string (no wildcard).
6. Remove the now-redundant second `app.UseHttpsRedirection();` while you are here is OPTIONAL and out of scope for this finding — do not touch lines 159-160 unless separately requested. Keep the change confined to the #region Cors block (lines 118-157).

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs  — #region Cors (lines 118-157)
// BEFORE
app.UseCors(
    builder =>
    {
        builder.WithMethods("GET");
        builder.WithMethods("PUT");
        builder.WithMethods("POST");
        builder.WithMethods("DELETE");
        builder.WithMethods("*");
        builder.WithHeaders("Authorization");
        builder.WithOrigins(
            "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
            "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
            "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com",
            "https://www.mpuecomuat.com",
            "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com/",
            "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com",
            "capacitor://localhost",
            "http://localhost:5173",
            "http://localhost:5173/",
            "http://localhost",
            "http://localhost/",
            "https://localhost",
            "https://localhost/",
            "http://localhost:*",
            "http://localhost:8100",
            "http://localhost:8100/",
            "http://localhost:3000",
            "http://localhost:3000/").AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader().AllowCredentials();
    }
);

// AFTER
var allowedOrigins = new List<string>
{
    "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
    "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
    "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com",
    "https://www.mpuecomuat.com",
    "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com"
};

// Local dev / Capacitor origins are only trusted in Development.
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    allowedOrigins.AddRange(new[]
    {
        "capacitor://localhost",
        "http://localhost",
        "https://localhost",
        "http://localhost:5173",
        "http://localhost:8100",
        "http://localhost:3000"
    });
}

app.UseCors(policy =>
    policy.WithOrigins(allowedOrigins.ToArray())
          .WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS")
          .WithHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type")
          .AllowCredentials());
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build the Backend project succeeds (the inline-middleware overload still type-checks; the lambda param renamed to `policy` is still a CorsPolicyBuilder).
- Production preflight rejects localhost: run the API with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production and send `curl -i -X OPTIONS https://<api>/api/<anyendpoint> -H 'Origin: http://localhost:3000' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'` — the response must NOT contain an `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:3000` header (origin not echoed = blocked).
- Production allows the real SPA: same curl with `-H 'Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com'` returns `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
- Dev still works: run with ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development and repeat with `Origin: http://localhost:5173` — it is now echoed back.
- Confirm the wildcard removal had no functional cost: previously `http://localhost:*` never matched any real browser Origin, so no legitimate caller loses access.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do not pair `.AllowCredentials()` with `.AllowAnyOrigin()` / a `*` origin — ASP.NET Core throws at runtime ("AllowCredentials and AllowAnyOrigin cannot be used together"). The new code only uses explicit origins, so this is fine; just don't reintroduce a wildcard.; Add only origins, never paths or query strings — `WithOrigins` matches scheme://host:port exactly, so trailing slashes (the removed `.../`) never match a real Origin header. Re-adding them is harmless but dead config.; If the mobile Capacitor app is built/run in a non-Development environment and genuinely needs `capacitor://localhost` / `http://localhost`, the dev-only gate will block it. If that is a real production client, add those specific origins to the production `allowedOrigins` list explicitly (still no wildcard) rather than removing the IsDevelopment gate.; Environment is driven by ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT; verify the deployed hosting (IIS/systemd/container) actually sets it to Production, otherwise the dev origins stay enabled.; Rollback is trivial: revert the single #region Cors block; no data, migration, or schema impact.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Confirmed parts: AllowCredentials + AllowAnyHeader/AllowAnyMethod over hardcoded localhost/capacitor origins (real), redundant WithMethods then AllowAnyMethod (real, cosmetic), trailing-slash origins (real but inert — browsers never send trailing-slash Origin). Refuted part: "http://localhost:*" is NOT a functioning wildcard in ASP.NET Core WithOrigins (exact string match, no wildcard-subdomain config, net8.0), so the "any localhost port" impact is invalid. Recommendation remains valid; severity lowered from High to Low. This is the only CORS configuration in the backend (grep found no other AddCors/SetIsOriginAllowed/WithOrigins).


---

### Backend — Architecture & Data Access

> The codebase is two architecturally distinct halves. The newer ExcelExport subsystem (Backend/Service/ExcelExport) is genuinely well-built: it uses the IOptions pattern, ILogger structured logging, scoped DbContext-per-scope in a BackgroundService, atomic DB leasing, and full CancellationToken propagation. The much larger legacy reporting surface (158 controllers in Backend/Controllers/Report, 83 static sp_* helpers, a 228-entity scaffolded TradeNetDbContext) lacks these disciplines. The cross-cutting gaps are the highest-impact: no global exception-handling middleware (only the dev exception page), no rate limiting, no health checks, no API versioning, and structured logging present in only 2 files of the whole app. The two-DbContext design is reasonable in principle (one code-first app DB, one DB-first read model) but only one is under migration control, and connection strings/JWT secrets are read via raw IConfiguration instead of bound/secret-managed options. A generic [AllowAnonymous] CRUD base controller over the User entity and the lack of CancellationToken on the synchronous paged endpoints round out the notable findings.

<a id="f-secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Connection strings and JWT signing key read via raw IConfiguration from checked-in appsettings.json (no options/secret binding)

**ID:** `secrets-raw-iconfiguration-no-options` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Configuration / Secrets &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:64-67 (DbContexts), Backend/Program.cs:74-89 (JWT setup, ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience=false at 83-84), Backend/appsettings.json:9-13 (JWT block; finding said 8-12), Backend/appsettings.json:24-28 (ConnectionStrings block; finding said 21-24). ExcelExport options pattern at Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.cs:20.`
- `Backend/Program.cs:64-67`
- `Backend/Program.cs:74-89`
- `Backend/appsettings.json:8-12`
- `Backend/appsettings.json:21-24`

**Problem.** Program.cs binds both DbContexts and the JWT key directly from builder.Configuration with no options object, no environment-variable/Key Vault/user-secrets override, and no validation beyond a null check on JWT:Key (Program.cs:74-78). appsettings.json (committed to the repo) contains the production SQL Server 'sa' credentials for 203.81.66.111,14330 (TemplateDB and TradeNetDB) and a guessable literal JWT key 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' with issuer/audience validation disabled (ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience=false, Program.cs:83-84). Note the ExcelExport subsystem already demonstrates the correct pattern (services.Configure<ExcelExportOptions>, ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.cs:20) — connection/JWT config simply never adopted it.

**Impact.** Anyone with repo access has full sysadmin ('sa') credentials to the production trade database and can forge valid admin JWTs offline using the literal key (signature is the only check since issuer/audience are not validated). This is direct, exploitable government-data compromise. Even rotating the password is futile while it lives in version control.

**Recommendation.** Move all secrets out of appsettings.json into environment variables / a secret store and load via the options pattern (e.g. AddOptions<JwtOptions>().Bind(...).ValidateOnStart()). Use a least-privilege SQL login, not 'sa'. Rotate the leaked password and JWT key immediately and purge them from git history. Set ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience=true with a strong (>=256-bit) random key.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours (code change ~1h; secret rotation + least-privilege SQL login + env/user-secrets wiring ~1h; git history purge + coordinated force-push ~1-2h))_**

Introduce a validated JwtOptions class (mirroring the existing ExcelExportOptions pattern), bind it with AddOptions().Bind().ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart(), and have both the JWT validation (Program.cs) and signing (JWTManagerService) read from it instead of raw IConfiguration; move all secrets (sa connection strings, JWT key/issuer/audience) out of the tracked appsettings.json into environment variables / user-secrets, untrack the file from git, rotate the leaked sa password and JWT key, and purge git history.

1. Create Backend/Service/Auth/JwtOptions.cs (new folder) mirroring ExcelExportOptions: `public sealed class JwtOptions { public const string SectionName = "JWT"; [Required, MinLength(32)] public string Key {get;set;}=""; [Required] public string Issuer {get;set;}=""; [Required] public string Audience {get;set;}=""; }` Add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;`. The MinLength(32) enforces a >=256-bit HMAC key.
2. In Backend/Program.cs, before the AddAuthentication block (line 68), register and validate the options: `builder.Services.AddOptions<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions>().Bind(builder.Configuration.GetSection(API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions.SectionName)).ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart();`
3. In Program.cs AddJwtBearer (lines 72-91), replace the raw `builder.Configuration["JWT:Key"]` read and the null-check (lines 74-79) with the bound options. Resolve via `var jwt = builder.Configuration.GetSection(API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions.SectionName).Get<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions>() ?? throw new InvalidOperationException("JWT options missing");` then `var Key = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(jwt.Key);`.
4. In Program.cs TokenValidationParameters (lines 81-90), flip `ValidateIssuer = true`, `ValidateAudience = true`, keep `ValidateLifetime = true` and `ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true`, and set `ValidIssuer = jwt.Issuer`, `ValidAudience = jwt.Audience`, `IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Key)`.
5. CRITICAL signing-side fix in Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs: the three token descriptors (lines 42, 72, 112) currently emit NO Issuer/Audience claims. If you enable ValidateIssuer/Audience without this, every login token fails validation. Inject options: change the field/ctor from `IConfiguration iconfiguration` to `private readonly API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions _jwt;` taking `Microsoft.Extensions.Options.IOptions<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions> jwtOptions` and assigning `_jwt = jwtOptions.Value;` (add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Options;`).
6. In each of the three SecurityTokenDescriptor blocks in JWTManagerService.cs, replace `var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(iconfiguration["JWT:Key"] ?? "");` with `var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(_jwt.Key);` and add `Issuer = _jwt.Issuer, Audience = _jwt.Audience,` to the descriptor so emitted tokens match the now-enabled ValidIssuer/ValidAudience.
7. Strip secrets from Backend/appsettings.json: set `JWT.Key` to "", and for ConnectionStrings keep only non-secret shape or empty placeholders (do NOT leave the sa credentials or literal key). Real values come from env vars / user-secrets at runtime via the options binder and GetConnectionString (which still reads ConnectionStrings__* env vars).
8. Provide the real secrets out-of-band. Dev (user-secrets, UserSecretsId f930729b-... already in API.csproj): `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<new 32+ char random>" --project Backend/API.csproj`, same for `ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB` and `ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest` with a NEW least-privilege login (not sa) and ROTATED password. Prod: set env vars `JWT__Key`, `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest` (double-underscore) in the host/container/secret store.
9. Stop tracking the file that .gitignore (line 162) already lists: `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` and commit. (appsettings.Development.json is also tracked but holds no secrets after this change; consider untracking it too.)
10. Rotate and purge: change the SQL 'sa' password (and create the new least-privilege login) on 203.81.66.111,14330, generate a brand-new JWT signing key, then scrub git history of the leaked values with `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths` (or BFG) and force-push, coordinating with all clones. Treat the old key as compromised — any tokens signed with it are forgeable.

```csharp
// NEW FILE: Backend/Service/Auth/JwtOptions.cs
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;

namespace API.Service.Auth
{
    public sealed class JwtOptions
    {
        public const string SectionName = "JWT";

        [Required, MinLength(32)] // >= 256-bit HMAC key
        public string Key { get; set; } = "";

        [Required]
        public string Issuer { get; set; } = "";

        [Required]
        public string Audience { get; set; } = "";
    }
}

// Program.cs — before AddAuthentication (around line 68)
builder.Services.AddOptions<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions>()
    .Bind(builder.Configuration.GetSection(API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions.SectionName))
    .ValidateDataAnnotations()
    .ValidateOnStart();

// Program.cs — AddJwtBearer block (replaces lines 73-90)
}).AddJwtBearer(o =>
{
    var jwt = builder.Configuration
        .GetSection(API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions.SectionName)
        .Get<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions>()
        ?? throw new InvalidOperationException("JWT options are missing.");
    var Key = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(jwt.Key);
    o.SaveToken = true;
    o.TokenValidationParameters = new TokenValidationParameters
    {
        ValidateIssuer = true,            // was false
        ValidateAudience = true,          // was false
        ValidateLifetime = true,
        ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true,
        ValidIssuer = jwt.Issuer,
        ValidAudience = jwt.Audience,
        IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(Key)
    };
});

// JWTManagerService.cs — ctor + 3 descriptors
// before:
//   private readonly IConfiguration iconfiguration;
//   public JWTManagerService(IConfiguration iconfiguration, ...) { this.iconfiguration = iconfiguration; ... }
//   var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(iconfiguration["JWT:Key"] ?? "");
// after:
private readonly API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions _jwt;
public JWTManagerService(Microsoft.Extensions.Options.IOptions<API.Service.Auth.JwtOptions> jwtOptions,
    ICommonService<TokenModel> tokenService, ICommonService<User> userService)
{
    _jwt = jwtOptions.Value;
    _tokenService = tokenService;
    _userService = userService;
}
// ...inside each SecurityTokenDescriptor:
var tokenKey = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(_jwt.Key);
var tokenDescriptor = new SecurityTokenDescriptor
{
    Issuer = _jwt.Issuer,       // NEW — required now that ValidateIssuer=true
    Audience = _jwt.Audience,   // NEW — required now that ValidateAudience=true
    Subject = new ClaimsIdentity(new Claim[] { /* unchanged */ }),
    Expires = DateTime.Now.AddDays(1),
    SigningCredentials = new SigningCredentials(new SymmetricSecurityKey(tokenKey), SecurityAlgorithms.HmacSha256Signature)
};

// appsettings.json — secrets removed (real values via env/user-secrets)
"JWT": { "Key": "", "Issuer": "", "Audience": "" },
"ConnectionStrings": { "DefaultConnection": "", "TemplateDB": "", "TradeNetDBTest": "" }
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build Backend/API.csproj` succeeds with the new JwtOptions and updated JWTManagerService ctor.
- Fail-fast check: run the app with an empty/short JWT:Key (no user-secret set) and confirm ValidateOnStart throws an OptionsValidationException at startup (proves validation + >=32-char rule fire) instead of silently running with a weak key.
- Set a valid 32+ char key + connection strings via user-secrets, start the app, POST to the login/authenticate endpoint, decode the returned JWT (jwt.io) and confirm `iss` = configured Issuer and `aud` = configured Audience are present.
- Call an [Authorize] report endpoint with that fresh token -> 200. Then forge/replay a token signed with the OLD literal key 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' -> expect 401 (proves the key was rotated and the old key no longer validates).
- `git ls-files Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing (file untracked); `git grep -n 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022\|supper secret key' $(git rev-list --all | head)` returns nothing after history purge.
- `grep -rn 'JWT:Key' Backend --include=*.cs` returns no matches outside this change (raw IConfiguration reads eliminated).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Ordering trap: do NOT enable ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience (Program.cs) without also adding Issuer/Audience to the three SecurityTokenDescriptors in JWTManagerService.cs in the same change — otherwise all newly issued tokens lack iss/aud claims and every authorized request 401s. Rotating the JWT key invalidates ALL currently issued tokens (login tokens last 1 day, the Tradenet2/refresh tokens up to 31 days) — every user must re-authenticate; schedule accordingly. The new sa-replacement SQL login must have the same schema/exec rights the reports need (the app currently runs as sysadmin); test report + Excel-export queries before cutover. GetConnectionString still works with env vars only if you use the ConnectionStrings__<Name> double-underscore convention. History purge (git filter-repo/BFG) rewrites SHAs and requires a coordinated force-push; treat the leaked sa password and JWT key as compromised regardless of purge — rotation is mandatory, not optional. appsettings.Development.json is also git-tracked; leaving it tracked is fine since it now holds no secrets.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Citation line numbers for appsettings.json are slightly off (off-by-one to off-by-a-few) but point at the right blocks; corrected above. Severity Critical is appropriate: committed 'sa' production credentials + an in-repo, low-entropy JWT signing key with issuer/audience validation disabled = direct offline admin-token forgery and full DB compromise. Additional supporting context not in the finding: Backend/appsettings.json is listed in .gitignore yet remains git-tracked, so the secrets persist in history and require a history purge + rotation, reinforcing the recommendation. CORS also includes a broad WithMethods("*") and many origins (out of scope here but adjacent). No change to severity.


<a id="f-allowanonymous-generic-crud-base"></a>
#### 🟠 HIGH — Generic [AllowAnonymous] CRUD base controller exposes the User entity table over HTTP

**ID:** `allowanonymous-generic-crud-base` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Authorization / Separation of concerns &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs:13-25`
- `Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs:91-126`
- `Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs:15-19`

**Problem.** BaseAPIController<T> is annotated [AllowAnonymous] at the class level (line 15) and provides full unparameterized CRUD (Get list, Get by id, Put, Post, PostDataList, Delete) directly against context.Set<T>(). UserController : BaseAPIController<User> (UserController.cs:15) inherits all of it and exposes /api/User. The individual actions carry [Authorize], which at runtime overrides the class-level [AllowAnonymous], but relying on that override ordering for an anonymous-by-default base class that wraps the credentials table is fragile: any future subclass or any action added without [Authorize] is silently public. PostData/PutData also accept the full entity T with no DTO/projection, enabling over-posting of fields like password hashes or roles.

**Impact.** A single missed [Authorize] (easy when the default is AllowAnonymous) exposes read/write/delete of the User table to anonymous callers. The over-posting surface lets an authenticated low-privilege user potentially escalate by setting fields the API never intended to be client-settable.

**Recommendation.** Remove [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController and make [Authorize] the secure default (apply at class level or via a fallback authorization policy in Program.cs). Replace the generic entity in/out with explicit DTOs to stop over-posting. If the generic CRUD base is not actually needed for User, delete it.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2 hours)_**

Remove the class-level [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController<T> so it is no longer anonymous-by-default (which currently short-circuits the per-action [Authorize] and exposes the User credentials table), make authenticated access the secure default via a fallback authorization policy in Program.cs, and introduce explicit request/response DTOs for UserController so the Password/Permission columns can no longer be over-posted.

1. Open Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs and DELETE the class-level [AllowAnonymous] attribute on line 15 (the line directly above `public class BaseAPIController<T> : ControllerBase where T : class`). This is the single most important change: in ASP.NET Core [AllowAnonymous] beats [Authorize] no matter where it sits, so while it is present the inherited Get/Get(id)/PutData/PostData/PostDataList/DeleteData actions on /api/User are reachable anonymously despite their per-action [Authorize].
2. Keep the per-action [Authorize] attributes already on every action (lines 28, 50, 65, 90, 100, 113). UserController.cs already declares [Authorize] at class level (line 13), so once [AllowAnonymous] is gone, /api/User is fully authenticated. No edit to UserController's attributes is required for the auth fix.
3. Add a fallback authorization policy as defense-in-depth so any future action that forgets [Authorize] is still protected. In Backend/Program.cs, immediately after the `builder.Services.AddAuthentication(...).AddJwtBearer(...)` block ends (after line 91), add an AddAuthorization call with a FallbackPolicy of RequireAuthenticatedUser(). This makes 'authenticated' the default for every endpoint that has no explicit auth attribute.
4. Verify the public login still works: Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs already has an explicit [AllowAnonymous] on its login action (line 23). An explicit per-action [AllowAnonymous] overrides the FallbackPolicy, so login remains public. Do not remove that attribute.
5. Stop over-posting on the User credentials table. Create Backend/Model/Dtos/UserDtos.cs with a UserCreateDto / UserUpdateDto (Name + Password + Permission, no Id allowed on update from client beyond the route id) and a UserResponseDto that omits Password. Then override the generic write/read actions in UserController so the wire contract is the DTO, not the raw User entity.
6. In UserController.cs, replace the empty body with overrides that hide the generic PostData/PutData/Get-by-id from the inherited base for the User type: add `[HttpPost]` Post(UserCreateDto), `[HttpPut("{id}")]` Put(string id, UserUpdateDto), and a Get-by-id that maps to UserResponseDto. Inside each, hash/validate as needed and map only the whitelisted fields onto the User entity before calling SaveChangesAsync via the injected ApplicationDbContext (add a `using API.Model.Dtos;` and inject the context the same way the base does). Because these new actions share the same [HttpPost]/[HttpPut("{id}")] routes, also mark the corresponding base actions to not be exposed for User — simplest is to make the base PostData/PutData/PostDataList `protected` (non-action) and re-expose only the DTO-based ones in concrete subclasses; alternatively keep them public but ensure the subclass routes win — prefer making base CRUD `protected virtual` so subclasses opt in deliberately.
7. If, after review, the generic CRUD base is not actually needed for User (the app's real user management may go through AuthController), delete BaseAPIController.cs and convert UserController to a plain [Authorize] ControllerBase exposing only the specific, DTO-based actions it needs. This removes the entire over-broad attack surface (Put/Post/PostDataList/Delete on the credentials table) in one move and is the recommendation's preferred end-state.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs  (lines 13-16)
// BEFORE
[Route("api/[controller]")]
[ApiController]
[AllowAnonymous]                                  // <-- delete this line
public class BaseAPIController<T> : ControllerBase where T : class

// AFTER
[Route("api/[controller]")]
[ApiController]
public class BaseAPIController<T> : ControllerBase where T : class


// Backend/Program.cs  (insert right after the AddJwtBearer(...) block, i.e. after line 91)
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.FallbackPolicy = new AuthorizationPolicyBuilder()
        .RequireAuthenticatedUser()
        .Build();
});
// requires: using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;


// Backend/Model/Dtos/UserDtos.cs  (new file - stops over-posting Password/Permission/Id)
namespace API.Model.Dtos
{
    public class UserCreateDto
    {
        public string Name { get; set; } = "";
        public string Password { get; set; } = "";
        public string Permission { get; set; } = "";
    }
    public class UserUpdateDto
    {
        public string Name { get; set; } = "";
        public string? Password { get; set; }     // null = keep existing
        public string Permission { get; set; } = "";
    }
    public class UserResponseDto                  // never returns Password
    {
        public string Id { get; set; } = "";
        public string Name { get; set; } = "";
        public string Permission { get; set; } = "";
    }
}
```

**Verify:**
- Before the fix, confirm the exposure: `curl -i https://<host>/api/User` (or http://localhost:5173-proxied API) with NO Authorization header returns 200 + a JSON list including Password/Permission values. After removing [AllowAnonymous], the same anonymous request must return 401 Unauthorized.
- Anonymous write/delete must also be blocked: `curl -i -X DELETE https://<host>/api/User/<someId>` and `curl -i -X POST https://<host>/api/User -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}'` with no token must each return 401 (previously 200/Created/Ok).
- With a valid JWT in the Authorization header, `GET /api/User` still returns 200 and the data, proving the per-action [Authorize] + class-level [Authorize] on UserController still permit authenticated access.
- Public login is unaffected: `POST /api/Auth` (the [AllowAnonymous] login action) with valid credentials and no prior token still returns a token (the FallbackPolicy does not apply to actions that carry explicit [AllowAnonymous]).
- Over-posting closed: with a valid token, `POST /api/User` with a body containing an `Id` or extra fields no longer lets the client set arbitrary primary keys/fields, and `GET /api/User/{id}` response (UserResponseDto) contains no Password field. Inspect the response JSON to confirm Password is absent.
- Build check: `dotnet build Backend` from the repo root succeeds with no missing-using errors (AuthorizationPolicyBuilder requires using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization in Program.cs).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Adding the FallbackPolicy makes EVERY endpoint require authentication by default, including any endpoint that was previously (incorrectly) open and relied on the base class being anonymous. Before deploying, grep all controllers for actions that legitimately need to be public; AuthController's login already has explicit [AllowAnonymous] (line 23) so it is safe, but verify there are no other intentionally-public endpoints (e.g. health checks, swagger in prod) that would suddenly start returning 401. None other were found in Controllers/, but confirm against any newly added controllers. Rollback is trivial (re-add the attribute / remove the AddAuthorization block) but should not be done since it re-opens the credentials table. The DTO change is a wire-contract change for /api/User Post/Put — coordinate with the React frontend (Frontend/src) if it currently posts the raw User shape, otherwise those calls may fail validation."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Correction to the finding's rationale: [AllowAnonymous] wins over [Authorize] in ASP.NET Core regardless of placement, so the inherited base-class [AllowAnonymous] most likely renders the /api/User Get/Get-by-id/Put/Post/PostDataList/Delete endpoints anonymously reachable right now (read/write/delete of the credentials+permission table), not merely "fragile for future subclasses." UserController also carries its own [Authorize] at line 13 (finding missed this), but that does not change the outcome — AllowAnonymous still short-circuits. Recommendation (remove [AllowAnonymous], make [Authorize] the default / add a fallback policy, replace generic T with DTOs) is correct and should be treated as urgent. Severity High is defensible; given likely-live anonymous access to a credentials table with write/delete, Critical is also reasonable — I leave it at High pending a runtime confirmation but flag the upgrade case.


<a id="f-no-global-exception-middleware"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — No global exception-handling middleware; in production unhandled errors leak raw stack traces or return opaque 500s

**ID:** `no-global-exception-middleware` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Reliability / Error handling &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:100-107 (only UseDeveloperExceptionPage in Dev / UseHsts otherwise; no global handler); Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs:70-76,160-164 (the only report-controller try/catch, narrow to missing _pagination SqlException)`
- `Backend/Program.cs:99-107`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs:57-77`

**Problem.** The pipeline only registers app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage() in Development and app.UseHsts() otherwise (Program.cs:100-107). There is no app.UseExceptionHandler(...), no IExceptionHandler, and no AddProblemDetails(). A grep across the whole backend shows zero UseExceptionHandler/IExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails registrations. Only 5 of 158 report controllers contain any try/catch, and those that do (e.g. AccountSummaryReportController.cs:70) catch only a specific SqlException for a missing _pagination proc. Any other exception (DB timeout against the remote 203.81.66.111 server, null reference in an aggregation, a SkiaSharp failure) propagates unhandled.

**Impact.** In production any unhandled exception returns the framework's default 500 with no consistent error contract for the React client, and there is no central place that logs the failure with a correlation id — operators are blind. If the environment is ever misconfigured to Development (a real risk given Swagger is already served in prod), full stack traces and SQL details are returned to callers.

**Recommendation.** Add a global handler before routing: in prod app.UseExceptionHandler() with builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(), or a custom IExceptionHandler that logs with a correlation id and returns a sanitized ProblemDetails. Keep UseDeveloperExceptionPage strictly for Development. Remove the per-controller SqlException catch in favor of a typed exception the handler can map.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 45 min)_**

Add a centralized error path: register the built-in ProblemDetails service plus a custom IExceptionHandler that logs every unhandled exception with a correlation id and returns a sanitized RFC-7807 response, then wire app.UseExceptionHandler() at the top of the pipeline in Program.cs (keeping UseDeveloperExceptionPage strictly for Development). No NuGet package is needed — IExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails/UseExceptionHandler ship in net8.0.

1. Create Backend/Service/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs declaring namespace API.Service. Make it a class implementing Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.IExceptionHandler with a constructor injecting ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> and IProblemDetailsService. In TryHandleAsync generate a correlation id (e.g. Activity.Current?.Id ?? httpContext.TraceIdentifier), log the exception at Error level with that id, set httpContext.Response.StatusCode = StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, and write a sanitized ProblemDetails (Title "An unexpected error occurred.", Status 500, an Extensions["correlationId"] entry) via IProblemDetailsService.TryWriteAsync. Never put ex.Message or ex.StackTrace in the response body. Return true.
2. In Backend/Program.cs, in the service-registration section (right after builder.Services.AddControllers(); on line 22, or anywhere before line 97 var app = builder.Build();), add builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(); and builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<API.Service.GlobalExceptionHandler>(); (add a using API.Service; or use the fully-qualified name).
3. In Backend/Program.cs replace the if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); } else { app.UseHsts(); } block (lines 100-107) so the non-Development branch registers app.UseExceptionHandler(); before app.UseHsts();. UseDeveloperExceptionPage() stays only inside the IsDevelopment() branch. This block already sits before app.UseRouting() (line 160), so the handler runs early enough to catch downstream exceptions.
4. Leave the AccountSummaryReportController.cs:70 catch (SqlException ex) when (IsMissingPaginationProcedure(ex)) block intact — it is a deliberate functional fallback to the non-paginated LINQ query when the *_pagination proc is missing, not error-swallowing. The new global handler only catches exceptions that escape the controller, so the two do not conflict. (Optional, separate cleanup: if you later introduce a typed MissingPaginationProcedureException, map it in the handler; do not do this as part of this finding.)
5. Confirm Swagger exposure is unaffected: app.UseSwagger()/UseSwaggerUI (lines 109-115) run after the exception handler registration, so they keep working; the audit's separate concern about Swagger-in-prod is out of scope here.

```csharp
// NEW FILE: Backend/Service/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs
using System.Diagnostics;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;
using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;

namespace API.Service
{
    public sealed class GlobalExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
    {
        private readonly ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> _logger;
        private readonly IProblemDetailsService _problemDetailsService;

        public GlobalExceptionHandler(
            ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> logger,
            IProblemDetailsService problemDetailsService)
        {
            _logger = logger;
            _problemDetailsService = problemDetailsService;
        }

        public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
            HttpContext httpContext,
            Exception exception,
            CancellationToken cancellationToken)
        {
            var correlationId = Activity.Current?.Id ?? httpContext.TraceIdentifier;

            _logger.LogError(
                exception,
```
