```csharp
                "Unhandled exception. CorrelationId={CorrelationId} Path={Path}",
                correlationId, httpContext.Request.Path);

            httpContext.Response.StatusCode = StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError;

            return await _problemDetailsService.TryWriteAsync(new ProblemDetailsContext
            {
                HttpContext = httpContext,
                ProblemDetails =
                {
                    Status = StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError,
                    Title = "An unexpected error occurred.",
                    // NOTE: never expose exception.Message / StackTrace to callers.
                    Extensions = { ["correlationId"] = correlationId }
                }
            });
        }
    }
}

// ---- Program.cs: service registration (add before line 97 `var app = builder.Build();`) ----
builder.Services.AddControllers();
+ builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();
+ builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<API.Service.GlobalExceptionHandler>();

// ---- Program.cs lines 100-107: pipeline ----
  if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
  {
      app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
  }
  else
  {
+     app.UseExceptionHandler();
      app.UseHsts();
  }
```

**Verify:**
- Build: dotnet build on Backend/Backend.csproj succeeds (no missing-package errors — IExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails/UseExceptionHandler are in net8.0).
- Force an exception in a non-Development run (e.g. set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production, point ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest at an unreachable host so sp_AccountSummaryReport throws a non-2812 SqlException, then POST /api/AccountSummaryReport). Expect HTTP 500 with Content-Type application/problem+json, a body containing title "An unexpected error occurred." and a correlationId, and NO stack trace or SQL text.
- Confirm the same request emits one Error-level log line with the matching CorrelationId and request Path.
- Confirm the normal happy path and the existing missing-_pagination SqlException fallback in AccountSummaryReportController still return 200 (the global handler must not intercept them).
- grep -rn "UseExceptionHandler\|AddExceptionHandler\|AddProblemDetails" Backend --include=*.cs now returns the three new registrations.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Register app.UseExceptionHandler() before app.UseRouting()/UseAuthentication()/UseAuthorization() (it currently sits at lines 100-107, well before line 160, which is correct) — placing it after routing would miss exceptions thrown in routing/auth. AddProblemDetails() is required for IProblemDetailsService to be resolvable; without it TryWriteAsync returns false and you fall back to an empty 500. Keep UseDeveloperExceptionPage() inside the IsDevelopment() branch only; do not move it to the shared path. Do not remove or weaken the IsMissingPaginationProcedure SqlException catch in AccountSummaryReportController — it is a functional fallback, and removing it would break reports whose *_pagination proc is not yet deployed (recall: SP .sql files are not auto-applied). Rollback is a clean revert of Program.cs plus deleting GlobalExceptionHandler.cs; no data migration involved.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Corrections: (1) Program.cs range is 100-107, not 99-107. (2) '5 of 158 report controllers' should read '5 controllers total in the backend, but only 1 of 158 report controllers (AccountSummaryReportController)'; the other 4 are Auth/Base/ExcelExport/Upload controllers, and report controllers do not inherit BaseAPIController so its DbUpdateConcurrencyException catch does not apply to them. (3) The 'leak raw stack traces' impact is conditional on the Development-misconfiguration path; under a correctly configured Production environment, the default behavior is an opaque empty 500, not a stack-trace leak — this is why I downgraded to Medium. The observability gap (no central logging / no correlation id / inconsistent error contract for the React client) is unconditionally true and is the real substance of the finding.


<a id="f-no-ratelimit-no-healthcheck-no-versioning"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — No rate limiting, health checks, or API versioning across 158 report endpoints

**ID:** `no-ratelimit-no-healthcheck-no-versioning` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Operability / Hardening &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:22-95`
- `Backend/Program.cs:158-170`

**Problem.** Program.cs registers controllers, memory cache, Swagger, two DbContexts, JWT and the Excel queue, but a full grep finds no AddRateLimiter/UseRateLimiter, no AddHealthChecks/MapHealthChecks, and no AddApiVersioning. The 158 report endpoints each run heavy paginated SQL against a remote DB (per-call SetCommandTimeout(120) in only 3 places, e.g. sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:45) and some build full unbounded result sets in-memory before paging (ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:101-125 materializes the whole join with .ToList() then groups in app memory).

**Impact.** Without rate limiting, a single authenticated user (or a leaked token) can issue many concurrent expensive report queries and exhaust the DB connection pool / remote SQL Server, a denial-of-service against a government reporting system. Without health checks, load balancers and ops have no liveness/readiness signal (e.g. DB unreachable) and route traffic to a dead instance. Without versioning, any breaking change to a report's response shape immediately breaks the React client.

**Recommendation.** Add ASP.NET Core rate limiting (a per-user fixed/sliding window on the report endpoints) and MapHealthChecks("/health") with AddDbContextCheck for both contexts. Introduce Asp.Versioning for the /api surface. Cap in-memory materialization in controllers like ImportLicenceBySectionReport by pushing the aggregation into SQL.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1 day)_**

Harden Backend/Program.cs with three built-in/standard operability features: a per-user partitioned rate limiter applied to the report endpoints, MapHealthChecks("/health") with AddDbContextCheck for both DbContexts, and Asp.Versioning on the /api surface; separately, push the ImportLicenceBySectionReport aggregation into SQL instead of materializing the whole join with .ToList() in app memory.

1. Add NuGet packages to Backend/API.csproj: `Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore` (v8.0.x to match the net8 runtime) and `Asp.Versioning.Mvc` (v8.x). Run from the Backend dir: `dotnet add API.csproj package Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore --version 8.0.17` and `dotnet add API.csproj package Asp.Versioning.Mvc --version 8.1.0`. Rate limiting (System.Threading.RateLimiting / Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting) is already in the net8 shared framework — no package needed.
2. In Backend/Program.cs add the usings: `using System.Threading.RateLimiting;`, `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting;`, `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;`, `using System.Security.Claims;`, `using Asp.Versioning;`.
3. Register services in Program.cs after `builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);` (line 95) and before `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97): add `AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>("TemplateDB").AddDbContextCheck<TradeNetDbContext>("TradeNetDB")` — both context types are already imported via `using API.DBContext;` and registered via AddDbContextPool at lines 64-67.
4. In the same block add `builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(...)` with a named policy `"per-user"` that partitions on the `ClaimTypes.Name` claim (the identifier already used in 160 places, e.g. ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:177 `User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value`), falling back to remote IP for unauthenticated calls; use a FixedWindowRateLimiter (PermitLimit 30 / 1 min) and set `RejectionStatusCode = 429`.
5. In the same block add `builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(...)` with `DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1,0)` and `AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true` so the existing 166 controllers (all `[Route("api/[controller]")]`) keep responding on the current URLs without immediate route changes.
6. In the request pipeline, insert `app.UseRateLimiter();` AFTER `app.UseAuthorization();` (currently line 167) and BEFORE `app.MapControllers();` (line 168) so the authenticated user claim is available to the partition function.
7. After `app.MapControllers();` add `app.MapHealthChecks("/health").AllowAnonymous();` so the endpoint is reachable by load-balancer liveness/readiness probes without a JWT. Place it before `app.UseStaticFiles();` (line 169).
8. Apply the rate-limit policy to the heavy report endpoints. Either decorate the report controllers' POST actions with `[Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting.EnableRateLimiting("per-user")]`, or — to cover all 166 controllers at once — add a global limiter as the default in AddRateLimiter via `options.GlobalLimiter = PartitionedRateLimiter.Create<HttpContext,string>(...)` using the same per-user key. For a focused first pass, add `[EnableRateLimiting("per-user")]` to ImportLicenceBySectionReportController and the other StoredProcedure-backed report controllers under Controllers/Report.
9. Fix the in-memory materialization called out in the finding: in Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs, the `BuildRowsAsync` method (lines 99-163) ends the LINQ query with `.ToList()` at line 125 then groups/counts in app memory (lines 132-151). Replace this path with the SQL aggregation that the Excel path already uses — `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync(_context, procedureRequest)` (already called in WriteRowsAsync at line 197) — so the section/currency grouping happens in SQL Server, not after pulling every joined row into memory. Map its rows to `ReportAggregateResult` and keep the existing IMemoryCache wrapper (lines 64-68) and ColumnTotals footer (lines 90-94).
10. Optionally centralize the per-call `db.Database.SetCommandTimeout(120)` (currently in only 3 places, e.g. sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:45) by setting `sqlOptions.CommandTimeout(120)` inside `UseSqlServer(...)` for both AddDbContextPool registrations (Program.cs lines 64-67) so every report query has a bounded timeout.

```csharp
// ===== Backend/Program.cs — service registration (after AddExcelExportQueue, before builder.Build()) =====

// 1) Health checks for BOTH DbContexts (needs NuGet:
//    Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore)
builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()
    .AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>("TemplateDB")
    .AddDbContextCheck<TradeNetDbContext>("TradeNetDB");

// 2) Per-user rate limiter (System.Threading.RateLimiting is built into net8)
builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    options.RejectionStatusCode = StatusCodes.Status429TooManyRequests;
    // Named policy applied to report controllers via [EnableRateLimiting("per-user")]
    options.AddPolicy("per-user", httpContext =>
    {
        var key = httpContext.User?.Identity?.IsAuthenticated == true
            ? httpContext.User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value ?? "authenticated"
            : httpContext.Connection.RemoteIpAddress?.ToString() ?? "anonymous";
        return RateLimitPartition.GetFixedWindowLimiter(key, _ => new FixedWindowRateLimiterOptions
        {
            PermitLimit = 30,                          // 30 report requests
            Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1),          // per user per minute
            QueueProcessingOrder = QueueProcessingOrder.OldestFirst,
            QueueLimit = 5
        });
    });
});

// 3) API versioning (needs NuGet: Asp.Versioning.Mvc)
builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;   // existing /api/* clients keep working
    options.ReportApiVersions = true;
    options.ApiVersionReader = ApiVersionReader.Combine(
        new UrlSegmentApiVersionReader(),
        new HeaderApiVersionReader("X-Api-Version"));
});

// ===== pipeline — order matters: UseRateLimiter AFTER UseAuthentication =====
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();
app.UseRateLimiter();            // <-- ADD after UseAuthorization so the user claim is populated
app.MapControllers();
app.MapHealthChecks("/health").AllowAnonymous();   // <-- ADD; unauthenticated so LB probes can reach it
app.UseStaticFiles();

// ===== required usings at top of Program.cs =====
using System.Threading.RateLimiting;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;
using System.Security.Claims;
using Asp.Versioning;
```

**Verify:**
- `dotnet build Backend/API.csproj` compiles with the new packages and usings.
- Run the app, then `curl -i http://localhost:<port>/health` returns 200 with body `Healthy` when both DBs are reachable; stop/block the TradeNetDB connection and confirm it returns 503 `Unhealthy` (proves AddDbContextCheck covers both contexts) — and that it works WITHOUT a JWT (AllowAnonymous).
- With a valid bearer token, fire >30 POSTs in one minute at a report endpoint that has the policy (e.g. `POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport`) and confirm the 31st returns HTTP 429; a second distinct user's token is unaffected (proves per-user partitioning on ClaimTypes.Name).
- Hit a versioned route / send `X-Api-Version: 1.0`; confirm the response carries the `api-supported-versions` header (ReportApiVersions=true) and that the existing `/api/...` URLs still resolve (AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified).
- For ImportLicenceBySectionReport, run the report with a broad date range and confirm the response rows and ColumnTotals (noOfLicences, totalValue) match the previous in-memory result, while a SQL profiler / EF logging shows aggregation executed server-side (no full-join row stream into the app).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Ordering: UseRateLimiter MUST come after UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization, otherwise User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name) is null and every request falls into one shared anonymous partition. MapHealthChecks MUST be AllowAnonymous or the LB probe will get 401 from the JWT pipeline. Package versions: pin the HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore package to 8.0.x (not 9.x) to match the net8 TargetFramework, even though EF Core itself is 9.0.6 — the 9.x health-check package targets net9 and will warn/fail to restore. AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified=true is essential on first rollout so the React client's existing /api/* axios calls don't break before any URL changes ship. PermitLimit=30/min is a starting value — tune per real report cadence; too low will throttle legitimate dashboard users who open several reports. For the ImportLicenceBySection SQL refactor, verify sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync returns the same section/currency cell shape (including the `cell.NoOfLicences>0 || cell.TotalValue>0` filtering at line 145) before swapping; rollback is trivial since the old LINQ path can be kept behind a flag. Rate limiting and SQL refactor are independent — ship rate-limit/health/versioning first (low risk) and the aggregation rewrite as a separate change."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Two mitigations the finding does not mention but that do not invalidate it: (1) ImportLicenceBySectionReportController wraps BuildRowsAsync in a 5-minute IMemoryCache (lines 32, 64-68) keyed on all filter params — this blunts repeated-identical-query abuse but not distinct-parameter flooding, so the DoS argument still stands. (2) DbContexts use AddDbContextPool, which bounds context object allocation but not SQL Server CPU/IO. Severity Medium is appropriate and I am leaving it: these are defense-in-depth / operability gaps behind [Authorize] (not an unauthenticated RCE/data-exposure issue), so not High; but they are real gaps on a production government reporting API (no readiness probe, no throttle, no versioning contract for the React client), so not Info/Low. Recommendation (add rate limiting, MapHealthChecks + AddDbContextCheck for both contexts, Asp.Versioning, push the ImportLicenceBySection aggregation into SQL) is sound.


<a id="f-cancellationtoken-not-propagated-sync-endpoints"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Synchronous paged report endpoints do not accept or propagate CancellationToken

**ID:** `cancellationtoken-not-propagated-sync-endpoints` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Reliability / Resource management &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs:42-77`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:42-97`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:47-51`

**Problem.** The CancellationToken is plumbed only through the Excel streaming path (WriteRowsAsync, e.g. AccountSummaryReportController.cs:101-116). The primary JSON Post(...) actions take no CancellationToken parameter and call sp_*.ExecuteAsync / ToListAsync with no token (sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:51 calls .ToListAsync() with no argument; ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:126 awaits ToListAsync with no token). When a client aborts a slow report request, the underlying SQL query keeps running to completion.

**Impact.** Abandoned report requests continue consuming a DB connection and remote SQL CPU until they finish or hit the 120s timeout, amplifying the DoS exposure above and wasting connection-pool slots. Under load the pool can be saturated by queries no one is waiting for.

**Recommendation.** Add CancellationToken cancellationToken to each Post action (model binding supplies HttpContext.RequestAborted), thread it through the sp_* helpers, and pass it to every ...Async EF call.

**Example:**

```
// current
public async Task<...> Post([FromBody] AccountSummaryReportRequest? request) {
    var rows = await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, ...);
}
// fixed
public async Task<...> Post([FromBody] AccountSummaryReportRequest? request, CancellationToken ct) {
    var rows = await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, ..., ct);
}
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2 hours)_**

Thread HttpContext.RequestAborted into every synchronous JSON report Post action by adding a `CancellationToken cancellationToken` parameter (model binding supplies it), then plumb that token through the sp_* helpers and the shared ApiResult paging methods down to each terminal EF call (ToListAsync / CountAsync / SqlQueryRaw), so an aborted client request cancels the running SQL instead of letting it run to the 120-180s timeout. Do NOT touch the existing WriteRowsAsync Excel-streaming path — it already receives and uses a token.

1. Add the optional token to the shared paging helpers in Backend/Model/APIResult.cs first (everything below depends on them). Change the two terminal helpers: `private static async Task<int> CountAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source, CancellationToken ct)` -> call `await source.CountAsync(ct)`; `private static async Task<List<T>> ToListAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source, CancellationToken ct)` -> call `await source.ToListAsync(ct)`. Add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` as the last parameter to `CreateAsync(...)` and `CreateFastPageAsync(...)`, pass it through to the CountAsyncSafe/ToListAsyncSafe calls and from CreateFastPageAsync into its `await CreateAsync(...)` call. Add `using System.Threading;` at the top.
2. In Backend/Service/Reports/ReportQueryService.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` to `CreatePagedResultAsync<T>(IQueryable<T> query, ReportQueryRequest request, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)` and forward it: `return ApiResult<T>.CreateFastPageAsync(query, pageIndex, pageSize, null, null, request.FilterColumn, request.FilterQuery, request.IncludeTotalCount, cancellationToken);` Add `using System.Threading;`.
3. In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_AccountSummaryReport.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` (add `using System.Threading;`) as the last parameter of `ExecuteAsync(...)` and change line 91-92 to `return await ExecuteQueryable(db, request, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, includeTotalCount).ToListAsync(cancellationToken);` Leave ExecuteQueryable (returns IQueryable, no terminal call) and Query untouched.
4. In Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken` as the second parameter of `Post([FromBody] AccountSummaryReportRequest? request, CancellationToken cancellationToken)`. Pass it to the sp call: `await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, includeTotalCount: true, cancellationToken)`, and to the SqlException fallback: `await ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync(query, request, cancellationToken)`. (`using System.Threading;` is already present.)
5. In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs add `CancellationToken cancellationToken` (add `using System.Threading;`) to `ExecuteAggregateStoredProcedureAsync(...)` and change line 51 to `.ToListAsync(cancellationToken);`
6. In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_HSCodeReport.cs (add `using System.Threading;`) thread the token through: add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` to `CreateAggregateResultAsync(...)`, forward it to `await ExecuteAggregateStoredProcedureAsync(db, request, pageIndex, pageSize, cancellationToken)` and to `await ApiResult<ReportAggregateResult>.CreateFastPageAsync(query, ..., pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount, cancellationToken)`. Also add the optional token to `GetAggregateRowsAsync(...)` -> `await AggregateQuery(db, request).ToListAsync(cancellationToken)` so the streaming WriteRowsAsync can pass its existing token (optional but consistent).
7. Update every HSCode aggregate controller's Post action to accept and forward the token. There are 8: ImportLicenceByHSCodeReportController.cs, ExportLicenceByHSCodeReportController.cs, ImportPermitByHSCodeReportController.cs, ExportPermitByHSCodeReportController.cs, BorderImportLicenceByHSCodeReportController.cs, BorderExportLicenceByHSCodeReportController.cs, BorderImportPermitByHSCodeReportController.cs, BorderExportPermitByHSCodeReportController.cs. In each, change `Post([FromBody] XxxRequest? request)` to `Post([FromBody] XxxRequest? request, CancellationToken cancellationToken)` and `await sp_HSCodeReport.CreateAggregateResultAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, request!)` to `await sp_HSCodeReport.CreateAggregateResultAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, request!, cancellationToken)`. (`using System.Threading;` is already imported in these files.)
8. Fix the ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs path (cited as the worst offender). Add `CancellationToken cancellationToken` to `Post([FromBody] ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest? request, CancellationToken cancellationToken)`. Add the token to `BuildRowsAsync(ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest request, CancellationToken cancellationToken)` and forward it from the GetOrCreateAsync delegate: `return await BuildRowsAsync(request, entry.Token == default ? cancellationToken : cancellationToken);` (simplest: just pass `cancellationToken`). Inside BuildRowsAsync change the fully-synchronous `.ToList()` at line 125 to `.ToListAsync(cancellationToken)` (make the LINQ assignment `await`), and add the token to the two existing `.ToListAsync()` calls at lines 126-127: `await _context.Currencies.AsNoTracking().Where(x => x.Code != null).ToListAsync(cancellationToken)` and `await _context.ExportImportSections.Where(x => x.Type == "Import Licence").AsNoTracking().ToListAsync(cancellationToken)`.
9. Build to catch any missed call sites: `dotnet build` from the Backend project directory. Fix any optional-parameter ambiguity or missing-token compile errors it surfaces.

```csharp
// --- Backend/Model/APIResult.cs (shared helpers) ---
// before
private static async Task<int> CountAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source) {
    if (source.Provider is ...IAsyncQueryProvider) return await source.CountAsync();
    return source.Count();
}
private static async Task<List<T>> ToListAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source) {
    if (source.Provider is ...IAsyncQueryProvider) return await source.ToListAsync();
    return source.ToList();
}
// after
private static async Task<int> CountAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source, CancellationToken ct) {
    if (source.Provider is ...IAsyncQueryProvider) return await source.CountAsync(ct);
    return source.Count();
}
private static async Task<List<T>> ToListAsyncSafe(IQueryable<T> source, CancellationToken ct) {
    if (source.Provider is ...IAsyncQueryProvider) return await source.ToListAsync(ct);
    return source.ToList();
}
// CreateAsync(...) and CreateFastPageAsync(...) gain a trailing `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default`
// and forward it to CountAsyncSafe/ToListAsyncSafe (and CreateFastPageAsync -> CreateAsync).

// --- AccountSummaryReportController.cs ---
// before
public async Task<ActionResult<ApiResult<sp_AccountSummaryReportResult>>> Post([FromBody] AccountSummaryReportRequest? request) {
    var rows = await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(
        _context, procedureRequest!, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, includeTotalCount: true);
    ...
    var result = await ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync(query, request);
}
// after
public async Task<ActionResult<ApiResult<sp_AccountSummaryReportResult>>> Post([FromBody] AccountSummaryReportRequest? request, CancellationToken cancellationToken) {
    var rows = await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(
        _context, procedureRequest!, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, includeTotalCount: true, cancellationToken);
    ...
    var result = await ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync(query, request, cancellationToken);
}

// --- sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs ---
// before
private static Task<List<sp_HSCodeAggregateReportResult>> ExecuteAggregateStoredProcedureAsync(
    TradeNetDbContext db, sp_HSCodeReportRequest request, int pageIndex, int pageSize) {
    ...
    return db.Database.SqlQueryRaw<sp_HSCodeAggregateReportResult>(sql, parameters).ToListAsync();
}
// after
private static Task<List<sp_HSCodeAggregateReportResult>> ExecuteAggregateStoredProcedureAsync(
    TradeNetDbContext db, sp_HSCodeReportRequest request, int pageIndex, int pageSize, CancellationToken cancellationToken) {
    ...
    return db.Database.SqlQueryRaw<sp_HSCodeAggregateReportResult>(sql, parameters).ToListAsync(cancellationToken);
}

// --- ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs (worst offender: sync .ToList() at line 125) ---
// before
public async Task<...> Post([FromBody] ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest? request) { ...
    return await BuildRowsAsync(request); ... }
private async Task<List<ReportAggregateResult>> BuildRowsAsync(ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest request) {
    var LicenceListQry = (from licence in _context.ImportLicences.AsNoTracking() ... ).ToList();
    var CurrencyList = await _context.Currencies.AsNoTracking().Where(x => x.Code != null).ToListAsync();
    var SectionList  = await _context.ExportImportSections.Where(x => x.Type == "Import Licence").AsNoTracking().ToListAsync();
}
// after
public async Task<...> Post([FromBody] ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest? request, CancellationToken cancellationToken) { ...
    return await BuildRowsAsync(request, cancellationToken); ... }
private async Task<List<ReportAggregateResult>> BuildRowsAsync(ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest request, CancellationToken cancellationToken) {
    var LicenceListQry = await (from licence in _context.ImportLicences.AsNoTracking() ... ).ToListAsync(cancellationToken);
    var CurrencyList = await _context.Currencies.AsNoTracking().Where(x => x.Code != null).ToListAsync(cancellationToken);
    var SectionList  = await _context.ExportImportSections.Where(x => x.Type == "Import Licence").AsNoTracking().ToListAsync(cancellationToken);
}
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build the Backend project — no missed call sites and no optional-parameter overload errors.
- Manual abort test: POST a slow report (e.g. /api/AccountSummaryReport or /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport with a wide FromDate/ToDate range) and abort the client mid-flight (curl --max-time 2, or cancel the browser request). Watch SQL Server sys.dm_exec_requests (or Activity Monitor) — the report query should disappear within ~1s of the abort instead of running to the 120/180s command timeout.
- Confirm the EF/SqlClient command is actually cancelled: the cancelled request should surface as an OperationCanceledException in logs (ASP.NET Core treats RequestAborted-driven cancellation as a 499/aborted response, not a 500).
- Regression: a normal (non-aborted) report request still returns the same paged ApiResult JSON with correct Data/TotalCount; the Excel queue path (POST .../Excel then background WriteRowsAsync) still produces the workbook unchanged.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do the APIResult.cs and ReportQueryService.cs signature changes FIRST; the controllers and sp_* helpers call into them, so leading edits will not compile until the shared helpers accept the token.; Add `CancellationToken cancellationToken = default` (optional) on shared/library methods (ApiResult, ReportQueryService, sp_HSCodeReport.CreateAggregateResultAsync/GetAggregateRowsAsync, sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync) so the many other callers that do not yet pass a token keep compiling. Make it non-optional ONLY on controller Post actions (model binding always supplies HttpContext.RequestAborted).; ExecuteQueryable in sp_AccountSummaryReport returns IQueryable and must stay token-free — only its ToListAsync terminal (in ExecuteAsync) takes the token. Same for sp_HSCodeReport.Query/AggregateQuery.; Do NOT modify the IStreamingExcelReport.WriteRowsAsync signature or the Excel ChunkAsync path; it already receives and propagates a token from the background worker. This finding is only about the synchronous JSON Post actions.; In ImportLicenceBySectionReportController the rows are wrapped in IMemoryCache.GetOrCreateAsync; passing the token into BuildRowsAsync means an aborted first request can cancel the cache population so a concurrent waiter may get no cached value — acceptable, but do not store a partially-built/cancelled list in the cache (GetOrCreateAsync already discards on exception, so an OperationCanceledException leaves the entry unpopulated, which is correct).; Rollback is a clean revert: every change is additive (new optional params + token forwarding) with no schema, migration, or stored-procedure change.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Medium is fair and I leave it unchanged. Real reliability/resource gap that is systemic across every synchronous report endpoint, which justifies Medium over Low. Mitigating factors that keep it from being High: this is an authenticated, internal Ministry admin reporting tool (not public-facing), command timeouts are bounded (e.g. 120s in sp_HSCodeReport and CommandTimeoutSeconds in sp_AccountSummaryReport), and the ImportLicenceBySection path is additionally fronted by a 5-minute IMemoryCache. The finding's phrase "amplifying the DoS exposure above" references a separate finding not in scope here; on its own this is a reliability/connection-pool concern rather than a standalone DoS. Note: the ImportLicenceBySection Post path also does a fully synchronous .ToList() materialization at line 125 (not just ToListAsync at 126), which is even less cancellable, reinforcing the finding.


<a id="f-no-structured-logging-correlation"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Structured logging and correlation IDs exist only in the Excel workers; the entire report/API surface logs nothing

**ID:** `no-structured-logging-correlation` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Observability &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:17-95`
- `Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportWorker.cs:24-35`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs:35-39`

**Problem.** Only 2 files in the backend inject ILogger — ExcelExportWorker.cs and ExcelExportCleanupWorker.cs. None of the 158 report controllers, the JWT service, the lookup controller, or any sp_* helper logs anything. There is no request-logging middleware, no correlation/trace-id enrichment, and the default Logging config is just console-level filters (appsettings.json:2-7). There is no Serilog/OpenTelemetry registration in Program.cs.

**Impact.** When a report fails, returns wrong totals (a recurring complaint per the project memory), or runs slowly, operators have no per-request log, no correlation id to tie a user complaint to a server event, and no audit trail of who ran which report against sensitive trade data — a significant gap for a government system.

**Recommendation.** Register structured logging (Serilog or built-in with a JSON console formatter), add UseW3CLogging or a request-logging middleware that emits a correlation id (echo X-Correlation-Id), and add scoped log statements around report execution including the report key, user (ClaimTypes.Name is already available, AccountSummaryReportController.cs:91), and duration. Consider OpenTelemetry for traces/metrics.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. Half a day for Program.cs + middleware + appsettings + reference controller; ~1-2 additional days to roll ILogger across the remaining controllers.)_**

Turn on ASP.NET Core's built-in structured logging (JSON console formatter), add the framework HttpLogging middleware plus a tiny correlation-id middleware that reads/echoes X-Correlation-Id and pushes it into the log scope, then inject ILogger into report controllers (starting with AccountSummaryReportController) to log report key, user, and duration around execution. No third-party package is required since Microsoft.Extensions.Logging is already referenced.

1. In Backend/Program.cs, immediately after `var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);` (line 21), configure structured JSON logging: `builder.Logging.ClearProviders(); builder.Logging.AddJsonConsole(o => { o.IncludeScopes = true; o.UseUtcTimestamp = true; });` and in Development also add `builder.Logging.AddDebug();`. IncludeScopes=true is what makes the correlation id (added via a log scope below) appear on every log line.
2. In Backend/Program.cs service-registration block (around line 95, after `builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(...)`), register HTTP request logging: `builder.Services.AddHttpLogging(o => { o.LoggingFields = HttpLoggingFields.RequestPath | HttpLoggingFields.RequestMethod | HttpLoggingFields.ResponseStatusCode | HttpLoggingFields.Duration; });` Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.HttpLogging;` to the using block at the top (lines 1-15).
3. Create a correlation-id middleware file Backend/Middleware/CorrelationIdMiddleware.cs (new folder). It reads the incoming `X-Correlation-Id` header (or generates a Guid if absent), stores it in HttpContext.Items, echoes it on the response via context.Response.OnStarting, and wraps the rest of the pipeline in `logger.BeginScope` so the id is attached to every log entry for that request. See the patch for the full class plus the `UseCorrelationId` extension method.
4. In Backend/Program.cs request-pipeline section, add the middleware EARLY so the correlation scope wraps everything — place `app.UseCorrelationId();` then `app.UseHttpLogging();` right after `app.UseRouting();` (line 160) and before `app.UseAuthentication();` (line 166). Order matters: correlation scope must be outermost so HttpLogging and controller logs inherit it. Add `using API.Middleware;` to the usings.
5. In Backend/appsettings.json, expand the Logging section so framework noise stays low while the app namespace logs at Information. Replace lines 2-7 with the LogLevel block in the patch (add `"Microsoft.AspNetCore.HttpLogging.HttpLoggingMiddleware": "Information"`, `"API": "Information"`, `"Backend": "Information"`). Keep `"Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning"` for the rest. Do NOT add anything that logs request/response bodies (sensitive trade data).
6. Add an ILogger to the representative controller as the rollout template: in Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs add field `private readonly ILogger<AccountSummaryReportController> _logger;`, add the parameter to the constructor at lines 35-39 (`ILogger<AccountSummaryReportController> logger`) and assign `_logger = logger;`. Add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;` to the usings (lines 1-15).
7. In AccountSummaryReportController.Post (lines 42-77), wrap report execution with timing and logging. Before the `try` capture `var sw = System.Diagnostics.Stopwatch.StartNew();` and `var user = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;` (ClaimTypes is already imported, line 4). On success log `_logger.LogInformation("Report {ReportKey} executed by {User} returned {RowCount} rows in {ElapsedMs}ms", ReportKey, user, data.Count, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);` and in the SqlException-fallback catch (line 70) log `_logger.LogWarning(ex, "Report {ReportKey} pagination proc missing, falling back to LINQ query for {User}", ReportKey, user);`. See patch.
8. Repeat steps 6-7 across the other 157 report controllers in Backend/Controllers/Report and the JWT/lookup controllers as a follow-up rollout. They share the same ctor+ReportKey shape, so the change is mechanical. Prioritize the controllers tied to the recurring wrong-total complaints first.

```csharp
// ---- Backend/Middleware/CorrelationIdMiddleware.cs (NEW FILE) ----
using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Threading.Tasks;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Builder;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;
using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;

namespace API.Middleware
{
    public sealed class CorrelationIdMiddleware
    {
        private const string HeaderName = "X-Correlation-Id";
        public const string ItemKey = "CorrelationId";

        private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
        private readonly ILogger<CorrelationIdMiddleware> _logger;

        public CorrelationIdMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, ILogger<CorrelationIdMiddleware> logger)
        {
            _next = next;
            _logger = logger;
        }

        public async Task Invoke(HttpContext context)
        {
            var correlationId = context.Request.Headers.TryGetValue(HeaderName, out var incoming)
                                && !string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(incoming)
                ? incoming.ToString()
                : Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N");

            context.Items[ItemKey] = correlationId;
            context.Response.OnStarting(() =>
            {
                context.Response.Headers[HeaderName] = correlationId;
                return Task.CompletedTask;
            });

            using (_logger.BeginScope(new Dictionary<string, object>
            {
                ["CorrelationId"] = correlationId,
                ["RequestPath"] = context.Request.Path.Value ?? string.Empty
            }))
            {
                await _next(context);
            }
        }
    }

    public static class CorrelationIdMiddlewareExtensions
    {
        public static IApplicationBuilder UseCorrelationId(this IApplicationBuilder app)
            => app.UseMiddleware<CorrelationIdMiddleware>();
    }
}

// ---- Backend/Program.cs ----
// after: var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);
builder.Logging.ClearProviders();
builder.Logging.AddJsonConsole(o => { o.IncludeScopes = true; o.UseUtcTimestamp = true; });
if (builder.Environment.IsDevelopment()) builder.Logging.AddDebug();

// in service registration (after AddExcelExportQueue):
builder.Services.AddHttpLogging(o =>
{
    o.LoggingFields = HttpLoggingFields.RequestPath
                    | HttpLoggingFields.RequestMethod
                    | HttpLoggingFields.ResponseStatusCode
                    | HttpLoggingFields.Duration; // no bodies — sensitive trade data
});

// in request pipeline, right after app.UseRouting();
app.UseCorrelationId();
app.UseHttpLogging();
// ... then app.UseAuthentication(); app.UseAuthorization(); as today

// ---- AccountSummaryReportController.cs ----
// BEFORE:
public AccountSummaryReportController(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService excelExportJobs)
{
    _context = context;
    _excelExportJobs = excelExportJobs;
}
// AFTER:
private readonly ILogger<AccountSummaryReportController> _logger;
public AccountSummaryReportController(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService excelExportJobs,
    ILogger<AccountSummaryReportController> logger)
{
    _context = context;
    _excelExportJobs = excelExportJobs;
    _logger = logger;
}

// inside Post(...):
var sw = System.Diagnostics.Stopwatch.StartNew();
var user = User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value;
try
{
    var rows = await sp_AccountSummaryReport.ExecuteAsync(...);
    var data = rows.Select(row => row.ToResult()).ToList();
    _logger.LogInformation("Report {ReportKey} executed by {User} returned {RowCount} rows in {ElapsedMs}ms",
        ReportKey, user, data.Count, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
    // ... existing Ok(result)
}
catch (SqlException ex) when (IsMissingPaginationProcedure(ex))
{
    _logger.LogWarning(ex, "Report {ReportKey} pagination proc missing, LINQ fallback for {User}", ReportKey, user);
    // ... existing fallback
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build "Backend/API.csproj"` succeeds.
- Run the API and call a report, e.g. `curl -i -X POST http://localhost:<port>/api/AccountSummaryReport -H 'Authorization: Bearer <jwt>' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}'` — confirm the response includes an `X-Correlation-Id` header.
- Send your own id: `curl -i ... -H 'X-Correlation-Id: test-123'` and confirm the same `test-123` is echoed back in the response header.
- Check the console: each request emits JSON log lines (HttpLogging request/response + the controller's 'Report AccountSummaryReport executed by ... returned N rows in Mms' line), and every line carries the same CorrelationId in its Scopes.
- Negative check: confirm no request/response BODY appears in logs (only path, method, status, duration) so sensitive trade data is not logged.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Middleware ordering: UseCorrelationId must come before UseHttpLogging and before UseAuthentication so the correlation scope wraps every downstream log; placing it after MapControllers would miss controller logs. IncludeScopes=true on the JSON console is required or the correlation id won't render. Do NOT enable HttpLoggingFields.RequestBody/ResponseBody — this system handles sensitive trade data and bodies would leak into logs. ClearProviders() removes the default console writer, so AddJsonConsole must stay or you lose all output. Adding the ILogger ctor param is a non-breaking DI change (controllers are container-resolved), but update any test that instantiates a controller manually. Rolling out to all 158 controllers is mechanical but large; do it incrementally with AccountSummaryReportController as the reference. Note: appsettings.json also ships plaintext sa DB credentials and a hardcoded JWT key (lines 10, 26-27) — out of scope for this finding, track separately.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Locations are accurate. Backend/Program.cs:17-95 correctly bounds the service-registration block where logging/middleware would be added (none present). ExcelExportWorker.cs:24-35 correctly shows the only ILogger injection pattern; the second logging file is Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportCleanupWorker.cs (the finding mentions it in prose but did not list it in locations — minor, not a defect). AccountSummaryReportController.cs:35-39 (ctor) is cited as a representative no-logger controller; note the user-identity line is at :91, which the finding also references correctly. Tangential observation found during review (out of scope for this finding): appsettings.json ships plaintext sa DB credentials and a hardcoded JWT key — separate security finding, not part of this observability finding.


<a id="f-second-dbcontext-not-under-migrations"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — TradeNetDbContext (228 entities, the real data) is a DB-first scaffold with no migrations or schema-version control

**ID:** `second-dbcontext-not-under-migrations` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Data access / Migration hygiene &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/DBContext/TradeNetDbContext.cs:8-13`
- `Backend/DBContext/TradeNetDbContext.cs:1574-1575`
- `Backend/Migrations/ApplicationDbContextModelSnapshot.cs:13-14`

**Problem.** Two DbContexts are registered as pooled (Program.cs:64-67). Only ApplicationDbContext has migrations — the lone model snapshot is [DbContext(typeof(ApplicationDbContext))] (ApplicationDbContextModelSnapshot.cs:13). TradeNetDbContext is a 1575-line reverse-engineered scaffold (228 DbSets, OnModelCreatingPartial hook at line 1574) with all mapping hand-maintained and no migration history. There is no startup Migrate()/EnsureCreated() call for either context, so deploying schema changes for the app DB is a manual step, and the scaffolded read-model can silently drift from the live TradeNetDB. This aligns with the project's documented 'deployed proc drift' problem where .sql files are not auto-applied.

**Impact.** Schema drift between the scaffolded TradeNetDbContext and the production database produces runtime EF mapping/translation errors that surface only when a specific report is run (consistent with the recurring report-parity complaints). For the app DB, the absence of an automated migration step at deploy time means a forgotten manual migration leaves the running code expecting columns that do not exist.

**Recommendation.** Document and automate the regeneration of TradeNetDbContext from the canonical schema and add a CI check that fails if the scaffold is stale. For ApplicationDbContext, run db.Database.MigrateAsync() at startup (guarded) or as an explicit deploy step, and add an integration test that validates the model against the real schema.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. half a day (4 hours))_**

Treat the two contexts per their nature: ApplicationDbContext is app-owned, so add a guarded MigrateAsync() at startup so deploys can't run code ahead of schema; TradeNetDbContext is a read-model over an externally-owned schema (TradeNetDBTest, keyless .ToView mappings), so don't author migrations for it — instead pin the exact re-scaffold command in a script and add a CI/local staleness check that fails when the committed scaffold no longer matches the canonical DB.

1. Add a guarded migration step for the app-owned context in Backend/Program.cs. After `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97) and before the pipeline config, resolve ApplicationDbContext from a scope and call MigrateAsync(). Guard it behind config so it can be disabled in environments where DBAs apply migrations manually: read `builder.Configuration.GetValue<bool>("Database:MigrateOnStartup")`.
2. In Backend/appsettings.json add a `"Database": { "MigrateOnStartup": true }` section (and set it to false in appsettings.Development.json or per-environment as the DBA team prefers). Only ApplicationDbContext gets MigrateAsync() — do NOT call Migrate()/EnsureCreated() on TradeNetDbContext, because that context has no migrations and maps a schema this app does not own.
3. Document and pin the TradeNetDbContext regeneration command. Create Backend/scaffold-tradenet.ps1 (or .sh) containing the exact `dotnet ef dbcontext scaffold` invocation: connection from the `TradeNetDBTest` connection string, provider Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer, `--context TradeNetDbContext --context-dir DBContext --output-dir Model/TradeNet --no-onconfiguring --use-database-names --force`. This is the canonical, repeatable way to refresh the 228-entity read-model (matches the existing API.Model.TradeNet namespace and DBContext/ folder).
4. Add a scaffold-staleness check the same script can run in --verify mode: re-scaffold into a temp directory, then `git diff --no-index` the generated TradeNetDbContext.cs and Model/TradeNet against the committed copies; exit non-zero on any diff. This is the CI gate the finding asks for — it fails the build when the committed scaffold has drifted from the live TradeNetDB schema.
5. Because the backend currently has no GitHub Actions workflow (only Frontend/.github/workflows/* exist) and deploys via deploy.ps1 to P:\WEBSITES, wire the verify step into deploy.ps1 right after `dotnet build` (around line 31): invoke the scaffold script in --verify mode against the canonical DB and abort the publish if it reports drift. If a backend CI pipeline is later added, run the same script there instead.
6. Add an integration test in Backend.Tests that asserts the scaffolded TradeNetDbContext model matches the real database. The project already has ReportSqlServerFixture.cs and ReportQueryTranslationTests.cs; add a test that opens a TradeNetDbContext against the canonical connection and calls `db.Database.GetService<IRelationalDatabaseCreator>().HasTables()` plus iterates each entity type and runs a `Take(0)` query (or `context.Model.GetEntityTypes()` mapped to a trivial query) so any column/view drift surfaces as a translation/mapping exception in the test rather than at report runtime. Mark it `[Trait("Category","RequiresDb")]` so it only runs where the canonical DB is reachable.

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs — after `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97)

var app = builder.Build();

// App-owned schema: apply EF migrations at startup so deployed code never
// runs ahead of the ApplicationDbContext schema. Guarded so DBA-managed
// environments can opt out. NOTE: do NOT migrate TradeNetDbContext — it is a
// re-scaffolded read-model over an externally-owned DB (TradeNetDBTest) with
// no migration history; refresh it via Backend/scaffold-tradenet.ps1 instead.
if (builder.Configuration.GetValue<bool>("Database:MigrateOnStartup"))
{
    using var scope = app.Services.CreateScope();
    var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<ApplicationDbContext>();
    db.Database.Migrate();
}

// ----------------------------------------------------------------------
// Backend/scaffold-tradenet.ps1  (run, or run with -Verify in deploy.ps1/CI)
param([switch]$Verify)
$conn = $env:TRADENET_SCAFFOLD_CONN  # canonical TradeNetDB connection string
$args = @(
  'ef','dbcontext','scaffold', $conn,
  'Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer',
  '--context','TradeNetDbContext',
  '--context-dir','DBContext',
  '--output-dir','Model/TradeNet',
  '--no-onconfiguring','--use-database-names','--force'
)
if ($Verify) {
  $tmp = Join-Path ([IO.Path]::GetTempPath()) ("tn_scaffold_" + [guid]::NewGuid())
  dotnet @($args + @('--output-dir',"$tmp/Model/TradeNet",'--context-dir',"$tmp/DBContext"))
  git --no-pager diff --no-index ".\DBContext\TradeNetDbContext.cs" "$tmp\DBContext\TradeNetDbContext.cs"
  if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Write-Error 'TradeNetDbContext scaffold is STALE — re-run scaffold-tradenet.ps1'; exit 1 }
} else {
  dotnet @args
}
```

**Verify:**
- Run `dotnet build` in Backend/ to confirm the Program.cs change compiles.
- Start the API with Database:MigrateOnStartup=true against a DB missing the latest migration; confirm __EFMigrationsHistory updates and ExcelExportJobs table exists.
- Run `pwsh Backend/scaffold-tradenet.ps1 -Verify`: exits 0 when in sync, non-zero after a hand edit to TradeNetDbContext.cs.
- Run `dotnet test Backend.Tests`: schema-validation test passes against canonical DB, fails with a mapping/translation error when a mapped column/view is missing.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Call Migrate() ONLY on ApplicationDbContext; calling Migrate()/EnsureCreated() on TradeNetDbContext (which has no migrations) would throw or try to create tables in an externally-owned DB. Order matters: build the app, then migrate inside a scope before app.Run(). MigrateOnStartup adds a brief startup delay and requires the app's DB principal to have DDL rights — if DBAs apply migrations out-of-band, set MigrateOnStartup=false and keep it as a deploy step instead. The scaffold -Verify check needs the canonical connection string injected via env var (TRADENET_SCAFFOLD_CONN); never commit that string. Re-scaffolding with --force overwrites Model/TradeNet and TradeNetDbContext.cs, so any manual edits to those generated files (e.g. partial OnModelCreatingPartial hooks) must live in a separate non-generated partial file to survive regeneration. The Backend.Tests fixture currently uses EnsureCreatedAsync() against the scaffold to build a throwaway DB — keep the new real-schema validation test separate (Category=RequiresDb) so it doesn't run in environments without the canonical DB."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Downgraded Medium -> Low. Two reasons. (1) The DB-first scaffold lacking migrations is largely by-design, not a defect: TradeNetDbContext is a read-model over an externally-owned schema (separate `TradeNetDBTest` connection string, heavy use of keyless `.ToView(...)` mappings such as vw_ImportLicenceItemTotalByCurrency). You do not author migrations for a schema another system owns — the standard EF approach is exactly to re-scaffold. So the genuine gap is narrow: no CI staleness check for the scaffold, plus no guarded Migrate() for the app-owned ApplicationDbContext. (2) The asserted impact linking schema drift to the recurring report-parity complaints is speculative; those documented complaints have concerned column/filter config parity (reportConfigs.ts / RDLC headers), not EF mapping/translation exceptions. No evidence in-repo ties this finding to an observed runtime failure. The recommendation (CI scaffold-staleness check + guarded MigrateAsync for ApplicationDbContext) is sound and worth doing, but this is latent process/hygiene debt rather than a confirmed runtime defect — Low is the appropriate rating. The locations are accurate and need no correction.


<a id="f-data-access-static-helpers-tight-coupling"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Data access is split across 83 static sp_* helpers and per-controller boilerplate with no injectable abstraction, hurting testability and consistency

**ID:** `data-access-static-helpers-tight-coupling` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Separation of concerns / Testability &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Large &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:24-52`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:99-163`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/AccountSummaryReportController.cs:70-76`
- `Backend/Service/Reports/ReportQueryService.cs:8-35`

**Problem.** Of 158 report controllers, data access goes through 83 static partial sp_* classes (e.g. sp_HSCodeReport.ExecuteAggregateStoredProcedureAsync) plus a static ReportQueryService and a static ReportAggregationService — none are interfaces registered in DI, so controllers are hard-bound to concrete static methods that take a concrete TradeNetDbContext. The only DI-registered domain services are ICommonService<>, IJWTManagerService, ICountryCache and the Excel queue (Program.cs:92-95). Each controller also re-implements the same validation (TryCreateReportRequest), paging math, and the brittle fallback that detects a missing _pagination proc by string-matching SqlException.Number==2812 and the proc name (AccountSummaryReportController.cs:160-164). One controller (ImportLicenceBySectionReport) still holds full inline LINQ join+aggregation logic in the controller (lines 101-163).

**Impact.** Static, context-bound data access cannot be mocked, so unit testing forces a real SQL Server fixture (confirmed by Backend.Tests/ReportSqlServerFixture.cs). The duplicated validation/paging/fallback across 158 files means a fix (e.g. correcting a total) must be repeated everywhere, which is the mechanism behind the repeated per-report parity complaints. The 2812/string-match fallback is fragile to message localization or proc renames.

**Recommendation.** Introduce a thin IReportQueryExecutor / per-report service interface registered in DI so controllers depend on abstractions; centralize request validation, paging, and the missing-proc fallback into one shared base/service. Detect the missing proc by checking proc existence once at startup rather than catching SqlException.Number on every call. Move the inline LINQ in ImportLicenceBySectionReport into a helper consistent with the rest.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. Validator + base class + AccountSummary conversion + executor/proc-existence + ImportLicenceBySection extraction + first unit test: ~1.5 days. Full rollout across the remaining 156 controllers: ~1 week of mechanical batched work.)_**

Introduce one injectable abstraction (IReportRequestValidator) plus an abstract ReportControllerBase to absorb the duplicated TryCreateReportRequest / paging logic, and wrap the static sp_*/ReportQueryService data access behind a thin DI-registered IReportQueryExecutor that takes the (already DI-scoped) TradeNetDbContext, so controllers depend on interfaces and become unit-testable without a real SQL fixture. Replace the one fragile SqlException.Number==2812 string-match fallback in AccountSummaryReportController with a startup proc-existence check cached in the new service.

1. Read the four cited files plus Backend/Program.cs, Backend/Model/ReportQueryRequest.cs, Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.cs (already done). Note the real shared shape: ReportQueryRequest exposes PageIndex/PageSize/SortColumn/SortOrder/FilterColumn/FilterQuery/IncludeTotalCount, and TryCreateReportRequest is duplicated in 157 of 158 controllers (confirmed via `grep -rl TryCreateReportRequest Backend/Controllers | wc -l`). The 2812 string-match fallback exists in exactly ONE file, AccountSummaryReportController.cs:160-164 — do NOT treat it as widespread.
2. Create Backend/Service/Reports/IReportRequestValidator.cs with interface IReportRequestValidator and sealed class ReportRequestValidator, exposing string? ValidateDateRange(DateTime fromDate, DateTime toDate) that reproduces the exact three checks currently inlined in every TryCreateReportRequest (FromDate==default -> 'FromDate is required.', ToDate==default -> 'ToDate is required.', ToDate<FromDate -> 'ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.'). Keep the messages byte-identical so API responses don't change.
3. Register it in Backend/Program.cs immediately after line 93 (the ICommonService<> registration, before AddExcelExportQueue): builder.Services.AddSingleton<API.Service.Reports.IReportRequestValidator, API.Service.Reports.ReportRequestValidator>(). Singleton is safe — the validator is stateless.
4. Create Backend/Controllers/Report/ReportControllerBase.cs: an abstract ControllerBase subclass that takes IReportRequestValidator via its constructor and exposes the shared DefaultPageSize=10 / MaxPageSize=1000 constants and a NormalizePaging(pageIndex, pageSize) helper matching the math already in AccountSummaryReportController.cs:49-52 and sp_HSCodeReport.StoredProcedure.cs:30-31.
5. Migrate AccountSummaryReportController to the new base as the reference conversion: make it inherit ReportControllerBase, inject IReportRequestValidator through the constructor (alongside the existing TradeNetDbContext and IExcelExportJobService), and rewrite the body of TryCreateReportRequest so the date checks call Validator.ValidateDateRange(...) and only the report-specific mapping to sp_AccountSummaryReportRequest stays local. Replace the inline paging math (lines 49-52) with NormalizePaging.
6. Introduce a thin data-access abstraction so controllers stop binding to static sp_* methods + concrete TradeNetDbContext. Add Backend/Service/Reports/IReportQueryExecutor.cs (interface) and a ReportQueryExecutor implementation registered AddScoped in Program.cs that takes TradeNetDbContext via DI. Give it (a) the generic CreatePagedResultAsync<T>(IQueryable<T>, ReportQueryRequest) currently living in the static ReportQueryService (move/forward that logic — keep ReportQueryService delegating to it for now to avoid touching all callers at once), and (b) a bool ProcedureExists(string name) that queries sys.objects (e.g. db.Database.SqlQueryRaw<int>("SELECT CASE WHEN OBJECT_ID(@p0,'P') IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END", name)) and caches the result per proc name for the lifetime of the app.
7. In AccountSummaryReportController, delete the IsMissingPaginationProcedure(SqlException) helper (lines 160-164) and the catch (SqlException ex) when (IsMissingPaginationProcedure(ex)) guard (lines 70-76). Instead, decide the path up-front: if _queryExecutor.ProcedureExists("sp_AccountSummaryReport_pagination") run the paginated proc, else fall back to sp_AccountSummaryReport.Query(...) + the executor's CreatePagedResultAsync. This removes the dependency on SqlException.Number==2812 and Message.Contains, which break under localized SQL Server messages or a proc rename.
8. Move the inline LINQ in ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.BuildRowsAsync (lines 101-163) out of the controller. Create a static helper (mirror the existing sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync already called in WriteRowsAsync at line 197, or add a sibling method) that returns the List<ReportAggregateResult>, so the controller no longer holds join+aggregation logic that every other report keeps in StoredProcedureToLinq. Keep the 5-minute IMemoryCache wrapper and the ColumnTotals footer (lines 64-94) in the controller.
9. Add a unit test in Backend.Tests proving the abstraction pays off: a test that constructs AccountSummaryReportController with a mocked IReportRequestValidator (and a mocked/fake IReportQueryExecutor) and asserts that a request with ToDate < FromDate returns BadRequest('ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.') without any SQL Server — i.e. it does NOT use ReportSqlServerFixture. This is the concrete testability win the finding asks for.
10. Roll the same base-class + validator + executor conversion across the remaining 156 controllers incrementally (this is the 'Large' part of the effort). Each conversion is mechanical: inherit ReportControllerBase, inject IReportRequestValidator, replace inline date checks with Validator.ValidateDateRange and inline paging with NormalizePaging. Do them in small batches and run `dotnet build` + `dotnet test` between batches.

```csharp
// 1) NEW: Backend/Service/Reports/IReportRequestValidator.cs
//    Centralizes the validation duplicated verbatim in 157/157 controllers.
namespace API.Service.Reports
{
    public interface IReportRequestValidator
    {
        // Returns null when valid; otherwise a 400-style message to surface as BadRequest.
        string? ValidateDateRange(System.DateTime fromDate, System.DateTime toDate);
    }

    public sealed class ReportRequestValidator : IReportRequestValidator
    {
        public string? ValidateDateRange(System.DateTime fromDate, System.DateTime toDate)
        {
            if (fromDate == default) return "FromDate is required.";
            if (toDate == default)   return "ToDate is required.";
            if (toDate < fromDate)   return "ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.";
            return null;
        }
    }
}

// 2) NEW: Backend/Controllers/Report/ReportControllerBase.cs
//    Abstract base every report controller inherits; hosts shared paging + validation.
using API.Service.Reports;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc;

namespace Backend.Controllers.Report
{
    public abstract class ReportControllerBase : ControllerBase
    {
        protected const int DefaultPageSize = 10;
        protected const int MaxPageSize = 1000;

        protected readonly IReportRequestValidator Validator;
        protected ReportControllerBase(IReportRequestValidator validator) => Validator = validator;

        protected (int pageIndex, int pageSize) NormalizePaging(int pageIndex, int pageSize)
            => (System.Math.Max(0, pageIndex),
                pageSize <= 0 ? DefaultPageSize : System.Math.Min(pageSize, MaxPageSize));
    }
}

// 3) Program.cs  (after line 93, alongside the other domain service registrations)
builder.Services.AddScoped(typeof(ICommonService<>), typeof(CommonService<>));
+ builder.Services.AddSingleton<API.Service.Reports.IReportRequestValidator,
+                               API.Service.Reports.ReportRequestValidator>();
builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);

// 4) AccountSummaryReportController.cs — replace the fragile string-match fallback (lines 160-164).
//    Detect the missing _pagination proc ONCE (cached) instead of catching SqlException.Number==2812
//    + Message.Contains("...") on every request, which is localization/rename fragile.
//    Move IsMissingPaginationProcedure off message text:
- private static bool IsMissingPaginationProcedure(SqlException ex)
- {
-     return ex.Number == 2812
-         && ex.Message.Contains("sp_AccountSummaryReport_pagination", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase);
- }
// and gate the try/catch on a startup-resolved flag from the new executor
// (e.g. _queryExecutor.ProcedureExists("sp_AccountSummaryReport_pagination")) so the
// fallback path is chosen up-front rather than via exception-number heuristics.
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build the Backend project succeeds with no errors after each batch: `dotnet build "Backend/Backend.csproj"` (or the solution).
- Run the new no-SQL unit test: `dotnet test Backend.Tests --filter FullyQualifiedName~AccountSummary` and confirm the BadRequest validation test passes WITHOUT instantiating ReportSqlServerFixture (proves controllers are now mockable).
- Functional parity: POST /api/AccountSummaryReport with a valid body and confirm the JSON (rows, totalCount, ColumnTotals) is byte-for-byte identical to pre-change output; POST with ToDate < FromDate and confirm the response is still 400 'ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.'
- Confirm the missing-proc fallback still works: on a DB where sp_AccountSummaryReport_pagination is absent, POST /api/AccountSummaryReport and verify it returns data via the Query() fallback path (now selected by ProcedureExists, not by catching SqlException 2812).
- POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport before and after the BuildRowsAsync extraction and diff the response — rows, paging, and the noOfLicences/totalValue ColumnTotals footer must be unchanged.
- Run the full existing suite: `dotnet test` — all previously green tests still pass.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Honor the verifier corrections: (a) the SqlException.Number==2812 + Message.Contains fallback lives in ONLY AccountSummaryReportController.cs:160-164 — do not refactor a 'widespread' fallback that doesn't exist; (b) do NOT frame this work as the cause of the per-report parity complaints (those have documented root causes: lookup wiring, missing ColumnTotals, header mismatches) — this is a Low-severity testability/maintainability change only. Keep validation message strings byte-identical or you change the API contract and break frontend error handling. TradeNetDbContext is registered with AddDbContextPool (scoped); IReportQueryExecutor must be AddScoped (not singleton) so it never captures a pooled DbContext beyond a request. The Excel handler path resolves controllers by reflection via ControllerStreamingExcelReportJobHandler / ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions (auto-discovers IStreamingExcelReport implementers) — controllers gaining a new constructor parameter (IReportRequestValidator) must still be resolvable from the request-scope DI container, so register the validator before AddExcelExportQueue and verify an Excel export still enqueues. Roll out the 156-controller conversion in small batches behind build+test gates; the base-class change alone is low-risk and independently revertable, but the executor extraction touches data access so land it after the validator/base-class change, not before.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Keep severity Low. Two corrections to the writeup before acting on it: (a) The SqlException.Number==2812 / proc-name string-match fallback exists in only ONE controller (AccountSummaryReportController.cs:160-164), not "across 158 files" — do not present it as widespread duplication. (b) Drop or soften the claim that this abstraction gap is the mechanism behind per-report parity complaints; it is unsubstantiated and conflicts with documented root causes (lookup wiring, missing ColumnTotals, header mismatches). The genuinely broad duplication is TryCreateReportRequest (157/157 controllers) and direct static-helper + concrete-DbContext coupling, which is the legitimate basis for the Low-severity testability/maintainability finding. sp_ class count is 82 (finding's 83 is fine). Locations cited are all accurate.


---

### Backend — Code Quality & Maintainability

> The backend is two codebases in one. The newer reporting infrastructure (Service/ExcelExport, Service/Reports/CountryCache, ReportLookupsController) is genuinely well-engineered: scoped DbContext access via IServiceScopeFactory, atomic DB leases, structured ILogger usage, magic strings hoisted to constants, and correct nullable handling. The older surface — AuthController, JWTManagerService, UploadController, ChatController, BaseAPIController, ApiResult's dynamic filter, and the ~145 hand-cloned report controllers plus ~93 StoredProcedureToLinq files — is low quality: pervasive copy-paste (157 identical TryCreateReportRequest blocks), ~34 unreferenced/dead SP classes, plaintext password handling, broad swallowed exceptions that leak raw messages, almost no logging outside the Excel queue, missing/inconsistent authorization, weak input validation, and unsafe conversions that turn bad input into 500s. The hardcoded prod 'sa' connection strings and literal JWT key sit in checked-in appsettings.json. Maintainability is the dominant risk: any cross-cutting change to report request handling must be applied ~145 times by hand.

<a id="f-hardcoded-prod-credentials-jwt-key"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Production SQL 'sa' credentials and weak literal JWT signing key checked into appsettings.json

**ID:** `hardcoded-prod-credentials-jwt-key` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Configuration / Secrets &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/appsettings.json:10 (JWT:Key), Backend/appsettings.json:26 (TemplateDB sa conn), Backend/appsettings.json:27 (TradeNetDBTest sa conn)`
- `Backend/appsettings.json:11`
- `Backend/appsettings.json:24`
- `Backend/appsettings.json:25`

**Problem.** appsettings.json hardcodes the JWT signing key as the literal string "This is my supper secret key for jwt" and two production connection strings to 203.81.66.111,14330 using 'User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022' for both TemplateDB and TradeNetDB, all with TrustServerCertificate=True. These are committed to source control.

**Impact.** Anyone with repo access (or a leaked artifact) gets sa-level access to the live government trade database and can forge valid JWTs for any user/role offline. This is the single highest-impact item: it converts a code read into full data and auth compromise. JWT:Key is consumed in Program.cs:74 and JWTManagerService.cs:41/71/111.

**Recommendation.** Rotate the sa password and JWT key immediately. Move all secrets to environment variables / user-secrets / a secret manager and inject at deploy time. Use a least-privilege SQL login instead of sa, set a long random JWT key (>=256 bits), and remove the values from the committed file.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours (incl. credential rotation, least-privilege login setup, config wiring, and coordinated history rewrite))_**

Rotate the leaked SQL `sa` password and JWT signing key out-of-band first, then move all three secrets (JWT:Key, TemplateDB, TradeNetDBTest) out of the committed Backend/appsettings.json into environment variables / .NET user-secrets / deploy-time injection, untrack the file from git, and purge it from history. The code already reads via builder.Configuration so no consumer code changes are required once the config providers supply the values.

1. ROTATE FIRST (out-of-band, before any commit): on SQL Server 203.81.66.111,14330 change the `sa` password (ideally disable `sa` entirely and create a least-privilege login per database, e.g. `tradenet_app` with only the rights the report API needs on TemplateDB and TradeNetDB). Generate a fresh >=256-bit JWT key, e.g. `openssl rand -base64 64`. Treat the old `Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022` password and the literal 'This is my supper secret key for jwt' as permanently compromised.
2. Strip the secrets out of the committed file. Edit Backend/appsettings.json so JWT:Key, ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB and ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest are empty placeholders (keep the keys present so structure is documented). Program.cs:75-78 already throws InvalidOperationException when JWT:Key is empty, so a misconfigured deploy fails fast rather than running with a blank key.
3. Provide the real values locally via .NET user-secrets (the project already has <UserSecretsId>f930729b-3190-42a6-9201-49e396edac05</UserSecretsId> in Backend/API.csproj, so no extra setup). From the Backend folder run: `dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "<new-random-key>"`, `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB" "Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<new>;..."`, `dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest" "Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<new>;..."`.
4. Provide the real values in production via environment variables injected at deploy time (the default ASP.NET host already loads env vars and user-secrets, so no Program.cs change is needed). Use the double-underscore section delimiter: `JWT__Key`, `ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB`, `ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest`. Set these in the container/orchestrator (Docker env, compose, or the host's secret store) — the csproj already targets Docker (DockerDefaultTargetOS/DockerfileContext).
5. Untrack the file from git so future edits to local secrets are not committed. The root .gitignore already lists `Backend/appsettings.json`, but `git check-ignore -v Backend/appsettings.json` returns nothing and `git ls-files --error-unmatch Backend/appsettings.json` succeeds — confirming it is STILL TRACKED and the ignore rule is inert. Run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json` then commit; the gitignore entry then takes effect for the working copy. Add a committed `Backend/appsettings.example.json` (placeholder values only) so other developers know the required keys.
6. Purge the secret from history. `git log --all -- Backend/appsettings.json` shows it lives in 5 commits (dadadc6 initial, 0453b61, 73edb0d, 07d95d8, f97eee7). Removing it from HEAD is not enough. Use git-filter-repo (preferred) or BFG to scrub it: `git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths` (or `bfg --delete-files appsettings.json` / `bfg --replace-text` with the leaked strings), then force-push all branches and tags and have every collaborator re-clone. Coordinate this rewrite with the team.
7. After rotation, also close the supporting auth gaps the verifier flagged: in Backend/Program.cs set ValidateIssuer=true and ValidateAudience=true (lines 83-84) now that JWT:Issuer/JWT:Audience are configured, so a leaked key alone cannot mint a universally-accepted token; and stop storing plaintext passwords — JWTManagerService.Authenticate (line 32) compares `x.Password == users.Password`, so introduce password hashing (e.g. ASP.NET Core PasswordHasher / BCrypt) and verify the hash instead of equality. Track these as follow-ups if out of scope for this finding.

```bash
// Backend/appsettings.json  (committed file — placeholders only, no real secrets)

// BEFORE
"JWT": {
  "Key": "This is my supper secret key for jwt",
  "Issuer": "https://sakbran.github.io",
  "Audience": "sakbran.github.io"
},
...
"ConnectionStrings": {
  "DefaultConnection": "Server=localhost;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;Trusted_Connection=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;",
  "TemplateDB":"Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;",
  "TradeNetDBTest": "Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TradeNetDB;User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;"
}

// AFTER  (real values come from user-secrets / env vars JWT__Key, ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB, ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest)
"JWT": {
  "Key": "",
  "Issuer": "https://sakbran.github.io",
  "Audience": "sakbran.github.io"
},
...
"ConnectionStrings": {
  "DefaultConnection": "Server=localhost;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;Trusted_Connection=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;",
  "TemplateDB": "",
  "TradeNetDBTest": ""
}

// ---- shell: untrack committed file + supply local secrets ----
git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json
# from Backend/ (UserSecretsId already in API.csproj):
dotnet user-secrets set "JWT:Key" "$(openssl rand -base64 64)"
dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TemplateDB"    "Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<NEW>;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;"
dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:TradeNetDBTest" "Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TradeNetDB;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<NEW>;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;"

// ---- production: deploy-time env vars (double-underscore = section delimiter) ----
JWT__Key=<new-256bit-key>
ConnectionStrings__TemplateDB=Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<NEW>;...
ConnectionStrings__TradeNetDBTest=Server=...;User ID=tradenet_app;Password=<NEW>;...

// ---- history purge (5 commits incl. initial) ----
git filter-repo --path Backend/appsettings.json --invert-paths
git push --force --all && git push --force --tags
```

**Verify:**
- `git ls-files --error-unmatch Backend/appsettings.json` should FAIL (file no longer tracked) and `git check-ignore -v Backend/appsettings.json` should now print the matching .gitignore rule.
- `grep -R "Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022\|supper secret key" .` returns nothing in the working tree; after history rewrite, `git log --all -S 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022' --oneline` and `git log --all -- Backend/appsettings.json` return no commits.
- With user-secrets set, run `dotnet run` from Backend/ and confirm the app starts (no InvalidOperationException 'JWT:Key configuration value is missing.' from Program.cs:77) and that report endpoints querying TemplateDB/TradeNetDB succeed — proving the connection strings resolve from config providers.
- Confirm rotation took effect: the OLD sa password no longer authenticates against 203.81.66.111,14330 (e.g. `sqlcmd -S 203.81.66.111,14330 -U sa -P 'Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022'` fails), and a JWT signed with the OLD literal key is rejected (401) by an authorized endpoint.
- If the auth-hardening follow-ups are done: a token with a wrong/absent issuer/audience is rejected once ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience are true, and stored user passwords are non-plaintext hashes.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Rotate credentials BEFORE the history rewrite/force-push, not after — the values are already public to anyone who cloned, so removal alone does not protect them.
