The root .gitignore listing is misleading: it does NOT ignore the file today because git was already tracking it; you must `git rm --cached` for the ignore to apply. History rewrite (filter-repo/BFG) changes every commit SHA — coordinate with the team, force-push all branches+tags, and have everyone re-clone or hard-reset; open PRs/forks must be rebased. Do not delete the JWT/ConnectionStrings keys outright from appsettings.json — keep them as empty placeholders so Program.cs:65/67/74 still resolve the config sections (env-var/user-secret values override empties). If you switch off `sa` to a least-privilege login, verify the new login has every permission the report stored procs and Excel-export queue use against both databases before cutover, or report endpoints will 500. Rollback: revert the appsettings.json edit and re-set the (rotated) values in config providers; never revert the credential rotation.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity correctly Critical; verdict is Adjusted purely to fix the line numbers (JWT key is line 10 not 11; sa conn strings are lines 26-27 not 24-25). Additional supporting risk not in the finding: Authenticate() at JWTManagerService.cs:32 compares x.Password == users.Password in a LINQ query, implying plaintext password storage, and ValidateIssuer/ValidateAudience are both false in Program.cs (83-84), so a forged token only needs the leaked signing key. Recommendation (rotate sa password + JWT key, move to env vars/secret manager, use least-privilege SQL login, >=256-bit random key, purge from committed file and history) is sound. Note the secret also lives in git history across multiple commits, so removing it from HEAD alone is insufficient — history rewrite + credential rotation is required.


<a id="f-plaintext-password-and-asparallel-auth"></a>
#### 🔴 CRITICAL — Authentication compares plaintext passwords and misuses AsParallel() on an EF IQueryable

**ID:** `plaintext-password-and-asparallel-auth` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Security / Correctness &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32`
- `Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:33`
- `Backend/Model/User.cs:21`

**Problem.** Authenticate() builds an IQueryable filtering on x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password — passwords are stored and compared in plaintext (User.Password is a plain string with no hashing). Worse, line 33 calls .AsParallel().Any() on an EF Core IQueryable. AsParallel() is a PLINQ operator; applied to an IQueryable it forces (at best) a degenerate client-side enumeration and is semantically meaningless for a DB query, indicating the author misunderstood the abstraction. The intended single-row existence check should be a server-side AnyAsync.

**Impact.** Plaintext passwords mean a single DB read (or a leaked backup) exposes every credential; the hardcoded 'sa' connection string in appsettings makes that read trivial for anyone with the repo. The AsParallel misuse can pull the predicate client-side, defeating SQL-level filtering and harming performance/correctness on the login hot path.

**Recommendation.** Hash passwords (ASP.NET Core PasswordHasher / BCrypt) and compare hashes. Replace UsersRecords.AsParallel().Any() with await _userService.Retrieve.AnyAsync(...) or fetch the user and verify the hash. Never store or compare raw passwords.

**Example:**

```
// current
IQueryable<User> UsersRecords = _userService.Retrieve.Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password);
if (!UsersRecords.AsParallel().Any()) return null;
// fixed
var user = await _userService.Retrieve.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Name == users.Name);
if (user is null || !_hasher.Verify(users.Password, user.PasswordHash)) return null;
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours (code change ~1h, migration script + testing ~2-3h))_**

Stop comparing plaintext passwords and stop misusing PLINQ on an EF query: inject ASP.NET Core's built-in PasswordHasher<User>, fetch the single user server-side with FirstOrDefaultAsync, and verify the supplied password against the stored hash. Because there is no in-app user-creation path (users are seeded directly into SQL), pair the code change with a one-time migration that rehashes existing plaintext rows, plus on-the-fly upgrade of legacy plaintext logins.

1. In Backend/Model/User.cs, the Password column currently holds plaintext (nvarchar(max), Migrations/20250622052201_userAndToken.cs). Keep the property named Password to avoid a schema migration, but treat its contents as a hash going forward. Update the XML/intent comment so future devs know it now stores a PasswordHasher hash, e.g. add `// stores Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.PasswordHasher hash, never plaintext` above the Password property at line 21.
2. Register the hasher in Backend/Program.cs next to the existing auth registrations (lines 92-93). Add `builder.Services.AddSingleton<Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.IPasswordHasher<API.Model.User>, Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.PasswordHasher<API.Model.User>>();`. No NuGet package is needed — PasswordHasher<T> ships in the Microsoft.AspNetCore.App shared framework already referenced by this Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web / net8.0 project.
3. In Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;` (EntityFrameworkCore is already imported on line 3), add a private readonly field `private readonly IPasswordHasher<User> _passwordHasher;`, add the constructor parameter `IPasswordHasher<User> passwordHasher` (lines 21-24) and assign `_passwordHasher = passwordHasher;` in the body (lines 25-29).
4. Rewrite the credential check in Authenticate (Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs:32-39). Remove the plaintext predicate `&& x.Password == users.Password` and the `.AsParallel().Any()` call. Fetch the candidate by Name server-side with `FirstOrDefaultAsync`, then verify the supplied password against the stored hash with `_passwordHasher.VerifyHashedPassword`. Return null if the user is missing or verification fails. Reuse the fetched entity as `tempUser` so the existing token-building block below (lines 40-64) is unchanged.
5. Handle the existing plaintext rows already in the DB. Option A (preferred, no downtime): write a one-time idempotent script/console step or an EF migration `Up()` that loads each User and replaces Password with `new PasswordHasher<User>().HashPassword(user, user.Password)` for any row whose value is not already a hash (PasswordHasher v3 hashes are Base64 and start with the 0x01 format marker; cheaply detect legacy rows by length/`!value.StartsWith("AQAAAA")`). Run it once before deploying the new Authenticate. Option B (transparent upgrade in code): in Authenticate, when `VerifyHashedPassword` returns `PasswordVerificationResult.Failed`, also compare `user.Password == users.Password` as a legacy fallback; on a legacy match, rehash and persist via `await _userService.Update(user.Id, user)` then continue. Ship Option B only as a short-lived bridge and remove it after all rows are migrated.
6. Confirm AuthController.Login (Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:25-43) needs no change — it already awaits `_jWTManager.Authenticate(data)` and maps null to 404. Note that the catch on line 41 returns `ex.Message` to the client; that is a separate finding and out of scope here.
7. Separately remediate the hardcoded 'sa' connection string called out in the impact (move it out of appsettings into a secret/managed identity) — tracked as its own finding; the hashing change above limits blast radius but does not by itself fix the credential exposure.

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs  (add near lines 92-93)
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IPasswordHasher<API.Model.User>, PasswordHasher<API.Model.User>>();
// (add: using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity; using API.Model;)

// Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs
// add: using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity;
private readonly IPasswordHasher<User> _passwordHasher;

public JWTManagerService(IConfiguration iconfiguration,
    ICommonService<TokenModel> tokenService,
    ICommonService<User> userService,
    IPasswordHasher<User> passwordHasher)   // <-- new
{
    this.iconfiguration = iconfiguration;
    _tokenService = tokenService;
    _userService = userService;
    _passwordHasher = passwordHasher;       // <-- new
}

public async Task<TokenModel?> Authenticate(User users)
{
    // BEFORE:
    // IQueryable<User> UsersRecords = _userService.Retrieve.Where(x => x.Name == users.Name && x.Password == users.Password);
    // if (!UsersRecords.AsParallel().Any()) { return null; }
    // var tempUser = await UsersRecords.FirstOrDefaultAsync();

    // AFTER:
    var tempUser = await _userService.Retrieve.FirstOrDefaultAsync(x => x.Name == users.Name);
    if (tempUser is null)
    {
        return null;
    }

    var verify = _passwordHasher.VerifyHashedPassword(tempUser, tempUser.Password, users.Password);
    if (verify == PasswordVerificationResult.Failed)
    {
        // OPTIONAL short-lived legacy-plaintext bridge; remove after migration:
        if (tempUser.Password != users.Password) return null;
        tempUser.Password = _passwordHasher.HashPassword(tempUser, users.Password);
        await _userService.Update(tempUser.Id, tempUser);
    }
    else if (verify == PasswordVerificationResult.SuccessRehashNeeded)
    {
        tempUser.Password = _passwordHasher.HashPassword(tempUser, users.Password);
        await _userService.Update(tempUser.Id, tempUser);
    }

    // ...existing token-building code (lines 40-64) unchanged, using tempUser...
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` in Backend/ — confirm IPasswordHasher resolves with no new package and the AsParallel/plaintext lines are gone (`grep -n AsParallel Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs` returns nothing; `grep -n 'x.Password == users.Password' Backend/Service/JWTManagerService.cs` returns nothing).
- Run the migration step, then inspect the Users table: every Password value should be a Base64 PasswordHasher string (starts with 'AQAAAA'), with no readable plaintext.
- POST /api/Auth with a valid {Name, Password} returns 200 with a token; POST with a wrong password returns 404 (NotFound); POST with an unknown Name returns 404.
- Enable EF SQL logging (or SQL Profiler) for one login and confirm the emitted query filters by Name on the server (SELECT TOP(1) ... WHERE [Name] = @__) and that the password is NOT part of the WHERE clause and no full-table client enumeration occurs.
- If the legacy bridge (Option B) is enabled, log in once with an old plaintext credential, confirm success, then re-query that row and confirm Password is now a hash; a second login still succeeds via the hash path.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Ordering: deploy/run the rehash migration BEFORE the new Authenticate goes live, OR ship the Option B legacy bridge in the same release — otherwise every existing user is instantly locked out because VerifyHashedPassword will reject their stored plaintext.; Idempotency: guard the migration so re-running it does not hash an already-hashed value (which would double-hash and lock users out). Detect already-migrated rows by the 'AQAAAA' prefix / length before hashing.; There is no in-app user-creation or password-reset endpoint, so any future tooling that inserts users must call PasswordHasher.HashPassword — do not let new plaintext rows reappear. Consider adding a guarded admin endpoint or a documented seeding helper.; Keep the Password property name to avoid a schema/migration churn; renaming to PasswordHash would require an EF migration and updating the User() constructor default at Model/User.cs:12.; Rollback: the code change is safe to revert, but once rows are rehashed you cannot recover the original plaintext — back up the Users table before running the migration.; Out of scope but related: the hardcoded 'sa' connection string and AuthController returning ex.Message are separate findings; do not conflate them with this fix.


<a id="f-upload-controller-path-traversal-and-leak"></a>
#### 🟠 HIGH — UploadController is unauthenticated, vulnerable to path traversal, and leaks exception messages

**ID:** `upload-controller-path-traversal-and-leak` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Security / Exception handling &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:43 (anonymous Postupload), :83 (Path.Combine with unsanitized filename), :63-65 (extension check on postedFile.FileName not the write name), :113/:136 (SaveAsWebpAsync forces .webp output); Program.cs:166-169 (UseAuthorization + UseStaticFiles, no global FallbackPolicy)`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:23`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:43`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:83`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:94-100`

**Problem.** The controller has no [Authorize] (class or method), so Postupload is anonymous. The destination is built as Path.Combine("wwwroot","Image", filename) using the caller-supplied `filename` query value with no sanitization, then SaveAsWebpAsync writes there — a filename like '..\..\appsettings' enables path traversal / arbitrary file write. The size/extension checks are evaluated against postedFile but the actual write uses `file` from the loop and the extension validation can be bypassed (it checks `extension` from postedFile while saving under the user-supplied filename's name). The catch block returns the raw ex.Message-derived content to the client. Half the class is dead commented-out code (lines 25-39).

**Impact.** An unauthenticated attacker can write files into the served wwwroot (or escape it), enabling defacement or, combined with static file serving (app.UseStaticFiles), hosting of malicious content; raw exception text aids reconnaissance.

**Recommendation.** Add [Authorize], generate a server-side safe filename (e.g. Guid + validated extension) and reject any path separators; validate content-type server-side; return generic 400/500 without ex.Message; delete the commented-out fields. Confirm whether this endpoint is even used (it references wwwroot/Image while the report app stores exports under App_Data).

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2-3 hours (backend ~1h; frontend Upload auth wiring + manual verification ~1-2h))_**

Lock down the public UploadController by requiring authentication and eliminating path traversal: add a class-level [Authorize], ignore the caller-supplied query `filename` for the on-disk path and instead derive a server-side-safe name (Guid + validated extension, no path separators), and validate against the loop file (`file`) actually being written. The exception-leak item is moot per the verifier (the dict is never serialized), so just log server-side and keep the existing 500.

1. In Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs, add `[Authorize]` to the UploadController class (line 23), directly above/below `[ApiController]`, matching ExcelExportController/UserController. The `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` is already present (line 12), so no new import is needed.
2. Stop using the raw query-string `filename` to build the disk path. Validate the extension of the file ACTUALLY being written (`file`, the loop variable) rather than `postedFile`, since the write uses `file`. Replace the `var ext = postedFile.FileName...` block (lines 63-64) and the AllowedFileExtensions check to operate on `file.FileName`.
3. Generate a server-side-safe name and reject any path separators. After the size/extension checks pass, in the `else` branch (lines 81-87), compute a safe base name: `var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + extension;` and build the path with the OS-correct, root-anchored directory: `var imageDir = Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "wwwroot", "Image"); var filePath = Path.Combine(imageDir, safeName);`. Then add a defense-in-depth check that the resolved full path stays under imageDir: `var fullDir = Path.GetFullPath(imageDir); var fullPath = Path.GetFullPath(filePath); if (!fullPath.StartsWith(fullDir, StringComparison.Ordinal)) return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);`.
4. Because SaveAsWebpAsync always rewrites the output to `<base>.webp` (lines 113 and 136 do `filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"`), the real persisted name is `safeName` without its original extension plus `.webp`. Capture the returned path from `SaveAsWebpAsync` and return the basename to the caller so the frontend can reference it: `var savedPath = await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath); dict.Add("success", Path.GetFileName(savedPath));`. (The current code passes the raw query `filename` into SaveAsWebpAsync's split logic; after this change it passes the Guid-based path, removing the traversal entirely.)
5. Harden SaveAsWebpAsync's filename derivation: replace the fragile `filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"` (lines 113 and 136) with `Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp")` so a Guid name containing no dot (or one accidentally containing multiple) is handled correctly.
6. Remove the dead commented-out members (lines 25, 27, 28 and the commented constructor params/body on lines 31, 33, 36, 38, 39) and the commented `using Amazon.S3` lines 8-9, leaving only the `IWebHostEnvironment _hostingEnvironment` field, constructor param, and assignment.
7. Exception handling (lines 94-100): per the verifier, nothing leaks today because `dict` is never serialized and only a bare 500 is returned. Do NOT spend effort scrubbing a response body that doesn't exist. Optionally replace the discarded `var res = string.Format(ex.Message...)` with a proper server-side log (inject `ILogger<UploadController>` and call `_logger.LogError(ex, "Upload failed")`) so the error is recorded rather than swallowed, then keep `return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError);`.
8. Verify whether this endpoint is still wanted: it is only called from Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx:221 (AntD Upload `action`) and writes to wwwroot/Image, while the report-export feature stores files outside wwwroot (Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportOptions.cs). If the Chat feature is dead, prefer deleting UploadController.cs and the ChatBox uploader entirely instead of hardening it.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs

// 1) Class attribute (line 21-23)
 [ApiController]
+[Authorize]
 [Route("api/[controller]")]
 public class UploadController : ControllerBase

// 2) Validate the file actually being written, and build a safe path (lines ~62-87)
 IList<string> AllowedFileExtensions = new List<string> { ".webp", ".PDF", ".pdf", ".jpg", ".gif", ".png", ".jpeg", ".svg" };
-var ext = postedFile.FileName[postedFile.FileName.LastIndexOf('.')..];
-var extension = ext.ToLower();
+var ext = file.FileName[file.FileName.LastIndexOf('.')..];
+var extension = ext.ToLower();
 if (!AllowedFileExtensions.Contains(extension)) { ... return BadRequest; }
 else if (postedFile.Length > MaxContentLength) { ... return BadRequest; }
 else
 {
-    var filePath = Path.Combine("wwwroot", "Image", filename);
-    await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath);
-    dict.Add("success", filename);
-    return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.OK);
+    // Ignore caller-supplied `filename` for the disk path: derive a safe server-side name.
+    var safeName = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N") + extension;
+    var imageDir = Path.Combine(_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath, "wwwroot", "Image");
+    var filePath = Path.Combine(imageDir, safeName);
+    // Defense in depth: ensure we never escape the image directory.
+    if (!Path.GetFullPath(filePath).StartsWith(Path.GetFullPath(imageDir), StringComparison.Ordinal))
+        return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);
+    var savedPath = await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath);
+    dict.Add("success", Path.GetFileName(savedPath));
+    return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.OK);
 }

// 3) Harden the .webp name derivation in SaveAsWebpAsync (lines 113 and 136)
-var newWebpFilePath = filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp";
+var newWebpFilePath = Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp");
...
-var webpFilePath = filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp";
+var webpFilePath = Path.ChangeExtension(filePath, ".webp");
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` the Backend project succeeds with no new warnings about unused fields.
- Unauthenticated request is rejected: `curl -i -X POST 'https://<host>/api/Upload/Postupload?filename=x.png' -F file=@test.png` returns 401 (was 201/200 before).
- Path traversal is neutralized: with a valid JWT, POST with `?filename=..%5C..%5Cappsettings` and a real image part; confirm the file is written ONLY as wwwroot/Image/<guid>.webp and that NO file appears outside wwwroot/Image (e.g., check ContentRoot for a stray `appsettings.webp`). The response `success` value should be the guid-based .webp name, not the attacker-supplied filename.
- Happy path still works for the Chat uploader once the frontend sends the bearer token (see pitfalls): uploading a .png from ChatBox produces a wwwroot/Image/<guid>.webp and the chat image renders.
- Static serving sanity: GET the returned wwwroot/Image/<guid>.webp succeeds; GET of any path outside Image is unaffected.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Frontend breakage from [Authorize]: ChatBox.tsx:219-223 uses an AntD `<Upload action={...}>` which does a bare browser POST and does NOT attach the axios JWT Authorization header. After adding [Authorize] this upload will 401. You must wire auth on the uploader too (add `headers={{ Authorization: 'Bearer ' + token }}` and/or `withCredentials`), or convert it to a `customRequest` that uses the existing axios instance. Coordinate this change with the backend change or the chat image upload feature will stop working.; Returned filename contract changed: previously the endpoint echoed back the caller's `filename` (and ChatBox derives the webp name client-side at ChatBox.tsx:203 via `filename.split('.')[0] + '.webp'`). Now the server owns the name (a Guid). The client-side derivation in ChatBox is unreliable anyway because SaveAsWebpAsync rewrites the extension; if you keep the feature, read the actual saved name from the response body rather than reconstructing it from the original filename.; Path.ChangeExtension expects the extension with a leading dot ('.webp'); keep the dot. With a Guid name (no dot) `filePath.Split(".")[0]` happened to work, but for names containing dots it truncated — ChangeExtension is the correct, non-lossy replacement.; Ensure `_hostingEnvironment.ContentRootPath` resolves to the app root containing wwwroot; if the deployment runs with a different working directory, the old relative `Path.Combine("wwwroot", ...)` and the new absolute path could point at different folders — verify the served static path matches in the target environment.; Rollback: revert UploadController.cs (and any ChatBox.tsx auth wiring) in one commit; no DB migration or data changes are involved.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Verdict Adjusted because the exception-message-leak claim (lines 94-100) is false: ex.Message is added to a local Dictionary `dict` that is never serialized into the returned HttpResponseMessage (no Content/Json/Ok, no AddWebApiConventions formatter), so nothing leaks to the client — in fact no error body is returned at all. The two load-bearing vulnerabilities (no [Authorize] on an anonymous endpoint + unsanitized path-traversal file write into the static-served wwwroot) are real and correctly High. Two accuracy corrections to the impact: (a) SaveAsWebpAsync always rewrites the extension to .webp (filePath.Split(".")[0] + ".webp"), so the write is an arbitrary-path .webp, not arbitrary extension; (b) only .webp uploads are copied verbatim, other types are re-encoded via SkiaSharp, so writing arbitrary text (e.g., a poisoned appsettings) is not straightforward. Recommendation guidance to add [Authorize], generate a server-side Guid filename, and reject path separators remains valid; the "return generic 500 without ex.Message" item is moot since nothing is currently returned.


<a id="f-inconsistent-authorization-across-controllers"></a>
#### 🟠 HIGH — Inconsistent authorization: ChatController and UploadController fully anonymous; BaseAPIController marked [AllowAnonymous] exposing User table

**ID:** `inconsistent-authorization-across-controllers` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Security / Consistency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs:12-14`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:21-23`
- `Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs:15-16`
- `Backend/Controllers/UserController.cs:15`

**Problem.** Authorization is applied ad hoc per controller with no convention. Report controllers use [Authorize], but ChatController has no auth attribute at all (anonymous GET/POST of chat messages), UploadController has none, and BaseAPIController<T> is decorated [AllowAnonymous] at the class level with [Authorize] sprinkled on individual methods. UserController : BaseAPIController<User> thus exposes a generic, sortable/filterable Get over the User table whose model includes the plaintext Password (User.cs:21).

**Impact.** Easy to ship a User endpoint that returns password hashes/plaintext to any caller; the [AllowAnonymous]-class + [Authorize]-method pattern is fragile (a new method added without [Authorize] is silently public). Anonymous chat read/write allows tampering and spam.

**Recommendation.** Adopt a global fallback authorization policy (RequireAuthenticatedUser) via AddAuthorization/FallbackPolicy in Program.cs so endpoints are secure by default and only explicit [AllowAnonymous] opts out. Remove [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController, add [Authorize] to ChatController, and never serialize Password (use a DTO / [JsonIgnore]).

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1.5 hours)_**

Make endpoints secure-by-default by adding a global FallbackPolicy (RequireAuthenticatedUser) in Program.cs, remove the class-level [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController, add [Authorize] to ChatController and UploadController, and stop serializing the plaintext Password on API.Model.User by marking it [JsonIgnore] so the generic Get on UserController can never leak it.

1. In Backend/Program.cs, register an authorization fallback policy so every endpoint requires a valid JWT unless it explicitly opts out with [AllowAnonymous]. Add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` to the usings, then after the JWT bearer registration block (after the `.AddJwtBearer(...)` call that ends around line 91) add: `builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options => { options.FallbackPolicy = new AuthorizationPolicyBuilder().RequireAuthenticatedUser().Build(); });`
2. Confirm the pipeline order in Program.cs is correct for the new fallback policy: `app.UseAuthentication()` (line 166) then `app.UseAuthorization()` (line 167) already run before `app.MapControllers()` (line 168) — no change needed there. The fallback policy is enforced by the routing/authorization middleware, so it applies to all mapped controller actions automatically.
3. Verify the only intentionally-public endpoint stays public: AuthController.Login already carries `[AllowAnonymous]` (Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:23), so the new FallbackPolicy will not break login. Do NOT remove that attribute.
4. In Backend/Controllers/BaseAPIController.cs, delete the class-level `[AllowAnonymous]` attribute on line 15. The per-method `[Authorize]` attributes (lines 28, 50, 65, 90, 100, 113) can stay (harmless/explicit) or be removed since the FallbackPolicy now covers them — leaving them is fine. This closes the fragile [AllowAnonymous]-class + [Authorize]-method pattern where a newly added method would otherwise be silently public.
5. In Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs, add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` (it is not currently imported) and put `[Authorize]` on the class, e.g. directly above `public class ChatController : ControllerBase` (line 14), alongside the existing `[ApiController]`/`[Route]` attributes. This stops anonymous read/write of chat messages.
6. In Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs, add `[Authorize]` to the class above `public class UploadController : ControllerBase` (line 23). The `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;` is already present (line 12). This stops anonymous file uploads to wwwroot/Image. (Defense-in-depth beyond auth: Postupload writes using the caller-supplied `filename` at Path.Combine("wwwroot","Image", filename) — line 83 — so also call Path.GetFileName(filename) before combining to prevent path traversal, but the auth fix is the in-scope change for this finding.)
7. Stop serializing the plaintext Password on the model that the generic UserController Get returns. In Backend/Model/User.cs (namespace API.Model — this is the type used by `UserController : BaseAPIController<User>`, NOT Backend/Model/TradeNet/User.cs), add `using System.Text.Json.Serialization;` and annotate the Password property (line 21) with `[JsonIgnore]` so `ApiResult<User>` returned by BaseAPIController.Get never emits the password. Keep the `[Required]` attribute.
8. If any login/write flow needs to bind Password on the way IN (AuthController.Login binds API.Model.User), prefer `[JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)]` is NOT sufficient — instead, if inbound binding of Password is required, replace the read path with a dedicated UserDto for GET responses rather than [JsonIgnore] on the entity. Verify AuthController.Login still receives Password after applying [JsonIgnore]; if login breaks, introduce a UserReadDto (Id, Name, Permission only) and have UserController/BaseAPIController project to it instead of returning the entity.

```csharp
// Program.cs — add using at top
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;

// Program.cs — AFTER the .AddJwtBearer(...) block (~line 91), BEFORE AddScoped<IJWTManagerService...>:
builder.Services.AddAuthorization(options =>
{
    options.FallbackPolicy = new AuthorizationPolicyBuilder()
        .RequireAuthenticatedUser()
        .Build();
});

// BaseAPIController.cs (lines 13-16) — remove [AllowAnonymous]
  [Route("api/[controller]")]
  [ApiController]
- [AllowAnonymous]
  public class BaseAPIController<T> : ControllerBase where T : class

// ChatController.cs — add using + [Authorize] on class (lines 12-14)
+ using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;
  [ApiController]
  [Route("api/[controller]")]
+ [Authorize]
  public class ChatController : ControllerBase

// UploadController.cs (lines 21-23) — add [Authorize] (using already present at line 12)
  [ApiController]
  [Route("api/[controller]")]
+ [Authorize]
  public class UploadController : ControllerBase

// Backend/Model/User.cs (API.Model) — never serialize Password
+ using System.Text.Json.Serialization;
  [Required]
+ [JsonIgnore]
  public string Password { get; set; }
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` from Backend/ — confirm no compile errors after the using/attribute changes.
- Anonymous is now blocked: `curl -i https://localhost:7xxx/api/Chat?room=a-AND-b` (no Authorization header) must return 401 (was 200). Same for `curl -i -X POST .../api/Upload/Postupload?filename=x.png` — must return 401 (was 201/200).
- Login still works: `curl -i -X POST .../api/Auth -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"name":"...","password":"..."}'` returns 200 with a token (AllowAnonymous on Login still honored).
- Authenticated report endpoints still work: call any existing report controller with a valid Bearer token and confirm 200 (FallbackPolicy doesn't break already-[Authorize]'d controllers).
- Password no longer leaks: with a valid token call `GET /api/User` and confirm the JSON `data` items contain Id/Name/Permission but NO `password`/`Password` field.
- Regression: confirm no controller that legitimately must stay public lost access — only AuthController.Login should be reachable anonymously (grep: `grep -rn AllowAnonymous Backend/Controllers` should show only AuthController.cs after BaseAPIController's is removed).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** FallbackPolicy applies ONLY to endpoints with no authorization metadata; existing [Authorize]/[AllowAnonymous] still win. After removing [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController, the inherited methods fall back to RequireAuthenticatedUser (they already had per-method [Authorize] anyway), so behavior tightens without breaking authenticated callers.; Static files: app.UseStaticFiles() (Program.cs:169) serves wwwroot publicly and is unaffected by the fallback policy. Files already written by the previously-anonymous Upload endpoint remain publicly readable — locking the endpoint does not retroactively secure existing uploaded files; audit wwwroot/Image if abuse is suspected.; Two different User types exist: API.Model.User (Backend/Model/User.cs, has plaintext Password — this is the one UserController exposes) and API.Model.TradeNet.User (Backend/Model/TradeNet/User.cs). Apply [JsonIgnore] to the API.Model one; the TradeNet one is a separate EF entity.; [JsonIgnore] on Password blocks BOTH read and write serialization. AuthController.Login binds API.Model.User from the request body and reads data.Password — verify login still binds Password after the change. System.Text.Json [JsonIgnore] (no condition) suppresses deserialization too, which can break login. If login breaks, do NOT keep plain [JsonIgnore] on the entity; instead introduce a UserReadDto for GET responses and leave the entity intact for inbound binding.; Order: add AddAuthorization in the service-registration section (before var app = builder.Build()), not after — registering services after Build() has no effect.; Rollback: revert the four files; the changes are additive attributes plus one service registration, no DB migration involved.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity High is appropriate: anonymous file upload (UploadController.Postupload writes to wwwroot using a caller-supplied `filename`, enabling overwrite/path-abuse and public-web persistence), anonymous chat read/write (tampering/spam, and Post trusts a fully client-supplied ChatModel), no FallbackPolicy in Program.cs, and a User model whose plaintext Password is serialized by the generic Get. Correction to the impact wording: UserController's password-exposing Get is NOT anonymous — UserController has class-level [Authorize] (UserController.cs:13) and the inherited Get methods are [Authorize]-decorated, so it requires a valid JWT (but lacks role/permission checks). The recommendations (global RequireAuthenticatedUser FallbackPolicy, remove class-level [AllowAnonymous] from BaseAPIController, add [Authorize] to ChatController/UploadController, never serialize Password via DTO/[JsonIgnore]) are all sound for this codebase.


<a id="f-dead-code-sp-to-linq-and-controllers"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — ~34 unreferenced StoredProcedureToLinq classes (explicit _old/_V2/_Seperated/Test dead code)

**ID:** `dead-code-sp-to-linq-and-controllers` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Dead code &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_LicencePermitSearch_old.cs`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_NewReport_old.cs`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_TestReport.cs`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_MPUReportV2.cs`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_MPUReport_Seperated_OnineFee.cs`

**Problem.** A reference scan of the 93 StoredProcedureToLinq files found ~34 classes with zero references anywhere in the codebase (outside their own file/migrations). Several are unambiguous abandoned versions: sp_LicencePermitSearch_old, sp_NewReport_old, sp_TestReport, sp_MPUReportV2, sp_MPUReport_Seperated_OnineFee. (Some others in the list are EICC/Dashboard/Wine variants that appear to have been superseded by *_Fast equivalents.) Note the typo 'OnineFee' in a committed filename.

**Impact.** Dead code inflates the 93-file 'sprawl', confuses parity checks against Tradenet 2.0, and tempts maintainers to copy the wrong (stale) version — a real trap given the documented MPU/Wine 'Fast' migrations. It also slows build and IDE navigation.

**Recommendation.** Delete the clearly-dead _old/_V2/_Seperated/Test files now. For the remaining unreferenced classes, confirm against routing then remove. Going forward, delete superseded versions in the same commit that introduces the replacement rather than renaming with _old.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 20 min (5 files now; +30 min if also triaging the ~29 _Fast-superseded leftovers))_**

Delete the 5 unambiguously-dead StoredProcedureToLinq files (`_old`/`V2`/`_Seperated`/`Test`) via `git rm`, since a repo-wide reference scan confirms zero call sites and `Backend/API.csproj` is SDK-style (default globbing, no explicit `<Compile>` entries), so the build is unaffected. Then optionally triage the remaining unreferenced superseded-by-`_Fast` variants after a routing check.

1. From the repo root /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report, delete the 5 verified-dead files with git rm so the removal is staged: git rm "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_LicencePermitSearch_old.cs" "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_NewReport_old.cs" "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_TestReport.cs" "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_MPUReportV2.cs" "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_MPUReport_Seperated_OnineFee.cs"
2. Each deleted file declares only its own prefix-named public types (e.g. sp_MPUReportV2.cs declares sp_MPUReportV2Request, sp_MPUReportV2Result, sp_MPUReportV2). These are all in namespace API.StoredProcedureToLinq and were confirmed to have zero references outside their own file. No companion partial classes or extension methods live elsewhere, so nothing else needs editing.
3. Do NOT touch the live class sp_LicencePermitSearch (no _old suffix) — sp_LicencePermitSearch_old.cs internally calls sp_LicencePermitSearch.Query(...), so the dependency runs one direction only; deleting the _old wrapper leaves the live class intact.
4. Rebuild the backend to confirm no dangling references: dotnet build "Backend/API.csproj". It must compile clean (API.csproj uses default SDK globbing with no explicit <Compile> Include entries, so removing the files needs no .csproj edit).
5. For the remaining ~29 unreferenced classes flagged by the scan (EICC/Dashboard/Wine/Permit/Licence variants superseded by *_Fast equivalents — e.g. sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport vs sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast, sp_WineImportationReport vs sp_WineImportationReport_Fast), do NOT bulk-delete in this commit. First confirm the active controller wiring in Backend/Controllers (grep each base class name across Backend/Controllers and Backend/Services for .Query( call sites) before removing, because the *_Fast cutover may be partial.
6. Going forward, when introducing a replacement (e.g. a *_Fast or *_V3 version), delete the superseded file in the SAME commit instead of renaming it to *_old, to prevent re-accumulating the stale-version trap that the project memory warns about (MPU/Wine Fast migrations).

```bash
# Run from repo root: /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report
git rm "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_LicencePermitSearch_old.cs" \
       "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_NewReport_old.cs" \
       "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_TestReport.cs" \
       "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_MPUReportV2.cs" \
       "Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_MPUReport_Seperated_OnineFee.cs"

# Verify the build still compiles cleanly after removal
dotnet build "Backend/API.csproj"
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build "Backend/API.csproj" succeeds with 0 errors after the git rm (proves no remaining references).
- Re-run the reference scan to confirm the 5 class names are gone: grep -rnwE "sp_LicencePermitSearch_old|sp_NewReport_old|sp_TestReport|sp_MPUReportV2|sp_MPUReport_Seperated_OnineFee" --include=*.cs Backend/ returns nothing.
- git status shows exactly 5 deletions staged and no other source files modified.
- Smoke-test that live reports still work: hit the existing report endpoints (MPU report, Licence/Permit search) and confirm they return data unchanged — the live sp_MPUReport / sp_MPUReport_V3 and sp_LicencePermitSearch classes are untouched.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do not delete the live siblings: sp_LicencePermitSearch (no _old), sp_MPUReport, sp_MPUReport_V3, and sp_NewReport are all still in use — only the _old/V2/_Seperated/Test variants are dead. sp_LicencePermitSearch_old.cs calls sp_LicencePermitSearch internally, so delete order does not matter as long as you remove only the _old file. The note about the EICC/Dashboard/Wine/*_Fast leftovers is a separate batch: removing those without first confirming controller routing risks deleting a class that is still wired up if a *_Fast cutover was only partial. Rollback is trivial (git checkout / git revert the delete commit) since these are pure deletions with no data migration.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** No runtime/security/correctness impact — this is purely maintainability dead code, so Medium sits at the upper bound for this category. However it is defensible here because: (1) ~35 of 93 files (~38%) are dead, and (2) the project's own memory notes explicitly warn of a 'stale version trap' from MPU/Wine Fast migrations, which this dead code directly creates. I leave severity at Medium. Minor caveat on methodology: my full-directory scan keyed on filename-base = primary class name, which is a sound heuristic here since each file's static class matches its filename; the 5 explicitly cited files were verified by direct class-name grep (not just filename), so the core claim is rock-solid. One adjacent observation outside the finding's scope: the LIVE sp_LicencePermitSearch (no _old) also has no controller references, but that does not affect this finding. Recommendation to delete the clearly-dead _old/V2/Seperated/Test files immediately is safe and correct; the remaining superseded-by-Fast files should be removed after a final routing confirmation as the finding advises.


<a id="f-apifilter-unguarded-datetime-convert"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Dynamic filter does an unguarded Convert.ToDateTime on user input (FormatException -> 500)

**ID:** `apifilter-unguarded-datetime-convert` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Input validation / Robustness &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:221-233`
- `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:299-316`

**Problem.** ApplyFilter, when the filter column name Contains("Date"), calls Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery) twice with no try/parse guard. Any non-date filterQuery throws FormatException, which (given no global handler) surfaces as an unhandled 500. The column-name-contains-'Date' heuristic is also brittle (matches e.g. 'UpdateDate', 'Validate'). The dynamic Where/OrderBy use System.Linq.Dynamic.Core with column names gated by IsValidProperty reflection, which mitigates injection, but the value path is unvalidated.

**Impact.** A malformed filter value from the SPA crashes the request with a 500 instead of a clean 400; combined with no logging, intermittent report failures are hard to diagnose.

**Recommendation.** Use DateTime.TryParse and return/ignore on failure (or surface a 400). Match date columns by actual property type (typeof(T).GetProperty(...).PropertyType) rather than a substring of the name.

**Example:**

```
// current
if (filterColumn.Contains("Date")) { var endate = Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery); ... }
// fixed
if (IsDateProperty(filterColumn)) { if (!DateTime.TryParse(filterQuery, out var d)) return source; ... }
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

In ApiResult<T>.ApplyFilter (Backend/Model/APIResult.cs), replace the two unguarded Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery) calls with a single DateTime.TryParse that bails out (returns the unfiltered source) on a bad value, and replace the brittle filterColumn.Contains("Date") name heuristic with an actual property-type check via reflection so only real date columns take the date branch.

1. Open Backend/Model/APIResult.cs. The class is `ApiResult<T>` in namespace `API.Model`. The target method is the private static `ApplyFilter` at lines 209-249; the unguarded date block is lines 221-233.
2. Add a private static helper `IsDateProperty(string propertyName)` (place it in the #region Methods block, e.g. right after `IsValidProperty` which ends at line 316). It looks up the property on `typeof(T)` with the same BindingFlags as IsValidProperty, unwraps `Nullable<>` with `Nullable.GetUnderlyingType`, and returns true only when the underlying type is `typeof(DateTime)` or `typeof(DateTimeOffset)`. This replaces the substring heuristic that wrongly matches names like `UpdateDate`/`Validate`.
3. In `ApplyFilter`, change the guard `if (filterColumn.Contains("Date"))` (line 221) to `if (IsDateProperty(filterColumn))`.
4. Inside that date branch, replace the two separate `Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery)` calls (lines 223 and 228) with one guarded parse: `if (!DateTime.TryParse(filterQuery, System.Globalization.CultureInfo.InvariantCulture, System.Globalization.DateTimeStyles.None, out var filterDate)) return source;`. Then build the range from `filterDate`: use `filterDate.AddDays(1)` for the `< @0` upper bound and `filterDate` for the `> @0` lower bound, keeping the existing `source.Where("ColumnName < @0", value)` dynamic-LINQ calls. Use InvariantCulture so behavior is deterministic regardless of server locale and matches the ISO dates the SPA date picker sends.
5. Note that `IsDateProperty` must NOT throw when the property is missing or non-date — it just returns false, so a non-date filterColumn correctly falls through to the existing Contains/== string filter below (lines 235-248). `IsValidProperty(filterColumn)` is already called earlier in ApplyFilter (line 216) and throws if the column doesn't exist, so the column name is still validated against injection before IsDateProperty runs.
6. Build the backend: `dotnet build` from Backend/. Confirm no nullable/usage warnings around the new helper.

```csharp
// Backend/Model/APIResult.cs — ApplyFilter date branch (was lines 221-233)

// BEFORE
if (filterColumn.Contains("Date"))
{
    var endate = Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery);
    endate = endate.AddDays(1);
    string EndDateSQLparam = filterColumn + " < @0";
    source = source.Where(EndDateSQLparam, endate);

    var startDate = Convert.ToDateTime(filterQuery);
    string StartDateSQLparam = filterColumn + " > @0";
    source = source.Where(StartDateSQLparam, startDate);

    return source;
}

// AFTER
if (IsDateProperty(filterColumn))
{
    if (!DateTime.TryParse(
            filterQuery,
            System.Globalization.CultureInfo.InvariantCulture,
            System.Globalization.DateTimeStyles.None,
            out var filterDate))
    {
        // Malformed date value: ignore the filter instead of throwing a 500.
        return source;
    }

    var endDate = filterDate.AddDays(1);
    source = source.Where(filterColumn + " < @0", endDate);
    source = source.Where(filterColumn + " > @0", filterDate);

    return source;
}

// New helper — add near IsValidProperty (after line 316), inside #region Methods
/// <summary>
/// True only when the named property is an actual DateTime/DateTimeOffset
/// (or nullable thereof), instead of guessing from the column name.
/// </summary>
private static bool IsDateProperty(string propertyName)
{
    var prop = typeof(T).GetProperty(
        propertyName,
        BindingFlags.IgnoreCase |
        BindingFlags.Public |
        BindingFlags.Static |
        BindingFlags.Instance);
    if (prop == null) return false;

    var t = Nullable.GetUnderlyingType(prop.PropertyType) ?? prop.PropertyType;
    return t == typeof(DateTime) || t == typeof(DateTimeOffset);
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` from Backend/ succeeds.
- Negative case (the actual fix): call any paginated report endpoint with a date column and a junk value, e.g. `GET /<report-route>?filterColumn=<aDateColumn>&filterQuery=not-a-date` with a valid bearer token. Before the fix this returns HTTP 500 (FormatException); after the fix it returns 200 with the filter ignored (full unfiltered page).
- Positive case: same endpoint with `filterQuery=2024-01-15` still narrows results to that day (the > startDate / < startDate+1 range), proving the date branch still works.
- Heuristic case: a non-date column whose name contains 'Date' (e.g. a string column) now goes through the Contains/== string-filter path instead of attempting a date parse — confirm such a filter returns 200 and filters as text.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** If any report DTO models a date column as a `string` (some report projections do), the old code would have date-filtered it via the name match, but the new IsDateProperty returns false for strings, so it now falls to the Contains/== branch. That is correct behavior for a string column, but if a particular report relied on string-typed date-range filtering, verify that report still filters as expected. No data migration or DB change; change is isolated to APIResult.cs and trivially reversible. Both CreateAsync and CreateFastPageAsync call ApplyFilter, so both pagination paths are fixed by this single edit.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Adjusting severity down to Low (finding is otherwise fully Confirmed). Mitigating factors: the endpoints are [Authorize]-gated, so only an authenticated admin can trigger it; the realistic caller is the SPA's own date picker which normally sends valid ISO dates; there is no data loss, no injection (column gated by reflection), no security impact. The outcome is purely a 500-instead-of-400 robustness/diagnosability defect. All technical claims in the finding are accurate; only the Medium rating is slightly generous for an auth-gated, hard-to-trigger crash. Locations are correct: APIResult.cs:221-233 is the unguarded date block; APIResult.cs:299-316 is IsValidProperty (the cited injection mitigation).


<a id="f-swallowed-exceptions-and-leaked-messages"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Broad catch blocks swallow context, leak raw exception messages, and there is almost no logging on the request path

**ID:** `swallowed-exceptions-and-leaked-messages` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Exception handling / Observability &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:39-42 (real message leak); Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:235-248 (bare catch fallback); Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:94-100 (swallowed exception, but message is NOT actually returned to client)`
- `Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:39-42`
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:94-100`
- `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:235-248`

**Problem.** AuthController.Login catches Exception and returns StatusCode(500, ex.Message) — internal error text returned to clients and never logged. UploadController does the same. ApiResult.ApplyFilter wraps its Contains() filter in a bare `catch {}` that silently falls back to an equality filter, hiding genuine errors. Only 2 files in the entire backend (both in Service/ExcelExport) inject ILogger; controllers and the LINQ report layer perform no logging at all, so production failures outside the Excel queue are invisible.

**Impact.** Operators get no telemetry for failed logins or report queries; attackers get stack/SQL detail via leaked messages; the silent catch in ApplyFilter can mask a misconfigured filter as 'no results', producing wrong reports with no trace.

**Recommendation.** Inject ILogger<T> and log exceptions with context; return a generic ProblemDetails (no ex.Message) to clients. Replace the bare catch in ApplyFilter with a specific, logged fallback or upfront type detection. Consider a global exception-handling middleware so the 145 controllers don't each reinvent error handling.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2-3 hours)_**

Stop the genuine client-facing message leak in AuthController, replace the bare swallowing catch in ApiResult.ApplyFilter with upfront type detection so it never silently masks errors, and add ILogger plus a single global IExceptionHandler/ProblemDetails so all ~167 controllers get consistent logging and generic error bodies instead of each reinventing try/catch.

1. Fix the real leak in Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs. Inject ILogger<AuthController> via the constructor (add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;`, add a `private readonly ILogger<AuthController> _logger;` field, accept it as a second ctor parameter and assign it). In the catch block at lines 39-42, log the exception with context (the username from `data`) and return a generic Problem result WITHOUT ex.Message.
2. Replace the AuthController catch body with: `_logger.LogError(ex, "Login failed for user {UserName}", data?.UserName); return Problem(detail: "An unexpected error occurred while processing the login request.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError);`. Confirm the property name on the API.Model.User type used for the username; if it is not `UserName`, adjust — and never log the password field.
3. Fix the swallowed exception in Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs lines 94-100. The verifier confirmed ex.Message is NOT returned here (it is put into `dict` which is never sent), so this is not a client leak — but it is silently swallowed. Inject ILogger<UploadController> (add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;`, add a field and ctor parameter alongside the existing IWebHostEnvironment) and replace the catch body with `_logger.LogError(ex, "Image upload failed for file {FileName}", filename);` before returning the existing `new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError)`. Remove the dead `dict.Add("error", res)` line that builds a response which is never returned.
4. Fix the bare catch in Backend/Model/APIResult.cs ApplyFilter (lines 235-248). ApiResult<T>.ApplyFilter is a `private static` method on a generic type, so injecting ILogger is awkward and unnecessary — instead remove the try/catch entirely and decide the operator upfront by inspecting the property type via reflection (the class already uses reflection in IsValidProperty). Only use `.Contains(@0)` when the target property is a string; otherwise use equality. This eliminates the silent fallback that could mask a genuinely broken filter as an equality match.
5. In ApplyFilter, replace the try/catch: `var prop = typeof(T).GetProperty(filterColumn, BindingFlags.IgnoreCase | BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance);` then `return prop?.PropertyType == typeof(string) ? source.Where(string.Format("{0}.Contains(@0)", filterColumn), filterQuery) : source.Where(string.Format("{0}==@0", filterColumn), filterQuery);`. filterColumn is already validated by the IsValidProperty guard at line 216 so it is safe to interpolate.
6. Add a single global exception handler so the remaining ~167 controllers stop needing per-method try/catch and never leak ex.Message. Create Backend/Infrastructure/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs implementing Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.IExceptionHandler (built into the Microsoft.AspNetCore.App framework in net8.0 — no extra package): inject ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler>, in TryHandleAsync call `_logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception for {Method} {Path}", httpContext.Request.Method, httpContext.Request.Path);`, set status 500 and write a generic ProblemDetails (Title "An unexpected error occurred.", no exception detail), return true.
7. Wire it up in Backend/Program.cs: after `builder.Services.AddControllers();` (line 22) add `builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();` and `builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<API.Infrastructure.GlobalExceptionHandler>();`. In the pipeline, add `app.UseExceptionHandler();` inside the non-Development branch at lines 104-107 (next to app.UseHsts()), keeping app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage() for Development so developers still see full detail locally.
8. Optional but recommended for the report-layer observability gap: WebApplication.CreateBuilder already adds Console/Debug logging by default, but add a Logging section to Backend/appsettings.json (e.g. `"Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Information", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning" } }`) so the new _logger calls surface at the intended level in production.

```csharp
// === Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs ===
// add: using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;
private readonly IJWTManagerService _jWTManager;
private readonly ILogger<AuthController> _logger;
public AuthController(IJWTManagerService jWTManager, ILogger<AuthController> logger)
{
    this._jWTManager = jWTManager;
    this._logger = logger;
}
// ...
// BEFORE:
catch (Exception ex)
{
    return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, ex.Message);
}
// AFTER:
catch (Exception ex)
{
    _logger.LogError(ex, "Login failed for user {UserName}", data?.UserName);
    return Problem(
        detail: "An unexpected error occurred while processing the login request.",
        statusCode: StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError);
}

// === Backend/Model/APIResult.cs  (ApplyFilter, lines 235-248) ===
// BEFORE:
try
{
    return source.Where(string.Format("{0}.Contains(@0)", filterColumn), filterQuery);
}
catch
{
    return source.Where(string.Format("{0}==@0", filterColumn), filterQuery);
}
// AFTER:
var prop = typeof(T).GetProperty(
    filterColumn,
    BindingFlags.IgnoreCase | BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance);
return prop?.PropertyType == typeof(string)
    ? source.Where(string.Format("{0}.Contains(@0)", filterColumn), filterQuery)
    : source.Where(string.Format("{0}==@0", filterColumn), filterQuery);

// === Backend/Infrastructure/GlobalExceptionHandler.cs (new) ===
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc;
using Microsoft.Extensions.Logging;

namespace API.Infrastructure;

public sealed class GlobalExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    private readonly ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> _logger;
    public GlobalExceptionHandler(ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> logger) => _logger = logger;

    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext httpContext, Exception exception, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        _logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception for {Method} {Path}",
            httpContext.Request.Method, httpContext.Request.Path);

        httpContext.Response.StatusCode = StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError;
        await httpContext.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError,
            Title = "An unexpected error occurred."
        }, ct);
        return true;
    }
}

// === Backend/Program.cs ===
builder.Services.AddControllers();
builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<API.Infrastructure.GlobalExceptionHandler>();
// ...
else
{
    app.UseExceptionHandler();
    app.UseHsts();
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: from repo root run `dotnet build "Backend/API.csproj"` and confirm it compiles (verifies the new ctor params, the IExceptionHandler signature, and the reflection-based ApplyFilter).
- Login leak gone: POST to /api/Auth with a payload that triggers an exception in JWTManagerService (e.g. force a DB/connection error). Confirm the 500 body is the generic ProblemDetails ('An unexpected error occurred...') with NO stack trace or SQL/exception text, and that a structured 'Login failed for user {UserName}' error line appears in the app logs/console.
- Global handler: in a non-Development environment, hit a report endpoint that throws (e.g. an invalid filterColumn that throws NotSupportedException from IsValidProperty) and confirm the client gets a generic ProblemDetails 500 plus an 'Unhandled exception for GET {path}' log entry — not the raw message.
- ApplyFilter behavior: call a paginated report endpoint with filterColumn = a numeric/date-like column and a non-matching value (equality path), then with a string column and a partial value (Contains path); confirm results are correct and no exception is silently swallowed. Compare row counts for a known string-column filter against pre-change behavior to confirm Contains still works.
- Logging coverage: grep the running console/container output to confirm log lines now originate from AuthController/UploadController/GlobalExceptionHandler, not only the two Service/ExcelExport workers.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Confirm the username property on API.Model.User before logging it — use the real property name and NEVER log the password. If unsure, log a fixed message instead of the object.; Order in Program.cs matters: AddProblemDetails() + AddExceptionHandler<>() must be registered before builder.Build(); UseExceptionHandler() must run early in the pipeline (before MapControllers) and only in the non-Development branch so it doesn't conflict with UseDeveloperExceptionPage, which should stay for local detail.; UploadController.Postupload returns HttpResponseMessage directly and swallows its own exception in the catch, so the global IExceptionHandler will NOT fire for it — that is why the per-method ILogger call there is still required.; ApplyFilter is generic/static: keep BindingFlags.IgnoreCase on GetProperty to preserve the current case-insensitive filterColumn tolerance (matching IsValidProperty). Nullable string columns are still typeof(string), so the Contains path is preserved for them.; Rollback is low-risk and isolated per change. If the global handler changes the response shape for clients that expected plain-text 500 bodies, remove only the UseExceptionHandler/AddExceptionHandler/AddProblemDetails lines while keeping the AuthController and ApplyFilter fixes.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Net: the underlying issues are real (genuine message leak on the unauthenticated login endpoint, bare catch in ApplyFilter, near-total absence of request-path logging, no global exception handler across ~167 controllers). Medium severity is appropriate for the aggregate observability/leak gap. Corrections: UploadController does NOT leak ex.Message to clients (message is added to a dict that is never returned); only AuthController is a true client-facing leak; controller count is 167 not 145; AccountSummaryReportController's ex.Message use is internal inspection, not a leak. Recommendation (ILogger injection, ProblemDetails instead of ex.Message, replace bare catch with typed/logged handling, global exception middleware) remains sound.


<a id="f-inconsistent-api-result-shapes"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Inconsistent API result shapes and return types across controllers

**ID:** `inconsistent-api-result-shapes` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** API consistency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs:43`
- `Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs:25`
- `Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs:22`
- `Backend/Controllers/ReportLookupsController.cs:43`
- `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:12`

**Problem.** Endpoints return wildly different envelopes: report controllers return ApiResult<T> (paged, with TotalCount/HasNextPage), ReportLookupsController returns bare List<T>/record, ChatController returns raw List<ChatModel>, AuthController returns Ok(TokenModel) or NotFound() with no body, and UploadController returns System.Net.Http.HttpResponseMessage (the WCF/HttpClient type, not an ASP.NET Core IActionResult) which serializes incorrectly through MVC. Error encoding is equally inconsistent (BadRequest(string) vs NotFound() vs StatusCode(500, ex.Message) vs a 'dict' that is built but never returned).

**Impact.** Frontend must special-case each endpoint; the HttpResponseMessage return in UploadController will not produce the intended status/body in ASP.NET Core, so its success/error signaling is effectively broken. Inconsistency increases bug surface and onboarding cost.

**Recommendation.** Standardize on ActionResult<T> / ProblemDetails for errors. Replace HttpResponseMessage with proper IActionResult (Ok/BadRequest/StatusCode). Consider a shared response envelope or rely consistently on ApiResult<T> for collections.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1-2 hours)_**

Fix the one genuine runtime defect first — UploadController.Postupload returns System.Net.Http.HttpResponseMessage, which ASP.NET Core MVC serializes as a JSON object with HTTP 200 (so failures never reach the frontend) and builds a `dict` it never returns. Convert it to a proper IActionResult (Ok/BadRequest/StatusCode) returning a small JSON envelope. Then standardize the remaining controllers on IActionResult/ActionResult<T> with ProblemDetails for errors so the frontend stops special-casing every endpoint.

1. PRIORITY 1 (functional bug): In Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs, change the action signature `public async Task<HttpResponseMessage> Postupload(String filename)` to `public async Task<IActionResult> Postupload(string filename)`.
2. Inside Postupload, replace every `return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.X)` with the MVC equivalent that carries the message you were stuffing into the unused `dict`: the validation branch at line 71 (bad extension) and line 79 (too large) -> `return BadRequest(new { error = message });`; the success branch at line 86 -> `return Ok(new { success = filename });`; the no-file fallthrough at line 92 -> `return BadRequest(new { error = "No file uploaded." });`; the catch at line 98 -> `return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, new { error = ex.Message });`. Delete the now-pointless `Dictionary<string,object> dict` and its `.Add(...)` calls since the messages are returned inline.
3. Remove the now-unused `using System.Net;` and `using System.Net.Http;` at the top of UploadController.cs (lines 5-6) and add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;` (for StatusCodes) — confirm it compiles with no HttpResponseMessage references left.
4. Note: the frontend at Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx (the antd Upload onChange handler, lines 198-217) keys off `info.file.status === 'done' | 'error'`, which antd derives from the real HTTP status. Once the controller returns real BadRequest/500 instead of an HTTP 200 wrapping an HttpResponseMessage body, the existing `Modal.error({ title: 'Fail' ... })` path will finally fire on rejected uploads — no frontend change required, but smoke-test it.
5. STANDARDIZE ERRORS (consistency): In Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs Login (line 25), the bare `return NotFound();` returns an empty body the frontend must special-case. Make failures structured: `return Problem(detail: "Invalid username or password.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status401Unauthorized);` (401 is more correct than 404 for a failed login) and in the catch (line 41) prefer `return Problem(detail: ex.Message, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError);` so all errors are ProblemDetails. Keep `return Ok(result)` for success.
6. In Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs Get (line 30), the error path already uses `BadRequest("Room parameter is required.")` (a bare string). For consistency change it to `return Problem(detail: "Room parameter is required.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status400BadRequest);` and add `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http;`. Note the GET success already returns `Ok(List<ChatModel>)` (an un-enveloped list, not a raw `List<ChatModel>` return type as the audit text loosely says) — leaving the list shape as-is is fine; only the error encoding needs unifying.
7. ReportLookupsController.cs already uses `ActionResult<T>` and `Ok(...)`/`NotFound()` correctly (lines 43, 89, 116) — its `NotFound()` for an unknown lookupName (line 74) is acceptable; no change required there beyond optionally returning `Problem(statusCode: 404)` if you want every error to be ProblemDetails. Treat as low priority.
8. OPTIONAL (broader consistency, do as a follow-up): decide on one collection contract. The report controllers already return `ApiResult<T>` (Backend/Model/APIResult.cs, with Data/TotalCount/HasNextPage). Either (a) leave non-paged lookups/chat as plain `Ok(list)` and document that paged endpoints use ApiResult<T>, or (b) wrap lookups in ApiResult<T> via `ApiResult<ReportLookupOption>.CreatePageFromRows(...)`. Do NOT migrate report endpoints — the frontend BasicTable already binds to ApiResult<T>.

```csharp
// Backend/Controllers/UploadController.cs  — PRIORITY 1 functional fix

// BEFORE
public async Task<HttpResponseMessage> Postupload(String filename)
{
    Dictionary<string, object> dict = new();
    try
    {
        var httpRequest = HttpContext.Request;
        var temp = HttpContext.Request.Query["file"];
        foreach (var file in httpRequest.Form.Files)
        {
            HttpResponseMessage response = new(HttpStatusCode.Created);
            var postedFile = httpRequest.Form.Files[0];
            if (postedFile != null && postedFile.Length > 0)
            {
                int MaxContentLength = 1024 * 500;
                IList<string> AllowedFileExtensions = new List<string> { ".webp", ".PDF", ".pdf", ".jpg", ".gif", ".png", ".jpeg", ".svg" };
                var ext = postedFile.FileName[postedFile.FileName.LastIndexOf('.')..];
                var extension = ext.ToLower();
                if (!AllowedFileExtensions.Contains(extension))
                {
                    var message = string.Format("Please Upload image of type .jpg,.gif,.png.");
                    dict.Add("error", message);
                    return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);
                }
                else if (postedFile.Length > MaxContentLength)
                {
                    var message = string.Format("Please Upload a file upto 1 mb.");
                    dict.Add("error", message);
                    return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);
                }
                else
                {
                    var filePath = Path.Combine("wwwroot", "Image", filename);
                    await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath);
                    dict.Add("success", filename);
                    return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.OK);
                }
            }
        }
        return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.BadRequest);
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        var res = string.Format(ex.Message.ToString());
        dict.Add("error", res);
        return new HttpResponseMessage(HttpStatusCode.InternalServerError);
    }
}

// AFTER
public async Task<IActionResult> Postupload(string filename)
{
    try
    {
        var httpRequest = HttpContext.Request;
        foreach (var file in httpRequest.Form.Files)
        {
            var postedFile = httpRequest.Form.Files[0];
            if (postedFile != null && postedFile.Length > 0)
            {
                int MaxContentLength = 1024 * 500; // 500 KB
                IList<string> AllowedFileExtensions = new List<string> { ".webp", ".PDF", ".pdf", ".jpg", ".gif", ".png", ".jpeg", ".svg" };
                var extension = postedFile.FileName[postedFile.FileName.LastIndexOf('.')..].ToLower();
                if (!AllowedFileExtensions.Contains(extension))
                {
                    return BadRequest(new { error = "Please Upload image of type .jpg,.gif,.png." });
                }
                if (postedFile.Length > MaxContentLength)
                {
                    return BadRequest(new { error = "Please Upload a file upto 1 mb." });
                }
                var filePath = Path.Combine("wwwroot", "Image", filename);
                await SaveAsWebpAsync(file, filePath);
                return Ok(new { success = filename });
            }
        }
        return BadRequest(new { error = "No file uploaded." });
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, new { error = ex.Message });
    }
}

// ---------------------------------------------------------------
// Backend/Controllers/AuthController.cs  — unify error encoding
// BEFORE
if (result != null) { return Ok(result); }
else { return NotFound(); }
// ...
catch (Exception ex) { return StatusCode(StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError, ex.Message); }

// AFTER
if (result != null) { return Ok(result); }
return Problem(detail: "Invalid username or password.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status401Unauthorized);
// ...
catch (Exception ex) { return Problem(detail: ex.Message, statusCode: StatusCodes.Status500InternalServerError); }

// ---------------------------------------------------------------
// Backend/Controllers/ChatController.cs  — unify error encoding
// BEFORE
return BadRequest("Room parameter is required.");
// AFTER
return Problem(detail: "Room parameter is required.", statusCode: StatusCodes.Status400BadRequest);
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` from the Backend project dir — must compile with no `HttpResponseMessage` / `System.Net.Http` references remaining in UploadController.cs.
- Reject path (the real bug): POST a >500KB image (or a .exe) to `POST /api/Upload/Postupload?filename=test.png` with multipart form-data. BEFORE the fix you get HTTP 200 with a serialized HttpResponseMessage JSON blob (so antd shows success); AFTER the fix you get HTTP 400 with body `{ "error": "..." }`. Example: `curl -i -F "file=@big.png" "http://localhost:5000/api/Upload/Postupload?filename=big.png"` and confirm the status line is `HTTP/1.1 400`.
- Success path: POST a small valid .png and confirm HTTP 200 `{ "success": "<filename>" }` and the .webp lands in wwwroot/Image.
- In the running app, open the Chat box (Frontend/src/components/Chat/ChatBox.tsx), upload an oversized image, and confirm the antd `Modal.error({ title: 'Fail' })` now appears (it previously never fired because every response was HTTP 200).
- Auth: POST bad credentials to `POST /api/Auth` and confirm HTTP 401 with a ProblemDetails JSON body instead of an empty 404.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do NOT migrate the report endpoints off ApiResult<T> — the React BasicTable binds to Data/TotalCount/HasNextPage and changing that shape breaks every report grid. The AuthController status-code change from 404 to 401 is more correct but is a behavior change; check that the frontend login error handler in Frontend/src (axios interceptor / login thunk) treats 401 the same as the old 404 (i.e. shows 'invalid credentials' rather than redirect-on-401 logout looping) before shipping — if a global 401 interceptor force-logs-out, keep the login failure as a non-401 (e.g. 400) or exempt the /api/Auth route. The Upload reject path was effectively broken before (always 200), so making it return real 4xx/5xx is the intended behavior change, not a regression.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Medium is defensible. The genuinely functional defect is isolated to one image-upload endpoint (HttpResponseMessage return + unreturned dict), not a core report path; the broader "consistency" concern is real but mostly a maintainability/onboarding cost rather than a runtime bug. The recommendation (replace HttpResponseMessage with proper IActionResult, standardize on ActionResult<T>/ProblemDetails) is sound. Locations are accurate; only the ChatController body-vs-signature phrasing should be tightened (it returns Ok(List<ChatModel>), an un-enveloped list, not a raw List<ChatModel> return type).


<a id="f-bysection-cross-join-in-memory"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — ImportLicenceBySectionReportController materializes all rows then nested-loops sections x currencies in memory

**ID:** `bysection-cross-join-in-memory` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Performance / Code smell &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:99-163`

**Problem.** BuildRowsAsync runs the licence query with a trailing .ToList() (materializing every matching detail row), then loops over every Section x every Currency and re-filters/re-sums the in-memory list per cell (LicenceListQry.Where(...).Count() and .Sum() inside a double foreach). This is O(sections * currencies * rows). Notably, the project already has a fully-in-SQL GROUP BY implementation for exactly this (sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.SectionGroups / CreateSectionPagedResultAsync) which the Excel path of this same controller uses (GetSectionRowsAsync) — but the interactive Post path does not, so the two paths can produce different numbers and the slow one is the user-facing one.

**Impact.** On large date ranges this loads the entire detail set into web-server memory and does quadratic work, risking slow responses / memory pressure, and creates a UI-vs-Excel discrepancy (a parity bug class the project is actively fighting per CLAUDE.md).

**Recommendation.** Have Post delegate to the existing SQL-grouped sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateSectionPagedResultAsync (as the Excel path already effectively does), deleting BuildRowsAsync and the in-memory cross join. This also removes the bespoke 5-minute IMemoryCache layer unique to this controller.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Replace the in-memory cross-join in ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.Post with the existing SQL-grouped aggregation, exactly mirroring the sibling BorderExportLicenceBySectionReportController: delegate to sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateAggregateResultAsync(..., ReportAggregateDimension.Section, includeSakhan: false, includeColumnTotals: true). This makes the interactive path group/page in SQL, deletes the quadratic BuildRowsAsync and the bespoke 5-minute IMemoryCache, and guarantees the UI matches the Excel path (both now run through the same SectionGroups SQL).

1. Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs.
2. Rewrite the [HttpPost] Post method (lines 41-97) so that after the TryCreateReportRequest guard it returns: `var result = await sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateAggregateResultAsync(_context, procedureRequest!, request!, ReportAggregateDimension.Section, includeSakhan: false, includeColumnTotals: true); return Ok(result);` — identical to BorderExportLicenceBySectionReportController.Post (lines 43-47). includeSakhan must be false here because this is the Oversea report (the source TryCreateReportRequest sets Type = "Oversea" and the legacy RDLC has no Sakhan column).
3. Delete the entire BuildRowsAsync method (lines 99-163), including the in-memory `.ToList()` query, the CurrencyList/SectionList loads, and the `foreach (section) foreach (currency)` cross join. CreateAggregateResultAsync -> AggregateInSqlAsync already performs the per-(Section,Currency) GROUP BY in SQL and only emits non-empty groups, so the old `if (cell.NoOfLicences > 0 || cell.TotalValue > 0)` filter is preserved by construction.
4. Remove the now-unused IMemoryCache field and caching: delete `private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;` (line 28), the `private static readonly TimeSpan RowsCacheDuration = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5);` constant (line 32), the `_cache` assignment in the constructor (line 37), and drop `IMemoryCache cache` from the constructor parameter list (line 34). Also delete the now-unused `New`/`Approved` constants (lines 30-31) since the filter constants now live in the _Fast class.
