5. Remove now-unused usings at the top of the file: `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;` (line 15) and `using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;` (line 16). Keep `using API.Service.Reports;` (it provides ReportAggregateDimension) and `using API.StoredProcedureToLinq;`. The local `ImportLicenceBySection` cells model is no longer referenced from this controller — leave that type definition wherever it is declared unless it has no other references; do not delete it blindly.
6. Leave the existing TryCreateReportRequest, Excel, and WriteRowsAsync members untouched — WriteRowsAsync already uses sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetSectionRowsAsync (backed by the same SectionGroups SQL), so after this change the interactive and Excel paths share one grouping definition.
7. Build the backend (`dotnet build` in /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend) and fix any leftover references to the removed _cache/BuildRowsAsync.

```csharp
// ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs

// constructor — BEFORE:
public ImportLicenceBySectionReportController(TradeNetDbContext context, IMemoryCache cache, IExcelExportJobService excelExportJobs)
{
    _context = context;
    _cache = cache;
    _excelExportJobs = excelExportJobs;
}
// AFTER:
public ImportLicenceBySectionReportController(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService excelExportJobs)
{
    _context = context;
    _excelExportJobs = excelExportJobs;
}

// Post — BEFORE: builds cacheKey, _cache.GetOrCreateAsync(BuildRowsAsync), manual paging + ColumnTotals (lines 41-97)
// AFTER:
[HttpPost]
public async Task<ActionResult<ApiResult<ReportAggregateResult>>> Post([FromBody] ImportLicenceBySectionReportRequest? request)
{
    if (!TryCreateReportRequest(request, out var procedureRequest, out var errorResult))
    {
        return errorResult!;
    }

    var result = await sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateAggregateResultAsync(
        _context, procedureRequest!, request!, ReportAggregateDimension.Section, includeSakhan: false,
        includeColumnTotals: true);

    return Ok(result);
}

// DELETE entirely: private async Task<List<ReportAggregateResult>> BuildRowsAsync(...) { ... }  (lines 99-163)
// DELETE fields/consts: _cache, RowsCacheDuration, New, Approved
// DELETE usings: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore, Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build the Backend project — it must compile with the IMemoryCache field, RractoCacheDuration, BuildRowsAsync, and the two usings removed (no CS0103/unused-symbol errors).
- POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport with a wide date range and a known PaThaKaTypeId/section filter. Confirm: (a) the rows array is one page of Section+Currency groups ordered by section then currency, (b) result.columnTotals.noOfLicences and result.columnTotals.totalValue are present and equal the grand totals, (c) only non-empty (Section,Currency) combinations appear.
- Parity check: compare the same request's interactive response totals/rows against the Excel export (POST /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport/Excel via the queue). They must now match exactly, since both run through sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast SectionGroups.
- Capture the generated SQL (enable EF Core query logging) for the Post request and confirm it contains a GROUP BY on Section/Currency with OFFSET/FETCH paging — no full detail set is materialized in app memory.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Pass includeSakhan: false — this is the Oversea Import Licence By Section report (TryCreateReportRequest hardcodes Type = \"Oversea\"); passing true would add a Sakhan group key and change the row counts vs the legacy RDLC. The ColumnTotals keys produced by ReportAggregationService.BuildColumnTotals are exactly noOfLicences and totalValue (same as the old manual code), so the frontend footer needs no change. Do NOT switch to CreateSectionPagedResultAsync — although it exists and uses the same SectionGroups SQL, it has zero callers and does not emit ColumnTotals, so the TOTAL footer row would disappear; the verifier confirmed CreateAggregateResultAsync(..., Section, ..., includeColumnTotals: true) is the correct precedent. Removing the IMemoryCache means each Post hits SQL directly; this is fine because the work is now a single grouped/paged query, but if a stress test shows hot repeated identical requests you can re-add caching at the ApiResult level later. NoOfLicences in the SQL path counts DISTINCT non-empty licence numbers (group.Select(...Distinct().Count())), whereas the old in-memory code used a plain .Count() of item rows; this is the intended/correct count and matches the RDLC and the Excel path, but it can legitimately differ from the old buggy number — treat that as the fix, not a regression."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** One minor imprecision in the recommendation: it says Post should delegate to CreateSectionPagedResultAsync "as the Excel path already effectively does." In fact CreateSectionPagedResultAsync has zero callers in the codebase; the Excel path uses GetSectionRowsAsync. Both are backed by the same SQL-grouped SectionGroups, so the substance (an SQL-grouped impl already exists and the Excel path uses it) is correct — only the exact method name cited is slightly off. The closest existing precedent for the interactive Post path is the sibling Border controller, which uses CreateAggregateResultAsync(..., ReportAggregateDimension.Section, ...). Location and severity are accurate as given.


<a id="f-report-controller-copypaste-sprawl"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — ~145 report controllers are near-identical copy-paste (157 duplicated TryCreateReportRequest blocks)

**ID:** `report-controller-copypaste-sprawl` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Maintainability / Duplication &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Large &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ (158 controllers; cited ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:94-140 and the ExportPermitAmendment vs BorderExportLicenceAmendment pair all verified)`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:94-140`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs:201-247`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ExportPermitAmendmentReportController.cs`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportController.cs`

**Problem.** There are ~145 controllers under Controllers/Report, almost all 150-200 lines, each repeating the same skeleton: private const ReportKey, the same ctor injecting (TradeNetDbContext, [cache], IExcelExportJobService), an identical Post/Excel pair, the IStreamingExcelReport members, and a private TryCreateReportRequest validator. A diff of two sibling controllers (ExportPermitAmendment vs BorderExportLicenceAmendment) shows they differ ONLY in class/type names, the ReportKey/title literals, and a single FormType string. grep finds 157 copies of `private bool TryCreateReportRequest` and 157 controllers implementing IStreamingExcelReport.

**Impact.** Every cross-cutting change (e.g. adding a new validation rule, an audit field, a date-range cap, or a uniform error shape) must be applied ~145 times by hand, which is exactly how the inconsistencies in this report arose. The CLAUDE.md memory even documents a 'roll out the remaining ~155 report controllers' chore — the duplication is institutionalized.

**Recommendation.** Introduce a generic base (e.g. ReportControllerBase<TRequest, TProcRequest, TResult>) or a shared request-validation/mapping helper that centralizes the date validation, paging extraction, Excel enqueue, and IStreamingExcelReport plumbing. Per-report controllers should declare only ReportKey, title, and the request->procedureRequest projection. This collapses ~25k lines of controller code by a large factor.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2-3 days (base class + Amendment proof-of-concept pair in ~half a day; the remaining ~155 controllers migrated in batches with per-batch build/verify make up the rest))_**

Introduce one abstract generic base, ReportControllerBase<TRequest, TProcRequest>, that owns the duplicated skeleton (the Excel action, IStreamingExcelReport plumbing, shared FromDate/ToDate validation, paging clamp, and Excel-enqueue) and require each report to supply only ReportKey, ExcelWorksheetTitle, a MapProcedureRequest projection, and the report-specific data-fetch/streaming. Migrate controllers incrementally (Amendment-style first, then the country-cache style), preserving each report's own paging/projection so behavior stays byte-for-byte identical.

1. Create a new file Backend/Controllers/Report/ReportControllerBase.cs declaring `public abstract class ReportControllerBase<TRequest, TProcRequest> : ControllerBase, IStreamingExcelReport where TRequest : ReportQueryRequest where TProcRequest : class`. Keep it in namespace Backend.Controllers.Report so it sits with the controllers and is covered by the same assembly scan.
2. In the base, add the abstract members each report must supply: `protected abstract string ReportKey { get; }`, `public abstract string ExcelWorksheetTitle { get; }`, `protected abstract TProcRequest MapProcedureRequest(TRequest request);` (the per-report projection + FormType/Type literal — this is the BorderExportLicence-vs-ExportPermit `FormType` and the ImportLicenceDetail `Type` null-coalesce / BySection hardcoded `"Oversea"` variation point), and `protected abstract Task FetchRowsAsync(TProcRequest procedureRequest, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken ct);` for the streaming body.
3. Move the identical validator into the base as `protected bool TryCreateReportRequest(TRequest? request, out TProcRequest? procedureRequest, out ActionResult? errorResult)` — copy verbatim the null check, FromDate/ToDate `== default` checks and the `ToDate < FromDate` check from ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:102-124, then call `procedureRequest = MapProcedureRequest(request)` in place of the inline `new sp_...Request { ... }` block. Because TRequest:ReportQueryRequest does not declare FromDate/ToDate (each concrete DTO redeclares them), add `protected abstract DateTime GetFromDate(TRequest r);` and `protected abstract DateTime GetToDate(TRequest r);` so the base can validate dates without reflection (cheapest, zero-migration option).
4. Implement IStreamingExcelReport plumbing once in the base: `public Type ExcelRequestType => typeof(TRequest);` and `[NonAction] public Task WriteRowsAsync(object request, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken ct) { TryCreateReportRequest((TRequest)request, out var pr, out _); return FetchRowsAsync(pr!, sink, chunkSize, ct); }` — this replaces the duplicated `[NonAction] WriteRowsAsync` shim + private overload pair in all 157 controllers.
5. Provide a concrete base Excel action so reports don't repeat it: `[HttpPost("Excel")] public async Task<IActionResult> Excel([FromBody] TRequest? request) { if (!TryCreateReportRequest(request, out _, out var err)) return err!; var result = await _excelExportJobs.EnqueueAsync(ReportKey, request!, GetToDate(request!), User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value); return Ok(result); }`. Inject TradeNetDbContext and IExcelExportJobService via a protected base ctor (`protected ReportControllerBase(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService jobs)`); concrete controllers that also need ICountryCache (e.g. ImportLicenceDetailReportController) add it to their own ctor and pass context/jobs to `base(...)`.
6. Add a shared paging helper `protected (int pageIndex, int pageSize, string? sortColumn, string? sortOrder) ResolvePaging(TRequest request)` that centralizes the DefaultPageSize=10 / MaxPageSize=1000 clamp and the SortColumn/SortOrder whitespace-to-null normalization (the block at ExportPermitAmendmentReportController.cs:48-54). Do NOT push the `sp_AmendReport.ExecuteAsync(...).ToResult()` projection or the country-cache `CreatePagedResultAsync` call into the base — those are report-family specific and stay in the concrete `[HttpPost] Post`.
7. Migrate the ExportPermit/BorderExport Amendment pair first as the proof-of-concept: each concrete controller shrinks to ReportKey, ExcelWorksheetTitle, GetFromDate/GetToDate, MapProcedureRequest (only the `FormType = "Export Permit"` vs `"Border Export Licence"` literal differs), a report-specific `[HttpPost] Post` using ResolvePaging + the existing ApiResult.CreatePageFromRows/CreateFastPageFromRows branch, and FetchRowsAsync wrapping the existing `sp_AmendReport.ExecuteQueryable(...).AsAsyncEnumerable().ChunkAsync(...)` loop. Confirm the diff between the two migrated controllers is now ONLY names + ReportKey + title + FormType.
8. Migrate the country-cache style (ImportLicenceDetailReportController) next: its FetchRowsAsync calls `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.StreamResolvedChunksAsync(_context, _countryCache, pr!, chunkSize, ct)` and its Post calls `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync(_context, _countryCache, pr!, request!)`. Keep MapProcedureRequest with the `Type = string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(request.Type) ? "Oversea" : request.Type` default; ImportLicenceBySectionReportController keeps its hardcoded `Type = "Oversea"` in its own MapProcedureRequest override. These per-report differences must remain in the overrides, not be flattened.
9. Roll out the remaining ~155 controllers in small batches grouped by data-fetch family (_Fast country-cache reports, sp_AmendReport reports, plain paged reports), rebuilding after each batch. This is the same chore tracked in the CLAUDE.md 'roll out the remaining ~155 report controllers' memory — reuse that batching cadence.
10. Verify auto-discovery still works without code changes: ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.RegisterReportHandlers (Backend/Service/ExcelExport/ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.cs:36-39) scans `t is { IsClass: true, IsAbstract: false } && typeof(IStreamingExcelReport).IsAssignableFrom(t)`. The new generic ReportControllerBase<,> is abstract so it is excluded, and concrete controllers stay non-abstract and assignable. ControllerStreamingExcelReportJobHandler.cs:39 uses ActivatorUtilities.CreateInstance(context.Services, _controllerType) on the concrete type, which still resolves ctor deps (context, jobs, and optionally countryCache).

```csharp
// NEW FILE: Backend/Controllers/Report/ReportControllerBase.cs
using System;
using System.Security.Claims;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;
using API.DBContext;
using API.Model;
using API.Service.ExcelExport;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authorization;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc;

namespace Backend.Controllers.Report
{
    [Authorize]
    [ApiController]
    [Route("api/[controller]")]
    public abstract class ReportControllerBase<TRequest, TProcRequest> : ControllerBase, IStreamingExcelReport
        where TRequest : ReportQueryRequest
        where TProcRequest : class
    {
        protected const int DefaultPageSize = 10;
        protected const int MaxPageSize = 1000;

        protected readonly TradeNetDbContext _context;
        protected readonly IExcelExportJobService _excelExportJobs;

        protected ReportControllerBase(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService excelExportJobs)
        {
            _context = context;
            _excelExportJobs = excelExportJobs;
        }

        // ---- the ONLY things a concrete report must supply ----
        protected abstract string ReportKey { get; }
        public abstract string ExcelWorksheetTitle { get; }
        protected abstract TProcRequest MapProcedureRequest(TRequest request); // FormType/Type literal lives here
        protected abstract DateTime GetFromDate(TRequest request);
        protected abstract DateTime GetToDate(TRequest request);
        protected abstract Task FetchRowsAsync(
            TProcRequest procedureRequest, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken cancellationToken);

        // ---- shared validation (was duplicated 157x, verbatim from ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:102-124) ----
        protected bool TryCreateReportRequest(TRequest? request, out TProcRequest? procedureRequest, out ActionResult? errorResult)
        {
            procedureRequest = null;
            errorResult = null;
            if (request == null) { errorResult = BadRequest("Request body is required."); return false; }
            if (GetFromDate(request) == default) { errorResult = BadRequest("FromDate is required."); return false; }
            if (GetToDate(request) == default) { errorResult = BadRequest("ToDate is required."); return false; }
            if (GetToDate(request) < GetFromDate(request))
            { errorResult = BadRequest("ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate."); return false; }
            procedureRequest = MapProcedureRequest(request);
            return true;
        }

        protected (int pageIndex, int pageSize, string? sortColumn, string? sortOrder) ResolvePaging(TRequest request)
            => (Math.Max(0, request.PageIndex),
                request.PageSize <= 0 ? DefaultPageSize : Math.Min(request.PageSize, MaxPageSize),
                string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(request.SortColumn) ? null : request.SortColumn,
                string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(request.SortOrder) ? null : request.SortOrder);

        // ---- shared Excel enqueue (was duplicated 157x) ----
        [HttpPost("Excel")]
        public async Task<IActionResult> Excel([FromBody] TRequest? request)
        {
            if (!TryCreateReportRequest(request, out _, out var errorResult)) return errorResult!;
            var result = await _excelExportJobs.EnqueueAsync(
                ReportKey, request!, GetToDate(request!), User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value);
            return Ok(result);
        }

        // ---- shared IStreamingExcelReport plumbing ----
        public Type ExcelRequestType => typeof(TRequest);

        [NonAction]
        public Task WriteRowsAsync(object request, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken cancellationToken)
        {
            TryCreateReportRequest((TRequest)request, out var procedureRequest, out _);
            return FetchRowsAsync(procedureRequest!, sink, chunkSize, cancellationToken);
        }
    }
}

// AFTER: BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportController.cs collapses from ~167 lines to ~40:
public sealed class BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportController
    : ReportControllerBase<BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportRequest, sp_AmendReportRequest>
{
    public BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportController(TradeNetDbContext context, IExcelExportJobService jobs)
        : base(context, jobs) { }

    protected override string ReportKey => "BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReport";
    public override string ExcelWorksheetTitle => "Border Export Licence Amendment Report";
    protected override DateTime GetFromDate(BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportRequest r) => r.FromDate;
    protected override DateTime GetToDate(BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportRequest r) => r.ToDate;

    protected override sp_AmendReportRequest MapProcedureRequest(BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportRequest r)
        => new()
        {
            FormType = "Border Export Licence",   // <-- the ONLY semantic diff vs ExportPermitAmendment
            FromDate = r.FromDate, ToDate = r.ToDate,
            ExportImportSectionId = r.ExportImportSectionId, AmendRemarkId = r.AmendRemarkId,
            CompanyRegistrationNo = r.CompanyRegistrationNo, SakhanId = r.SakhanId,
        };

    [HttpPost]
    public async Task<ActionResult<ApiResult<sp_AmendReportResult>>> Post([FromBody] BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReportRequest? request)
    {
        if (!TryCreateReportRequest(request, out var pr, out var err)) return err!;
        var (pageIndex, pageSize, sortColumn, sortOrder) = ResolvePaging(request!);
        var rows = await sp_AmendReport.ExecuteAsync(_context, pr!, sortColumn, sortOrder, pageIndex, pageSize, request!.IncludeTotalCount);
        var data = rows.Select(row => row.ToResult()).ToList();
        var result = request.IncludeTotalCount
            ? ApiResult<sp_AmendReportResult>.CreatePageFromRows(data, rows.Count > 0 ? (rows[0].TotalCount ?? 0) : 0, pageIndex, pageSize, request.SortColumn, request.SortOrder, request.FilterColumn, request.FilterQuery)
            : ApiResult<sp_AmendReportResult>.CreateFastPageFromRows(data, pageIndex, pageSize, request.SortColumn, request.SortOrder, request.FilterColumn, request.FilterQuery);
        return Ok(result);
    }

    protected override async Task FetchRowsAsync(sp_AmendReportRequest pr, IExcelRowSink sink, int chunkSize, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        await foreach (var chunk in sp_AmendReport.ExecuteQueryable(_context, pr).AsAsyncEnumerable().ChunkAsync(chunkSize, ct))
            sink.Append(chunk.Select(row => row.ToResult()).ToList());
    }
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build the Backend project: run `dotnet build` from the Backend folder — it must compile with zero new errors after each migration batch.
- Functional parity (response shape unchanged): POST api/BorderExportLicenceAmendmentReport and api/ExportPermitAmendmentReport with a valid FromDate/ToDate body before and after migration and diff the JSON — identical rows/paging/total-count. Repeat for api/ImportLicenceDetailReport (country-cache path) to confirm ConsignedCountry/CountryofOrigin still resolve.
- Validation parity: POST each migrated endpoint with (a) empty body -> 400 'Request body is required.', (b) missing FromDate -> 400 'FromDate is required.', (c) ToDate < FromDate -> 400 'ToDate must be greater than or equal to FromDate.'
- Excel queue still discovers handlers: at startup verify one IExcelReportJobHandler is registered per migrated controller (log/breakpoint in ExcelExportServiceCollectionExtensions.RegisterReportHandlers, or assert the registered handler count equals the IStreamingExcelReport controller count). Then POST api/<Report>/Excel and confirm an EnqueueResult (Queued/Ready/Processing) returns and the background worker produces the file.
- Confirm the base class is excluded from scanning: assert `typeof(ReportControllerBase<,>).IsAbstract` is true so RegisterReportHandlers never instantiates it.
- Diff sanity: after migrating the Amendment pair, `git diff` the two controllers — the only differences should be class/request names, ReportKey, ExcelWorksheetTitle, and the FormType literal.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do this incrementally and rebuild per batch — it touches ~155 files; a single bad base signature breaks every controller at once. Preserve per-report variations the verifier flagged: ImportLicenceDetailReportController's `Type = IsNullOrWhiteSpace ? \"Oversea\" : request.Type` default vs ImportLicenceBySectionReportController's hardcoded `Type = \"Oversea\"`, and the country-cache paging path (sp_..._Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync / StreamResolvedChunksAsync taking _countryCache) vs the inline sp_AmendReport.ExecuteAsync + ToResult() path — keep these in the concrete overrides, do not flatten into the base. The base reads FromDate/ToDate, which are NOT on ReportQueryRequest (each DTO redeclares them); use the GetFromDate/GetToDate abstracts shown (zero migration), OR promote FromDate/ToDate onto ReportQueryRequest only if you also remove the shadowing declarations from all DTOs (riskier, do not mix). Keep the [HttpPost(\"Excel\")] and [NonAction] WriteRowsAsync on the BASE only — leaving stale copies on a concrete class double-registers the Excel route. ActivatorUtilities.CreateInstance (ControllerStreamingExcelReportJobHandler.cs:39) resolves the concrete ctor, so any controller adding ICountryCache must keep it in its own ctor signature and pass context/jobs to base(...). Migration is purely mechanical with no DB/schema change, so rollback is per-file `git revert`. CardListsByCompanyRegistrationNumberController does not implement IStreamingExcelReport and is intentionally out of scope.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** The finding's count "~145" actually undercounts: there are 158 controllers / 157 duplicated validator+IStreamingExcelReport copies. That strengthens the finding. I downgraded severity from High to Medium: this is real, large-scale, accurately-described duplication, but it is mechanical low-risk boilerplate (validation + paging + Excel-enqueue plumbing) with no correctness or security defect. The recommendation (generic ReportControllerBase / shared validation-mapping helper) is sound. One nuance for any refactor: controllers are not 100% uniform — e.g. ImportLicenceDetailReport injects ICountryCache and uses a country-resolving paged path, while the Amendment controllers do their own paging inline with DefaultPageSize/MaxPageSize/MaxExcelDataRows constants and a ToResult() projection, and the BySection validator hardcodes Type="Oversea" vs the Detail controller's null-coalescing default. A base class would need to parameterize these variations, so the "collapses by a large factor" claim is directionally right but the per-report projection/paging differences are real and must be preserved.


<a id="f-no-dto-validation-attributes"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Report request DTOs rely on hand-rolled per-controller checks instead of validation attributes

**ID:** `no-dto-validation-attributes` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Input validation &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Model/ReportQueryRequest.cs:1-13`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:143-155`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs:108-124`

**Problem.** ReportQueryRequest and the ~145 per-report request classes carry no DataAnnotations ([Required], [Range], etc.). PageSize/PageIndex are unbounded ints on the request (the MaxPageSize=1000 clamp lives only inside some _Fast SP files, not all paths — e.g. ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.Post uses request.PageSize directly with no upper bound). Date validation is duplicated as imperative if-blocks in every TryCreateReportRequest. Because [ApiController] is present, attribute validation would be enforced automatically, but it is unused.

**Impact.** An oversized PageSize on the uncapped paths can pull large result sets into memory; validation logic is duplicated 145x and can drift between reports (a maintainability and DoS-surface concern rather than an exploit).

**Recommendation.** Add [Range] on PageSize (e.g. 1-1000) and [Required] where appropriate to the shared base request, and let [ApiController] auto-return 400s. Apply the page-size clamp uniformly (centralize in ApiResult/base controller) so no report path is uncapped.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Add DataAnnotations to the shared base DTO (ReportQueryRequest) so [ApiController] auto-returns 400s for bad PageSize/PageIndex, and harden the single shared clamp point (ApiResult.NormalizePageSize) to cap at MaxPageSize=1000 so the one uncapped path (ImportLicenceBySectionReportController) is covered without touching ~157 controllers. Date-range validation stays as-is (per-report DTOs have report-specific FromDate/ToDate and live below the base class).

1. Open Backend/Model/ReportQueryRequest.cs and add validation attributes to the shared base DTO. Add `using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;` at the top. Put `[Range(1, 1000, ErrorMessage = "PageSize must be between 1 and 1000.")]` on `PageSize` and `[Range(0, int.MaxValue, ErrorMessage = "PageIndex must be zero or greater.")]` on `PageIndex`. Keep `PageSize`'s default of 10 (= 10). Because every report request class (e.g. ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest, line 143) derives from ReportQueryRequest and every controller has [ApiController], invalid values now auto-return ProblemDetails 400 before the action body runs.
2. Centralize the upper-bound clamp so no report path can exceed it as a defense-in-depth backstop (attribute validation only fires if the client actually sends an out-of-range value through the normal binder; the clamp guarantees the cap regardless). In Backend/Model/APIResult.cs change `NormalizePageSize` (lines 204-207) from `return pageSize <= 0 ? 10 : pageSize;` to clamp to a new `MaxPageSize` constant of 1000, mirroring the constants already in ReportQueryService.cs (lines 10-11) and the _Fast SP files (e.g. sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs lines 22-23). Add private const fields `DefaultPageSize = 10` and `MaxPageSize = 1000` to ApiResult<T> and use them in NormalizePageSize. This closes the ImportLicenceBySectionReportController path, which calls ApiResult<ReportAggregateResult>.CreatePageFromRows (line 77) -> NormalizePageSize.
3. Fix the one controller that computes pageSize inline before paging, so its in-memory `Take(pageSize)` slice is also capped. In Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs line 71, change `var pageSize = request.PageSize <= 0 ? 10 : request.PageSize;` to also bound the upper end: `var pageSize = request.PageSize <= 0 ? 10 : Math.Min(request.PageSize, 1000);`. (CreatePageFromRows now also clamps, but this avoids materializing an oversized page slice before the call.)
4. Do NOT remove the existing per-controller `TryCreateReportRequest` date guards (e.g. ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs lines 108-124). They validate report-specific FromDate/ToDate/cross-field rules (ToDate >= FromDate) that the base ReportQueryRequest does not own; converting those to attributes is a separate, larger refactor and out of scope for this Low finding. The attribute work here is limited to the shared paging fields.
5. Build the backend to confirm no compile errors: run `dotnet build` from the Backend project directory.

```csharp
// Backend/Model/ReportQueryRequest.cs
using System.ComponentModel.DataAnnotations;

namespace API.Model
{
    public class ReportQueryRequest
    {
        [Range(0, int.MaxValue, ErrorMessage = "PageIndex must be zero or greater.")]
        public int PageIndex { get; set; }

        [Range(1, 1000, ErrorMessage = "PageSize must be between 1 and 1000.")]
        public int PageSize { get; set; } = 10;

        public string? SortColumn { get; set; }
        public string? SortOrder { get; set; }
        public string? FilterColumn { get; set; }
        public string? FilterQuery { get; set; }
        public bool IncludeTotalCount { get; set; }
    }
}

// Backend/Model/APIResult.cs  (inside ApiResult<T>)
private const int DefaultPageSize = 10;
private const int MaxPageSize = 1000;

// before:
private static int NormalizePageSize(int pageSize)
{
    return pageSize <= 0 ? 10 : pageSize;
}
// after:
private static int NormalizePageSize(int pageSize)
{
    return pageSize <= 0 ? DefaultPageSize : Math.Min(pageSize, MaxPageSize);
}

// Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceBySectionReportController.cs  (line 71)
// before:
var pageSize = request.PageSize <= 0 ? 10 : request.PageSize;
// after:
var pageSize = request.PageSize <= 0 ? 10 : Math.Min(request.PageSize, 1000);
```

**Verify:**
- POST to any report endpoint (e.g. /api/ImportLicenceDetailReport) with a valid JWT and a body containing `"pageSize": 999999`. With the attribute in place [ApiController] returns HTTP 400 with a ProblemDetails body whose errors include the PageSize range message; previously it returned 200.
- POST the same with `"pageSize": -5` or `"pageIndex": -1` and confirm 400.
- POST with a valid body (`"pageSize": 50`) and confirm a normal 200 with at most 50 rows, proving valid requests are unaffected and the default of 10 still applies when pageSize is omitted.
- Defense-in-depth check on the previously-uncapped path: POST to /api/ImportLicenceBySectionReport. Even if a request reaches NormalizePageSize/CreatePageFromRows with a large pageSize, the returned ApiResult.PageSize is now <= 1000 and Data length is capped at 1000.
- Run `dotnet build` in the Backend project and confirm it compiles with no errors.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** PageSize keeps its default of 10, and [Range(1,1000)] permits 10, so omitting pageSize still binds to the default and validates fine — do not set the Range minimum above the default. Apply the NormalizePageSize clamp (step 2) and the controller clamp (step 3) regardless of the attribute work: attribute validation only rejects values that actually arrive through the action's model binding, while the clamp is the backstop that guarantees the 1000 cap for every code path (and matches the existing MaxPageSize=1000 already used in ReportQueryService.cs and the _Fast SP files, so values stay consistent). Per the verifier note, the ImportLicenceBySection rows are already materialized/cached in BuildRowsAsync before paging, so this mitigates forced serialization of an oversized page slice, not a DB-side large read — keep expectations framed that way. Do not delete the per-controller date guards; they cover report-specific fields the base DTO doesn't have. Rollback is trivial: revert the three files; no data migration or config change is involved.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Counts were ~157 (not "~145") request classes / ~158 controllers — the finding's "145x" is a slight under-count but in the same ballpark, not material. The DoS framing should say "force serialization of an already-materialized result set" rather than "pull large result sets into memory from the DB" for the ImportLicenceBySection path specifically; the finding already hedges this as non-exploit. No location or severity change needed.


---

### Backend — Performance & Scalability

> The report stack has clearly been reworked for scalability and the "happy paths" are mostly sound: the `_Fast` detail reports use AsNoTracking + DTO projection, push GROUP BY/ORDER BY/OFFSET into SQL, resolve country/lookup CSVs from an in-memory cache (avoiding per-row correlated subqueries), and the async Excel queue streams rows to disk in chunks with a bounded StreamingExcelWriter. The most impactful remaining issues are: (1) several generic detail reports paginate with OFFSET/FETCH but no stable ORDER BY, so pages silently drop or duplicate rows; (2) the "fast" paging path re-runs a separate full COUNT query joining the item fan-out on every page when IncludeTotalCount is true, and the aggregate/Daily reports re-execute the entire SQL GROUP BY (plus a second FX query for Daily) on every page request; (3) the Border `_Fast` paths order a UNION ALL of two heavy multi-join queries by a non-unique CreatedDate, an unstable OFFSET sort; (4) SQL connection pooling (Max Pool Size 100) combined with MARS=False and a 1024-context EF pool is unconfigured; and (5) a large body of legacy non-Fast LINQ that loads full entities with per-row correlated country subqueries still ships in the assembly (mostly dead, but two reports' active paths still buffer a full UNION in memory). None are remote-exploitable, but they degrade large date-range report runs and produce subtly wrong/duplicated paged data.

<a id="f-offset-without-stable-order-by"></a>
#### 🟠 HIGH — OFFSET/FETCH pagination without a stable, unique ORDER BY drops and duplicates rows

**ID:** `offset-without-stable-order-by` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Pagination correctness &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:112-143`
- `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs:251-273`
- `Backend/Service/Reports/ReportQueryService.cs:25-33`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ReExportReport.cs:70-97`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:441-456`

**Problem.** CreateFastPageAsync applies Skip(pageIndex*pageSize).Take(pageSize+1) for OFFSET/FETCH paging, but ApplySort (APIResult.cs:251-273) returns the source UNCHANGED when no sortColumn is supplied — and the generic report path (ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync) always passes sortColumn=null. Many generic detail report Query() methods (e.g. sp_ReExportReport.Query at sp_ReExportReport.cs:70-97) have no internal `orderby`, so EF emits OFFSET/FETCH over an unordered result set. Even where an ORDER BY exists, it is on a non-unique column: the _Fast detail rows order by `licence.CreatedDate` only (Rows() at sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:441), and the item fan-out means many rows share a CreatedDate. SQL Server does not guarantee a stable order for ties across separate OFFSET calls.

**Impact.** Users paging through a report can silently miss rows entirely or see the same row on two pages — for a government trade-licence report this is a data-integrity/audit problem, not just cosmetic. The bug is invisible in testing with small datasets and surfaces exactly when a report has many same-timestamp rows (bulk-issued licences).

**Recommendation.** Append a unique tiebreaker to every paged ORDER BY (e.g. the licence/permit primary key Id), and make the generic fast-page path refuse to page an unordered query. Concretely: in each Rows()/Query() add `.ThenBy(row => row.Id)` (carry the PK into the projection), and in ApplySort fall back to a deterministic key column instead of returning the source unsorted.

**Example:**

```
// sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.Rows (current)
return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);
// fixed: add a unique tiebreaker so OFFSET/FETCH is stable
return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate).ThenBy(row => row.LicenceItemId);
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours (core fix ~1h; auditing/spot-fixing high-traffic detail SP Query()s and cross-endpoint verification the rest))_**

Give OFFSET/FETCH a stable, fully-unique sort everywhere it pages: add a deterministic tiebreaker key to the generic ApplySort fallback so the generic fast-page path can no longer page an unordered query, and carry the per-item composite PK into the _Fast Import Licence projection so its CreatedDate ordering breaks ties deterministically.

1. In Backend/Model/APIResult.cs, fix ApplySort (lines 251-273) so it never returns an unordered source. When sortColumn is null/invalid, instead of `return source;` return `source.OrderBy(DeterministicSortKey())` so OFFSET/FETCH (the Skip/Take in CreateAsync line 74-76 and CreateFastPageAsync line 119-120) always has a stable order. ApplySort already uses System.Linq.Dynamic.Core (imported at line 5), so OrderBy("PropA, PropB") is valid.
2. Add a private helper DeterministicSortKey() to APIResult.cs next to ApplySort. It reflects typeof(T).GetProperties(BindingFlags.Public|BindingFlags.Instance), prefers a natural key if present ("Id"/"UniqueId"/"LicenceNo"/"CreatedDate", emitting "Id, UniqueId" together when both exist for composite-PK shapes), and otherwise falls back to every simple/comparable public property name (value types + string) joined by comma so ties cannot occur. Cache the computed string per typeof(T) in a static ConcurrentDictionary to avoid reflecting on every request. Call IsValidProperty with throwExceptionIfNotFound:false inside ApplySort's guard so an invalid client sortColumn falls through to the deterministic key instead of throwing.
3. In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs, carry the item composite PK into the projection. Item entities ImportLicenceItem and BorderImportLicenceItem both declare [PrimaryKey("Id", "UniqueId")] (Id is string, UniqueId is int) and neither is currently projected. Add two properties to the private ImportLicenceDetailFastRow class (around lines 802-848): `public string ItemId { get; init; } = null!;` and `public int ItemUniqueId { get; init; }`.
4. Populate the two new fields in all three projections building ImportLicenceDetailFastRow: OverseaRows (`select new` at line 575), BorderPaThaKaRows (line 651), BorderIndividualTradingRows (line 730) — add `ItemId = item.Id, ItemUniqueId = item.UniqueId,`. The `item` range variable is db.ImportLicenceItems in OverseaRows and db.BorderImportLicenceItems in the two Border methods; both expose Id and UniqueId.
5. Change Rows() at line 441 from `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);` to `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate).ThenBy(row => row.ItemId).ThenBy(row => row.ItemUniqueId);` so the per-item fan-out (many rows sharing one CreatedDate) is fully ordered. This source feeds the Skip/Take at lines 48-51 plus the Excel (line 94) and stream (line 122) paths.
6. No change needed for SectionGroups (line 272): its `.OrderBy(SectionName).ThenBy(Currency)` is unique per group, and AggregateInSqlAsync materializes full groups with ToListAsync (no OFFSET).
7. Audit the ~60 StoredProcedureToLinq/*.cs Query() methods with no orderby (e.g. sp_ReExportReport.cs Query lines 70-97 and ReExportDetailQuery 159-185 build `select new sp_ReExportReportResult{...}` unordered). The ApplySort fallback (steps 1-2) makes these deterministic automatically via ReportQueryService.CreatePagedResultAsync (70 controllers use it). For the high-traffic detail reports, also add an explicit `.OrderBy` on a meaningful unique column (e.g. project reExport.Id/registration.Id into the result and order by it) so the SQL is index-friendly rather than relying solely on the reflective all-columns fallback.
8. Run `grep -rn "OrderBy(" Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/` and confirm every paged Query()/Rows() either ends in a unique key (Id/UniqueId/LicenceNo) or is fully materialized (ToListAsync without Skip).

```csharp
// ── Backend/Model/APIResult.cs : ApplySort (lines 251-273) ──
// BEFORE
private static IQueryable<T> ApplySort(IQueryable<T> source, string? sortColumn, ref string? sortOrder)
{
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(sortColumn) || !IsValidProperty(sortColumn))
    {
        return source;                       // <-- BUG: unordered source feeds OFFSET/FETCH
    }
    sortOrder = !string.IsNullOrEmpty(sortOrder) && sortOrder.ToUpper() == "ASC" ? "ASC" : "DESC";
    return source.OrderBy(string.Format("{0} {1}", sortColumn, sortOrder));
}

// AFTER
private static readonly System.Collections.Concurrent.ConcurrentDictionary<Type, string> _stableKeyCache = new();

private static IQueryable<T> ApplySort(IQueryable<T> source, string? sortColumn, ref string? sortOrder)
{
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(sortColumn) || !IsValidProperty(sortColumn, throwExceptionIfNotFound: false))
    {
        // Deterministic fallback so OFFSET/FETCH (Skip/Take) is stable.
        return source.OrderBy(DeterministicSortKey());
    }
    sortOrder = !string.IsNullOrEmpty(sortOrder) && sortOrder.ToUpper() == "ASC" ? "ASC" : "DESC";
    // Tie-break a user sort with the deterministic key so ties don't shuffle across pages.
    return source.OrderBy(string.Format("{0} {1}, {2}", sortColumn, sortOrder, DeterministicSortKey()));
}

private static string DeterministicSortKey()
{
    return _stableKeyCache.GetOrAdd(typeof(T), static t =>
    {
        var props = t.GetProperties(BindingFlags.Public | BindingFlags.Instance)
            .Where(p => p.CanRead)
            .ToList();
        foreach (var preferred in new[] { "Id", "UniqueId", "LicenceNo", "CreatedDate" })
        {
            var match = props.Where(p => string.Equals(p.Name, preferred, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)).ToList();
            if (match.Count > 0)
            {
                var keys = props.Where(p => p.Name is "Id" or "UniqueId").Select(p => p.Name).ToList();
                return string.Join(", ", keys.Count > 0 ? keys : match.Select(p => p.Name));
            }
        }
        // No natural key: order by every simple comparable property so ties cannot occur.
        var allKeys = props
            .Where(p => p.PropertyType.IsValueType || p.PropertyType == typeof(string))
            .Select(p => p.Name);
        return string.Join(", ", allKeys);
    });
}

// ── Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs ──
// Rows() (line 441)
// BEFORE
return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);
// AFTER
return RowsUnordered(db, request)
    .OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate)
    .ThenBy(row => row.ItemId)
    .ThenBy(row => row.ItemUniqueId);

// ImportLicenceDetailFastRow (add to the private class, ~line 804)
public string ItemId { get; init; } = null!;
public int ItemUniqueId { get; init; }

// each of OverseaRows / BorderPaThaKaRows / BorderIndividualTradingRows projection (e.g. line 575)
// add inside `select new ImportLicenceDetailFastRow { ... }`:
ItemId = item.Id,
ItemUniqueId = item.UniqueId,
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build Backend` succeeds (the new dynamic OrderBy strings are validated at runtime by System.Linq.Dynamic.Core, so also smoke-test a real request).
- Determinism test for the _Fast report: pick a date range with many same-CreatedDate bulk-issued licences, request page 1 then page 2 with a small pageSize (?pageIndex=0&pageSize=5 then pageIndex=1) on the Import Licence detail endpoint, and confirm no LicenceNo/HSCode row repeats across pages and none is skipped vs a full unpaged pull; repeat the two requests several times and confirm identical order each time.
- Generic path: hit any ReportQueryService-backed endpoint (70 controllers, e.g. ReExportDetailReportController) paging through all pages with pageSize=5, concatenate all pages, and compare row count and set against an IncludeTotalCount=true total — must match with no dupes/gaps.
- Inspect generated SQL via EF logging or ToQueryString to confirm every paged query emits `ORDER BY ... OFFSET ... FETCH` ending in a unique key column rather than a bare OFFSET or ORDER BY on CreatedDate alone.
- Run `grep -rn "OrderBy(" Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/` to confirm remaining paged sources terminate in a unique key or are fully materialized.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** The item PK is composite (string Id + int UniqueId) — ordering by ItemId alone is NOT unique across the fan-out; you must add BOTH ThenBy clauses. In ApplySort, call IsValidProperty with throwExceptionIfNotFound:false (the public default is true, relied on elsewhere) so an invalid client sortColumn falls through to the deterministic key instead of throwing. The deterministic fallback must apply to BOTH CreateAsync (exact-count path, used when IncludeTotalCount=true) and CreateFastPageAsync (estimate path); they share ApplySort so one fix covers both, but verify both paths now use the SAME ordering or their page sets won't line up. Ordering by all columns when no natural key exists adds SQL sort cost on wide projections — for the hottest reports add an explicit single indexed-PK OrderBy instead of relying on the all-columns fallback. Rollback is safe: reverting APIResult.cs and the _Fast projection restores prior behavior; no schema/data migration involved.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity High is appropriate and arguably conservative given breadth: the generic unordered-OFFSET path backs 35 controllers and 60/93 SP files lack any ordering, and the high-volume _Fast Import Licence detail report orders only by non-unique CreatedDate with a per-item fan-out that guarantees ties. The recommended fix (carry the licence/permit PK into the projection and add .ThenBy(row => row.Id), plus a deterministic fallback key in ApplySort) is correct and low-risk. Note ImportLicenceDetailFastRow does not currently project an Id, so implementing the tiebreaker requires adding the PK to the projection in OverseaRows/BorderPaThaKaRows/BorderIndividualTradingRows.


<a id="f-border-union-unstable-and-heavy"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Border report pages order a UNION ALL of two 11-table joins by a non-unique key

**ID:** `border-union-unstable-and-heavy` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Pagination correctness / query cost &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:444-456`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:621-777`

**Problem.** For request.Type == 'Border', RowsUnordered concatenates BorderPaThaKaRows and BorderIndividualTradingRows (each an 11-table join), and Rows() then orders the UNION ALL by CreatedDate alone before Skip/Take. SQL Server must materialize and sort the entire combined result to satisfy OFFSET/FETCH, and CreatedDate is not unique across the two branches, so the same instability as finding offset-without-stable-order-by applies, compounded by the union. CountBorderPaThaKaAsync + CountBorderIndividualTradingAsync also run two more full counts.

**Impact.** Border licence/permit reports (a major report family) are the slowest to page and the most prone to row skip/dupe across pages, because the cost and the ordering ambiguity both scale with the union of two large joins.

**Recommendation.** Add a deterministic, branch-disambiguating tiebreaker to the ORDER BY (e.g. CreatedDate, then a (source-discriminator, Id) pair carried into the row shape). Consider giving each branch its own contiguous key range or sorting by (CreatedDate, Id) within a single UNION-aware ordering so OFFSET is reproducible.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 45 min)_**

Add a deterministic tiebreaker to the single shared ORDER BY in Rows() so OFFSET/FETCH paging is reproducible across all Types and across the Border UNION ALL. Carry a unique per-item key (the item.Id PK) plus a source discriminator into ImportLicenceDetailFastRow, then order by CreatedDate, then SourceOrdinal, then RowKey. This fixes the cross-page skip/dupe bug (shared with offset-without-stable-order-by) without changing the result set or column output.

1. Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs.
2. In the private sealed class ImportLicenceDetailFastRow (starts line 802), add two new properties used only for ordering (not surfaced in ToResult): `public string RowKey { get; init; } = null!;` (the item primary key, which is unique per fan-out row) and `public int SourceOrdinal { get; init; }` (a branch discriminator: 0 for the PaThaKa/Oversea branch, 1 for the Border Individual Trading branch). These are NOT added to ToResult() so the response shape is unchanged.
3. In OverseaRows (projection starting line 575), add `RowKey = item.Id,` and `SourceOrdinal = 0,` to the `select new ImportLicenceDetailFastRow { ... }` initializer. item is the ImportLicenceItem (item.Id is the string PK).
4. In BorderPaThaKaRows (projection starting line 651), add `RowKey = item.Id,` and `SourceOrdinal = 0,` (item here is BorderImportLicenceItem; item.Id is its string PK).
5. In BorderIndividualTradingRows (projection starting line 730), add `RowKey = item.Id,` and `SourceOrdinal = 1,` so the two Border branches never collide on the tiebreaker even if a CreatedDate and an item.Id were ever shared.
6. Change the ORDER BY in Rows() (line 441) from `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);` to `return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate).ThenBy(row => row.SourceOrdinal).ThenBy(row => row.RowKey);` so OFFSET/FETCH has a fully deterministic key. This is the single shared sort for Oversea and Border, so it also fixes the sibling offset-without-stable-order-by instability on the Oversea path.
7. Build the Backend project to confirm the EF query still translates (the added ORDER BY columns must be in the SELECT projection over a UNION ALL, which EF Core 9 supports because RowKey/SourceOrdinal are now real columns in both branches).
8. Do NOT change CountRowsAsync / CountBorderPaThaKaAsync / CountBorderIndividualTradingAsync — the two counts are an inherent property of the UNION (count = countA + countB) and the verifier confirmed severity is bounded by MaxPageSize=1000; reducing count cost is out of scope for this finding.

```csharp
// --- Rows(): add deterministic tiebreaker (line 441) ---
// before:
return RowsUnordered(db, request).OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate);
// after:
return RowsUnordered(db, request)
    .OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate)
    .ThenBy(row => row.SourceOrdinal)
    .ThenBy(row => row.RowKey);

// --- ImportLicenceDetailFastRow: add ordering-only fields (after line 804) ---
public DateTime? CreatedDate { get; init; }
public string RowKey { get; init; } = null!;   // item PK; unique per fan-out row
public int SourceOrdinal { get; init; }         // 0 = PaThaKa/Oversea branch, 1 = Border Individual Trading branch
// ...remaining properties unchanged; ToResult() is NOT modified.

// --- OverseaRows projection (select new, ~line 575) ---
select new ImportLicenceDetailFastRow
{
    CreatedDate = licence.CreatedDate,
    RowKey = item.Id,
    SourceOrdinal = 0,
    // ...rest unchanged
};

// --- BorderPaThaKaRows projection (~line 651) ---
select new ImportLicenceDetailFastRow
{
    CreatedDate = licence.CreatedDate,
    RowKey = item.Id,
    SourceOrdinal = 0,
    // ...rest unchanged
};

// --- BorderIndividualTradingRows projection (~line 730) ---
select new ImportLicenceDetailFastRow
{
    CreatedDate = licence.CreatedDate,
    RowKey = item.Id,
    SourceOrdinal = 1,
    // ...rest unchanged
};
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build the Backend project (from /Users/.../Backend) to confirm the EF Core 9 query translates with ORDER BY over the UNION ALL.
- Call the Import Licence Detail report endpoint that drives CreatePagedResultAsync with request.Type='Border' and a date range that returns more than 2 pages. Request page 0 then page 1 with the same PageSize; capture the LicenceNo+HSCode+RowKey set. Confirm no row appears on both pages and no row is missing across the page boundary (the previous behavior could skip/dupe rows whose CreatedDate ties).
- Repeat the two-page check with request.Type='Oversea' to confirm the shared-ORDER BY fix also stabilizes that path.
- Enable EF Core query logging (or capture via SQL Server Profiler) and confirm the generated SQL ends with ORDER BY [CreatedDate], [SourceOrdinal], [RowKey] before OFFSET ... FETCH NEXT.
- Confirm the JSON response shape is unchanged (no RowKey/SourceOrdinal leak) since ToResult() was not modified.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** RowKey must be a column present in BOTH Border branches of the Concat/UNION ALL, otherwise EF Core cannot order the combined query — that is why the property is added to all three projections (Oversea, BorderPaThaKa, BorderIndividualTrading), not just Border. SourceOrdinal is a constant per branch, so it sorts the two Border branches into contiguous reproducible blocks but does NOT reorder within a branch. Keep RowKey/SourceOrdinal out of ToResult() and out of sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportResult so the API contract and front-end columns are untouched. This change is read-only and additive (no DB migration, no schema change); rollback is reverting the OrderBy chain to the single .OrderBy(row => row.CreatedDate) and removing the two row fields. Note item.Id is a string PK, so the ThenBy(RowKey) is a string sort — that is fine for a tiebreaker (only determinism matters, not numeric ordering).

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Two minor refinements to the framing, not the verdict: (1) The ordering instability is not exclusively caused by the union — the item fan-out alone already makes CreatedDate non-unique within a single branch, so the same paging-instability bug exists on the Oversea path too (line 441 is shared by all Types). The union compounds cost and collision probability but is not the root cause; the root cause is "no unique tiebreaker in ORDER BY," shared with the sibling finding offset-without-stable-order-by. (2) Practical impact is bounded: page size is capped at MaxPageSize=1000 (line 23) and IncludeTotalCount paths add the two counts, but the slow full sort happens regardless of page. Severity Medium is appropriate — correctness (cross-page skip/dupe) plus avoidable query cost on a major report family, but not data corruption or a hard failure. Locations are accurate.


<a id="f-count-and-groupby-rerun-per-page"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Full COUNT and full GROUP BY (plus FX query) re-executed on every page request

**ID:** `count-and-groupby-rerun-per-page` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** EF Core / query cost &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Large &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:44-46 and :299-408 (incl. Daily FX :402-405); Backend/Service/Reports/ReportAggregationService.cs:182-220; Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx:199`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:44-51`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:466-545`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:299-408`
- `Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceDailyReportNewLicenceReportController.cs:43-58`
- `Backend/Service/Reports/ReportAggregationService.cs:182-220`

**Problem.** Two separate cost problems on the hot paging path. (1) When IncludeTotalCount=true, the detail report runs CountRowsAsync (sp_..._Fast.cs:466) as a SECOND full query that re-joins the licence+item fan-out and re-applies every filter, on EVERY page navigation — so each page click costs two full table scans of the filtered set. (2) The aggregate reports (By Section/Method/Country/Company/HSCode/Daily/TotalValue) call GetAggregateRowsAsync, which executes the ENTIRE SQL GROUP BY over the full filtered detail set, returns ALL groups, then pages them in memory (ReportAggregationService.CreatePagedResultFromGroups). For the Daily report this additionally fires ReportUsdConversionService.FillDailyUsdValuesAsync (a second ExchangeRate+Currency query) every time. None of this is cached, so paging a By-Company or Daily report over a wide date range re-runs the full grouping + FX join for every page and every 'Total' footer render.

**Impact.** Large date-range report runs (a year of licences across all sections) hammer the SQL server with full re-scans/re-groupings on each page turn, multiplying DB load by the number of pages a user clicks through and making wide reports feel slow under concurrent analyst use.

**Recommendation.** For detail reports prefer keyset/cursor paging so COUNT can be skipped, or cache the COUNT for a (filter-hash) for the life of a paging session and only recompute when filters change. For aggregate reports, push OFFSET/FETCH into the grouped SQL query (the grouped query is already an IQueryable in SectionGroups) instead of materializing all groups and paging in memory, and compute ColumnTotals with a single SUM query rather than summing a fully-materialized list. Cache the grouped result per filter-hash for a short window so repeated page turns reuse it.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1 day)_**

Add a short-lived (e.g. 60s) per-filter-hash IMemoryCache layer over the two re-run hot paths: (1) cache the grouped List<ReportAggregateResult> produced by AggregateInSqlAsync (the GROUP BY + Daily FX query that ~47 aggregate controllers re-run on every page turn / footer render), and (2) cache the detail-report total COUNT in sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync (BasicTable always sends includeTotalCount=true, so it re-runs per page). Reuse the existing AddMemoryCache() registration and the GetOrCreateAsync idiom already established in ReportLookupCache; the OFFSET/FETCH-in-SQL rewrite the finding mentions is skipped per the verifier (minor win, and the grouped set is already SQL-grouped not the detail fan-out).

1. Add a cache-key helper for the aggregate request. In Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs add `using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;` and a private static method that builds a stable string key from sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest + dimension + includeSakhan, e.g. BuildAggregateCacheKey(request, dimension, includeSakhan) returning $"ImportLicAgg:v1:{request.Type}:{request.FromDate:O}:{request.ToDate:O}:{request.PaThaKaTypeId}:{request.ExportImportSectionId}:{request.ExportImportMethodId}:{request.ExportImportIncotermId}:{request.SellerCountryId}:{request.CompanyRegistrationNo}:{request.SakhanId}:{(int)dimension}:{includeSakhan}". Include every field of sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest (defined in sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:11-23) so two different filters never collide.
2. Thread IMemoryCache into GetAggregateRowsAsync (sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:165) and CreateAggregateResultAsync (:128). Add an `IMemoryCache cache` parameter and, inside, wrap the existing `await AggregateInSqlAsync(db, request, dimension, includeSakhan)` call in `cache.GetOrCreateAsync(BuildAggregateCacheKey(...), async entry => { entry.AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(60); return await AggregateInSqlAsync(db, request, dimension, includeSakhan); })` and fall back to an empty list if null — exactly the pattern in ReportLookupCache.GetAsync (ReportLookupCache.cs:111-125). This caches the GROUP BY result AND the Daily FX fill (AggregateInSqlAsync :402-405 runs ReportUsdConversionService.FillDailyUsdValuesAsync before returning, so the cached list already carries TotalUSDValue). Keep CreateAggregateExcelWorkbookAsync (:148) uncached since it streams the full set for export.
3. Update the ~47 aggregate controllers that call GetAggregateRowsAsync / CreateAggregateResultAsync to pass the cache. For ImportLicenceDailyReportNewLicenceReportController.cs: inject IMemoryCache via the constructor (currently TradeNetDbContext + IExcelExportJobService only, :27), add `private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;` and `using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;`, then pass `_cache` to the GetAggregateRowsAsync call at :43-44 (and the WriteRowsAsync call at :95-96 may stay uncached or share it). IMemoryCache is already DI-registered (Program.cs:23 AddMemoryCache()), so no Program.cs change is needed. The Daily controller's footer at :53-58 keeps summing the SAME `groups` list it already holds, so the footer no longer triggers a second DB round-trip.
4. Cache the detail-report COUNT. In sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync (:25) add an `IMemoryCache cache` parameter (mirroring the Export twin sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync at sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:25-27 which already takes IMemoryCache). Replace the line `var totalCount = pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount ? await CountRowsAsync(db, request) : (int?)null;` (:44-46) with a GetOrCreateAsync over a count-specific key (e.g. $"ImportLicCount:v1:{...same request fields...}") and the same 60s AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow, so only the FIRST page of a paging session pays the second full COUNT scan; subsequent page clicks within 60s reuse it. CountRowsAsync (:466) itself is unchanged.
5. Wire IMemoryCache into the two detail controllers that call CreatePagedResultAsync: ImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs (constructor at :28, call at :48-49) and BorderImportLicenceDetailReportController.cs. Add `IMemoryCache cache` to each constructor, store `_cache`, add the `using`, and pass `_cache` into CreatePagedResultAsync. (StreamResolvedChunksAsync at :108 / the Excel path do not use the COUNT, so leave them.)
6. Do NOT change BasicTable.tsx:199 (includeTotalCount: true stays). The server-side count cache makes the repeated COUNT cheap without altering the frontend paging contract or the total-row display.

```csharp
// --- Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs ---
// at top of file
using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;

private static readonly TimeSpan AggregateCacheTtl = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(60);

private static string BuildAggregateCacheKey(
    sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest r, ReportAggregateDimension dimension, bool includeSakhan) =>
    $"ImportLicAgg:v1:{r.Type}:{r.FromDate:O}:{r.ToDate:O}:{r.PaThaKaTypeId}:{r.ExportImportSectionId}:" +
    $"{r.ExportImportMethodId}:{r.ExportImportIncotermId}:{r.SellerCountryId}:{r.CompanyRegistrationNo}:" +
    $"{r.SakhanId}:{(int)dimension}:{includeSakhan}";

// GetAggregateRowsAsync (was :165) — add IMemoryCache cache param, wrap the call:
public static async Task<List<ReportAggregateResult>> GetAggregateRowsAsync(
    TradeNetDbContext db, IMemoryCache cache,
    sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest request,
    ReportAggregateDimension dimension, bool includeSakhan)
{
    ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(db);
    ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(cache);
    ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(request);

    var cached = await cache.GetOrCreateAsync(
        BuildAggregateCacheKey(request, dimension, includeSakhan),
        async entry =>
        {
            entry.AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow = AggregateCacheTtl;
            // AggregateInSqlAsync also fills Daily TotalUSDValue (:402-405) before returning,
            // so the cached list already carries the FX result — no re-query on later pages.
            return await AggregateInSqlAsync(db, request, dimension, includeSakhan);
        });
    return cached ?? new List<ReportAggregateResult>();
}

// --- CreatePagedResultAsync (was :44-46): cache the detail COUNT ---
var totalCount = pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount
    ? await cache.GetOrCreateAsync(
        $"ImportLicCount:v1:{request.Type}:{request.FromDate:O}:{request.ToDate:O}:{request.PaThaKaTypeId}:" +
        $"{request.ExportImportSectionId}:{request.ExportImportMethodId}:{request.ExportImportIncotermId}:" +
        $"{request.SellerCountryId}:{request.CompanyRegistrationNo}:{request.SakhanId}",
        async entry => { entry.AbsoluteExpirationRelativeToNow = AggregateCacheTtl; return await CountRowsAsync(db, request); })
    : (int?)null;
// (signature gains `IMemoryCache cache` — see sp_ExportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.cs:25-27 twin)

// --- Backend/Controllers/Report/ImportLicenceDailyReportNewLicenceReportController.cs ---
// add: using Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.Memory;  + private readonly IMemoryCache _cache; (ctor)
var groups = await sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.GetAggregateRowsAsync(
    _context, _cache, procedureRequest!, ReportAggregateDimension.Daily, includeSakhan: false);
// footer at :53-58 keeps summing this same `groups` list — no extra DB hit.
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` from Backend/ must succeed after the signature changes (compiler flags every un-updated call site so none are missed).
- Run the API, open the Import Licence Daily report over a wide date range, enable SQL logging (or SQL Server Profiler / EF Core `LogTo`). Click through pages 1->2->3 within ~60s: confirm the GROUP BY query and the ExchangeRate+Currency FX query execute ONCE (first page) and are NOT re-emitted on subsequent page clicks; the Total footer also fires no new query.
- For a detail report (Import Licence Detail), page forward several times and confirm the CountRowsAsync COUNT query runs once per filter set within the TTL, not once per page.
- Change a filter (e.g. date range) and confirm a fresh GROUP BY / COUNT runs (new cache key), proving stale results are not served for a different filter.
- Confirm displayed totals (NoOfLicences, TotalValue, Total USD Value) and pagination total are unchanged vs. before the change.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Cache key correctness: the key MUST include every filter field of sp_ImportLicenceDetailReportRequest and the dimension+includeSakhan flag, or two different reports/filters will collide and show wrong numbers in this sensitive gov system. Use ISO 'O' date formatting to avoid culture issues. Keep TTL short (~60s) so newly-approved licences appear quickly; the aggregate values are not real-time-critical but should not be cached for minutes. IMemoryCache is per-process — fine for a single instance, but if the API is ever scaled out, different instances will have independent short-lived caches (acceptable; results still consistent within a session). Ordering: change the static method signatures first, then fix all ~47 aggregate call sites and the 2 detail call sites (the compiler enumerates them) before running. Do not cache the Excel-export paths (CreateAggregateExcelWorkbookAsync, StreamResolvedChunksAsync) — they intentionally stream the full set. Rollback is trivial: revert the GetOrCreateAsync wrappers to the direct AggregateInSqlAsync/CountRowsAsync calls and drop the added parameters."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** BasicTable.tsx:199 always sends includeTotalCount=true so the detail COUNT genuinely re-runs per page. The aggregate 'page in memory' is over the SQL-grouped result, not the detail fan-out — the finding's main exaggeration. ~96 controller references use the aggregate group path, so the modest per-page recompute is widespread. A short-lived per-filter-hash cache would address both halves; pushing OFFSET/FETCH into the generic aggregate GROUP BY is a minor win since SectionGroups already demonstrates it.


<a id="f-legacy-notracking-and-client-eval"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Legacy non-Fast LINQ loads full entities with per-row country subqueries and an in-memory UNION buffer

**ID:** `legacy-notracking-and-client-eval` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** EF Core anti-patterns &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Large &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Live issue: Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.cs:47-55 + :137 (Rows() typed IEnumerable forces in-memory Count/Skip/Take before paging). Dead-code issue: Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:221-226 (in-memory union buffer) and :289-296 (per-row correlated country subquery), reachable only via the also-dead sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs. sp_ReExportReport.cs:70-97 pages in SQL (not a memory issue); only valid sub-claim there is missing ORDER BY on OFFSET/FETCH.`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:218-231`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:289-296`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.cs`
- `Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ReExportReport.cs:70-97`

**Problem.** The non-Fast detail Query() methods build their result with NO AsNoTracking and resolve ConsignedCountry/CountryofOrigin via correlated `string.Join(from country in db.Countries where (','+csv+',').Contains(...))` subqueries (sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:289-296) — one extra LATERAL/APPLY per output row, plus a string-LIKE on the Countries table that cannot use an index. Worse, the Border branch does `.AsEnumerable().Concat(...).OrderBy(...).AsQueryable()` (sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs:221-226), which fully buffers BOTH unioned joins in application memory before sorting — defeating any OFFSET/FETCH. The _Fast versions already fixed all of this via the cache and SQL grouping. The import-licence non-Fast class appears to be dead (no controller references it), but the same buffering pattern is live in sp_WineImportationReport_Fast, and sp_ReExportReport.Query (live) projects without AsNoTracking and without an ORDER BY.

**Impact.** Any report still routed through these legacy methods loads the full filtered set into memory and sorts there, with a non-indexable per-row country lookup — memory pressure and slow queries on large ranges. Carrying the dead non-Fast import-licence code also invites a future caller to reintroduce the slow path.

**Recommendation.** Route every live report through the _Fast/SQL-grouped + ICountryCache path (as the active import-licence controllers already do), delete the confirmed-dead non-Fast classes, and for any remaining live non-Fast query add AsNoTracking, replace the correlated country subquery with cache-based CSV resolution after materialization (ReportLookupCache.ResolveCsv), and remove the `.AsEnumerable()...AsQueryable()` buffering so the union/sort/page runs in SQL.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2-3 hours)_**

placeholder

1. LIVE FIX — Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.cs. Root cause: the private `Rows(...)` method (line 137) is declared `IEnumerable<WineImportationFastRow>`. Even though `DefaultRows` and `WineImportationDetailRows` return `IQueryable`, the upcast to `IEnumerable` makes `rows.Count()` (line 49) and `rows.Skip(...).Take(...)` (lines 52-54) bind to `System.Linq.Enumerable`, so EF Core materializes the ENTIRE filtered multi-join into memory before counting/paging. The Summary path is genuinely in-memory but returns at most 5 rows, so it is fine.
2. In `CreatePagedResultAsync`, branch the Summary path (small, in-memory) away from the detail path (must page in SQL). Replace the single `var rows = Rows(db, request);` block (lines 47-55) so that for Summary you keep the current in-memory `Count()/Skip()/Take().ToList()`, but for the detail paths you obtain an `IQueryable<WineImportationFastRow>` via a new `DetailQuery(db, request)` helper and run `await query.CountAsync()` and `await query.Skip(...).Take(...).ToListAsync()` (add `using Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore;` is already present at line 6).
3. Add a private `IQueryable<WineImportationFastRow> DetailQuery(TradeNetDbContext db, sp_WineImportationReportRequest request)` that returns the same three branches that are currently detail (not Summary): `request.ApplyType == Valid` -> `WineImportationDetailRows(db, w => w.EndDate > request.Date)`; `request.ApplyType == Invalid` -> `WineImportationDetailRows(db, w => w.EndDate < request.Date)`; else `DefaultRows(db, request)`. These three already return `IQueryable`, so no query body changes are needed — only the static type that flows to Count/Skip/Take.
4. Keep `private static IEnumerable<WineImportationFastRow> SummaryRows(...)` exactly as-is (it concats 5 `GroupBy` projections via `.AsEnumerable()`), and route only `request.Type == Summary` through it.
5. Update the Excel and streaming paths the same way so they page/stream against `IQueryable` for detail and the small in-memory enumerable for Summary: in `CreateExcelWorkbookAsync` (lines 98-100) and `StreamResolvedChunksAsync` (lines 119-129), call `DetailQuery(...)` and use `await ...ToListAsync()` / `.AsAsyncEnumerable()` for detail (matching `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast.CreateExcelWorkbookAsync` line 94 and `StreamResolvedChunksAsync` line 122), while Summary keeps the in-memory enumeration. Resolve the WineType CSV via `ReportLookupCache.ResolveCsv` in `ToResult` AFTER materialization — it already does this (line 424), so do not push it back into SQL.
6. DEAD-CODE DELETION — delete Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs entirely. Verified: no controller or other class references the type `sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report` (grep returned only its own declarations). It is the SOLE caller of `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.Query` (line 86 of that file).
7. DEAD-CODE DELETION — delete Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs entirely (this is the legacy non-Fast class containing the `.AsEnumerable().Concat(...).OrderBy(...).AsQueryable()` in-memory union at :221-226 and the correlated `string.Join(from country in db.Countries ... Contains ...)` at :289-296). Once the Daily class above is removed it has zero references — all live import-licence controllers use `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport_Fast`. Removing it also deletes the now-orphaned `sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.Query` slow path.
8. Build the backend to confirm nothing else referenced the deleted symbols: `dotnet build` from the Backend project directory. A clean build proves both classes were dead.
9. OPTIONAL (verifier-confirmed minor) — Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_ReExportReport.cs: the `Query(...)` result is paged in SQL by `ApiResult.CreateFastPageAsync` (OFFSET/FETCH), so it is NOT a memory issue — do NOT add AsNoTracking (no-op on DTO projections) and do NOT 'remove buffering' (there is none). The only valid sub-claim is OFFSET/FETCH without a stable ORDER BY. If desired, add a deterministic `orderby reExport.ReExportNo` (and for the detail/summary helpers) so page boundaries are stable; this is cosmetic for correctness, not a perf fix.

```csharp
// Backend/StoredProcedureToLinq/sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.cs

// BEFORE (lines ~47-55): rows is IEnumerable -> Count/Skip/Take run in LINQ-to-Objects
var rows = Rows(db, request);
var totalCount = pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount
    ? rows.Count()
    : (int?)null;

var pageRows = rows
    .Skip(pageIndex * pageSize)
    .Take(pageSize + (totalCount.HasValue ? 0 : 1))
    .ToList();

// AFTER: Summary stays tiny+in-memory; detail pages in SQL via IQueryable
List<WineImportationFastRow> pageRows;
int? totalCount;

if (request.Type == Summary)
{
    var summary = SummaryRows(db, request);          // <= 5 rows, fine in memory
    totalCount = pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount ? summary.Count() : (int?)null;
    pageRows = summary
        .Skip(pageIndex * pageSize)
        .Take(pageSize + (totalCount.HasValue ? 0 : 1))
        .ToList();
}
else
{
    var query = DetailQuery(db, request);            // IQueryable -> SQL
    totalCount = pagingRequest.IncludeTotalCount
        ? await query.CountAsync()                   // SQL COUNT
        : (int?)null;
    pageRows = await query
        .Skip(pageIndex * pageSize)
        .Take(pageSize + (totalCount.HasValue ? 0 : 1))
        .ToListAsync();                              // SQL OFFSET/FETCH
}

// NEW helper — returns IQueryable so the static type drives SQL translation
private static IQueryable<WineImportationFastRow> DetailQuery(
    TradeNetDbContext db,
    sp_WineImportationReportRequest request)
{
    if (request.ApplyType == Valid)
        return WineImportationDetailRows(db, w => w.EndDate > request.Date);
    if (request.ApplyType == Invalid)
        return WineImportationDetailRows(db, w => w.EndDate < request.Date);
    return DefaultRows(db, request);
}
// (Old `private static IEnumerable<WineImportationFastRow> Rows(...)` can be removed
//  or kept only as the Summary entry point; SummaryRows stays unchanged.)
```

**Verify:**
- Build: from Backend run `dotnet build` — must succeed after deleting sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs and sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs (proves they were dead) and after the Wine refactor.
- SQL translation check: enable EF logging (or attach SQL Server Profiler / set Logging:LogLevel for Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Database.Command to Information) and POST to the Alcoholic Beverages Importation Detail report endpoint (AlcoholicBeveragesImportationDetailReportController Post, which calls sp_WineImportationReport_Fast.CreatePagedResultAsync) with a wide FromDate/ToDate. Confirm the emitted SQL contains `OFFSET @__p ROWS FETCH NEXT @__p ROWS ONLY` and a separate `SELECT COUNT(*)`, and that a single page returns only ~PageSize rows from the DB (no full-set materialization).
- Behavioral check: request page 2 with PageSize 10 and verify the same rows/totalCount as before the change (parity), and that the Summary report (Type=Summary) still returns its 5 aggregate rows.
- Regression grep: `grep -rn "sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport\." Backend --include=*.cs` and `grep -rn "sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report" Backend` return no hits after deletion.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Do NOT route the Summary path through IQueryable/ToListAsync as one query — SummaryRows is built from `.AsEnumerable().Concat(...)` of 5 separate GroupBy projections and is intentionally composed in memory; forcing it to a single IQueryable will not translate. Keep Summary on the in-memory branch (it is only ~5 rows). Order of deletion matters: remove sp_ImportLicenceDaily_Detail_Report.cs first (or together) — deleting sp_ImportLicenceDetailReport.cs alone while the Daily class still references its .Query will break the build. The fast-page sentinel still relies on Take(pageSize + 1) when totalCount is null (CreateFastPageFromRows), so preserve the `+ (totalCount.HasValue ? 0 : 1)` term in both branches.
