- `docker run --rm -d -p 3000:80 tradenet-frontend` then `curl -s http://localhost:3000/ | grep -i '<div id="root"'` returns the built index.html (non-empty served content).
- Deep-link / SPA check: `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:3000/some/react-route` returns 200 (served index.html via try_files), not 404.
- Compose path: `docker compose -f docker-compose.production.yml up -d --build` brings up the react-app service on host port 3000 and the site loads in a browser.
- `docker run --rm tradenet-frontend ls /usr/share/nginx/html` shows index.html and the hashed assets/ directory.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Build context grows with `COPY . .` — ensure `.dockerignore` excludes `node_modules/` (it does) and add `dist/` so the local build artifact isn't shipped into the build stage.; Do not keep `npm ci --only=production` 'for image size' — the devDependencies only live in the build stage and never reach the final nginx image, so the slim runtime is preserved automatically by multi-stage.; If `npm run build` fails on real TypeScript errors after this fix (the `tsc` step now actually runs and may surface pre-existing type errors that were never caught because the build never ran in-container), those are genuine type errors to resolve — they are not a regression from this change.; The filename is lowercase `dockerfile`; `docker-compose.production.yml` and `docker-compose.yml(.dev)` reference it explicitly. Do not rename it to `Dockerfile` without updating those compose `dockerfile:` keys (case matters on Linux).; Rollback: revert the single `Frontend/dockerfile` (and remove `Frontend/nginx.conf`); the active deploy path is `deploy.ps1` robocopying `Frontend/dist`, so reverting causes no production outage.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity High is defensible but sits at the upper edge. The broken artifact is real and reproducible-deploy/parity goals are undermined, but a working alternative deploy path (deploy.ps1 robocopying Frontend/dist) means there is no live outage. Reasonable to keep High given the documented prod compose references this broken image; could equally be Medium since the container path is aspirational rather than the active production mechanism. Locations are all accurate (dockerfile:11, :17, :21). No nginx.conf exists in Frontend (recommendation to add one for SPA routing is valid).


<a id="f-swagger-served-in-production"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Swagger UI and OpenAPI spec served unconditionally in production

**ID:** `swagger-served-in-production` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Attack Surface / Deployment Config &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Trivial &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:109`
- `Backend/Program.cs:110`
- `Backend/Properties/launchSettings.json:6`

**Problem.** app.UseSwagger() and app.UseSwaggerUI() are called outside the IsDevelopment() guard, so the full API surface (every report, export, and admin endpoint) is documented and interactively callable at /swagger in every environment including production. launchSettings additionally sets launchUrl to 'swagger'.

**Impact.** An unauthenticated attacker can enumerate the entire government API, parameter shapes, and error behavior from production, dramatically lowering the cost of attacks — especially combined with the trivial JWT key (they can craft tokens and exercise every endpoint directly from the Swagger UI).

**Recommendation.** Wrap UseSwagger/UseSwaggerUI inside if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()), or gate them behind authentication and a non-public route in production.

**Example:**

```
// current: always on
app.UseSwagger();
app.UseSwaggerUI(...);

// fixed
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) {
    app.UseSwagger();
    app.UseSwaggerUI(...);
}
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 10 min)_**

Move the existing `app.UseSwagger()` / `app.UseSwaggerUI(...)` calls (Program.cs:109-115) inside an `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` block so the OpenAPI spec and interactive UI are never served in production. The real defect is purely in Program.cs; launchSettings.json only affects local launch and needs no change.

1. Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend/Program.cs.
2. Locate the unconditional Swagger calls at lines 109-115: `app.UseSwagger();` followed by `app.UseSwaggerUI(options => { ... });`. These currently sit AFTER the existing `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); } else { app.UseHsts(); }` block (lines 100-107), so they run in every environment.
3. Wrap both calls in `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { ... }`. The cleanest option is to fold them into the existing development branch at line 100-103 (add them right after `app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();`), or add a new dedicated `if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())` block immediately below — keep the `SwaggerEndpoint`, `RoutePrefix = "swagger"`, and `DisplayRequestDuration()` options exactly as they are.
4. Do NOT remove `builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(...)` or `builder.Services.AddEndpointsApiExplorer()` (Program.cs:27-63) — registering the generators is harmless and keeps the dev experience intact; only the middleware that exposes the endpoint must be gated.
5. Leave Backend/Properties/launchSettings.json unchanged: its `launchUrl: "swagger"` (lines 6, 16, 24) and `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development` only affect local browser launch, not the deployed/production host (which sets ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT via the deployment). Per the verifier, launchSettings:6 is not the real exposure vector.
6. Confirm the production host does NOT set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development (check the deployment/IIS/Docker env vars). If for some operational reason production must run as Development, instead gate Swagger behind authentication and a non-guessable RoutePrefix rather than the environment check.
7. Build the Backend project to ensure no compile errors: `dotnet build "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend"`.

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs (lines 99-115)

// BEFORE
        // Configure the HTTP request pipeline.
        if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
        {
            app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();
        }
        else
        {
            app.UseHsts();
        }

        app.UseSwagger();
        app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
        {
            options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "TradeNet Admin Report API v1");
            options.RoutePrefix = "swagger";
            options.DisplayRequestDuration();
        });

// AFTER
        // Configure the HTTP request pipeline.
        if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
        {
            app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage();

            app.UseSwagger();
            app.UseSwaggerUI(options =>
            {
                options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "TradeNet Admin Report API v1");
                options.RoutePrefix = "swagger";
                options.DisplayRequestDuration();
            });
        }
        else
        {
            app.UseHsts();
        }
```

**Verify:**
- Run the app with the production environment: `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production dotnet run --project "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Backend"`, then `curl -k https://localhost:8000/swagger/v1/swagger.json` and `curl -k https://localhost:8000/swagger/index.html` — both must return 404 (not the OpenAPI JSON / UI HTML).
- Run with `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development` (or the default `dotnet run` which uses launchSettings) and confirm /swagger still loads the UI and /swagger/v1/swagger.json still returns the spec, so the dev workflow is unaffected.
- Confirm `dotnet build` succeeds with no warnings/errors after the edit.
- After deploy, hit https://<prod-host>/swagger and /swagger/v1/swagger.json and confirm 404.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** If the production deployment (IIS web.config, Docker env, or host) sets ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT to 'Development' or leaves it unset on a Development-defaulting host, the IsDevelopment() guard will still expose Swagger — verify the deployed env var is 'Production'/'Staging'. Per the verifier, this finding on its own is Medium because unauthenticated report/export endpoints already reject calls; the severe attacker-runs-every-endpoint scenario only materializes combined with the weak-JWT-key finding, so address that JWT key finding too. Rollback is trivial: move the two calls back outside the if-block. No data migration or ordering concerns with other middleware.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Recommendation is sound: wrap UseSwagger/UseSwaggerUI in if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) or gate behind auth + non-public route. Note ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT in launchSettings is 'Development' for local profiles; production environment is set by the host/deployment, and with no guard Swagger is served regardless. The launchSettings:6 location is technically correct but only affects local browser launch, not production exposure — the real defect is purely Program.cs:109-115. The 'unauthenticated attacker can exercise every endpoint directly from Swagger' wording is only true when combined with the separate weak-JWT-key finding; on its own the report/export endpoints reject unauthenticated calls.


<a id="f-compose-hardcoded-sa-password"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — Hardcoded SQL SA password and personal host path baked into docker-compose

**ID:** `compose-hardcoded-sa-password` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P0 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Secrets Management &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `docker-compose.dev.yml:11`
- `docker-compose.dev.yml:14`
- `docker-compose.dev.yml:29`

**Problem.** docker-compose.dev.yml hardcodes the MSSQL SA password (SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994) directly in the committed compose file and repeats it in the backend ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection env. It also bind-mounts the database data directory from a developer-specific absolute path (/Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB), and uses TrustServerCertificate=True. This file is tracked in git.

**Impact.** A second, different SQL admin password is leaked in the repo. The personal-path volume mount means the compose file is non-portable and will silently fail or mount nothing for any other operator/CI, and bind-mounting raw mssql data dirs across host OSes (this is a macOS path) commonly corrupts the database. TrustServerCertificate=True disables TLS validation, enabling MITM on the DB channel.

**Recommendation.** Move SA_PASSWORD and connection strings into a git-ignored .env consumed via ${SA_PASSWORD}; use a named Docker volume (e.g. mssql-data:) instead of a host-specific absolute bind mount; remove TrustServerCertificate or pin a real CA. Rotate the leaked dev password.

**Example:**

```
# current
environment:
  - SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994
volumes:
  - /Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB:/var/opt/mssql/data

# fixed
environment:
  - SA_PASSWORD=${SA_PASSWORD:?set in .env}
volumes:
  - mssql-data:/var/opt/mssql/data
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Externalize the SA password and connection string into a git-ignored .env file referenced via ${SA_PASSWORD} interpolation, replace the developer-specific host bind mount with a named Docker volume, and rotate the leaked dev password.

1. Pick a new strong dev SA password to replace the leaked `Saobran131994` (MSSQL requires >=8 chars with upper/lower/digit/symbol). This rotation is mandatory because the old value is in git history.
2. Create `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.env` (already git-ignored by lines 11-12 of `.gitignore`) containing the secret, e.g. a single line `SA_PASSWORD=<new-strong-password>`. Docker Compose auto-loads a `.env` file sitting next to the compose file.
3. Create a committed template `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.env.example` (allowed by `.gitignore` line 13 `!.env.example`) with `SA_PASSWORD=` (no value) so other operators know what to set.
4. In `docker-compose.dev.yml` line 11, replace the hardcoded `SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994` under the `db` service with the interpolated, fail-fast form `SA_PASSWORD=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env}`.
5. In `docker-compose.dev.yml` line 14, replace the host-specific bind mount `/Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB:/var/opt/mssql/data` with the named volume `mssql-data:/var/opt/mssql/data` so the file is portable across operators/CI and avoids cross-OS mssql data-dir corruption.
6. In `docker-compose.dev.yml` line 29, change the `backend` service's `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection` to interpolate the same secret and stop trusting any server cert: `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env};MultipleActiveResultSets=true;` (drop `TrustServerCertificate=True`).
7. Add a top-level `volumes:` block at the bottom of `docker-compose.dev.yml` (there is currently none) declaring the named volume: `volumes:` / `  mssql-data:` so the `mssql-data` reference resolves.
8. Optional but recommended: purge the leaked value from history with `git filter-repo --replace-text` (or BFG) on `docker-compose.dev.yml`, since rotating alone does not remove `Saobran131994` from prior commits.
9. Separately file/flag the higher-severity sibling issue the verifier called out: the production SA password `Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022` is committed in plaintext in `Backend/appsettings.json` (and duplicated under `Backend/bin/...`); that is a distinct finding and should be rotated and externalized too.

```yaml
# docker-compose.dev.yml

  db:
    ...
    environment:
      - ACCEPT_EULA=Y
-     - SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994
+     - SA_PASSWORD=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env}
      - MSSQL_PID=Express
    volumes:
-     - /Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB:/var/opt/mssql/data
+     - mssql-data:/var/opt/mssql/data
    ...

  backend:
    ...
    environment:
      - ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development
-     - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=Saobran131994;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;TrustServerCertificate=True;
+     - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=${SA_PASSWORD:?set SA_PASSWORD in .env};MultipleActiveResultSets=true;
    ...

networks:
  app-network:
    external: true

+volumes:
+  mssql-data:

# .env  (NOT committed — already matched by .gitignore lines 11-12)
SA_PASSWORD=<new-strong-password>

# .env.example  (committed — allowed by .gitignore line 13 "!.env.example")
SA_PASSWORD=
```

**Verify:**
- Run `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml config` from the repo root: the rendered output must show the resolved `SA_PASSWORD`/connection string with no literal `Saobran131994`, `TrustServerCertificate`, or `/Users/saobranaung/...` path.
- Temporarily rename `.env` and run `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml config` again — it must fail with `error while interpolating ... set SA_PASSWORD in .env`, proving the secret is required and not defaulted.
- `grep -RnE 'Saobran131994|/Users/saobranaung/Database|TrustServerCertificate' docker-compose.dev.yml` returns nothing.
- `git status` shows `.env` is untracked/ignored and `.env.example` is the only new tracked env file; `git check-ignore .env` prints `.env`.
- Bring the stack up with `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up -d`, confirm `docker volume ls` lists the `mssql-data` named volume, and that the backend connects to SQL Server (db healthy, no login failures in `docker logs dev-sqlserver`).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Rotation is required, not optional: editing the compose file does not remove `Saobran131994` from git history — change the actual SQL SA password and consider a history rewrite (git filter-repo/BFG), which requires a coordinated force-push. Switching from the host bind mount to a named `mssql-data` volume starts with an EMPTY database; the prior data under `/Users/saobranaung/Database/Template/DB` is not migrated, so reseed/restore TemplateDB after first `up` (or do a one-time copy into the named volume) if existing data matters. Dropping `TrustServerCertificate=True` means SQL Server must present a cert the client trusts; the official mssql image uses a self-signed cert, so for local dev you may need `Encrypt=False` (acceptable on the internal compose network) rather than re-adding `TrustServerCertificate=True`, which silently disables TLS validation. The `app-network` is declared `external: true`, so `docker network create app-network` must exist before `up`. The MSSQL image enforces password complexity; a weak rotated value makes the `db` container exit on boot.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** All three cited locations (lines 11, 14, 29) are exact and correct. The recommendation (move to git-ignored .env via ${SA_PASSWORD}, use a named volume instead of host bind mount, drop TrustServerCertificate, rotate the dev password) is sound. One caveat for the report: the more impactful secrets exposure is actually Backend/appsettings.json (production SA password Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022 committed in plaintext, also duplicated under Backend/bin/... build output) — that is a separate, higher-severity finding the team should be aware of if not already filed.


<a id="f-cors-allowcredentials-localhost-wildcards"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — CORS allows credentials with localhost wildcards and a trailing-slash production origin

**ID:** `cors-allowcredentials-localhost-wildcards` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Deployment Config &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Program.cs:135`
- `Backend/Program.cs:149`
- `Backend/Program.cs:153`

**Problem.** The single global CORS policy registers many localhost origins (including the pattern 'http://localhost:*') alongside production origins and finishes with .AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader().AllowCredentials(). The WithMethods chain also redundantly adds '*'. The same policy (including localhost) is applied in production since there is no environment branch.

**Impact.** Permitting localhost and wildcard-port origins with AllowCredentials in the production policy lets a malicious page running locally (or any attacker who can get a victim to a localhost-bound listener, common in desktop/mobile webview scenarios) make authenticated cross-origin calls to the government API and read responses. Mixing dev origins into the prod policy widens the trust boundary unnecessarily.

**Recommendation.** Define separate CORS policies per environment: in production restrict origins to the exact HTTPS production domains only (no localhost, no '*' methods), and drop AllowCredentials unless cookie auth is actually used (this API uses bearer tokens). Remove the duplicate WithMethods('*').

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30 min)_**

Replace the single inline `app.UseCors(builder => ...)` block in Backend/Program.cs with a config-driven, environment-aware named policy: production gets only the exact HTTPS production origins (no localhost, no trailing slash, no method/header wildcards) and no AllowCredentials (this API authenticates with JWT bearer tokens, not cookies); development keeps the localhost origins via appsettings.Development.json.

1. In Backend/appsettings.json, add a top-level `Cors` section listing only the exact production HTTPS origins (no trailing slashes, no localhost). Top-level keys today are Logging, AllowedHosts, JWT, ExcelExport, ConnectionStrings; add: "Cors": { "AllowedOrigins": [ "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com", "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com", "https://www.mpuecomuat.com", "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com" ] }
2. In Backend/appsettings.Development.json, add a `Cors:AllowedOrigins` array that includes the dev origins (capacitor://localhost, http://localhost, http://localhost:3000, http://localhost:5173, http://localhost:8100, https://localhost) so local React/Vite (5173) and Ionic webviews still work without touching prod config.
3. In Backend/Program.cs, register a named CORS policy via builder.Services.AddCors(...) right before `var app = builder.Build();` (currently line 97). Read origins from config: `var corsOrigins = builder.Configuration.GetSection("Cors:AllowedOrigins").Get<string[]>() ?? Array.Empty<string>();` (Array.Empty is in System, already imported at line 14).
4. In the AddCors call, build one named policy 'TradeNetCors' that calls `.WithOrigins(corsOrigins)`, `.WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE")`, `.WithHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type")`. Do NOT call AllowAnyMethod(), AllowAnyHeader(), AllowCredentials(), or add the "*" method — the API uses bearer tokens (see AddJwtBearer at Program.cs:72), so credentialed CORS is unnecessary.
5. Replace the entire inline CORS block at Backend/Program.cs lines 118-156 (the `#region Cors` ... `#endregion` wrapping `app.UseCors(builder => { ... })`) with a single `app.UseCors("TradeNetCors");`, keeping it after app.UseHttpsRedirection() and before app.UseRouting()/UseAuthentication().
6. Delete the now-dead inline origin list entirely, including the literal "http://localhost:*" (a no-op: WithOrigins does exact string matching and never matches a real Origin header), every trailing-slash origin (e.g. "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com/", "http://localhost:3000/" — origins are sent without a trailing slash so these never match), and the redundant per-method WithMethods(...) chain plus the duplicate WithMethods("*").
7. Leave the pre-existing duplicate `app.UseHttpsRedirection()` calls (Program.cs:117 and :159) as-is; they are out of scope for this finding.

```csharp
// Backend/Program.cs

// 1) Add BEFORE `var app = builder.Build();` (currently line 97):
var corsOrigins = builder.Configuration
    .GetSection("Cors:AllowedOrigins").Get<string[]>() ?? Array.Empty<string>();
builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("TradeNetCors", policy =>
    {
        policy.WithOrigins(corsOrigins)
              .WithMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE")
              .WithHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type");
        // No AllowCredentials(): this API uses JWT bearer tokens, not cookies.
    });
});

// 2) REPLACE the whole block at lines 118-156:
//    #region Cors
//    app.UseCors(builder => { builder.WithMethods("GET"); ... builder.WithMethods("*");
//        builder.WithOrigins("https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com", ...,
//        "http://localhost:*", "http://localhost:3000/")
//        .AllowAnyMethod().AllowAnyHeader().AllowCredentials(); });
//    #endregion
// WITH:
#region Cors
app.UseCors("TradeNetCors");
#endregion

/* Backend/appsettings.json  (production — no localhost, no trailing slashes) */
"Cors": {
  "AllowedOrigins": [
    "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
    "https://testingvehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
    "https://www.mpu-ecommerce.com",
    "https://www.mpuecomuat.com",
    "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com"
  ]
}

/* Backend/appsettings.Development.json  (dev only — localhost allowed here) */
"Cors": {
  "AllowedOrigins": [
    "capacitor://localhost",
    "http://localhost",
    "http://localhost:3000",
    "http://localhost:5173",
    "http://localhost:8100",
    "https://localhost",
    "https://vehicle.myanmartradenet.com",
    "https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com"
  ]
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: `dotnet build` in Backend/ succeeds with no CORS-related errors.
- Run the API in Production environment (ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production) and send a preflight from a disallowed origin: `curl -i -X OPTIONS https://<host>/api/<any-controller> -H 'Origin: http://localhost:5173' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'` — the response must NOT contain an Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.
- Send the same preflight with an allowed prod origin: `curl -i -X OPTIONS https://<host>/api/... -H 'Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET'` — response includes `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://reportuat.myanmartradenet.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET,POST,PUT,DELETE`, and does NOT include `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
- Run the app in Development and confirm the Vite frontend at http://localhost:5173 still reaches the API (browser console shows no CORS error on report calls).
- Grep confirms cleanup: `grep -nE 'localhost:\*|AllowCredentials|AllowAnyMethod|WithMethods\("\*"\)' Backend/Program.cs` returns nothing.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Order matters: AddCors must be called on builder.Services BEFORE builder.Build(); keep UseCors before UseAuthentication/UseAuthorization as it already is. Origins in WithOrigins must have NO trailing slash and NO path — the browser Origin header is scheme+host+port only; a trailing slash silently never matches. Dropping AllowCredentials is safe ONLY because auth is bearer-token (Authorization header) per AddJwtBearer at Program.cs:72 — if cookie-based auth is later added, you'd need credentialed CORS with an explicit origin allowlist (never combined with AllowAnyOrigin). Ensure the Production deployment actually sets ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production so appsettings.json (not Development) supplies the origins; otherwise prod could pick up dev localhost origins. Rollback: revert Program.cs and the two appsettings files; no data/migration impact.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** One technical correction to the impact narrative: ASP.NET Core's WithOrigins does NOT support wildcard ports. "http://localhost:*" is treated as a literal origin string matched by exact (case-insensitive) comparison, so it does not actually match arbitrary localhost ports — no browser sends Origin: http://localhost:* . The real exposure comes from the explicitly listed localhost origins (http://localhost, :3000, :5173, :8100), not from a functioning port wildcard. The finder's phrase "the pattern 'http://localhost:*'" overstates this. Severity stays Medium but with a caveat on exploitability: because this API uses bearer tokens rather than cookies, AllowCredentials() does not cause the browser to auto-attach ambient credentials, so a malicious localhost page would still need a valid token to read authenticated responses; the practical attack surface is narrower than the "make authenticated cross-origin calls and read responses" framing implies. Still a legitimate defense-in-depth/hardening issue for a government API (dev/localhost origins should not be in the production policy, and AllowCredentials should be dropped), and the trailing-slash production origin at line 140 is dead weight (origins never include a trailing slash). Locations are accurate as cited.


<a id="f-containers-run-as-root-no-multistage"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — All containers run as root; backend uses dev SDK image with no multi-stage or .dockerignore

**ID:** `containers-run-as-root-no-multistage` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P2 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Container Hardening &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/Dockerfile.dev:2`
- `Backend/Dockerfile.dev:21`
- `Backend/Dockerfile.dev:31`
- `Frontend/dockerfile:20`

**Problem.** No Dockerfile declares a USER directive, so every container runs as root. The only backend Dockerfile (Dockerfile.dev) is built FROM the full dotnet/sdk:8.0 image, runs `dotnet watch run` (a dev server) and `dotnet ef database update` at container start, and there is no .dockerignore in Backend (confirmed) so the entire context — including the secret-laden appsettings.json, bin/, obj/, and EF tooling — is copied into the image. There is no production multi-stage runtime (aspnet) image at all.

**Impact.** A container breakout or RCE in the app runs with root privileges. Shipping the SDK + EF migration tooling + source + secrets in the image massively enlarges the attack surface and image size, and auto-applying `dotnet ef database update` at startup means a compromised or misconfigured container can mutate the live schema. Running `dotnet watch` (hot-reload dev server) as the deployable artifact is not production-safe.

**Recommendation.** Create a proper multi-stage production Dockerfile: build on sdk:8.0, publish, then run on the slim aspnet:8.0 runtime; add `USER app` (or a created non-root user); add a Backend/.dockerignore excluding bin/, obj/, appsettings*.json, .git; do not run dotnet watch or auto EF migrations in production (run migrations as a gated, separate step).

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours)_**

Replace the SDK-based dev image as the deployable artifact with a real multi-stage production Dockerfile (sdk build/publish -> slim aspnet runtime) that runs as the built-in non-root `app` user on non-privileged port 8080, add a Backend/.dockerignore to keep secrets/bin/obj/EF tooling out of the context, and add a `USER nginx` directive to the Frontend nginx stage. Keep Dockerfile.dev for local dev only; move EF migrations out of container startup into a gated step.

1. Create `Backend/.dockerignore` (does not currently exist) to shrink the build context and keep secrets/build artifacts out of the image. At minimum exclude: `bin/`, `obj/`, `**/bin/`, `**/obj/`, `.git`, `.gitignore`, `Dockerfile*`, `appsettings*.json` (the committed `appsettings.json` holds the live `sa` credentials), `*.user`, `App_Data/`, `.vs/`. Note: excluding appsettings*.json means config must be supplied at runtime via env vars (the docker-compose already passes `ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection`), so do NOT rely on the baked-in file in prod.
2. Add a new file `Backend/Dockerfile` (production, separate from the existing `Dockerfile.dev`) as a multi-stage build. Stage 1 `build` uses `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0`, restores against `API.csproj` (the assembly/output is `API.dll`), and runs `dotnet publish API.csproj -c Release -o /app/publish --no-restore`. Stage 2 `final` uses the slim runtime `mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0`, installs tzdata for the existing `TZ=Asia/Yangon` requirement, copies only `/app/publish` from the build stage, switches to the image's built-in non-root user with `USER app`, exposes 8080, and sets `ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://0.0.0.0:8080`. The entrypoint is `dotnet API.dll` only — NOT `dotnet watch run` and NOT `dotnet ef database update` (see step 4).
3. Do NOT install `dotnet-ef` or run `dotnet ef database update` in the production image (the dev image does this at `Dockerfile.dev:26-31`). The `app` user is non-root and the runtime image has no SDK/EF tooling. Run migrations as a separate, explicitly gated operational step (e.g. CI/CD job or one-off `dotnet ef database update` against a connection string, or generate an idempotent SQL script via `dotnet ef migrations script --idempotent` and apply it with DBA review) so a running app container can no longer mutate the live schema on boot.
4. Harden the Frontend image at `Frontend/dockerfile:20`. The final stage is `FROM nginx:alpine` and runs as root. Add a `USER nginx` directive after configuring nginx. The stock nginx config listens on port 80 which a non-root user cannot bind; either (a) keep root only for the master process by leaving USER as-is and document it, or (b) preferred: switch the listen port to 8080 in a custom nginx.conf, `COPY` it in, then add `USER nginx`. Also fix the latent path bug on `Frontend/dockerfile:21`: it copies `--from=0 /app/build/` but Vite outputs to `dist/` (confirmed: `Frontend/dist` exists, no `build.outDir` override in `vite.config.ts`), so change the source to `/app/dist/`.
5. Update `docker-compose.dev.yml` only if you want compose to build the prod image; otherwise leave it pointing at `Dockerfile.dev` for local development. If you add a production compose file, point `backend.build.dockerfile` at the new `Backend/Dockerfile`, drop the `volumes: - ./Backend:/app` bind mount (it would shadow the published app), and map the host port to container `8080` instead of `5000`.
6. Remove the hardcoded SA password from `docker-compose.dev.yml` (`SA_PASSWORD=Saobran131994`, lines 11 and 29) by moving it to a `.env`/secret — this is the same credential the .dockerignore now keeps out of the image, so the two changes must be consistent. (Tracked under the hardcoded-secrets finding; mentioned here so the .dockerignore exclusion of appsettings*.json doesn't break the app while leaving the secret in compose.)

```dockerfile
# ===== NEW FILE: Backend/.dockerignore =====
bin/
obj/
**/bin/
**/obj/
.git
.gitignore
.vs/
Dockerfile*
**/*.user
App_Data/
# keep secrets out of the image; supply config via env vars at runtime
appsettings*.json

# ===== NEW FILE: Backend/Dockerfile (production, multi-stage, non-root) =====
# ---------- Build stage ----------
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY API.csproj ./
RUN dotnet restore API.csproj
COPY . .
RUN dotnet publish API.csproj -c Release -o /app/publish --no-restore

# ---------- Runtime stage (slim, non-root) ----------
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 AS final
ENV TZ=Asia/Yangon
RUN apt-get update \
    && DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends tzdata \
    && ln -fs /usr/share/zoneinfo/$TZ /etc/localtime \
    && apt-get clean && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /app/publish .
# aspnet:8.0 ships a built-in non-root user named 'app'
USER app
EXPOSE 8080
ENV ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://0.0.0.0:8080
# NO dotnet watch, NO `dotnet ef database update` at startup
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "API.dll"]

# ===== EDIT: Frontend/dockerfile (final nginx stage) =====
# BEFORE
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=0 /app/build/ /usr/share/nginx/html
EXPOSE 80
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
# AFTER (fix dist path + run as non-root nginx)
FROM nginx:alpine
COPY --from=0 /app/dist/ /usr/share/nginx/html
COPY nginx.conf /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf   # listen 8080; user dirs writable by nginx
EXPOSE 8080
USER nginx
CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]
```

**Verify:**
- Build the prod image: `docker build -f Backend/Dockerfile -t tradenet-api:prod ./Backend`. Confirm it succeeds and is built on aspnet (not sdk): `docker image inspect tradenet-api:prod --format '{{.Config.Cmd}} {{.Config.Entrypoint}}'` shows `dotnet API.dll` and no `watch`/`ef`.
- Confirm the container runs as non-root: `docker run --rm tradenet-api:prod id` (or `docker run --rm --entrypoint id tradenet-api:prod`) prints `uid=1654(app)` style output, NOT `uid=0(root)`.
- Confirm secrets/tooling are gone: `docker run --rm --entrypoint sh tradenet-api:prod -c 'ls /app; which dotnet-ef || echo no-ef'` shows `API.dll` present, no `appsettings.json`, and `no-ef`.
- Confirm context shrank: with `Backend/.dockerignore` in place, `docker build` output shows a small build context (not hundreds of MB including bin/obj), and the final image is materially smaller than the SDK-based dev image (`docker images` size comparison).
- Run end-to-end with config injected via env: `docker run --rm -p 8080:8080 -e ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection='...' tradenet-api:prod` and hit a controller route (e.g. swagger or a report endpoint) on `http://localhost:8080` — verify it serves without the baked appsettings.json.
- Frontend: `docker build -f Frontend/dockerfile -t tradenet-fe:prod ./Frontend`, then `docker run --rm --entrypoint id tradenet-fe:prod` shows the nginx (non-root) user, and the served HTML is non-empty (proves the `/app/dist/` COPY path fix worked).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** The aspnet:8.0 image's built-in `app` user cannot bind to privileged port 80, which is why the runtime uses 8080 and `ASPNETCORE_URLS` is set explicitly — any compose/ingress port mapping must target 8080, not 5000/80.; Excluding `appsettings*.json` via .dockerignore means the app will fail to start if it relies on a baked connection string. Ensure all required config (ConnectionStrings, JWT signing key, AWS S3 settings) is provided at runtime via env vars before deploying; otherwise the container starts as non-root but crashes on missing config.; Removing `dotnet ef database update` from startup means schema changes will NOT auto-apply. Migrations must be applied as a deliberate step before/at deploy; coordinate so a new release doesn't run against an un-migrated DB. This is intentional (prevents a compromised container from mutating the live schema) but is an operational behavior change.; The Frontend `USER nginx` change requires the listen port to move to 8080 (custom nginx.conf) and the cache/run/pid dirs to be writable by the nginx user; the stock nginx:alpine config listens on 80 and writes pid to /run as root. If you skip the custom conf, nginx will fail to bind/write as non-root.; Keep `Backend/Dockerfile.dev` for local hot-reload development; do not delete it. The new `Backend/Dockerfile` is the deployable artifact. Make sure CI/deploy references the new `-f Backend/Dockerfile`, not `Dockerfile.dev`.; Rollback: the change is additive (new Dockerfile, new .dockerignore) plus a small Frontend edit; reverting is just removing the two new files and restoring the Frontend nginx stage lines.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Title's 'no multi-stage' applies to Backend only; the Frontend dockerfile IS multi-stage (node->nginx) and is cited only for the root-user issue (line 20 nginx stage), which is valid. All four locations are accurate. Caveat: these are explicitly dev artifacts (Dockerfile.dev, docker-compose.dev.yml) and no production backend Dockerfile exists in the repo — the finding's risk hinges on the dev image being what gets deployed (a reasonable inference since it is the only backend image present). Could justify a higher rating because real live sa credentials are committed in appsettings.json, but that secret-exposure angle is likely covered by a separate hardcoded-secrets finding; Medium for the container-hardening dimension is sound.


<a id="f-no-ci-security-scanning"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — No CI security scanning or backend CI; main branch excluded from tests

**ID:** `no-ci-security-scanning` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** CI/CD &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml:6-12 (branches-ignore main/master on push+pull_request), test.yml:18-20 (Node 16/18 matrix), test.yml:36 (npm test step — file is only 37 lines, so cited :39 overshoots), release.yml:18 (Node 20.18.0)`
- `Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml:7`
- `Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml:39`
- `Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml:1`

**Problem.** The only CI workflows are frontend-only (changeset release, commitlint, test). There is no dependency/secret/SAST scanning (no CodeQL, npm audit, dotnet, Trivy, gitleaks, Dependabot). test.yml is gated with branches-ignore: [main, master] and pull_request branches-ignore main — so nothing runs on the main branch or PRs into it. test.yml targets Node 16/18 while the app builds on Node 20 (release.yml), and runs `npm test` though no test script exists in package.json. There is no CI at all for the .NET backend or its Backend.Tests project.

**Impact.** Vulnerable dependencies, leaked secrets (such as the committed appsettings.json), and backend regressions ship undetected. A secret scanner in CI would have caught the production sa credentials before merge. The main branch — what actually deploys — has zero automated gating.

**Recommendation.** Add gitleaks/secret-scanning and Dependabot to the repo; add CodeQL for JS and C#; run frontend build/lint and `dotnet build`/`dotnet test` on PRs into main on the supported Node 20 / .NET 8 versions; fix or remove the dead `npm test` step.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 3-4 hours)_**

Add repo-root CI that gates the main branch: secret scanning (gitleaks), dependency/SAST scanning (Dependabot + CodeQL for JS & C#), and real build/lint/test jobs for both the Node 20 frontend and the .NET 8 backend. Fix the broken existing test.yml (wrong branch gating, EOL Node 16/18, dead `npm test`) and relocate it to where GitHub will actually run it.

1. FIRST, fix the workflow LOCATION bug. All workflows currently live in `Frontend/.github/workflows/` but this is a SINGLE git repo rooted at `tradenet-admin-report/` (confirmed: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` = repo root, no `Frontend/.git`). GitHub Actions ONLY reads `<repo-root>/.github/workflows/`, so test.yml/release.yml/commitlint.yml almost certainly never run today. Create `/.github/workflows/` at the repo root and place ALL new workflows there. Move (git mv) the three existing files from `Frontend/.github/workflows/` to the repo-root `.github/workflows/` as part of this work, adjusting their `working-directory`/paths since they now run from repo root.
2. Create `/.github/dependabot.yml` (repo root) with three ecosystems: `npm` rooted at `/Frontend`, `nuget` rooted at `/Backend` (and `/Backend.Tests`), and `github-actions` rooted at `/` — each on a `weekly` schedule. This wires automated dependency-update PRs (npm audit equivalent) for the React app, the .NET 8 API, and the Actions themselves.
3. Add `/.github/workflows/codeql.yml` for SAST. Use `github/codeql-action` v3 with a matrix of two languages: `javascript-typescript` (autobuild is fine for the Vite/TS frontend) and `csharp` (manual build mode: run `dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release` so CodeQL can trace the .NET 8 compilation). Trigger on `push` to `main`, `pull_request` into `main`, and a weekly `schedule`. Grant `security-events: write` permissions.
4. Add `/.github/workflows/gitleaks.yml` for secret scanning using `gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2` with `fetch-depth: 0` checkout so full history is scanned. Trigger on `push` to `main` and `pull_request`. This is the control that would have caught the committed `appsettings.json` sa credentials (a separate finding). Set `GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}` env. Note: gitleaks-action requires `GITLEAKS_LICENSE` only for GitHub orgs; for the personal `SakBran` repo it runs free.
5. Replace the broken frontend test workflow. In the relocated `/.github/workflows/test.yml`: (a) change the `on:` triggers from `branches-ignore: [main, master]` (which excludes the deploying branch) to `pull_request: { branches: [main] }` plus `push: { branches: [main] }`; (b) replace the Node `16.x`/`18.x` matrix with a single `node-version: 20.18.0` to match release.yml and the actual build target; (c) bump `actions/checkout@v3`/`actions/setup-node@v3` to `@v4` and add `cache: npm` with `cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json`; (d) add `working-directory: ./Frontend` (via `defaults.run.working-directory`) since the workflow now runs from repo root; (e) replace the dead `npm test` step with `npm run lint` (the real script that exists in Frontend/package.json — there is no `test` script) and keep `npm run build`; use `npm ci` instead of `npm install`.
6. Add a new `/.github/workflows/backend-ci.yml` for the .NET 8 backend (none exists today). Trigger on `pull_request`/`push` to `main`. Steps: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` with `dotnet-version: 8.0.x`, `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`, `dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-restore`, and `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-build` (runs the existing xUnit `Backend.Tests` project).
7. After verifying the new root workflows run green, configure GitHub branch protection on `main` (Settings → Branches): require the new CodeQL, gitleaks, frontend test, and backend-ci checks to pass before merge. CI files alone do not block merges without this rule.
8. Optional cleanup: the relocated `release.yml` already correctly targets `push: branches: [main]` and Node 20.18.0 — only update its checkout/setup-node to `@v4` and add `working-directory: ./Frontend` for the npm steps. `commitlint.yml` needs no logic change beyond the relocation and a checkout bump to `@v4`.

```yaml
name: Backend CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  build-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
      - run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      - run: dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-restore
      - run: dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release --no-build
---
# .github/workflows/codeql.yml
name: CodeQL
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request: { branches: [main] }
  schedule: [{ cron: '23 4 * * 1' }]
jobs:
  analyze:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions: { security-events: write, actions: read, contents: read }
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        include:
          - language: javascript-typescript
            build-mode: none
          - language: csharp
            build-mode: manual
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: github/codeql-action/init@v3
        with: { languages: ${{ matrix.language }}, build-mode: ${{ matrix.build-mode }} }
      - if: matrix.build-mode == 'manual'
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with: { dotnet-version: 8.0.x }
      - if: matrix.build-mode == 'manual'
        run: dotnet build tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release
      - uses: github/codeql-action/analyze@v3
---
# .github/workflows/test.yml  (BEFORE -> AFTER, key changes)
# - on.push/pull_request: branches-ignore [main, master]  ->  branches [main]
# - node-version matrix 16.x/18.x  ->  single 20.18.0
# - run: npm test (no such script)  ->  run: npm run lint
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request: { branches: [main] }
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults: { run: { working-directory: ./Frontend } }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20.18.0, cache: npm, cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint
      - run: npm run build
---
# .github/dependabot.yml
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: npm
    directory: /Frontend
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
  - package-ecosystem: nuget
    directory: /Backend
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
  - package-ecosystem: github-actions
    directory: /
    schedule: { interval: weekly }
---
# .github/workflows/gitleaks.yml
name: gitleaks
on:
  push: { branches: [main] }
  pull_request:
jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { fetch-depth: 0 }
      - uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
        env: { GITHUB_TOKEN: '${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}' }
```

**Verify:**
- Confirm location fix: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` returns the repo root and the new files exist under `<root>/.github/workflows/` — NOT under Frontend/. Push a no-op commit/PR to `main` and open the Actions tab: previously zero runs appeared for main; now CodeQL, gitleaks, Test, and Backend CI should all trigger.
- Frontend test job: green run that executes `npm run lint` and `npm run build` on Node 20.18.0 (no more 'Missing script: test' failure that the old `npm test` produced).
- Backend CI job: `dotnet build` and `dotnet test` both succeed against `tradenet-admin-report.sln` (net8.0); the xUnit Backend.Tests run is reported.
- Secret scanning: temporarily add a fake high-entropy key to a branch and open a PR — the gitleaks job must fail. Remove the test key before merge.
- CodeQL: after first run, Security tab → Code scanning shows results for both `javascript-typescript` and `csharp`.
- Dependabot: Insights → Dependency graph → Dependabot shows the npm, nuget, and github-actions ecosystems enabled (Dependabot will open update PRs on its schedule).
- Run locally before pushing to catch obvious failures: `cd Frontend && npm ci && npm run lint && npm run build` and `dotnet test tradenet-admin-report.sln -c Release`.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "LOCATION IS THE #1 TRAP: workflows under `Frontend/.github/workflows/` do NOT run for this single repo — GitHub only reads `<repo-root>/.github/workflows/`. If you add CodeQL/backend CI under Frontend/ it will silently never execute. Put everything at the repo root .github/. CI files alone do not enforce anything until you add branch-protection required-status-checks on `main`. `dotnet build` for CodeQL csharp/manual mode and for backend-ci can be slow and may need NuGet restore to succeed in CI — if the API has any machine-specific build deps (SkiaSharp native libs), the ubuntu-latest runner usually has them but watch for native-lib failures; pin a known-good SDK if needed. gitleaks scanning full history (`fetch-depth: 0`) WILL flag the already-committed appsettings.json sa credentials — that is expected and is the point; treat it as the trigger to rotate those creds and purge them (a separate finding), and use a `.gitleaksignore` only for confirmed false positives, never to suppress the real secret. Use `npm ci` not `npm install`; it requires Frontend/package-lock.json to be committed and in sync. Rollback is trivial: the workflow files are additive — delete them and remove dependabot.yml to revert; no runtime/data impact."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Confirmed at Medium. This is a legitimate CI-hygiene / defense-in-depth gap, not a directly exploitable vulnerability — the leaked sa credentials are a separate standalone finding; missing CI scanning is the preventative control that would have caught them. For an internal government admin tool, Medium is appropriate. The only correction is the line number: test.yml is 37 lines, so location :39 should be :36 (the npm test step) and the branches-ignore gating is at lines 6-12 (covering both push and pull_request, not just one). The substance of every claim holds.


<a id="f-no-monitoring-logging-alerting"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — No structured logging, monitoring, or alerting; default console logging only

**ID:** `no-monitoring-logging-alerting` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Observability &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/API.csproj:11`
- `Backend/Program.cs:97`
- `Backend/appsettings.json:2`

**Problem.** The backend has no logging/monitoring packages (no Serilog, Application Insights, OpenTelemetry, or Sentry in API.csproj) and Program.cs adds none — only the framework's default console logger configured at Information level. There is no audit logging of who runs which trade reports/exports, no centralized log sink, and no alerting. The Excel export queue and DB-mutating operations have no externally observable health/audit trail.

**Impact.** For a government system handling sensitive trade data, the absence of audit and centralized logging means a breach (e.g. via the leaked sa creds) would be undetectable and uninvestigable, and operators have no visibility into failures, abuse, or the background export worker's health. No alerting means outages and attacks go unnoticed.

**Recommendation.** Add structured logging (Serilog) with a centralized sink, add request/audit logging for report and export endpoints (user id, report, params), and integrate at least basic health and error alerting (e.g. ASP.NET HealthChecks + an APM/monitoring backend).

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 4-6 hours (code + config); +1-2 hours to stand up the central sink and wire alerting)_**

Add Serilog as the structured logging provider with a centralized/file (and optional Seq) sink, capture an audit trail of every report and Excel-export call via a single request-logging enricher middleware (so all 158 controllers are covered without per-controller edits, using the existing ClaimTypes.Name user id), and expose ASP.NET Core HealthChecks (DB + export worker) for external monitoring/alerting.

1. Add the logging/health packages to Backend/API.csproj inside the existing <ItemGroup> (after the Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Debug line at :25): Serilog.AspNetCore (8.0.x), Serilog.Sinks.File, Serilog.Sinks.Seq (optional central sink), and AspNetCore.HealthChecks.SqlServer (8.0.x). The framework-provided Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks / Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore come transitively with the Web SDK, so no separate add is needed for the EF/self check.
2. In Backend/Program.cs, wire Serilog into the host BEFORE 'var app = builder.Build();' (currently line 97). Add 'using Serilog;' and 'using Serilog.Events;' at the top, then add 'builder.Host.UseSerilog((ctx, sp, cfg) => cfg.ReadFrom.Configuration(ctx.Configuration).ReadFrom.Services(sp).Enrich.FromLogContext());' right after 'var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);' (line 21). This replaces the default console logger with Serilog driven by the Serilog config block in appsettings.json.
3. Register health checks before Build(): 'builder.Services.AddHealthChecks().AddSqlServer(builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("TemplateDB")!, name: "templatedb", tags: new[] {"db"}).AddDbContextCheck<API.DBContext.TradeNetDbContext>("tradenetdb", tags: new[] {"db"});'. (AddDbContextCheck ships with the framework's HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore.)
4. In the HTTP pipeline (after 'app.UseRouting();' near line 160, before UseAuthentication), add Serilog's request logging: 'app.UseSerilogRequestLogging();'. Leave it early enough to time the whole pipeline.
5. Add the audit enrichment so the request log line includes who/what/params. Immediately after UseSerilogRequestLogging, add a lightweight inline middleware that pushes the authenticated user id (the existing ClaimTypes.Name claim used across the report controllers, e.g. ImportPermitVoucherReportController.cs:84) and the query string into Serilog's LogContext: see patch. This makes every POST to /api/<Report> and /api/<Report>/Excel produce an auditable structured line with UserId, path, status, elapsed ms — no edits to the 158 controllers needed.
6. Expose the health endpoints AFTER 'app.MapControllers();' (line 168): 'app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready", new Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = c => c.Tags.Contains("db") });' and 'app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = _ => false });' (liveness = process up, no dependency checks). Add 'using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks;'. Note: /health/* must be reachable for the monitoring probe — the report controllers are [Authorize] but MapHealthChecks endpoints are anonymous by default, which is what you want for an uptime/alert probe.
7. Add the Serilog config block to Backend/appsettings.json. Keep the existing top-level "Logging" block (Serilog ignores it once UseSerilog is active, but leaving it avoids breaking any non-Serilog reader) and add a sibling "Serilog" section: MinimumLevel Information with Microsoft.AspNetCore at Warning, WriteTo File (rolling daily, App_Data/logs/log-.json with a 31-day retention) using CompactJsonFormatter, and optionally WriteTo Seq with serverUrl from config/env for the centralized sink. Do NOT hardcode a Seq API key; read it from configuration/secret. See patch for the exact JSON.
8. Add the same "Serilog" block (or just an override raising console verbosity) to Backend/appsettings.Development.json so local runs still log to console; in Development also add a Console sink. For production, point WriteTo:Seq:Args:serverUrl at the real central log server (or swap the Seq sink for Serilog.Sinks.ApplicationInsights / an OTLP sink if the ministry already runs an APM).
9. Alerting (process/config, no code): configure the monitoring/uptime system (or a load-balancer health probe) to poll GET /health/ready every 30-60s and page on non-200, and in Seq (or the chosen sink's backend) create a signal/alert on '@Level = "Error"' or on the existing 'Excel export {JobId} ... failed' message from ExcelExportWorker.cs:197 so background-worker failures and unhandled errors raise notifications.

```csharp
// ---- Backend/API.csproj : add inside the existing <ItemGroup> (after line 25) ----
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.AspNetCore" Version="8.0.3" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.File" Version="6.0.0" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Seq" Version="8.0.0" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Formatting.Compact" Version="3.0.0" />
<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.SqlServer" Version="8.0.2" />

// ---- Backend/Program.cs ----
// usings (top of file, near line 12)
using Serilog;
using Serilog.Context;
using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks;

// right after: var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args);  (line 21)
builder.Host.UseSerilog((ctx, sp, cfg) => cfg
    .ReadFrom.Configuration(ctx.Configuration)
    .ReadFrom.Services(sp)
    .Enrich.FromLogContext());

// before: var app = builder.Build();  (line 97)  -- add health checks
builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()
    .AddSqlServer(
        builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("TemplateDB")!,
        name: "templatedb", tags: new[] { "db" })
    .AddDbContextCheck<API.DBContext.TradeNetDbContext>(
        "tradenetdb", tags: new[] { "db" });

// in the pipeline, right after app.UseRouting();  (line 160)
app.UseSerilogRequestLogging();   // one structured line per request: method, path, status, elapsed
// audit enricher: attaches the authenticated user id + query string to that line.
// Uses the SAME claim the report controllers read (User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.Name)),
// so it covers all 158 report controllers + ExcelExportController with zero per-controller edits.
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    var userId = context.User?.FindFirst(System.Security.Claims.ClaimTypes.Name)?.Value ?? "anonymous";
    using (LogContext.PushProperty("UserId", userId))
    using (LogContext.PushProperty("QueryString", context.Request.QueryString.Value))
    {
        await next();
    }
});

// after app.MapControllers();  (line 168)
app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready", new HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = c => c.Tags.Contains("db") });
app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live",  new HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = _ => false });

// ---- Backend/appsettings.json : add this sibling section (keep existing "Logging") ----
"Serilog": {
  "MinimumLevel": {
    "Default": "Information",
    "Override": { "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning", "Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore": "Warning" }
  },
  "Enrich": [ "FromLogContext", "WithMachineName" ],
  "WriteTo": [
    { "Name": "File",
      "Args": {
        "path": "App_Data/logs/log-.json",
        "rollingInterval": "Day",
        "retainedFileCountLimit": 31,
        "formatter": "Serilog.Formatting.Compact.CompactJsonFormatter, Serilog.Formatting.Compact"
      } },
    { "Name": "Seq",
      "Args": { "serverUrl": "http://your-central-seq:5341", "apiKey": "" } }
  ]
}
```

**Verify:**
- Build: dotnet build Backend/API.csproj -- must restore the new packages and compile (confirms namespaces/APIs match .NET 8).
- Run the API and curl GET http://localhost:5xxx/health/live -> 200 'Healthy' even with DB down; curl GET /health/ready -> 200 'Healthy' when both SQL connections succeed, 503 'Unhealthy' if you point TemplateDB at a bad server (confirms the DB check actually probes the connection).
- Hit any report endpoint with a valid JWT, e.g. POST /api/ImportPermitVoucherReport, then tail Backend/App_Data/logs/log-<date>.json: confirm a structured line exists with the request method/path, StatusCode, Elapsed, and the enriched UserId (the user Id from the token's ClaimTypes.Name) and QueryString -- this is the audit trail of who ran which report.
- POST /api/ImportPermitVoucherReport/Excel and confirm the corresponding audit line plus, when the background worker processes it, the existing 'Excel export {JobId} ... completed/failed' lines now appear in the file/Seq sink (proves worker logs flow into the centralized sink, not just console).
- If Seq is configured, open the Seq UI and confirm events arrive and that a signal on @Level='Error' fires on a forced failure.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Order matters in Program.cs: UseSerilogRequestLogging and the audit middleware must come AFTER UseRouting/UseAuthentication so context.User is populated (place the audit Use() after UseAuthentication if you find UserId is 'anonymous' for authenticated calls -- the request-logging summary still captures it via LogContext as long as PushProperty wraps next()). The /health/* endpoints are intentionally anonymous; do not put them behind [Authorize] or the uptime probe can't reach them. The File sink writes to App_Data/logs -- ensure the container/app pool user has write permission and that this path is on a persisted volume in the Dockerfile.dev deployment, otherwise logs are lost on restart; for true centralization prefer the Seq/APM sink over File alone. Do NOT commit a real Seq apiKey -- read it from env/UserSecrets. This change does NOT remediate the hardcoded sa credentials in appsettings.json (separate finding); logging the QueryString/params could itself record sensitive trade filters, so restrict log file/sink access accordingly. Rollback is low-risk: remove the UseSerilog/UseSerilogRequestLogging/health lines and the packages to fall back to default console logging.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Medium is appropriate: this is an observability/audit/defense-in-depth gap that amplifies other findings (notably the hardcoded sa credentials in appsettings.json) rather than a directly exploitable vulnerability, so not Critical/High on its own. Cited locations are accurate representative anchors (API.csproj:11 = start of the single ItemGroup of PackageReferences; Program.cs:97 = builder.Build() with no prior logging config; appsettings.json:2 = Logging block). Minor nuance: the workers do use the default ILogger, so it is not literally 'no logging at all' — but the claim is specifically about structured/centralized logging, monitoring, alerting, and audit logging, all of which are genuinely absent.


<a id="f-no-healthchecks-no-resource-limits"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — No container healthchecks or resource limits; restart policies inconsistent

**ID:** `no-healthchecks-no-resource-limits` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Container Orchestration &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `docker-compose.dev.yml:19`
- `docker-compose.dev.yml:35`
- `Frontend/docker-compose.production.yml:1`
- `Backend/Dockerfile.dev:29`

**Problem.** No compose service or Dockerfile defines a HEALTHCHECK, and no service sets CPU/memory limits (no deploy.resources / mem_limit). depends_on is used without condition: service_healthy, so backend starts before the DB is actually accepting connections (only ordering, not readiness). Restart policies are inconsistent: db has restart: unless-stopped but the backend and frontend services in docker-compose.dev.yml have none.

**Impact.** Failed or hung containers are not detected or restarted, a single runaway report/export can starve the host of memory/CPU, and the backend can crash-loop on startup because it boots before SQL Server is ready. This reduces availability and makes incident recovery manual.

**Recommendation.** Add HEALTHCHECK (e.g. an ASP.NET /health endpoint and an nginx/curl check), add depends_on condition: service_healthy for the DB, set mem_limit/cpus (or deploy.resources.limits) per service, and apply a consistent restart policy across all services.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1.5 hours)_**

Add a DB-aware /health endpoint in the .NET backend, then add HEALTHCHECKs, service_healthy gating, a consistent restart policy, and resource limits across the compose services — prioritizing backend-on-DB readiness because the backend CMD runs an EF migration at boot.

1. Add health-check packages to Backend/API.csproj. Add `<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.SqlServer" Version="8.0.2" />` (this version targets net8.0). The EF-context check API (`AddDbContextCheck`) is part of `Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore` — add `<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Diagnostics.HealthChecks.EntityFrameworkCore" Version="8.0.17" />` if you prefer the EF-context approach over the raw SqlServer check.
2. In Backend/Program.cs, register health checks during service configuration. Insert immediately after the Excel export queue line (`builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);`, currently line 95) and before `var app = builder.Build();` (line 97). Register a DbContext check against ApplicationDbContext so /health reflects DB reachability: `builder.Services.AddHealthChecks().AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>(name: "templatedb");` (using the EF package). ApplicationDbContext is already pooled (registered at line 64 against the TemplateDB/DefaultConnection string), so the DI scope resolves correctly.
3. In Backend/Program.cs, map the health endpoint. Add `app.MapHealthChecks("/health");` right after `app.MapControllers();` (line 168). Map it as anonymous/unauthenticated so the container probe needs no JWT — `MapHealthChecks` is not affected by `app.UseAuthorization()` unless you add `.RequireAuthorization()`, so leaving it bare keeps it public. If you want a liveness probe that does NOT hit the DB, also add `app.MapHealthChecks("/health/live", new HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = _ => false });` (requires `using Microsoft.AspNetCore.Diagnostics.HealthChecks;`).
4. Edit docker-compose.dev.yml `db` service (lines 4-17): keep `restart: unless-stopped`, and add a HEALTHCHECK using the sqlcmd inside the mssql image plus resource limits. Add under the service:
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "/opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P \"$$SA_PASSWORD\" -No -Q 'SELECT 1' || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10
      start_period: 30s
    mem_limit: 2g
    cpus: 2.0
(Note: the 2022-latest image ships sqlcmd under /opt/mssql-tools18/bin and requires the -No/-C flag for the self-signed cert; verify the path with `docker exec dev-sqlserver ls /opt/mssql-tools18/bin`.)
5. Edit docker-compose.dev.yml `backend` service (lines 19-33): change the bare `depends_on: - db` (lines 32-33) to gate on DB readiness, add a HEALTHCHECK hitting the new /health endpoint on the in-container port 5000, add a restart policy, and add resource limits:
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:5000/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 15s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 60s
    mem_limit: 1g
    cpus: 1.5
(The sdk:8.0 Debian image includes wget; if not, swap to `curl -fsS`. Use port 5000, not the host-mapped 5001, since the probe runs inside the container.)
6. Edit docker-compose.dev.yml `frontend` service (lines 35-46): gate it on backend health, add restart + resource limits. Change `depends_on: - backend` (lines 43-44) to:
    depends_on:
      backend:
        condition: service_healthy
    restart: unless-stopped
    mem_limit: 512m
    cpus: 1.0
For the Vite dev server (node:lts-alpine) add a wget-based check on 5173:
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:5173 || exit 1"]
      interval: 20s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 30s
7. Add a HEALTHCHECK to the production frontend image. In Frontend/dockerfile (final nginx:alpine stage, after `EXPOSE 80`), add `HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 CMD wget -qO- http://localhost/ || exit 1` (nginx:alpine includes wget via busybox). Optionally also add resource limits + the same healthcheck to Frontend/docker-compose.production.yml `react-app` service which already has `restart: unless-stopped`.
8. Optional but recommended: add a HEALTHCHECK directly in Backend/Dockerfile.dev after `EXPOSE 5000 5001` so the image is self-describing even when run outside compose: `HEALTHCHECK --interval=15s --timeout=5s --start-period=60s --retries=5 CMD wget -qO- http://localhost:5000/health || exit 1`.

```yaml
// Backend/Program.cs — register + map health checks
// after line 95 (AddExcelExportQueue), before line 97 (builder.Build())
builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);
builder.Services.AddHealthChecks()
    .AddDbContextCheck<ApplicationDbContext>(name: "templatedb");   // <-- ADD

var app = builder.Build();
...
app.MapControllers();
app.MapHealthChecks("/health");                                      // <-- ADD (anonymous)
app.UseStaticFiles();
app.Run();

# docker-compose.dev.yml — backend service (was lines 19-33)
  backend:
    build:
      context: ./Backend
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.dev
    ports:
      - "5001:5000"
    volumes:
      - ./Backend:/app
    environment:
      - ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development
      - ConnectionStrings__DefaultConnection=Server=db;Database=TemplateDB;User=sa;Password=Saobran131994;MultipleActiveResultSets=true;TrustServerCertificate=True;
    networks:
      - app-network
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy      # was:  - db   (ordering only)
    restart: unless-stopped             # ADD (was missing -> inconsistent)
    healthcheck:                        # ADD
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:5000/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 15s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 60s
    mem_limit: 1g                       # ADD
    cpus: 1.5                           # ADD

# docker-compose.dev.yml — db service healthcheck (add under existing restart: unless-stopped, line 17)
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "/opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd -S localhost -U sa -P \"$$SA_PASSWORD\" -No -Q 'SELECT 1' || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 10
      start_period: 30s
    mem_limit: 2g
    cpus: 2.0
```

**Verify:**
- Build the backend: `dotnet build` in Backend/ must succeed after adding the NuGet packages (confirms AddHealthChecks/AddDbContextCheck/MapHealthChecks resolve).
- Run `docker compose -f docker-compose.dev.yml up --build`. With the SQL volume cold-starting, the backend container should now WAIT (status 'created'/'health: starting') until `dev-sqlserver` reports healthy, instead of failing `dotnet ef database update` and crash-looping.
- Run `docker ps` and confirm each container shows a `(healthy)` status column after start_period elapses. `docker inspect --format '{{json .State.Health}}' <container>` should show Status: healthy.
- Hit the endpoint: `curl -i http://localhost:5001/health` should return HTTP 200 with body `Healthy` while the DB is up, and a 503 `Unhealthy` if you stop the db container (`docker stop dev-sqlserver`) — confirming the probe is DB-aware and unauthenticated (no JWT required).
- Confirm resource caps applied: `docker inspect --format '{{.HostConfig.Memory}} {{.HostConfig.NanoCpus}}' <backend-container>` returns non-zero values.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Inside-container probes must target the container port (backend 5000, not host 5001; frontend 5173, not 3000). The mssql healthcheck uses $$SA_PASSWORD (double $ escapes compose interpolation) and the tools path differs by image: mssql/server:2022-latest uses /opt/mssql-tools18/bin/sqlcmd and needs -No (or -C) for the self-signed cert — verify with `docker exec dev-sqlserver ls /opt/mssql-tools18/bin` before committing. `mem_limit`/`cpus` are Compose v2 spec keys honored by `docker compose` for non-swarm runs (do not use `deploy.resources.limits` here, which is ignored outside swarm). depends_on: condition: service_healthy makes startup slower (backend now waits on DB start_period); keep start_period generous on first cold-volume migration. The /health endpoint is intentionally public — do not chain `.RequireAuthorization()` or the container probe will get 401 and report unhealthy. Rollback is config-only: revert the compose/Program.cs/csproj edits; no data migration involved.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Locations are accurate. Note line 19 in the finding points at the `backend:` service key (missing restart) and line 35 at `frontend:` (missing restart); both are correct. depends_on without condition appears at lines 32-33 and 43-44. The db `restart: unless-stopped` is line 17. The most actionable sub-issue is readiness/restart on the backend given its CMD runs an EF migration at startup; resource limits and frontend healthchecks are secondary. Severity Low is correct given the dev/small-deployment scope; no change recommended.


<a id="f-https-hsts-gaps"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — HTTPS/HSTS configuration gaps: AllowedHosts wildcard, duplicate redirect, dev SSL trust

**ID:** `https-hsts-gaps` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Transport Security &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/appsettings.json:8`
- `Backend/Program.cs:117`
- `Backend/Program.cs:159`
- `Frontend/dockerfile:24`

**Problem.** AllowedHosts is '*' (no host filtering). app.UseHttpsRedirection() is called twice (lines 117 and 159) which is harmless but indicates copy-paste drift, while UseHsts() only runs in non-development with no max-age/preload tuning. The nginx frontend image listens only on port 80 with no TLS config, relying entirely on an undocumented external reverse proxy for HTTPS. Connection strings use TrustServerCertificate=True throughout.

**Impact.** Wildcard AllowedHosts enables host-header attacks (cache poisoning, password-reset/link poisoning patterns). Without an explicit, tuned HSTS policy and with TLS termination left implicit, there is risk of downgrade/MITM if the proxy is misconfigured. TrustServerCertificate=True disables DB TLS validation, allowing MITM on the database channel.

**Recommendation.** Set AllowedHosts to the explicit production hostnames; remove the duplicate UseHttpsRedirection; configure HstsOptions (MaxAge, IncludeSubDomains, Preload); document/codify TLS termination; replace TrustServerCertificate=True with a validated CA certificate for SQL connections.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 2 hours (most of it coordinating the DB server certificate / CA trust with infra))_**

Harden transport config in Backend/Program.cs and Backend/appsettings.json: prioritize removing TrustServerCertificate=True from the two remote, internet-exposed DB connections (203.81.66.111) and switching to validated TLS; add a tuned HstsOptions policy; tighten AllowedHosts; drop the duplicate UseHttpsRedirection; and document that the nginx frontend image relies on an external TLS-terminating reverse proxy. The verifier flagged the SQL DB-channel item as the only substantive fix here, so do that first.

1. PRIORITY 1 (real security item) — fix DB-channel TLS in Backend/appsettings.json lines 26-27. Both 'TemplateDB' and 'TradeNetDBTest' point at the internet-exposed host 203.81.66.111,14330 with Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;. Remove TrustServerCertificate=True so the server certificate's chain/name is actually validated. If the SQL Server presents a cert issued by a private/internal CA, install that CA into the app host's trust store and add the host's real DNS name (matching the cert CN/SAN) to the Server= value instead of the bare IP; if it presents a self-signed cert that cannot be replaced short-term, pin it via HostNameInCertificate=<cert-subject> rather than blanket-trusting. Leave the local 'DefaultConnection' (Server=localhost;Trusted_Connection=True) as-is for dev.
2. Note for the report (do NOT fix here): the same lines 26-27 hardcode User ID=sa;Password=Pr0fessi0nal@IM2022 in source control, and appsettings.json line 10 has a weak hardcoded JWT:Key. Those are secrets-management defects tracked under a separate finding (verifier note 4); flag them but keep them out of this transport change.
3. PRIORITY 2 — tune HSTS in Backend/Program.cs. Today the else branch at lines 104-107 calls bare app.UseHsts() (default 30-day max-age, no preload). Register a tuned policy in the service-configuration section (before line 97 'var app = builder.Build();'), e.g. after the AddExcelExportQueue call on line 95, add a builder.Services.AddHsts(...) block setting MaxAge to 365 days, IncludeSubDomains, and Preload. Keep the existing app.UseHsts() inside the non-development else branch (lines 104-107) — UseHsts then picks up the configured options.
4. PRIORITY 3 (cosmetic, per verifier note 1) — remove the duplicate app.UseHttpsRedirection(). It is called at both line 117 and line 159. Delete the first one (line 117) and keep the call at line 159 so redirection runs in correct pipeline order alongside UseRouting/UseForwardedHeaders. This is copy-paste cleanup, not a security fix.
5. PRIORITY 4 (low/info hygiene, per verifier note 2) — narrow AllowedHosts in Backend/appsettings.json line 8 from "*" to the explicit production hostnames already trusted by CORS in Program.cs lines 135-153, e.g. "reportuat.myanmartradenet.com;myanmartradenet.com". Provide the dev override in appsettings.Development.json (currently only has a Logging block) so local hosts/localhost still work without editing the committed prod file.
6. PRIORITY 5 (documentation/codify TLS termination) — the Frontend nginx image (Frontend/dockerfile line 20 'FROM nginx:alpine', EXPOSE 80 only) ships the stock nginx default.conf and serves plain HTTP on :80; docker-compose.production.yml maps 3000:80 with no TLS. Document in the dockerfile header and docker-compose.production.yml that HTTPS/TLS is terminated by an external reverse proxy (the same proxy that must forward X-Forwarded-Proto, which Program.cs lines 161-165 already honor via UseForwardedHeaders). Optionally codify it: add a custom nginx.conf (listen 443 ssl; with cert/key mounts) and COPY it in the dockerfile, or commit the reverse-proxy config so TLS termination is no longer undocumented/implicit.
7. Build and confirm: dotnet build the Backend project to ensure the AddHsts/connection-string edits compile and the app still starts.

```text
// Backend/appsettings.json — PRIORITY 1: remove TrustServerCertificate=True on remote DB conns (lines 26-27)
// before:
"TemplateDB":"Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;User ID=sa;Password=...;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;",
"TradeNetDBTest": "Server=203.81.66.111,14330;Initial Catalog=TradeNetDB;User ID=sa;Password=...;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=True;"
// after (validated TLS; use the cert's DNS name as Server, or pin HostNameInCertificate if name differs):
"TemplateDB":"Server=db.myanmartradenet.com,14330;Initial Catalog=TemplateDB;User ID=sa;Password=...;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=False;",
"TradeNetDBTest": "Server=db.myanmartradenet.com,14330;Initial Catalog=TradeNetDB;User ID=sa;Password=...;MultipleActiveResultSets=False;Encrypt=True;TrustServerCertificate=False;"

// Backend/appsettings.json — PRIORITY 4: line 8
// before:
"AllowedHosts": "*",
// after:
"AllowedHosts": "reportuat.myanmartradenet.com;myanmartradenet.com",

// Backend/appsettings.Development.json — keep localhost working in dev
{
  "Logging": { "LogLevel": { "Default": "Information", "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning" } },
  "AllowedHosts": "*"
}

// Backend/Program.cs — PRIORITY 2: add tuned HSTS after line 95 (AddExcelExportQueue), before line 97 build
builder.Services.AddExcelExportQueue(builder.Configuration);
builder.Services.AddHsts(options =>
{
    options.MaxAge = TimeSpan.FromDays(365);
    options.IncludeSubDomains = true;
    options.Preload = true;
});

var app = builder.Build();
// ... existing else branch at lines 104-107 keeps: app.UseHsts();
```
