```text
// Backend/Program.cs — PRIORITY 3: delete the duplicate at line 117, keep the one at line 159
-        app.UseHttpsRedirection();   // line 117 (remove this first occurrence)
         #region Cors
         ...
         #endregion
         app.UseHttpsRedirection();   // line 159 (keep)
         app.UseRouting();
```

**Verify:**
- dotnet build on Backend succeeds and the app starts in Production environment (ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production dotnet run).
- DB-channel TLS: with TrustServerCertificate=False, the two TemplateDB/TradeNetDBTest connections still open successfully against 203.81.66.111 (hit any report endpoint that queries them, e.g. via Swagger at /swagger). A failure here means the server cert chain/name isn't trusted yet — install the CA / fix the Server= DNS name rather than reverting to TrustServerCertificate=True.
- HSTS: curl -I https://<prod-host>/ shows 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload' (only in Production, since UseHsts is in the non-development branch).
- HTTPS redirect: curl -I http://<prod-host>/ returns 307/308 to https. grep -c 'UseHttpsRedirection' Backend/Program.cs returns 1 (no longer 2).
- AllowedHosts: a request with a spoofed Host header (curl -H 'Host: evil.example.com' http://<host>/swagger) returns 400 Bad Request in Production, while localhost still works in Development.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Order/priority: do PRIORITY 1 (DB TLS) first — it is the only item the verifier considers substantive. Removing TrustServerCertificate=True will BREAK DB connectivity if the SQL Server's cert is self-signed or its CN/SAN does not match the connection's Server= value; have the validated cert / CA trust (or HostNameInCertificate pinning) ready before deploying, or staging will fail to reach the DB. Roll back by temporarily restoring TrustServerCertificate=True only if connectivity is lost and re-prioritize the cert fix. Do NOT touch the local DefaultConnection. Tightening AllowedHosts will 400 any host not listed — include every prod hostname and the load-balancer/proxy host name, and add the dev override in appsettings.Development.json or local debugging breaks. The duplicate UseHttpsRedirection removal is cosmetic (verifier note 1) — keep the SECOND occurrence (line 159) which sits in correct order with UseForwardedHeaders so X-Forwarded-Proto from the proxy is respected. Keep the hardcoded sa password and JWT key untouched here; they are a separate secrets-management finding (verifier note 4)."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Low is correct and should stay. Caveats for the report: (1) The duplicate UseHttpsRedirection is cosmetic, not a security defect. (2) The AllowedHosts="*" impact is largely theoretical here — no link-poisoning or cache surface exists in this codebase, so it is closer to Info-level hygiene. (3) The strongest real item is TrustServerCertificate=True on remote, internet-exposed DB connections (203.81.66.111) — that is the part worth prioritizing. (4) Out of scope but adjacent and more severe: lines 26-27 hardcode the SQL 'sa' account with a plaintext password in source-controlled appsettings.json, and line 10 has a hardcoded weak JWT signing key — those belong to a secrets-management finding, not this transport one.


<a id="f-deploy-script-manual-robocopy-no-env-isolation"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Production deploy is a manual PowerShell robocopy to a Windows share with no env isolation or rollback

**ID:** `deploy-script-manual-robocopy-no-env-isolation` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Deployment Process &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `deploy.ps1:33`
- `deploy.ps1:38`
- `deploy.ps1:50`
- `deploy.bat:4`

**Problem.** Deployment is a developer-run PowerShell script that git pulls, dotnet publishes Release, and robocopies the output to P:\WEBSITES\... shares. deploy.bat launches it with -ExecutionPolicy Bypass. It excludes appsettings.json/web.config from the copy (/XF), so production config is whatever already exists on the target with no versioning. There is no ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT set (defaults to Production, which is fine, but is implicit), no build artifact pinning, no health verification, no atomic swap, and no rollback path. The frontend target folder name is misspelled ('tradenenet-admin-frontend').

**Impact.** Deployments are non-reproducible and operator-dependent: an in-place robocopy over a live site can serve half-deployed files, there is no way to roll back to a known-good build, and the excluded-but-unmanaged appsettings.json means prod secrets live untracked on the box (and the committed copy still leaks them). -ExecutionPolicy Bypass habituates running unsigned scripts.

**Recommendation.** Move to artifact-based CI/CD: build once in CI, produce a versioned artifact, deploy via atomic swap (publish to a new folder + repoint) with a health check and documented rollback. Manage production appsettings via a secret store, set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT explicitly, and fix the target folder typo.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. half a day (scripted atomic swap + health endpoint); 2-3 days if migrating to a true CI/CD pipeline)_**

Make deploy.ps1 deploy atomically and reproducibly: publish to a versioned/timestamped staging folder, set ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Production explicitly, swap the live folder by repointing a directory junction (keeping the previous build for rollback), add a post-deploy health check with auto-rollback, and fix the misspelled frontend target. Drop -ExecutionPolicy Bypass in deploy.bat. (Secret/appsettings leakage is the separate secrets-in-source finding; keep the /XF exclusion so a local publish never overwrites prod config.)

1. Open /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/deploy.ps1. Add params for base deploy roots and a health-check URL, and compute a unique release tag. After the existing param(...) block (lines 10-13) add `[string]$BackendBase = 'P:\WEBSITES\tradenet-admin-backend'`, `[string]$FrontendBase = 'P:\WEBSITES\tradenet-admin-frontend'`, and `[string]$HealthUrl = 'http://localhost/health'`. Right after `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` (line 15) add `$ReleaseTag = Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss'` and `$Sha = (git rev-parse --short HEAD)`.
2. Set the environment explicitly before publishing. After `Set-Location $backendDir` (line 27) add `$env:ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT = 'Production'` so the publish step and any local tooling are unambiguous about the target environment (currently it is implicit).
3. Replace the single in-place backend robocopy (line 38) with an atomic-swap to a release folder. Keep publishing to `$publishOutput` (line 33). Then robocopy `$publishOutput` into `Join-Path $BackendBase "releases\$ReleaseTag-$Sha"` (NOT directly onto the live `current` folder), keeping the existing `/XF appsettings.json appsettings.*.json` exclusion. Check robocopy success with `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ge 8) { throw }` (robocopy exit codes <8 mean success). Then repoint a `current` directory junction to the new release and retain the prior target as `previous` for rollback.
4. Copy the unversioned prod config forward into the new release before swap so the live site keeps its appsettings/web.config. After the robocopy into the release folder, loop over `appsettings.json` and `appsettings.Production.json`, and for each `Copy-Item (Join-Path "$BackendBase\current" $f) (Join-Path $releaseDir $f) -Force` guarded by Test-Path so the first-ever deploy still works.
5. Apply the same atomic-swap pattern to the frontend block (lines 46-50), and FIX THE TYPO: change `'P:\WEBSITES\tradenenet-admin-frontend'` to use `$FrontendBase` ('...\tradenet-admin-frontend'). Keep the `/XF web.config` exclusion and copy the existing web.config forward.
6. Add a post-swap health check with auto-rollback. After the backend `current` junction is repointed, call `Invoke-WebRequest -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 30 -Uri $HealthUrl`; on non-200 or exception, repoint `current` back to `previous` and `throw`. This requires a /health endpoint: Backend/Program.cs has none today (it only has the `app.Environment.IsDevelopment()` check at line 100 and uses minimal-hosting `app`), so add `builder.Services.AddHealthChecks();` near the other service registrations and `app.MapHealthChecks("/health");` after the app is built. If you cannot add it now, point $HealthUrl at an existing known-200 route and leave a TODO.
7. Edit deploy.bat line 4: remove `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass`. Sign deploy.ps1 (or set the target machine policy to RemoteSigned once) and launch with `powershell -NoProfile -File "%SCRIPT_DIR%deploy.ps1" %*`. Interim: use `-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned` (never Bypass).
8. Stop shipping prod secrets in the repo (compounding context owned by the secrets finding): `git ls-files` confirms Backend/appsettings.json is still tracked even though .gitignore:162 lists it. Run `git rm --cached Backend/appsettings.json`, commit a sanitized `Backend/appsettings.Template.json`, keep prod config only on the target via the copy-forward in step 4, and rotate the leaked sa password and JWT key.
9. Document rollback: add a header comment in deploy.ps1 (and/or AGENTS.md) and optionally a small rollback.ps1 that repoints `current` to the `previous` release via `cmd /c rmdir current` then `New-Item -ItemType Junction -Path current -Target (Get-Item previous).Target`.

```bash
## deploy.ps1 — backend block, BEFORE (line 35-38):
$backendTarget = 'P:\WEBSITES\tradenet-admin-backend'
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $backendTarget | Out-Null
Write-Host "Copying backend files to: $backendTarget"
robocopy $publishOutput $backendTarget /E /MT:8 /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS /nc /ns /np /XF appsettings.json appsettings.*.json

## AFTER (atomic release + swap + health check + rollback):
$env:ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT = 'Production'   # also set this before `dotnet publish`

$current    = Join-Path $BackendBase 'current'
$releaseDir = Join-Path $BackendBase "releases\$ReleaseTag-$Sha"
New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $releaseDir | Out-Null
Write-Host "Staging backend to: $releaseDir"
robocopy $publishOutput $releaseDir /E /MT:8 /NFL /NDL /NJH /NJS /nc /ns /np /XF appsettings.json appsettings.*.json
if ($LASTEXITCODE -ge 8) { throw "robocopy failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE)" }  # <8 = success

# carry forward unversioned prod config that lives only on the target
foreach ($f in 'appsettings.json','appsettings.Production.json') {
    $src = Join-Path $current $f
    if (Test-Path $src) { Copy-Item $src (Join-Path $releaseDir $f) -Force }
}

# swap via directory junction; keep prior build as `previous`
$previous = Join-Path $BackendBase 'previous'
if (Test-Path $current) {
    $live = (Get-Item $current).Target           # release current points at
    cmd /c "rmdir `"$current`""                   # removes the junction, not target
    if (Test-Path $previous) { cmd /c "rmdir `"$previous`"" }
    if ($live) { New-Item -ItemType Junction -Path $previous -Target $live | Out-Null }
}
New-Item -ItemType Junction -Path $current -Target $releaseDir | Out-Null

# health check + auto-rollback
try {
    $resp = Invoke-WebRequest -UseBasicParsing -TimeoutSec 30 -Uri $HealthUrl
    if ($resp.StatusCode -ne 200) { throw "health $($resp.StatusCode)" }
    Write-Host "Health OK ($HealthUrl)"
} catch {
    Write-Warning "Health check failed: $_  -- rolling back"
    cmd /c "rmdir `"$current`""
    if (Test-Path $previous) { New-Item -ItemType Junction -Path $current -Target (Get-Item $previous).Target | Out-Null }
    throw 'Deployment rolled back due to failed health check.'
}

## frontend block — fix typo, use $FrontendBase (was 'tradenenet-admin-frontend')

## deploy.bat line 4, BEFORE:
powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File "%SCRIPT_DIR%deploy.ps1" %*
## AFTER:
powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -File "%SCRIPT_DIR%deploy.ps1" %*
```

**Verify:**
- Dry run on a non-prod share: `.\deploy.ps1 -BackendBase C:\temp\be -FrontendBase C:\temp\fe -NoGit` twice; confirm two folders under releases\, `current` junction points at the newest and `previous` at the first — check with `(Get-Item C:\temp\be\current).Target`.
- Env isolation: temporarily add `Write-Host $env:ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT` and confirm it prints Production during the run.
- Health/rollback: with AddHealthChecks/MapHealthChecks added, `curl -i http://localhost/health` returns 200; then point $HealthUrl at a down URL and confirm the script logs 'rolling back', repoints `current` to `previous`, and exits non-zero.
- Typo fix: confirm frontend lands in ...\tradenet-admin-frontend (single 'net'), not tradenenet-...
- Config preservation: place an appsettings.json in `current`, deploy a new release, confirm it is present in the new release and was NOT overwritten by publish (the /XF + copy-forward).
- grep deploy.bat and confirm `-ExecutionPolicy Bypass` is gone.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Directory junctions require the path NOT already exist; always `cmd /c rmdir` the junction (removes the link, not the target) before recreating — never `Remove-Item -Recurse` on `current` or you delete the real release. The first-ever deploy has no current/previous, so every swap/rollback step must be Test-Path-guarded (the patch does this). robocopy returns 0-7 on success and >=8 on failure, and $ErrorActionPreference='Stop' does NOT catch native exit codes — rely on the explicit `if ($LASTEXITCODE -ge 8)` check. Health-check/rollback only works once Program.cs actually exposes /health (it currently does not) — ship the AddHealthChecks/MapHealthChecks change in the same PR or the check will false-fail and trigger needless rollbacks. Carrying appsettings.json forward only protects prod config if the secrets fix (step 8) keeps config on the target; until `git rm --cached` is done and the sa/JWT secrets rotated, the committed copy still leaks. Removing -ExecutionPolicy Bypass breaks the .bat on any box whose policy is Restricted until the script is signed or the machine is set to RemoteSigned once.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Low is defensible and I did not inflate it. The deployment risks (non-atomic in-place robocopy over a live site can serve half-deployed files, no rollback, operator-dependent/non-reproducible) are real but operational. The more severe issue (sa-credential and JWT-key leak in committed appsettings.json, verified present) is referenced here only as compounding context and is properly the subject of a separate secrets-in-source finding; keeping this devops finding at Low avoids double-counting that severity. The /XF exclusion of appsettings.json is intentional (so a local publish does not overwrite prod config), but it does mean prod config is unversioned on the target — a legitimate point. No corrections needed to location or severity.


---

### Dependencies & Supply Chain

> This government trade-reporting admin system has serious supply-chain hygiene gaps. An offline `npm audit` against the committed lockfile reports 32 advisories on the frontend alone (2 Critical, 12 High, 15 Moderate). The most impactful are in the production runtime path: `xlsx@0.18.5` (the well-known prototype-pollution + ReDoS SheetJS CVEs, used directly in the Excel export code), `axios@1.9.0` (a long list of HTTP-client advisories including SSRF/proxy-credential-leak/prototype-pollution, plus it pulls the Critical `form-data` boundary CVE), `lodash@4.17.21` (prototype pollution / `_.template` code injection), and `protobufjs@7.5.3` pulled transitively by `firebase@11.9.1` (Critical arbitrary-code-execution / DoS). There is no dependency scanning anywhere in the repo — the only CI present is leftover template workflows that test on EOL Node 16/18 and do not run any audit. There is also dead weight: `moment@2.30.1` is a direct dependency with zero imports in the source (the app uses `dayjs` everywhere), and heavyweight single-use deps (firebase, mqtt) inflate the bundle and the vulnerability surface. The backend .csproj versions are internally consistent (EF Core 9.0.6 on net8.0 with JwtBearer 8.0.17 is supported, not a true skew) but have no automated vulnerability scanning either.

<a id="f-axios-vulnerable-1-9-0"></a>
#### 🟠 HIGH — axios 1.9.0 (production HTTP client) carries 20+ advisories incl. SSRF, proxy-credential leak, prototype pollution

**ID:** `axios-vulnerable-1-9-0` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Known-vulnerable dependency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package.json:26`
- `Frontend/package-lock.json (node_modules/axios version 1.9.0)`

**Problem.** axios is declared "^1.9.0" and resolves to 1.9.0, the app's primary API client. npm audit flags it High with a very long advisory list affecting <1.16.0, including NO_PROXY hostname-normalization SSRF (GHSA-3p68-rc4w-qgx5), Proxy-Authorization credential leak across redirects (GHSA-p92q-9vqr-4j8v, GHSA-j5f8-grm9-p9fc), multiple prototype-pollution gadgets enabling response tampering / credential injection / MitM via config.proxy (GHSA-pf86-5x62-jrwf, GHSA-35jp-ww65-95wh, GHSA-3g43-6gmg-66jw), DoS via missing data-size checks (GHSA-4hjh-wcwx-xvwj), and ReDoS via cookie-name injection (GHSA-hfxv-24rg-xrqf). axios 1.9.0 also depends on the Critical-vulnerable form-data 4.0.2 (see separate finding).

**Impact.** All admin/report API traffic flows through this client. The proxy/NO_PROXY and Proxy-Authorization advisories are directly relevant to a server-side or proxied deployment of a government system, where a credential leak or SSRF can pivot into internal networks. The prototype-pollution gadgets let crafted server responses tamper with subsequent requests. Because the caret range stops below the patched line, a fresh install keeps the vulnerable version.

**Recommendation.** Upgrade axios to >=1.16.0 (latest 1.x) and re-pin: "axios": "^1.16.0". This is a non-breaking minor for the 1.x line and simultaneously resolves the transitive form-data Critical. Run `npm audit fix` afterward and re-run audit to confirm.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 15-30 min)_**

Upgrade the frontend's axios dependency from the vulnerable 1.9.0 to the latest 1.x line (currently 1.17.0), re-pin the caret range in Frontend/package.json, and regenerate the lockfile so a fresh install no longer resolves below the patched line. This is a non-breaking minor upgrade within the 1.x series and also clears the transitive form-data 4.0.2 Critical.

1. Work in the Frontend directory: all commands below run from "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" (quote the path because of the spaces).
2. Edit Frontend/package.json line 26: change the dependency entry from "axios": "^1.9.0" to "axios": "^1.17.0" (the current latest 1.x; do NOT pin to ^1.16.0 as the audit recommendation text suggested — the verifier notes the NO_PROXY incomplete-fix advisory GHSA-pjwm-pj3p-43mv still affects 1.15.x, so target the latest 1.x).
3. Install/upgrade and regenerate the lockfile by running: npm install axios@^1.17.0  (this rewrites the node_modules/axios entry at package-lock.json:6563 from 1.9.0 to 1.17.0 and bumps the transitive node_modules/form-data entry at package-lock.json:8872 from 4.0.2 to a patched 4.0.4+).
4. Run npm audit fix to pull any remaining transitive patches, then re-run npm audit to confirm the axios and form-data advisories are gone.
5. Confirm the bundle still builds: npm run build (script is "tsc && vite build"). No source changes are needed — src/services/AxiosInstance.ts only uses axios.create() + a request interceptor (Authorization header from localStorage and baseUrl prefixing), which is unchanged across the 1.x line.
6. Commit both Frontend/package.json and Frontend/package-lock.json together so the pinned version and the resolved lockfile stay in sync.

```json
// Frontend/package.json  (line 26)  BEFORE -> AFTER
  "dependencies": {
    ...
-   "axios": "^1.9.0",
+   "axios": "^1.17.0",
    ...
  }

# Then regenerate node_modules + lockfile:
#   npm install axios@^1.17.0
#   npm audit fix
#   npm audit          # expect 0 axios / form-data advisories
#   npm run build      # tsc && vite build still passes

# Frontend/package-lock.json effect (node_modules/axios ~line 6563):
-      "version": "1.9.0",
+      "version": "1.17.0",
# and node_modules/form-data (~line 8872): 4.0.2 -> 4.0.4 (patched)
```

**Verify:**
- Run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --omit=dev`) in Frontend and confirm no High/Critical advisories remain for axios or form-data.
- Run `npm ls axios` and confirm the resolved version is 1.17.0 (>=1.16.0), and `npm ls form-data` shows >=4.0.4.
- Grep the lockfile: `grep -A1 '"node_modules/axios"' Frontend/package-lock.json` should show "version": "1.17.0".
- Run `npm run build` and confirm tsc + vite build succeed with no type or runtime errors against the new axios.
- Smoke-test the running SPA: load a report page (e.g. via src/Report/Page/GenericReportPage.tsx) and confirm authenticated API calls through src/services/AxiosInstance.ts still send the Bearer token and resolve normally.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Always edit package.json and regenerate package-lock.json in the same change — bumping only package.json leaves the vulnerable 1.9.0 pinned in the lockfile, so CI/clean installs (`npm ci`) would still install the vulnerable version. Do not hand-edit version strings in package-lock.json; let `npm install` rewrite it so integrity hashes and the transitive form-data bump are correct. The verifier confirmed the most alarming items (NO_PROXY/proxy SSRF, Proxy-Authorization redirect leak, maxContentLength/maxBodyLength stream bypass, formDataToStream CRLF) are Node HTTP-adapter-only and NOT exploitable in this browser Vite/React bundle — so this is routine dependency hygiene, not a live-exploit emergency; the genuinely applicable in-browser residual risks it closes are the prototype-pollution gadgets and DoS/ReDoS items. Rollback is trivial: revert the two files and run `npm ci`. The 1.x upgrade is non-breaking, so no source migration in AxiosInstance.ts or any consuming page is required.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Impact narrative is partially overstated for THIS app's context. The codebase is a browser-side Vite/React SPA (vite build, @vitejs/plugin-react-swc); AxiosInstance.ts uses axios.create() with localStorage tokens and no proxy/Node adapter, and grep found zero proxy config anywhere in src. Consequently the most alarming impacts the finding emphasizes — NO_PROXY/proxy SSRF "pivot into internal networks," Proxy-Authorization credential leak across redirects, maxContentLength/maxBodyLength stream bypasses, formDataToStream CRLF — are Node.js HTTP-adapter-only and are NOT exploitable in a browser bundle. The finding's phrase "directly relevant to a server-side or proxied deployment of a government system" does not match how this client actually runs. The genuinely applicable residual risk in-browser is the prototype-pollution gadgets (response tampering / header injection / XSRF token leakage) and the DoS/ReDoS items. Also, the npm form-data package isn't used directly in source (browser uses native FormData), though it remains in the dependency tree.  Recommendation is slightly stale: audit shows advisories affecting up to <1.15.1 and an incomplete-fix NO_PROXY advisory (GHSA-pjwm-pj3p-43mv) still affecting 1.15.x, so the patched target should be the latest 1.x (>=1.15.x is insufficient per current advisories), not ^1.16.0 specifically. Upgrading to latest axios 1.x and running npm audit fix remains the correct action and also clears the transitive form-data Critical.


<a id="f-no-dependency-scanning"></a>
#### 🟠 HIGH — No dependency scanning (Dependabot/Renovate/audit) anywhere; only leftover template CI on EOL Node

**ID:** `no-dependency-scanning` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Supply-chain process &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml:18-22 (Node 16.x / 18.x matrix)`
- `Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml:20 (npm install --force)`
- `(repo root: no .github, no dependabot.yml, no renovate.json — confirmed by find)`

**Problem.** There is no Dependabot config, no Renovate config, and no `npm audit` / dotnet vulnerability scan in any pipeline. The only CI present is template leftover under Frontend/.github/workflows: test.yml runs on EOL Node 16.x and 18.x, release.yml runs `npm install --force` (which bypasses peer-dependency safety and can pull unexpected versions), and there is no workflow at the repository root covering the backend at all. The 32 advisories surfaced by a manual offline audit are therefore completely invisible to the team's process.

**Impact.** Vulnerable packages (including the 2 Criticals above) accumulate undetected. `--force` installs in release CI can silently change the dependency tree away from the audited lockfile. Building/testing against Node 16/18 (both end-of-life) means the CI environment itself is unsupported and unpatched. For a government system this is an unmanaged supply-chain risk.

**Recommendation.** Add a repo-root .github/dependabot.yml covering both the npm (Frontend) and nuget (Backend) ecosystems; add an `npm audit --audit-level=high` and `dotnet list package --vulnerable --include-transitive` gate to CI; replace `npm install --force` with `npm ci` against the committed lockfile; and bump the CI Node matrix to a supported LTS (20.x/22.x). Delete the unused template workflows if they are not adapted.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1-2 hours (plus triage time for whatever advisories the new gates surface))_**

Move CI to the repo root and add automated supply-chain scanning: a root .github/dependabot.yml covering both the npm (Frontend) and nuget (Backend/Backend.Tests) ecosystems, plus a CI workflow that gates on `npm audit` and `dotnet list package --vulnerable`. Modernize the existing Frontend workflows by replacing `npm install --force` with `npm ci` against the committed lockfile and bumping the Node matrix off EOL 16/18 to supported LTS (20.x/22.x).

1. Create the repo-root directory `.github/workflows/` (currently the only `.github` is under `Frontend/`; the backend has no CI at all). All new files go at the absolute repo root: `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/.github/`.
2. Add `/.github/dependabot.yml` with three update blocks: `package-ecosystem: npm` with `directory: "/Frontend"` (where `Frontend/package-lock.json` lives), `package-ecosystem: nuget` with `directory: "/Backend"` (API.csproj) and a second nuget block for `directory: "/Backend.Tests"` (Backend.Tests.csproj) — or a single nuget block at `directory: "/"` since `tradenet-admin-report.sln` is at root and Dependabot's nuget resolver follows the solution. Use the per-project form (shown in patch) to be explicit. Set `schedule.interval: weekly` and `open-pull-requests-limit: 10` on each.
3. Add a root CI workflow `/.github/workflows/security.yml` that runs on pull_request and push to main with two jobs. Job `frontend-audit`: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4` with `node-version: 20.x` and `cache: npm` / `cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json`, then `npm ci` and `npm audit --audit-level=high` run with `working-directory: Frontend`. Job `backend-audit`: `actions/checkout@v4`, `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` with `dotnet-version: 8.0.x`, `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`, then fail-on-vulnerable using `dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` piped through a grep gate (see patch — `dotnet list` exits 0 even when vulnerabilities exist, so you must grep the output and fail explicitly).
4. Fix the existing `/Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml`: change the matrix `node-version` from `['16.x','18.x']` to `['20.x','22.x']`, bump `actions/checkout@v3`->`@v4` and `actions/setup-node@v3`->`@v4`, replace `run: npm install` with `run: npm ci` (deterministic install against the lockfile), and DELETE the `Test` step (`run: npm test`) — there is no `test` script in `Frontend/package.json` (scripts are dev/build/lint/preview/changeset/storybook/build-storybook/prettier:write), so that step would always error. Keep `npm run build`.
5. Fix `/Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml`: replace `run: npm install --force` with `run: npm ci` so the release build installs the exact audited lockfile tree instead of bypassing peer-dependency resolution. Bump `actions/checkout@v3`->`@v4` and `actions/setup-node@v3`->`@v4` (keep `node-version: 20.18.0`). Leave the `changesets/action@v1` step intact — changesets is genuinely configured (`Frontend/.changeset/config.json` exists), this is not pure template leftover.
6. Optionally pin the supported runtime by adding `"engines": { "node": ">=20" }` to `Frontend/package.json` so local installs also warn on EOL Node.
7. Commit all four files (new `.github/dependabot.yml`, new `.github/workflows/security.yml`, edited `Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml`, edited `Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml`). Push on a branch and open a PR so the new `security.yml` runs on its own PR event and you can confirm the gates execute.

```yaml
# 1) NEW FILE: /.github/dependabot.yml
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "npm"
    directory: "/Frontend"            # Frontend/package-lock.json
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
  - package-ecosystem: "nuget"
    directory: "/Backend"             # Backend/API.csproj (net8.0)
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
  - package-ecosystem: "nuget"
    directory: "/Backend.Tests"       # Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10

---
# 2) NEW FILE: /.github/workflows/security.yml
name: Dependency Security
on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
jobs:
  frontend-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: Frontend
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20.x
          cache: npm
          cache-dependency-path: Frontend/package-lock.json
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm audit --audit-level=high
  backend-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: 8.0.x
      - run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      # dotnet list exits 0 even with vulns -> grep + fail explicitly
      - name: Scan for vulnerable packages
        run: |
          dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive 2>&1 | tee audit.log
          if grep -q -E "Critical|High" audit.log; then
            echo "::error::High/Critical vulnerable NuGet packages found"; exit 1
          fi

---
# 3) EDIT: /Frontend/.github/workflows/test.yml
   strategy:
     matrix:
       node-version:
-        - '16.x'
-        - '18.x' # whichever node versions you support
+        - '20.x'
+        - '22.x'
   steps:
-    - uses: actions/checkout@v3
+    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
     - name: Use Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }}
-      uses: actions/setup-node@v3
+      uses: actions/setup-node@v4
       with:
         node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
-    - name: Clean install dependencies
-      run: npm install
+    - name: Clean install dependencies
+      run: npm ci
     - name: Build application
       run: npm run build
-    - name: Test
-      run: npm test        # DELETE: no "test" script exists in Frontend/package.json

---
# 4) EDIT: /Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml
-      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
-      - uses: actions/setup-node@v3
+      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
+      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
         with:
           node-version: 20.18.0
-      - run: npm install --force
+      - run: npm ci
```

**Verify:**
- Open a PR with these files and confirm the new `Dependency Security` workflow appears in the PR Checks tab and runs both `frontend-audit` and `backend-audit` jobs (the existing `test.yml` runs on `branches-ignore: [main, master]`, so it also fires on a feature-branch PR).
- Run the frontend gate locally: `cd Frontend && npm ci && npm audit --audit-level=high` — exit code 0 means no high/critical advisories; nonzero reproduces the CI failure (the 32 advisories from the companion finding should now be visible).
- Run the backend gate locally from repo root: `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln && dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` and confirm the output lists transitive packages and that High/Critical entries cause the workflow step to exit 1.
- Confirm `Frontend/.github/workflows/release.yml` no longer contains `npm install --force` (`grep -R "install --force" Frontend/.github` returns nothing) and that `test.yml` no longer references `16.x`/`18.x` or `npm test`.
- After merge, verify Dependabot is active: GitHub repo > Insights > Dependency graph > Dependabot tab should list the npm + nuget update configs and begin opening update PRs on the weekly schedule.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Enabling `npm audit --audit-level=high` and the dotnet vulnerable-package grep will turn existing (currently invisible) advisories into red CI on the very first run — that is the intended behavior, but coordinate so it doesn't block an unrelated in-flight PR; if you need a soft rollout, temporarily append `|| true` to the `npm audit`/grep step (or use `continue-on-error: true`) and remove it once the companion vulnerable-dependency finding is remediated. `npm ci` requires `Frontend/package-lock.json` to be in sync with `package.json`; if a contributor hand-edited package.json without updating the lock, `npm ci` fails where `npm install` would have silently rewritten the lock — run `npm install` once locally to resync and commit the lockfile before merging. `dotnet list package --vulnerable` returns exit code 0 even when vulnerabilities exist, so the explicit grep-and-exit-1 in the patch is load-bearing — do not replace it with a bare `dotnet list`. Dependabot nuget on a directory containing only a test csproj is harmless but will open separate PRs; if you prefer fewer PRs, collapse the two nuget blocks into one at `directory: \"/\"` (it resolves via the root .sln). Rollback is trivial: delete `.github/` and revert the two Frontend workflow edits.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity is borderline. The finding's "High" rests partly on cross-referenced "2 Criticals / 32 advisories" from a separate vulnerable-dependency finding, not on this finding's own evidence; on its own merits this is a supply-chain *process/hygiene* gap (no scanning + EOL CI Node), not a directly exploitable code defect. Practical exploitability is also tempered: test.yml only runs on non-main branches and would error on the nonexistent `npm test` script, so the "building against EOL Node" impact is largely theoretical, and `npm install --force` in release.yml runs only on push to main. I'd rate this Medium. That said, all factual claims are accurate; if the org weights "unmanaged supply chain for a government system" heavily, High is defensible. One minor wording nit: release.yml is not pure "template leftover" — changesets is genuinely configured — but the cited code (the `--force` line) is correct. The recommendation (repo-root dependabot.yml for npm+nuget, npm audit + dotnet list package --vulnerable gates, replace npm install --force with npm ci, bump Node matrix to 20.x/22.x) is sound and actionable.


<a id="f-react-router-7-advisory"></a>
#### 🟡 MEDIUM — react-router-dom 7.6.2 carries router advisories (DoS / pre-render data spoofing)

**ID:** `react-router-7-advisory` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Known-vulnerable dependency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package.json:41 (react-router-dom ^7.6.2)`
- `Frontend/package-lock.json (react-router-dom resolved via react-router)`

**Problem.** npm audit flags react-router-dom (Moderate) and its underlying react-router (High) with the range 7.0.0-pre.0 - 7.11.0 covering the installed 7.6.2. The React Router 7.x advisories include a DoS and a pre-render/data-spoofing issue.

**Impact.** The router is core to every admin page. The advisories enable client-side DoS and, depending on usage of data routers/loaders, response/data spoofing affecting what operators see.

**Recommendation.** Upgrade react-router-dom to >7.11.0 (latest 7.x patched line) and re-run audit. This is within the same major, so it should be a low-risk bump; smoke-test routing afterward.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 20 min)_**

Bump react-router-dom (and its transitive react-router) from 7.6.2 to the patched line >=7.15.0 (latest 7.17.0) within the same major version, refresh the lockfile, and smoke-test routing. The audit range for react-router is 7.0.0–7.14.2, so the verifier-corrected floor of >=7.15.0 is required; >7.11.0 is insufficient.

1. cd into the frontend project: the working directory is "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" (path contains spaces — quote it).
2. Run the upgrade so both package.json and package-lock.json update together: `npm install react-router-dom@^7.17.0`. This rewrites Frontend/package.json:41 from "^7.6.2" to "^7.17.0" and bumps the transitive react-router entry in Frontend/package-lock.json (currently 7.6.2 at line ~11980) up to 7.17.0. 7.17.0 is the latest 7.x (dist-tag latest), and >=7.15.0 is the patched floor since the react-router advisory range is 7.0.0–7.14.2.
3. If you prefer to edit declaratively, change Frontend/package.json line 41 to "react-router-dom": "^7.17.0", then run `npm install` to regenerate the lockfile — but verify afterward that the transitive react-router resolved version is also 7.17.0 (npm may keep an old pin otherwise; see verification).
4. Confirm React peer compatibility is satisfied: react-router-dom@7.17.0 requires react/react-dom >=18 and this repo has react 19.1.0 (package.json:34), so no React change is needed.
5. Do NOT add or change any vite.config SSR/prerender settings or convert routes to data-router loaders/actions. Frontend/src/routes/routes.tsx uses createBrowserRouter with element-only routes (no loader:/action: keys) and the app is a pure client-side SPA, so the SSR/single-fetch/turbo-stream/__manifest advisories are not exercised — the bump is the entire fix.
6. Re-run `npm audit` and confirm there are no remaining react-router / react-router-dom advisories.
7. Build to catch any API breakage: `npm run build` (runs `tsc && vite build`).

```json
// Frontend/package.json (dependencies)
- "react-router-dom": "^7.6.2",
+ "react-router-dom": "^7.17.0",

// Then regenerate Frontend/package-lock.json so the resolved
// "react-router-dom" AND its transitive "react-router" both move
// from 7.6.2 -> 7.17.0 (the lockfile currently pins react-router 7.6.2
// at package-lock.json:11980).
```

**Verify:**
- From Frontend/, run: `node -e "const l=require('./package-lock.json'); for(const k in l.packages){ if(k.includes('react-router')&&!k.includes('@types')) console.log(k, l.packages[k].version);}"` — both node_modules/react-router-dom and node_modules/react-router must report 7.17.0 (not 7.6.2).
- Run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --json | grep -i react-router`) — the previously-flagged react-router (high, range 7.0.0–7.14.2) and react-router-dom (moderate, range 7.0.0-pre.0–7.11.0) advisories must no longer appear.
- Run `npm run build` and confirm it completes with no TypeScript/Vite errors (no breaking-API regressions from the bump).
- Smoke-test routing in `npm run dev`: load /auth/signin (root redirect via the single <Navigate> in routes.tsx:66), sign in to hit the ProtectedRoute tree, then exercise a /Report/* page, a /User/Edit/:id param route, and the ScrollToTop behavior on navigation — all should render without console errors.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Verifier correction honored: do not stop at >7.11.0 — the react-router advisory range extends to 7.14.2, so the patched floor is >=7.15.0; this repo should land on 7.17.0. Use `npm install react-router-dom@^7.17.0` rather than hand-editing only package.json, because the vulnerable version is the transitive react-router pin in the lockfile (line ~11980); a stale lockfile can leave react-router@7.6.2 installed even after package.json is bumped. The unrelated `storybook-addon-react-router-v6@2.0.15` and `@types/react-router-dom@^5.3.3` are for React Router v5/v6 tooling/types and are out of scope — do not touch them. This is a same-major (7.x) bump, so it is low-risk, but the build/dev smoke-tests above are still required because no automated route tests exist. Rollback is trivial: revert the package.json line and lockfile and run `npm install`."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Two caveats. (1) Fix version is understated: the recommendation "Upgrade to >7.11.0" is insufficient because several advisory ranges extend beyond it (DoS <7.15.0, single-fetch DoS <7.14.0, turbo-stream RCE <=7.14.1, protocol-relative open redirect <7.14.1). The actual patched line is >=7.15.0. Recommend bumping react-router-dom to >=7.15.0 (still within major 7, low-risk) and re-running audit. (2) Real exploitability for THIS app is on the lower end of the advisory set: it is a pure client-side SPA — vite.config has no SSR config, and src/routes/routes.tsx defines zero loaders/actions (grep count = 0) and only one client-side Navigate. So the worst server/framework-mode advisories (SSR ScrollRestoration XSS, __manifest DoS, single-fetch DoS, turbo-stream RCE) are not exercised; the residually-applicable ones are client-side XSS-via-untrusted-paths and open-redirect. Medium severity is therefore appropriate-to-slightly-generous but not wrong; leaving severity at Medium.


<a id="f-form-data-critical-transitive"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Critical form-data 4.0.2 (unsafe random boundary) pulled transitively by axios

**ID:** `form-data-critical-transitive` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Known-vulnerable transitive dependency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Trivial &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package-lock.json (node_modules/form-data @ line 8872-8885, version 4.0.2, declared by node_modules/axios @ line 6563-6573 as form-data ^4.0.0)`
- `Frontend/package-lock.json (node_modules/form-data 4.0.2, via axios@1.9.0)`

**Problem.** npm audit reports form-data as Critical (GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4): versions >=4.0.0 <4.0.4 choose the multipart boundary using an unsafe (predictable) random function. `npm ls form-data` confirms the path antd-multi-dashboard -> axios@1.9.0 -> form-data@4.0.2.

**Impact.** A predictable multipart boundary lets an attacker who can influence part of a multipart body craft content that injects additional parts (parameter/part smuggling) into outbound requests. In a file/report-upload flow this can corrupt or spoof submitted data. It is rated Critical by the advisory database.

**Recommendation.** This is fixed transitively by upgrading axios to >=1.16.0 (which depends on form-data >=4.0.4). If staying on axios 1.9.x temporarily, add an npm `overrides` entry forcing form-data >=4.0.4. Then re-run npm audit to confirm the Critical clears.

**Example:**

```
// package.json — interim override if axios upgrade is deferred
"overrides": {
  "form-data": ">=4.0.4"
}
```

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 15 min)_**

Clear the transitive form-data <4.0.4 advisory (GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4) by bumping the direct axios dependency in Frontend/package.json to >=1.16.0 (which pulls form-data >=4.0.4). This is npm-audit hygiene only: the browser SPA uses the native FormData object, not the npm package, so the vulnerable path is not exercised at runtime (per verifier).

1. Work in /Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend (the lockfile, package.json, and node_modules all live here).
2. Primary fix: edit Frontend/package.json line 26 and change the axios dependency from "axios": "^1.9.0" to "axios": "^1.16.0". axios 1.16.0+ declares form-data "^4.0.4", so the transitive resolution moves off the vulnerable 4.0.x range.
3. Regenerate the lockfile: run `npm install` from the Frontend directory. This rewrites Frontend/package-lock.json so node_modules/axios resolves to >=1.16.0 and node_modules/form-data (line ~8872) resolves to >=4.0.4.
4. If the axios major-minor bump is judged too risky to ship right now, use the interim fallback instead: add a top-level `overrides` block to Frontend/package.json (a sibling of the existing dependencies/devDependencies keys, e.g. after line 81) forcing form-data, leaving axios at ^1.9.0. There is currently NO overrides key in this package.json, so it must be added.
5. After whichever fix, run `npm audit` (or `npm audit --omit=dev`) from Frontend and confirm GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4 / the form-data Critical no longer appears.
6. Commit both Frontend/package.json and Frontend/package-lock.json together so CI installs the patched tree.

```json
// Frontend/package.json — PRIMARY: bump the direct axios dep
// before (line 26)
  "axios": "^1.9.0",
// after
  "axios": "^1.16.0",

// Frontend/package.json — INTERIM FALLBACK ONLY (if axios bump is deferred):
// add a new top-level key alongside "dependencies"/"devDependencies"
  "overrides": {
    "form-data": ">=4.0.4"
  }
```

**Verify:**
- From Frontend/: run `npm ls form-data` — the resolved version under axios must be >=4.0.4 (previously antd-multi-dashboard -> axios@1.9.0 -> form-data@4.0.2).
- From Frontend/: run `npm audit` and confirm the form-data Critical (GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4) is gone (0 critical from this advisory).
- Grep the regenerated Frontend/package-lock.json: the node_modules/form-data entry (~line 8872) shows version 4.0.4 or higher; if axios was bumped, node_modules/axios (~line 6563) shows >=1.16.0.
- Build still works: from Frontend/ run `npm run build` (tsc && vite build) to confirm the axios bump introduced no type/runtime breaks.
- Smoke-test the app's HTTP layer (any axios call, e.g. a report fetch via the configured axios instance) to confirm requests still succeed after the axios upgrade.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** "Always edit package.json AND re-run npm install so package-lock.json is regenerated; editing only the lockfile (or only package.json) leaves the tree inconsistent and CI's `npm ci` will fail or restore the vulnerable version. The `overrides` fallback also requires `npm install` to take effect (npm 10.9.8 here supports overrides). The two approaches are mutually exclusive in spirit — prefer the axios bump; if you keep the override long-term, remember to drop it once axios is upgraded so it doesn't silently pin form-data. Rollback is a clean `git checkout -- package.json package-lock.json`. Low priority: the verifier confirmed the SPA uses native browser FormData and has no multipart-upload code in src, so this is audit-cleanliness, not an exploitable runtime path — sequence it after higher-severity findings."

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Verified at lockfile lines: axios 6563-6573, form-data 6570 (edge) and 8872-8885 (resolved node 4.0.2). The vulnerable version, the transitive path via axios@1.9.0, and the suggested fix are all factually correct. Severity adjusted Critical -> Low because (1) browser SPA uses native FormData, not the npm package, so the vulnerable path is not exercised at runtime, and (2) no direct multipart/upload code exists in src to enable the described smuggling. Still recommend remediating (axios >=1.16.0 or overrides form-data >=4.0.4) to clear npm audit hygiene.


<a id="f-moment-unused-direct-dep"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — moment 2.30.1 is a direct dependency with zero source imports (app uses dayjs)

**ID:** `moment-unused-direct-dep` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Unused / duplicate-purpose dependency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Trivial &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package.json:32 (moment)`
- `Frontend/package.json:28 (dayjs)`
- `Frontend/src (grep for moment imports returns nothing; dayjs imported in 10 files)`

**Problem.** package.json lists both moment ^2.30.1 and dayjs ^1.11.13. A source-wide grep finds zero `import ... moment` / `from 'moment'` references, while dayjs is imported across ~10 report/page files and is also what AntD 5 uses internally (via rc-picker 4.11.3). The moment entries elsewhere in the lockfile are optional peer deps of unrelated libs, not real usage. moment is therefore dead weight, and it is also in long-term maintenance mode (no new features, project recommends migrating off it).

**Impact.** Unnecessary bundle weight (moment + its locale data is large and not tree-shakeable) and an extra maintenance/vulnerability surface for a library that does nothing. It also creates confusion about which date library is canonical.

**Recommendation.** Remove moment from dependencies (npm uninstall moment) and standardize on dayjs, which AntD already requires. Verify the build/storybook still pass.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 10 min)_**

Remove the dead `moment` direct dependency from the Frontend project and standardize date handling on `dayjs` (already required transitively by AntD 5 / rc-picker and imported in 10 source files). This is pure dependency hygiene — no source code change is needed because nothing in `src` imports `moment`.

1. Confirm there are truly zero `moment` usages before removing: from the Frontend dir run `grep -rn "moment" src/` — it must return nothing (verified empty during audit). Also confirm no config reference: `grep -rn "moment" vite.config.* tsconfig*.json .storybook/` (also empty).
2. Uninstall the package so both package.json and package-lock.json are updated atomically. From `/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend` run: `npm uninstall moment`. This removes the `"moment": "^2.30.1"` entry at Frontend/package.json:32 and the root-level `"moment": "^2.30.1"` entry at Frontend/package-lock.json:24.
3. Verify package.json no longer lists moment: `grep -n moment Frontend/package.json` should return nothing. The remaining lockfile hits (package-lock.json lines ~5869, ~11476, ~11490) are optional/peer deps of unrelated libraries and must be left untouched — do NOT hand-edit those.
4. Do NOT add `@types/moment` or any moment shim, and do NOT touch the existing `dayjs` entry (package.json:28). The 10 files importing dayjs continue to work unchanged.
5. Run the production build to confirm nothing broke: `npm run build` (runs `tsc && vite build`).
6. Run the Storybook build since this app ships Storybook: `npm run build-storybook`.
7. Commit both Frontend/package.json and Frontend/package-lock.json together (single dependency-removal commit).

```json
"dependencies": {
    ...
    "axios": "^1.9.0",
    "d3-color": "^3.1.0",
    "dayjs": "^1.11.13",
    "firebase": "^11.9.1",
    "interactjs": "^1.10.27",
    "lodash": "^4.17.21",
-   "moment": "^2.30.1",
    "mqtt": "^5.13.1",
    "react": "^19.1.0",
    ...
  }
```

**Verify:**
- `grep -rn "moment" Frontend/package.json` returns nothing (entry removed).
- `grep -n '"moment"' Frontend/package-lock.json` no longer shows the root entry at line ~24 (only the unrelated nested optional/peer-dep hits remain).
- `npm run build` succeeds (tsc type-check + vite build) with no missing-module errors — proves no source file depended on moment.
- `npm run build-storybook` succeeds.
- `npm run lint` still passes (eslint with --max-warnings 0).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Use `npm uninstall moment` rather than hand-editing JSON so package-lock.json stays consistent; if you edit package.json by hand you must also run `npm install` to regenerate the lockfile, otherwise CI installs will drift. Do not delete the nested `moment` references deeper in package-lock.json (they belong to other packages' optional/peer deps and removing them will corrupt the lockfile). Rollback is trivial: `npm install moment@^2.30.1`. Note the audit verifier's correction — the production bundle impact is negligible (unused, so Rollup/Vite never bundled it); the real wins are smaller node_modules, fewer audit/vulnerability surfaces, and a single canonical date library (dayjs). Do not overstate bundle-size savings in the PR description.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Severity Low is correct — this is dependency hygiene, not a correctness/security bug. One caveat: the stated "unnecessary bundle weight" impact is slightly overstated; since nothing in src imports moment, Vite/Rollup tree-shaking will not include it in the production bundle. The genuine costs are install-time/node_modules weight, an extra audit/vulnerability surface, and confusion over which date library is canonical. Recommendation (npm uninstall moment, standardize on dayjs) is sound; verifying build + storybook afterward is appropriate since this app uses Storybook.


<a id="f-lodash-prototype-pollution"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — lodash 4.17.21 (direct) vulnerable to prototype pollution and _.template code injection

**ID:** `lodash-prototype-pollution` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Known-vulnerable dependency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package.json:31 (direct dep lodash ^4.17.21); Frontend/package-lock.json:10086-10091 (node_modules/lodash 4.17.21)`
- `Frontend/package.json:31`
- `Frontend/package-lock.json (node_modules/lodash 4.17.21)`

**Problem.** lodash is a direct dependency at 4.17.21. Newer advisories now cover this version: prototype pollution in _.unset/_.omit (GHSA-xxjr-mmjv-4gpg, GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh, affected <=4.17.23) and code injection via _.template import key names (GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc, affected <=4.17.23). 4.17.21 was historically the 'safe' pin but is now flagged High by npm audit.

**Impact.** If any of the affected functions process attacker-influenced keys/paths (common in report-filter / object-merge code), the app is exposed to prototype pollution and, for _.template usage, client-side code injection.

**Recommendation.** Upgrade to the patched lodash release (>4.17.23 once published) or, as remediation today, replace the handful of lodash uses with native JS / per-method packages and drop the full lodash dependency. Add an `overrides`/resolution if a transitive copy lingers. Audit the codebase for _.template, _.unset, _.omit usage specifically.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 15 min (lockfile bump); ~1 hour if also removing lodash entirely)_**

Bump lodash to the patched line (>=4.18.0 / >4.17.23, per CVE-2026-4800) and refresh the lockfile so npm audit goes clean. The caret range in package.json already permits the patched version, so the real fix is regenerating package-lock.json; add an `overrides` pin as belt-and-suspenders. Severity is genuinely Low here: only `_.sumBy/_.chain/_.sortBy/_.toLowerCase` are used (no `_.template/_.unset/_.omit`), so there is no reachable exploit path — this is hygiene, not an active vuln.

1. In Frontend/package.json line 31, change the lodash pin from "^4.17.21" to "^4.18.0" (the patched line per CVE-2026-4800). Leave "@types/lodash": "^4.17.17" on line 59 alone — the types package versioning is independent and unaffected.
2. Add an `overrides` block to Frontend/package.json (top-level, e.g. right after the "dependencies" object) to force any transitive copies onto the patched version: "overrides": { "lodash": "^4.18.0" }. This guarantees the single node_modules/lodash entry at package-lock.json:10086 resolves above 4.17.23.
3. From the Frontend/ directory, run `npm install` to resolve and rewrite package-lock.json. Confirm the node_modules/lodash block (currently lines 10086-10091, version 4.17.21) now shows version >=4.18.0 with a new integrity hash.
4. Run `npm audit` (from Frontend/) and confirm the lodash GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh / GHSA-xxjr-mmjv-4gpg (prototype pollution) and GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc (_.template) advisories no longer report against lodash.
5. Optional hardening (recommended by the verifier since the lodash files are template boilerplate): the only methods used are `_.sumBy`, `_.chain`, `_.sortBy`, and `_.toLowerCase` across 6 files (EarningsCard.tsx, CampaignsAdsCard.tsx, StudyStatisticsCard.tsx, DeliveryAnalyticsCard.tsx, Faqs.tsx, Help.tsx). These are trivially replaceable with native JS (Array.prototype.sort / reduce / String.prototype.toLowerCase), after which lodash and @types/lodash can be removed from package.json entirely, eliminating the advisory surface for good. Note `_.toLowerCase()` in CampaignsAdsCard.tsx:42 is called with no argument and is almost certainly a pre-existing bug — verify intended behavior if you refactor that file.
6. Commit both Frontend/package.json and the regenerated Frontend/package-lock.json together so CI installs the patched tree.

```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "lodash": "^4.18.0"
  },
  "overrides": {
    "lodash": "^4.18.0"
  }
}
// Frontend/package.json line 31 change: "lodash": "^4.17.21"  ->  "lodash": "^4.18.0"
// Then `npm install` rewrites Frontend/package-lock.json:10086-10091 from 4.17.21 to the patched version.
```

**Verify:**
- From Frontend/: `npm install` then `npm ls lodash` shows a single resolved version >=4.18.0.
- `npm audit` from Frontend/ reports zero lodash advisories (no GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh, GHSA-xxjr-mmjv-4gpg, or GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc).
- grep node_modules/lodash in package-lock.json shows version >=4.18.0 with a fresh integrity hash, not 4.17.21.
- `npm run build` (tsc && vite build) succeeds — confirms the upgraded lodash API surface (sumBy/chain/sortBy/toLowerCase) is unchanged and nothing broke.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do not regenerate package-lock.json by hand; run npm install so the integrity hash matches the published tarball or CI/installs will fail.; Verify a patched lodash >4.17.23 (4.18.0) is actually published on the registry when you run this; if not yet released, the `overrides` pin to ^4.18.0 will fail to resolve — in that case keep 4.17.21, add a documented npm audit exception noting no reachable exploit path (verified: no _.template/_.unset/_.omit usage), and revisit when the patch ships.; If you take the optional removal path, the `_.toLowerCase()` call at CampaignsAdsCard.tsx:42 has no argument and looks broken — don't blindly translate it; confirm intended behavior first.; Commit package.json and package-lock.json in the same change; committing only one leaves the lockfile and manifest out of sync.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Recommendation is sound: upgrade lodash to >=4.18.0 (the fix per CVE-2026-4800). Minor factual error in the finding: the prototype-pollution advisories (GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh, GHSA-xxjr-mmjv-4gpg) are Moderate, not High; only the _.template advisory (GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc) is High, and _.template is not used here. Because the lodash-using files are template boilerplate, the team should also consider dropping the dependency entirely as suggested. No reachable exploit path exists in current source.


<a id="f-storybook-version-skew-dev"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Storybook devDependency version skew (mixed 8.6.x and 9.0.x packages) yielding High/Moderate audit hits

**ID:** `storybook-version-skew-dev` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P3 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Version skew / dev-dependency hygiene &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package.json:49-53 (@storybook/addon-essentials/blocks/manager-api/theming ^8.6.14, addon-interactions ^8.6.14)`
- `Frontend/package.json:51,54-56,72,76 (@storybook/addon-links 9.0.9, @storybook/react/react-vite ^9.0.9, storybook ^9.0.9)`

**Problem.** The Storybook toolchain mixes core 9.0.x with several addons pinned to 8.6.x (addon-essentials, addon-interactions, blocks, manager-api, theming). Storybook 9 is a breaking major over 8; mixing the two trees is unsupported and is what produces the `storybook` (High) and `@storybook/addon-essentials`/`addon-actions` (Moderate) audit entries. This is dev-only tooling, not shipped to users.

**Impact.** Flaky/broken Storybook builds and audit noise. No production runtime exposure since these are devDependencies, but the version mismatch makes `npm install` fragile and obscures real findings.

**Recommendation.** Align the whole Storybook toolchain on a single major (move all @storybook/* to 9.x using the `npx storybook@latest upgrade` migration, or pin everything to 8.x if staying). This clears the related audit entries and stabilizes dev builds.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 30-45 min)_**

Align the entire Storybook dev toolchain on the 9.x major. Because Storybook 9 folded addon-essentials, addon-interactions, blocks, manager-api, and theming into the core `storybook` package, the fix is not a simple version bump: remove those standalone packages, update `.storybook/main.ts` addons and the config-file imports, then run the official upgrade/dedupe so the lockfile resolves a single tree.

1. Run the official migration from Frontend/ to let Storybook rewrite config and deps: `cd "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" && npx storybook@latest upgrade`. This handles most of the steps below automatically; verify the results against the manual steps and finish anything it skips.
2. In Frontend/package.json devDependencies, remove the four packages that no longer exist as standalone modules in Storybook 9 (folded into core): delete `@storybook/addon-essentials` (line 49), `@storybook/addon-interactions` (line 50), `@storybook/blocks` (line 53). Also remove `@storybook/manager-api` (line 54) and `@storybook/theming` (line 58) — in v9 these are re-exported from the `storybook` package.
3. Bump the remaining mixed-major entries to 9.x and drop the exact pin on addon-links: change `@storybook/addon-links` from `"9.0.9"` to `"^9.0.9"` (line 51). Confirm `@storybook/addon-onboarding`, `@storybook/react`, `@storybook/react-vite`, `eslint-plugin-storybook`, and `storybook` are all `^9.0.9` (they already are).
4. Also remove the stale, deprecated test helper `@storybook/testing-library` (line 57, pinned 0.2.2) — it is superseded by `storybook/test` in v9 and is unused in the codebase (no source imports it). If you prefer to keep test utilities, replace its usages with `storybook/test` instead; a repo grep shows zero `@storybook/testing-library` imports, so removal is safe.
5. Edit Frontend/.storybook/main.ts: drop `'@storybook/addon-essentials'` and `'@storybook/addon-interactions'` from the `addons` array (these are built into v9 core). Keep `'@storybook/addon-links'`, `'@storybook/addon-onboarding'`, and `'storybook-addon-react-router-v6'`. If you still want docs/controls behavior, no addon entry is needed in v9.
6. Edit Frontend/.storybook/manager.ts: change `import { addons } from '@storybook/manager-api';` to `import { addons } from 'storybook/manager-api';`.
7. Edit Frontend/.storybook/theme.ts: change `import { create } from '@storybook/theming/create';` to `import { create } from 'storybook/theming/create';`.
8. Edit Frontend/.storybook/preview.tsx: change `import { themes } from '@storybook/theming';` to `import { themes } from 'storybook/theming';`.
9. Edit Frontend/src/stories/Configure.mdx (auto-generated docs page): replace the `@storybook/blocks` import with `@storybook/addon-docs/blocks` (the v9 location for Meta/doc blocks). If that file is not used as a docs entry, you may delete it instead.
10. Delete the existing lockfile and reinstall to collapse the duplicated 8.6.x/9.0.9 trees: `rm Frontend/package-lock.json && cd "/Users/saobranaung/Code/Ministry of Commerce/tradenet-admin-report/tradenet-admin-report/Frontend" && npm install`. Then confirm no 8.6.x @storybook packages remain in the new lockfile.

```json
// Frontend/package.json — devDependencies (before -> after)
// BEFORE
"@storybook/addon-essentials": "^8.6.14",
"@storybook/addon-interactions": "^8.6.14",
"@storybook/addon-links": "9.0.9",
"@storybook/addon-onboarding": "^9.0.9",
"@storybook/blocks": "^8.6.14",
"@storybook/manager-api": "^8.6.14",
"@storybook/react": "^9.0.9",
"@storybook/react-vite": "^9.0.9",
"@storybook/testing-library": "0.2.2",
"@storybook/theming": "^8.6.14",
// AFTER (removed: addon-essentials, addon-interactions, blocks, manager-api, theming, testing-library)
"@storybook/addon-links": "^9.0.9",
"@storybook/addon-onboarding": "^9.0.9",
"@storybook/react": "^9.0.9",
"@storybook/react-vite": "^9.0.9",

// Frontend/.storybook/main.ts — addons (before -> after)
  addons: [
    '@storybook/addon-links',
-   '@storybook/addon-essentials',
    '@storybook/addon-onboarding',
-   '@storybook/addon-interactions',
    'storybook-addon-react-router-v6',
  ],

// Frontend/.storybook/manager.ts
- import { addons } from '@storybook/manager-api';
+ import { addons } from 'storybook/manager-api';

// Frontend/.storybook/theme.ts
- import { create } from '@storybook/theming/create';
+ import { create } from 'storybook/theming/create';

// Frontend/.storybook/preview.tsx
- import { themes } from '@storybook/theming';
+ import { themes } from 'storybook/theming';

// Frontend/src/stories/Configure.mdx
- import { Meta } from '@storybook/blocks';
+ import { Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
```

**Verify:**
- From Frontend/: `grep -E '8\.6\.' package-lock.json | grep storybook` returns nothing (no 8.6.x Storybook packages remain).
- `npm ls storybook @storybook/react @storybook/react-vite @storybook/addon-links @storybook/addon-onboarding` shows every entry at 9.x with no 'invalid'/'deduped to 8.x' warnings.
- `npm run storybook` boots the dev server on :6006 and renders existing stories (e.g. Card, Logo) without 'Cannot find module @storybook/manager-api' or addon-resolution errors.
- `npm run build-storybook` completes successfully.
- `npm audit --omit=dev` (prod-only) is unaffected; `npm audit` no longer lists the `storybook` (High) or `@storybook/addon-essentials`/`addon-actions` (Moderate) advisories tied to the 8.6.x tree.
- `npm run lint` passes (eslint-plugin-storybook 9.x rules load cleanly).

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Do not just bump @storybook/addon-essentials, addon-interactions, and blocks to ^9.0.9 — those packages do not exist on the 9.x line (they were merged into core `storybook`), so npm install would fail. They must be removed, not version-bumped. Update .storybook/main.ts (addons array) and the manager-api/theming/blocks imports in the SAME change as the package.json edit; otherwise the dev server throws module-not-found at startup. Regenerate package-lock.json (delete + npm install) rather than hand-editing it, so the duplicated 8.6.x transitive deps (addon-actions, addon-docs, test, instrumenter, csf-plugin) get pruned. This is dev-only tooling (devDependencies) with zero production runtime exposure, so there is no app/data-migration risk; rollback is `git checkout` of package.json, package-lock.json, and the .storybook/*.ts(x) + Configure.mdx files.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Substance is correct; only nit is that the finder's line-range labels are slightly imprecise — e.g. the "49-53" 8.6.x range actually includes line 51 (@storybook/addon-links) and 52 (addon-onboarding) which are 9.0.x, and the 9.0.x group should also include line 52 (addon-onboarding ^9.0.9) and line 72 (eslint-plugin-storybook ^9.0.9). Net: same set of packages, mislabeled by a line or two — does not change the verdict. Severity Low is appropriate (dev-only tooling, zero production exposure); could even be Info, but Low is defensible since it can break the storybook dev/build scripts and pollutes audit output.


<a id="f-backend-no-vuln-scan"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — Backend NuGet packages have no automated vulnerability scanning

**ID:** `backend-no-vuln-scan` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Supply-chain process &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Small &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Backend/API.csproj:11-31 (packages); repo-wide gap: no Directory.Build.props, no root .github CI, no dependabot.yml — only Frontend/.github/workflows/* exists and is node-only`
- `Backend/API.csproj:11-31`
- `Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj:10-16`

**Problem.** The backend references several runtime packages (AWSSDK.S3 4.0.2, EF Core 9.0.6 stack, JwtBearer 8.0.17, System.Linq.Dynamic.Core 1.6.6, SkiaSharp 3.119.0, Swashbuckle 9.0.1) with no `dotnet list package --vulnerable` step, no NuGet audit in CI, and no Dependabot nuget ecosystem entry. The version pairing (net8.0 + JwtBearer 8.0.17 + EF Core 9.0.6) is supported by Microsoft, so this is not a true incompatibility, but the absence of scanning means any future NuGet advisory (e.g., in System.Linq.Dynamic.Core, which has had injection-style concerns, or SkiaSharp native CVEs) would go unnoticed. System.Linq.Dynamic.Core in particular is security-sensitive because it evaluates dynamic LINQ expressions.

**Impact.** Server-side dependency vulnerabilities accumulate undetected. Given the backend handles trade data and JWT auth, an unscanned advisory in the auth/serialization/dynamic-query stack could be exploitable without the team being aware.

**Recommendation.** Enable NuGet auditing (set <NuGetAudit>true</NuGetAudit> and <NuGetAuditMode>all</NuGetAuditMode> in the csproj / Directory.Build.props), add `dotnet list package --vulnerable --include-transitive` as a CI gate, and add a nuget ecosystem block to dependabot.yml. Specifically review System.Linq.Dynamic.Core usage to ensure no user input reaches dynamic LINQ string parsing.

**🛠 How to Fix _(est. 1-2 hours)_**

Add NuGet vulnerability scanning at three layers (Directory.Build.props audit props, a CI gate, and a Dependabot nuget entry); no dynamic-LINQ code change is required because the sort/filter columns are already allowlisted.

1. Create a new file `Directory.Build.props` at the REPO ROOT (next to `tradenet-admin-report.sln`, the dir that contains the Backend/ and Backend.Tests/ folders). MSBuild auto-imports it into every project under that folder, so both `Backend/API.csproj` and `Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj` inherit it without per-project edits. Put a single PropertyGroup enabling restore-time auditing of ALL (direct + transitive) packages at warning level 'low' and treat NU1901-NU1904 vulnerability warnings as errors so a vulnerable restore fails the build (see patch).
2. Do NOT also add these props to `Backend/API.csproj` / `Backend.Tests/Backend.Tests.csproj` — keep them only in Directory.Build.props to avoid duplicate/conflicting values. The existing `<PackageReference>` lines in API.csproj (lines 11-31) stay untouched.
3. Create the CI workflow directory `.github/workflows/` at the REPO ROOT (this repo currently has NO root `.github`; only `Frontend/.github/workflows/*` exists and is node-only). Add `.github/workflows/backend-audit.yml` that: checks out the repo, runs `actions/setup-dotnet@v4` pinned to `dotnet-version: 8.0.x` (the projects target net8.0), runs `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln`, then runs `dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` and fails the job if any vulnerability is reported. `dotnet list package` returns exit 0 even when vulnerabilities exist, so grep the output and explicitly exit 1 on a hit (see patch).
4. Create `.github/dependabot.yml` at the REPO ROOT with two ecosystems: a `nuget` entry rooted at `/` (Dependabot discovers `tradenet-admin-report.sln` and scans both csproj files) and an `npm` entry rooted at `/Frontend` to cover the existing React app. Schedule weekly. (See patch.)
5. Audit the System.Linq.Dynamic.Core call sites in `Backend/Model/APIResult.cs` for the record — confirmed safe but worth a code comment: `ApplySort` (line 251) and `ApplyFilter` (line 209) both gate `sortColumn`/`filterColumn` through `IsValidProperty` (line 299), which resolves the name against `typeof(T)` reflection, so only real model properties reach the parser. The user value is passed as the parameterized `@0` placeholder in every `source.Where(...)` call (lines 226, 230, 237-240, 244-247), never string-concatenated. Add a one-line `// security: column allowlisted via IsValidProperty; value bound as @0 parameter` comment above the Where calls so future edits don't break the invariant. No behavioral code change.
6. Run `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln` locally once to confirm the new audit props don't surface an already-known vulnerable transitive package that would now break the build; if one appears, bump that package before merging so main stays green.

```yaml
Directory.Build.props (NEW, repo root):
-----------------------------------------------------------
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <!-- Supply-chain: audit direct + transitive NuGet packages on every restore -->
    <NuGetAudit>true</NuGetAudit>
    <NuGetAuditMode>all</NuGetAuditMode>
    <NuGetAuditLevel>low</NuGetAuditLevel>
    <!-- Fail the build (not just warn) when a vulnerable package is restored -->
    <WarningsAsErrors>$(WarningsAsErrors);NU1901;NU1902;NU1903;NU1904</WarningsAsErrors>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

.github/workflows/backend-audit.yml (NEW, repo root):
-----------------------------------------------------------
name: Backend NuGet Audit
on:
  push:
    branches: [ main ]
  pull_request:
    branches: [ main ]
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * 1'   # weekly Monday 06:00 UTC catches new advisories
jobs:
  audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: '8.0.x'
      - name: Restore
        run: dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln
      - name: List vulnerable packages
        run: |
          dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive 2>&1 | tee audit.txt
          if grep -qiE 'has the following vulnerable packages|Critical|High|Moderate|Low' audit.txt; then
            echo "::error::Vulnerable NuGet packages detected"
            exit 1
          fi

.github/dependabot.yml (NEW, repo root):
-----------------------------------------------------------
version: 2
updates:
  - package-ecosystem: "nuget"
    directory: "/"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
  - package-ecosystem: "npm"
    directory: "/Frontend"
    schedule:
      interval: "weekly"
    open-pull-requests-limit: 10
```

**Verify:**
- Local audit: from the repo root run `dotnet list tradenet-admin-report.sln package --vulnerable --include-transitive` — output should list each project and report no vulnerable packages (or surface real ones to fix).
- Props effective: run `dotnet restore tradenet-admin-report.sln` and confirm it completes; to prove auditing is active, temporarily add a reference to a known-vulnerable package (e.g. an old `System.Net.Http`) and confirm restore now FAILS with an NU1903/NU1902 error rather than only warning — then revert.
- CI: open a PR; the new `Backend NuGet Audit` check must appear and pass. Confirm it fails when a vulnerable package is present (the grep-based `exit 1`).
- Dependabot: after merge to main, check the repo Insights > Dependency graph > Dependabot tab shows both `nuget` (root) and `npm` (/Frontend) ecosystems enabled and that it has scanned the manifests.

**Pitfalls / rollback.** Ordering: create Directory.Build.props BEFORE wiring the CI gate, and run a local `dotnet restore` first — if any current transitive dependency already has an open advisory, `WarningsAsErrors` (NU1901-1904) will immediately break the build for everyone, so fix/upgrade that package in the same PR or main goes red. NuGetAuditMode=all + AuditLevel=low is intentionally strict; if it proves too noisy for a transitive package with no available fix, you can add a `<NuGetAuditSuppress Include="https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-..." />` ItemGroup rather than weakening the level globally. Note Directory.Build.props auto-applies to ALL projects under the repo root, including Backend.Tests — that is desired here. The `dotnet list package --vulnerable` grep can false-positive if a column header literally contains one of the severity words; the current .NET 8 output format does not, but pin a comment so a future SDK output change is reviewed. Rollback is clean: delete the three new files (no source code changes), since the IsValidProperty/parameterized-@0 audit in step 5 requires no behavioral edit.

> 🔍 **Verifier note.** Caveats on the recommendation: (1) The 'review System.Linq.Dynamic.Core usage' action item is largely already satisfied — ApplySort allowlists the sort column via IsValidProperty before dynamic parsing, so no obvious user-controlled string reaches the LINQ parser; the team should still audit the Where(...) call sites (APIResult.cs:226-244) for completeness, but the core sort path is guarded. (2) The locations are accurate but incomplete — the finding is really about repo-level files that do NOT exist (no root .github, no dependabot.yml, no Directory.Build.props), so any remediation lands on new files plus the two csproj files. (3) NuGetAudit defaults to true on .NET 8 SDK during restore but only warns (not errors) by default and only covers direct deps unless NuGetAuditMode=all is set — so the explicit hardening the finder recommends is still meaningful.


<a id="f-xlsx-sheetjs-cve"></a>
#### 🔵 LOW — xlsx (SheetJS) pinned to vulnerable 0.18.5 with prototype-pollution + ReDoS CVEs, used in live export path

**ID:** `xlsx-sheetjs-cve` &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Phase:** P1 &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Category:** Known-vulnerable dependency &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Effort:** Medium &nbsp;·&nbsp; **Confidence:** High

**Location(s):**
- `Frontend/package.json:44; Frontend/package-lock.json:13695-13718; Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx:19,242-243 (sole consumer); reachable via Frontend/src/pages/User/UserList.tsx (no onExcel → client-side XLSX path)`
- `Frontend/package.json:44`
- `Frontend/package-lock.json:13695 (node_modules/xlsx version 0.18.5)`
- `Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx:19`
- `Frontend/src/components/My Components/Table/BasicTable.tsx:242-243`

**Problem.** package.json declares "xlsx": "^0.18.5" and the lockfile resolves exactly 0.18.5 from the npm registry. This version is affected by CVE-2023-30533 (Prototype Pollution, advisory GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6, fixed in 0.19.3) and CVE-2024-22363 (ReDoS, advisory GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9, fixed in 0.20.2). Crucially these versions are NOT published to npm — the maintainer pulled SheetJS from npm, so the latest you can get from npmjs.org is the vulnerable 0.18.5; the fix is only available from the SheetJS CDN (cdn.sheetjs.com). The library is not dead weight — it is actively invoked in the client-side Excel export at BasicTable.tsx:242-243 via XLSX.utils.table_to_book(...) and XLSX.writeFile(...), exactly the parse/build code paths the CVEs target.

**Impact.** When report data containing attacker-influenced cell content is rendered into the HTML table and exported, the SheetJS workbook builder can be driven into prototype pollution or catastrophic ReDoS in the operator's browser. In a government reporting context the table content originates from trade-form submissions (BusinessType/company names, etc.), so an unprivileged data submitter could plant a payload that fires in an admin's browser when they export — a stored-input-to-admin attack surface. No upgrade is reachable via npm, so a plain `npm update` will never fix it, masking the risk.

**Recommendation.** Stop sourcing xlsx from npm.
